facts: Rivian delayed construction of its previously announced $5-billion manufacturing campus in Stanton Springs, Ga.
The EV-only producer now plans to start making its upcoming R2 SUVs at an existing plant in Normal, Ill.
Rivian still plans to build the Georgia facility.
The announcement of the construction pause came after Rivian shared its annual financial results last month, revealing a $5.4-billion net loss over the year.
CEO R. J. Scaringe told investors that the company is focused on cost efficiency and long-term growth.
Rivian had planned to start construction of the 16-million-sq-ft Georgia plant early this year, for production to begin in 2026.
Grading and site preparation are complete, under a state-led project.
It was not immediately clear whether Rivian’s contractor for the plant, Clayco, Inc., had mobilized.
Anthony Johnson, president of Clayco’s industrial business unit, said in a statement to ENR that Clayco and Rivian “remain dedicated partners.”
Johnson stated: “When the time is right to break ground and advance construction of the plant, our Clayco team will be ready to support and execute our plans seamlessly.”
The contractor did not share additional details.
Moving R2 manufacturing to Rivian’s Illinois plant will allow production to start earlier in 2026 than originally planned.
This move will save more than $2.25 billion in capital spending, product development and supplier sourcing compared with launching R2 production in Georgia, according to the automaker.
Rivian did not say how soon it would resume construction in Georgia as it prepares to launch R2 production in Illinois.
Scaringe wrote in an op-ed published in The Atlanta Journal-Constitution March 8 that the company is “committed to building our future in Georgia.”
Scaringe wrote: “This shift in launch cadence puts us in a stronger position prior to launching our Georgia plant.”
Scaringe added that the company is adjusting its plans to “stay ahead” of high interest rates and geopolitical uncertainty.
In his op-ed, Scaringe wrote that the Georgia plant “remains an extremely important part of our strategy to scale production of R2 and R3.”
Rivian said the Illinois plant has capacity to produce 215,000 units per year, including the R2 and other vehicles.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp has said the planned plant would have capacity for as many as 400,000 vehicles per year.
The R2 and R3 are SUVs.
The Normal plant had previously built only Rivian's first generation R1T truck and the larger R1S SUV.
The Illinois Dept. of Commerce & Economic Opportunity said it is in discussions with Rivian for a new economic development package for building the R2 in the state.
To build and operate the plant in Georgia, the state offered Rivian an incentive package worth $1.5 billion in discretionary funding, tax credits and local incentives.
Under the terms of the economic development agreement, Rivian has until the end of 2030 to invest at least $5 billion and create 7,500 full-time jobs.
A spokesperson for the Georgia Dept. of Economic Development noted that the agreement has not been amended since Rivian announced the construction pause.
In a statement, the Georgia Chamber of Commerce said that Rivian still plans to begin operations within the period allowed under the agreement.
The Georgia Chamber stated: “The Georgia Chamber remains confident in both Rivian and the viability of the Stanton Springs North site.”
Scaringe wrote in the op-ed that Rivian “will care for the site in the run-up to construction with the goal of minimizing inconveniences this delay may cause.”
source_title: Rivian Pauses $5B Electric Vehicle Plant in Georgia | Engineering News-Record

facts: Kia Georgia is adding another electric vehicle to its production lineup.
The announcement was made Thursday at the Los Angeles Auto Show.
Company officials said the 2025 EV6 SUV will be assembled by team members at the West Point facility beginning early next year.
Governor Brian P. Kemp stated that Kia's decades-long partnership with Georgia has been highly beneficial as a job creator for the state and its people.
Governor Kemp noted that the partnership helped make Georgia the No. 1 state for business and accelerated its development as the electric mobility capital of America.
Governor Kemp thanked President Sean Yoon and Stuart Countess, as well as their teams, for the decision and the opportunities it will bring to hardworking Georgians.
The facility recently completed a $217 million expansion, adding about 200 jobs in Troup County, according to the company.
The EV6 will join the EV9 SUV and the Telluride, Sorento and Sportage SUVs on the plant’s assembly line.

Kia North America President and CEO SeungKyu (Sean) Yoon stated that the award-winning EV6 has been an integral part of Kia’s ‘Plan S’ strategy since day one and this enhancement will further the brand’s commitment to electrification.
Yoon noted that building the EV6 in Georgia is a testament to Kia’s ongoing commitment to the U.S. market and the state of Georgia.

Yoon highlighted that the facility is the home to previous World Car of the Year winners Telluride and EV9, and expressed confidence that the team will continue its legacy of successful launches with the EV6.
Kia Georgia employs more than 3,200 team members.
The past Saturday marked 15 years since the company began manufacturing in Georgia.
source_title: Kia Georgia adds 2nd electric vehicle to production lineup

facts: On September 4, 2025, American law enforcement conducted an immigration raid at the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, an electric vehicle (EV) production site in Ellabell, Bryan County, Georgia, detaining approximately 475 workers.
The raid was described as the largest immigration enforcement operation carried out at a single location by the United States Department of Homeland Security.
The raid led to a diplomatic dispute between the United States and South Korea, with over 300 Koreans detained, and increased concerns about foreign companies investing in the United States.
In 2023, Hyundai Motor and LG Energy Solution announced a joint venture in Georgia to produce EV battery cells as a $4.3 billion investment, with both companies holding a 50% stake.

The plant cost $7.6 billion and is located on a 3,000-acre site that holds a Hyundai electric vehicle manufacturing site and an EV battery joint venture plant, together employing about 1,200 people at the time of the raid.
Georgia state leaders had promised the facility would bring in 8,500 jobs and transform the rural area's economy.
In July 2025, South Korea agreed to purchase $100 billion in US energy and make a $350 billion investment in the US in return for the lowering of tariff rates by the US.

The raid involved hundreds of federal and state officers, including members of ICE, Georgia State Patrol, the FBI, DEA, ATF, and others, targeting the battery plant which was still under construction.

Homeland Security Investigations special agent Steven Schrank indicated the raid culminated a months-long investigation that included a search warrant filed on September 2 identifying four people to be searched.
During the raid, officials alleged several individuals attempted to flee into a sewage pond on the property, with one person allegedly attempting to swim under an arrest boat and flip it.
An anonymous eyewitness reported company bosses ordered all operations shut down via a deluge of phone calls during the raid.
The majority of those arrested were identified as South Korean by Schrank, though no further nationality breakdown was provided; twenty-three Mexican nationals were also detained.
On September 9, 2025, Japanese Foreign Minister Takeshi Iwaya reported the detention of three Japanese nationals during the raid.
On September 10, 2025, it was reported that 10 Chinese nationals and one Indonesian national were detained.

Republican candidate for Georgia's 12th congressional district, Tori Branum, announced she played a part in causing the raid by investigating rumors of safety concerns and undocumented immigrants using a site contact, recording conversations, and submitting evidence through the ICE website.
At least some of the detained workers were in the US on visa waivers intended for tourists and business trips up to 90 days.

A leaked document reported on September 10 showed one detained worker had a valid B1/B2 working visa and had not violated it, despite initial DHS statements claiming unauthorized work; a voluntary departure option was ordered by the Atlanta Field Office Director.
On September 12, more than 300 Korean workers returned to South Korea on a chartered Korean Air jet after President Trump delayed their departure by a day, during which only one worker chose to stay.
On September 6, dozens of protesters gathered outside the Hyundai megasite protesting against government overreach.
At least 22 other factory sites in America involving other Korean business groups were nearly halted following the raid.
Hyundai CEO José Muñoz stated the raid would delay the opening of the plant by a minimum of 2–3 months.
About two weeks after the raid, Hyundai announced the site was scheduled to receive an about $2.7 billion investment to expand production rates and create more jobs.

On September 15, South Korea announced an investigation into the raid for potential human rights violations and reviewed if demands were properly addressed, triggered by workers reporting they were not read their rights and mocked by ICE agents about North Korea and "rocket man".

Savannah Economic Development Authority President and CEO Trip Tollison told reporters on September 17 that discussions were underway to bring back detained South Korean nationals, stating it was imperative they return as they were the only ones who could install and teach future employees how to use factory technology.
A Hyundai spokesperson stated they did not believe anyone arrested was a direct employee of Hyundai Motor Company and emphasized full commitment to complying with all laws and regulations.

On September 7, 2025, Donald Trump posted on Truth Social calling on foreign companies investing in the US to respect immigration laws, encouraging them to legally bring technical talent while hiring and training American workers.

US Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem told reporters she did not think the mass detention of South Korean nationals would deter investment in the US, as it demonstrated policy certainty under the Trump administration.

On September 15, after backlash, Trump posted on Truth Social stressing foreign companies are welcome to bring skilled employees to train the domestic workforce but are expected to return home, adding he did not want to frighten off or disincentivize investment into America.

The Wall Street Journal's editorial board issued an opinion on September 12 criticizing the Trump administration, writing that raids like the one in Georgia are a deterrent to the foreign investment Donald Trump says he wants.

In a September 11, 2025 news conference, South Korean President Lee Jae Myung stated the raid could significantly impact future direct investment in the US and make South Korean companies very hesitant about investing there, calling the raid and its images bewildering.
The South Korean government expressed concern and regret over the detention of its citizens, leading to broader concerns among South Korean politicians about foreign investment in the United States.
South Korea's labour minister Kim Younghoon told the Financial Times that the treatment of detained workers felt worse than prisoners of war, causing shock among many.
The conservative South Korean daily Chosun Ilbo urged officials to convey the seriousness of the situation to Washington, questioning what the US alliance really means given the development.
Trade minister Yeo Han-koo told reporters after arriving in Washington, D.C. to finalize a bilateral tariff deal that many US officials felt the raid was excessive.
source_title: 2025 Georgia Hyundai plant immigration raid - Wikipedia

facts: Georgia no longer offers a tax credit for EV purchases or leases; it was ended by the legislature in 2016.

Carroll Electric Membership Cooperative offers a Drive Free for a Year program providing a $30 monthly energy credit during the first year of EV ownership for purchasing a new battery electric vehicle, totaling $360 in savings.
Snapping Shoals EMC offers a similar EV incentive for new purchases and leases with a $30 monthly credit intended to cover charging costs during the first year.
Tri-County EMC residential customers qualify for the EV365 program, which provides a $30 monthly bill credit during the first year of EV ownership.
EV owners in Georgia can use high occupancy vehicle (HOV) lanes on highways regardless of passenger count.
With a PeachPass, EV owners can access high occupancy toll (HOT) lanes at no additional cost.

Commercial entities registered to do business in Georgia can claim a state tax credit of up to $2,500 (up to 10%) to cover a portion of EV charger installation costs for Level 2 and DC fast charging stations.
Businesses may qualify for the federal 30C commercial charging tax credit worth up to $100,000 per installed EV charging port, available until June 30, 2026.
Georgia residents may qualify for the federal Alternative Fuel Vehicle Refueling Property Credit worth up to $1,000 to offset home EV charger installation costs.
Twelve utilities in Georgia offer rebates on EV chargers to reduce installation costs by offsetting equipment and labor costs.

Georgia Power Company (GPC) serves 2.5 million customers and offers residential customers up to $150 toward a Level 2 charger via website purchase or proof of purchase submission, with no enrollment in the GPC EV charging rate required for the rebate.
Georgia Power commercial customers can claim rebates for installing EV charging stations worth up to $30,000 per project and up to $60,000 per year.
Carroll EMC offers a $250 rebate for Level 2 EV charger installation, requiring enrollment in the Drive Free for a Year program for a total savings of $610.

Cobb EMC residential customers qualify for a $250 rebate on new Energy Star-certified Level 2 chargers with connected features, which includes automatic enrollment in the utility's EV charging program.
Cobb EMC offers a non-residential EV charger rebate program with grants ranging from $500 to $5,000 for publicly available charging projects.
Coweta-Fayetteville EMC offers a $100 rebate toward a Level 2 charger for at-home charging.
Diverse Power offers a $50 rebate per NIMA 14-30 or 14-50 outlet to help build EV-ready homes during new residential construction.
GreyStone Power Corporation offers a $250 Georgia EV charger rebate for installing a Level 2 charger for at-home charging.
Jackson EMC provides a $250 rebate toward a residential Level 2 charger upon completion of the utility's application form.
Sawnee EMC residential customers receive a $200 rebate for Level 2 chargers, requiring sign-up for the plug-in vehicle rate for at least one year.
Snapping Shoals EMC offers a $250 rebate on Level 2 chargers.
Sumter EMC customers qualify for a $250 Level 2 charger rebate for home charging.
Sumter EMC commercial customers qualify for a $500 rebate per charger valid for Level 2 and DC Fast charging.
Tri-County EMC offers a $250 rebate on Level 2 EV chargers.
The city of LaGrange is the only MEAG public power community offering a Georgia EV incentive, providing a $250 rebate toward a Level 2 charger for residential use.
Nine Georgia utilities offer time-of-use programs that provide lower rates for shifting energy consumption to off-peak hours to save on EV charging.
Georgia Power residential TOU plan charges $0.137 per kWh during on-peak hours (2 to 7 PM weekdays) and $0.0146 per kWh off-peak, with a demand charge of up to $11.70 per kWh.
Georgia Power's Overnight Advantage plan waves demand charges in exchange for higher per-kWh rates: $0.286 on-peak, $0.098 off-peak, and $0.021 super-off-peak (11 PM to 7 AM).
Georgia Power offers commercial time-of-use plans based on business size.
Georgia Power medium business rates are $0.237 per kWh on-peak and $0.059 per kWh off-peak.
Georgia Power small business rates are $0.236 per kWh on-peak and $0.1118 per kWh off-peak, dropping to $0.042 per kWh from October through May for consumption over 1,500 kWh.
Coastal Electric Cooperative offers a TOU program with rates ranging from $0.20 per kWh on-peak to $0.05 per kWh super off-peak (10 PM to 6 AM).

Cobb EMC residential NiteFlex program provides up to 400 kWh of free energy monthly between midnight and 6 AM, followed by $0.05 per kWh, plus an off-peak rate of $0.075 between 6 AM to 1 PM and 9 PM to midnight.
Cobb EMC commercial customers can sign up for a pilot program for EV charging that reduces costs by around 33% through off-peak shifting.
Coweta-Fayetteville EMC offers a TOU program with a super-off-peak rate as low as $0.065 per kWh, $0.095 per kWh off-peak, and $0.30 per kWh on-peak.
Coweta-Fayetteville EMC uses a tiered system for commercial electric rates where higher energy usage lowers the per-kWh rate.

Flint Energies EV rate includes a $35 monthly basic charge, super-off-peak rate of $0.05 per kWh (midnight to 6 AM), off-peak rate of $0.09 per kWh, a $25 first-year bill credit, and potential additional $8 monthly night charging rewards for eligible models.
GreyStone Power Corporation offers an EV rate of $0.04 per kWh during super-off-peak hours (midnight to 7 AM), which is 80% cheaper than on-peak charging (2 to 8 PM summer).
Jackson EMC offers residential EV rates on a case-by-case basis: $30 monthly flat service, $0.34 per kWh on-peak, $0.07 per kWh off-peak, and $0.06 per kWh super-off-peak.
Jackson EMC commercial TOU program charges $49 monthly for single-phase or $77 for three-phase power, with rates of $0.34 per kWh on-peak and $0.15 per kWh off-peak.
Sawnee EMC offers a standard TOU program with $0.0445 per kWh off-peak and $0.335 per kWh on-peak, plus a critical peak pricing program with a $0.95 per kWh critical peak rate.
Sawnee EMC EV charging program requires a second meter and charges $0.04 per kWh for off-peak and $0.28 per kWh for on-peak charging.
As of October 2025, Georgia has more than 2,200 public EV charging stations including over 1,800 Level 2 and 484 DC fast stations.
Georgia has 76 Tesla Supercharger stations and 1,005 Tesla Supercharger ports.
Public EV charging pricing varies by charging level and operator, generally costing more than at-home charging.
Level 3/DC Fast charging at the Atlantic Station Parking Deck in Downtown Atlanta costs $0.30 per minute.
Level 2 charging at the Honda dealership on Morrisson Parkway in Alpharetta costs $0.50 per hour for the first two hours and $3 per hour thereafter.
Free EV charging stations are available at public facilities like municipal libraries, including a free Level 2 station deployed by the City of Blakely on Main Street.
EV charging stations are located along Alternative Fuel Corridors such as I-85 and I-75, and in major cities like Atlanta, Savannah, and Athens.
Tesla's Supercharger network locations are approximately 150 miles apart along I-75 and I-85.
Georgia is offering home energy rebates funded through the Inflation Reduction Act, allocating $109.8 million for home efficiency rebates and $109.2 million for home electrification.
Home electrification rebates cover appliance rebates, insulation and air sealing, heat pumps for space and water heating, and smart electrical panel upgrades.
Qmerit is North America's most trusted and largest network of certified EV charger installers, rated #1 in customer satisfaction.
Qmerit's expert network has installed over 770,000 EV charging stations across the U.S. and Canada.
Qmerit partners with EnergySage to provide competing solar quotes from vetted installers in Georgia.
source_title: Georgia EV Rebates, Incentives & Tax Credits

facts: A multi-institutional research team led by Georgia Tech’s Hailong Chen has developed a new, low-cost cathode that could radically improve lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) — potentially transforming the electric vehicle (EV) market and large-scale energy storage systems.
The revolutionary material is iron chloride (FeCl3), which costs a mere 1-2% of typical cathode materials and can store the same amount of electricity.
Cathode materials affect capacity, energy, and efficiency, playing a major role in a battery’s performance, lifespan, and affordability.
The Chen team describes its work in Nature Sustainability.
Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) were first commercialized by Sony in the early 1990s.
Batteries are currently responsible for about 50% of an EV’s total cost, which makes these clean-energy cars more expensive than their internal combustion counterparts.
Chen’s cathode would represent a big step forward in battery technology: the development of an all-solid-state LIB.
Conventional LIBs use liquid electrolytes to transport lithium ions for storing and releasing energy, but they have hard limits on how much energy can be stored and can leak and catch fire.
All-solid-state LIBs use solid electrolytes, dramatically boosting a battery’s efficiency and reliability and making it safer and capable of holding more energy.
With the FeCl3 cathode, a solid electrolyte, and a lithium metal anode, the cost of their whole battery system is 30-40% of current LIBs.
The Chen team’s cathode contains only iron (Fe) and chlorine (Cl)—abundant, affordable, widely used elements found in steel and table salt.
In their initial tests, FeCl3 was found to perform as well as or better than the other, much more expensive cathodes.
FeCl3 has a higher operational voltage than the popularly used cathode LiFePO4 (lithium iron phosphate).
This technology may be less than five years from commercial viability in EVs.
The work was led by Chen and postdoc Zhantao Liu, who is the lead author of the study.

Collaborators included researchers from Georgia Tech’s Woodruff School (Ting Zhu) and the School of Earth and Atmospheric Sciences (Yuanzhi Tang), as well as the Oak Ridge National Laboratory (Jue Liu) and the University of Houston (Shuo Chen).
Funding was provided by the National Science Foundation under Grant Nos. 1706723 and 2108688.
Starting in 2019, Chen’s lab tried to make solid-state batteries using chloride-based solid electrolytes with traditional commercial oxide-based cathodes, but the materials did not get along.
Researchers thought a chloride-based cathode could provide a better pairing with the chloride electrolyte to offer better battery performance.
Chen holds appointments in Georgia Tech’s George W. Woodruff School of Mechanical Engineering and the School of Materials Science and Engineering.
source_title: New Battery Cathode Material Could Revolutionize EV Market and Energy Storage

facts: Is a subsidiary of South Korean-based Enchem Co., Ltd.
Specializes in manufacturing electrolytes and high-functionality additives for rechargeable batteries.
Was founded to develop essential components for electric vehicles and energy storage systems.
Is headquartered in Commerce, Georgia.
Has expanded operations to Tennessee to optimize supply chain management and meet market demand.
