facts: Solvay Specialty Polymers plans to build an $850 million plant in Augusta, Georgia, to manufacture key lithium-ion EV battery parts.
The company announced the plan last Thursday.
The polymer company will produce EV battery-grade binders and separator coatings made from polyvinylidene fluoride.
With the new site, Solvay says it will have the largest production capacity of the lithium-ion chemical ingredient in North America.
The project is a joint venture between Solvay and chemical manufacturer Orbia.
Orbia will supply raw materials for the joint venture.
The companies plan to open two production sites.
Both production sites are expected to be operational by 2026.
The Belgium-based chemical manufacturer said the joint venture would fill a “significant supply gap” for the EV battery part.
The joint venture will build off “favorable conditions promoting regional production and material security.”
Last month, Solvay received a $178 million grant for the facility through the Battery Materials Processing and Battery Component Manufacturing and Recycling program.
The grant is funded under the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law.

President of Solvay Growth Initiatives and Chief North American Officer Mike Finelli said, “We are proud to receive the federal government’s support for manufacturing technologies that will provide the U.S. with critical raw materials for building an independent, sustainable EV value chain,” regarding the grant.
Rivian made the single largest economic investment in Georgia’s history last year with plans for a $5 billion EV production facility in East Augusta, according to the governor’s office.
Last month, Hyundai broke ground on a $5.5 billion “mega campus” in Savannah, Georgia, where it will manufacture batteries and a diverse line of EVs.
EV component makers are taking note of Augusta in particular.
Electrodeposited copper foil manufacturer Denkai America will locate its North American headquarters and new manufacturing facility in the area, focusing on EV battery foil.
source_title: Georgia to get $850M EV battery parts plant | Utility Dive

facts: South Korean automaker Hyundai announced a second large supplier for its new electric car plant on the Georgia coast.
Hyundai Mobis plans to invest nearly $1 billion in a new facility in Bryan County.
The facility could employ around 1,500 workers, according to Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp.
The plant will be located in Richmond Hill.
The plant will make powertrains for Hyundai's electric vehicles manufactured at its new plant scheduled to open in 2025 about 30 miles west of Savannah.
The plant will also manufacture components for a Kia plant in West Point.
The plant will also manufacture components for another Hyundai plant in Montgomery, Alabama.
Earlier this month, Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp announced that Joon Georgia would invest $317 million and hire 630 workers for a Hyundai parts plant in Statesboro.

Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp stated: “When we celebrated the groundbreaking of Hyundai’s new electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facility in Bryan County, we knew it would unleash transformational job creation and investment in that entire region of our state.”
In October, Kemp attended a groundbreaking ceremony for the new Hyundai plant in Bryan County.
The company projects the new Hyundai plant will make up to 300,000 vehicles a year.
Georgia officials say it’s the largest economic development project the state has ever seen.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis to invest nearly $1B in new supply plant in Bryan County - The Christian Index

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group and SK On EV Battery Manufacturing Facility
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and SK On Joint Venture
facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Hyundai Motor Group (HMG) and SK On have selected a site in Bartow County for a new electric vehicle (EV) battery manufacturing facility that will supply Hyundai Motor Group’s plants in the U.S.
The project is considered one of the largest economic development projects in Georgia state history.
Stakeholders estimate it will create more than 3,500 new jobs through approximately $4-5 billion of investment in Bartow County.
Hyundai Motor Group and SK On recently signed a memorandum of understanding (MOU) regarding the partnership for a new EV battery facility in the U.S., with details still in development.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that Hyundai Motor Group and SK On are valued partners and key players in Georgia’s ever-growing automotive industry, emphasizing his administration's focus on bringing jobs to overlooked communities and praising their investment in the No. 1 state for business.
SK On was established in 2021 as the lithium-ion battery subsidiary branch of SK Innovation.
SK On currently employs more than 2,000 Georgians at its SK Battery America facility in Commerce.
In July, SK Battery America announced it would partner with the Work for Warriors Georgia program to hire veterans, servicemembers, and their families at its Georgia operations.
Subject to execution of relevant agreements and final board decisions, the new facility will be located at Bartow Centre, a zoned manufacturing and industrial site on Highway 411.
The project is aiming to begin operations in 2025.

Bartow County Sole Commissioner Steve Taylor expressed pleasure with the decision, thanked the former property owners, and noted the project will establish Bartow in Georgia’s EV ecosystem while leveraging the area's advanced manufacturing talent base.
Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on the competitive project.
The project involved partnerships with Georgia EMC, Development Authority of Bartow County, Cartersville-Bartow County Department of Economic Development, and Georgia Quick Start.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson highlighted that HMG and SK have been pioneering partners for decades, driving Georgia’s automotive renaissance, and noted the partnership helps create a fully integrated supply chain connecting battery manufacturers with recyclers.
Georgia’s prime location, major ports system, extensive infrastructure, skilled workforce, and pro-business climate position it as a hub for the electric transportation industry.
Excluding this announcement, EV-related projects announced in Georgia since 2020 total approximately $17 billion in investment and more than 22,800 new jobs.
Hyundai Motor Group separately announced in May plans to invest $5.54 billion in Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, an EV manufacturing facility in Bryan County.
Hyundai Motor Group broke ground on the Metaplant America facility in October with Governor Kemp and other state, local, and federal officials.
Hyundai secures EV battery supply from SK On for its plants in North America.
source_title: Hyundai and SK On to build electric vehicle battery manufacturing facility in Bartow County

facts: South Korea-based Hanon Systems is a global provider of automotive thermal and energy management solutions for conventional and electric vehicles.
Hanon Systems will invest more than $40 million in a manufacturing plant in Bulloch County.
The Hanon Systems project is expected to create 160 jobs.
Gov. Brian welcomed Hanon Systems to Georgia on Tuesday while in Israel leading a trade mission, stating that Georgia’s automotive and e-mobility industries continue to grow.
Gov. Brian stated that Georgia is furthering its position as the EV capital of America with new jobs and opportunities coming.
Billy Allen, chairman of the Development Authority of Bulloch County, said Hanon will work closely with Hyundai Motor Group.
Hyundai Motor Group is building a huge EV manufacturing plant in Bryan County west of Savannah with a $5.5 billion investment.
The Hanon Systems plant is expected to begin operations in May of next year.
Min Sung, Hanon’s president and CEO, said the company is pleased to be contributing to the local economy of Bulloch County through job creation.
Min Sung stated that the announcement aligns with Hanon's strategy to support the industry shift toward electrification while growing its North America footprint.
The Hyundai plant broke ground last October and has been attracting EV suppliers to Bulloch County and other areas near Savannah.
Ecoplastic Corp. announced plans in January to build an automotive parts plant in Bulloch that will create 456 jobs.
Auto parts manufacturer Joon Georgia unveiled plans last November for a new manufacturing plant in Bulloch that will create 630 jobs.

Pat Wilson, commissioner of the Georgia Department of Economic Development, noted that Bulloch County is part of the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority and has been part of this growing success story from the beginning.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development's Global Commerce team worked on the Hanon Systems project with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Power, the Georgia Ports Authority, and the Technical College System of Georgia’s Quick Start program.
source_title: Bulloch County draws second EV supplier - The Augusta Press

facts: The I-16 Frontage Road Project, US 280 Widening Project, and Old Cuyler Road Interchange are three initiatives currently overseen by the Georgia Department of Transportation (GDOT) in Bryan County, Georgia.
These infrastructure projects are situated just outside of Savannah and aim to reinforce and expand infrastructure around the Bryan County Mega-Site.
The Bryan County Mega-Site is hosting both a Hyundai electric vehicle assembly plant and an adjacent EV battery factory.
The mega-site development promises to generate 8,000 employment opportunities for the region.
C.W. Matthews is contracted for all three infrastructure projects.
The I-16 Frontage Road Project is priced at $29.7 million.
The US 280 Widening Project is priced at $83 million.
The Old Cuyler Interchange project is priced at $75 million.
The I-16 Frontage Road Project provides a new access road alongside I-16 to enhance connectivity for the Mega-Site between US280 and the new Old Cuyler Road Interchange.
The US 280 Widening Project focuses on expanding the roadway and incorporating five roundabouts along US 280 to improve traffic flow and safety in the area.
The Old Culyer project adds additional direct interstate access and egress to and from I-16 and the mega-site via the I-16 Frontage Road project.
A significant increase in regional traffic is anticipated due to the ongoing development of the mega-site, underscoring the critical role these projects will play in alleviating impending congestion.
C.W. Matthews has formed a joint venture with McLendon Enterprises to ensure effective execution of these projects.
McLendon Enterprises has been an established presence in Southeast Georgia since 1979 and brings invaluable local expertise to the partnership.
C.W. Matthews contributes its Design Build knowledge to the joint venture.
Both C.W. Matthews and McLendon will actively participate in the construction of these projects.
The collaboration creates an opportunity for C.W. Matthews to engage in an area of Georgia that is newer to the company.
The collaborative approach underscores a shared commitment to advancing infrastructure and fostering economic growth in Bryan County, Georgia.
These infrastructure projects hold substantial promise for the local community and economy.
source_title: Making Way for Bryan County's Hyundai Megasite » C.W. Matthews

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) is an electric vehicle production site operated by Hyundai located in Ellabell, Bryan County, Georgia, United States.
The facility was built between 2022 and 2024 and began operations in October 2024.
The $7.6billion campus is located along Interstate16 about 25 miles west of downtown Savannah.
The site contains over 16million square feet of factory floor space across 2,284 acres.
The address is 1500 Genesis Drive Ellabell, Georgia, U.S.
The owner is Hyundai Motor Group.
The company will produce batteries in a joint venture with LG Energy Solution at a separate site now under construction.
The land was previously designated by Bryan County as the Bryan County Mega Site.
Volvo considered the area for a new manufacturing plant in 2015 but ultimately built a factory in South Carolina instead.

In 2021, the state of Georgia, Bryan and Chatham counties, and the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority purchased the area for $61million, combining three separately-owned parcels.
The site lies directly off Interstate16, with existing ClassI rail links and close access to Interstate 95 and the Port of Savannah.
Discussions between Hyundai and government officials began in January 2022.
Hyundai announced the factory in April 2022.
Construction on the site began in October 2022.

A groundbreaking ceremony was held on October25, attended by Hyundai CEO and chairman Chung Eui-sun, Georgia governor Brian Kemp, U.S. senators Jon Ossoff and Raphael Warnock, U.S. representative Buddy Carter, and deputy Secretary of Commerce Don Graves.

Hyundai announced it would establish both the automotive and battery manufacturing facilities prior to the introduction of the Inflation Reduction Act and stated that the law had not influenced the company's decisions.
Full production at HMGMA began in October 2024, with the first model produced being the 2025 Ioniq5.
Hyundai plans to offer tours of the factory to the public.
Immigration authorities raided the site on September4, 2025, detaining about 450 workers, most of them South Korean nationals.
The United States Department of Homeland Security described the raid as the largest immigration enforcement operation carried out at a single location.
The detained workers had been involved with the construction of a battery plant to be operated in partnership with LG Energy Solution.
The raid prompted diplomatic concern from the South Korean government and delayed production by two to three months.
LG stated that its employees and those from its partnering companies had been taken into custody.
Hyundai said that none of those detained were its employees.
HMGMA and its supporting factories have been called the largest economic development project in Georgia history.
The automobile assembly plant represents a $7.6billion investment by Hyundai.
The plant employed over 1,400 people as of October 2024, with plans to hire 8,100 by 2031.
Around 3,200 people worked at the factory as of October 2025.
The site includes a $4.3billion battery manufacturing facility called the HL-GA Battery Company, operated as a joint venture between Hyundai and LG Energy Solution.
In the surrounding area, 17 suppliers have announced new factories to support HMGMA, which combined will invest an additional estimated total of $2.7billion and employ another 7,000 workers.
Supporting manufacturers include DAS Corporation (metal seat frames), Doowon Climate Control America, Hanon Systems, PHA, Seoyon E-HWA, and Woory Industrial.
The Georgia Department of Transportation has planned infrastructure upgrades including widening US280, constructing frontage roads along I-16, and expanding the interchange at Old Cuyler Road.
Hyundai calls the plant a Metaplant and its employees Meta Pros.
According to Hyundai, the factory uses autonomous vehicles instead of conveyor belts to move vehicle bodies.
Hyundai expects around 70% of completed vehicles to be shipped by rail via the Georgia Central Railway, and constructed an additional rail siding to handle the volume of autorack cars.
Logistical solutions for the factory are handled by Hyundai GLOVIS EV Logistics America, a subsidiary of Hyundai Glovis.
The water tower at the factory site along I-16 features artwork by students from Savannah College of Art and Design.
Hyundai plans to reach full production of over 500,000 vehicles per year at HMGMA.
Vehicles produced include the Hyundai Ioniq5 (2024–present), Hyundai Ioniq9 (2024–present), and Kia Sportage Hybrid (2026–present).

A 2024 investigation by Savannah television station WTOC11 found that during construction from April 2023 to July 2024, 53 injuries were reported with 14 described as traumatic and at least one worker died.
The Occupational Safety and Health Administration opened more than a dozen investigations, and multiple subcontractors were sued or fined for safety violations.
In March 2025, a man was killed by a forklift driver, and in May 2025, another worker was crushed to death.

In October 2024, the Georgia Environmental Protection Division approved withdrawals of 6.625million U.S. gallons of water per day from the Floridan aquifer and allowed for the drilling of four new wells in Bulloch County.
Bryan County is legally required to implement an alternative water source for HMGMA within 25 years as a temporary solution.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America - Wikipedia

entity_name: Battery Belt / Southern EV Manufacturing Boom
facts: Construction is underway at the Toyota Battery mega-site in Liberty, North Carolina.
Production at the Toyota Battery site is slated to begin in 2025.
By 2030, the Toyota Battery complex will be fully operational as a 7 million-square-foot facility.
The Toyota Battery complex will feature 14 production lines: 10 dedicated to batteries for electric vehicles and plug-in hybrid electrics, and four for hybrid electric vehicles.
The Toyota Battery complex will be operated by 5,100 workers.
The total population of Liberty, North Carolina is 2,655.
The Toyota Battery mega-site carries a $13.9 billion price tag.
Federal and state governments are dubbing the region the “Battery Belt” to describe a manufacturing boom across nearly every aspect of the EV supply and production chain.
In Kings Mountain, North Carolina, two companies backed by a $90 million Department of Defense grant plan to drain a former open-pit lithium mine lake and resume mining.
The Kings Mountain mining project is expected to yield enough lithium for 1.2 million electric vehicles per year.
This lithium will be refined at a soon-to-open $1.3 billion facility in Chester County, South Carolina.
Much of the dramatic expansion in EV investment is a result of the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) passed in 2022.
The IRA included tremendous financial incentives, largely grants and tax credits, to encourage cleaner energy production.
State governments are adding record-breaking incentive deals on EV manufacturers in the form of tax breaks, infrastructure development, and workforce development funding.
Georgia will give Hyundai up to $2.1 billion in tax breaks and other state assistance for its $7.9 billion capital investment in an EV and battery production complex outside Savannah.
The Hyundai complex in Georgia is forecast to employ up to 8,500 workers.
Alongside reducing greenhouse gas emissions and slowing climate change, the IRA is part of a U.S. effort to compete with China, which currently produces the most EVs at the lowest prices.
Stephen Roach, former chair of Morgan Stanley Asia, told Axios in July: “We want to contain China, even if it comes at the expense of addressing the world’s climate change crisis.”
The Biden administration recently imposed massive tariffs on imports from China related to various aspects of EV production.
These policies aim to create an entirely domestic supply and production chain for electric vehicles.
Since the IRA passage, companies have announced $110 billion in capital investments in new EV manufacturing.
Roughly half of that money and corresponding jobs are destined for the U.S. South, according to the EV Jobs Hub.
Eighty-five percent of the investments are concentrated in right-to-work states.
S&P Global Market Intelligence estimates that as many as two-thirds of EV jobs may eventually be located in the South.
Michigan tops the list in EV capital investments, followed closely by Georgia ($23 billion), North Carolina ($20 billion), and Tennessee ($17 billion).
In Tennessee, a joint venture between Ford and SK On is investing $5.6 billion to develop BlueOval City outside of Memphis.
BlueOval City is a six-square-mile complex where 6,000 workers will produce EVs and batteries.
The company boasts BlueOval City will be “among the largest auto manufacturing facilities in U.S. history.”
The BlueOval City site will receive batteries from a BlueOval SK Battery Park in Kentucky, a $5.8 billion project that will employ another 5,000 people.
Outside of Columbia, South Carolina, Scout Motors (owned and operated by Volkswagen) broke ground on a $2 billion site.
The Scout Motors site is expected to be operational in 2026, employ 4,000 workers, and produce 200,000 vehicles a year.
South Carolina is the country’s number one exporter of tires and home to BMW’s massive 11,000-worker plant in Spartanburg.
The BMW Spartanburg plant will soon begin receiving batteries from a nearby facility operated by AESC.
In April, AESC announced an expansion adding a second battery production facility for a total investment of $3.1 billion.

Auto and other industries have been steadily increasing their footprint in Southern states for several decades, drawn to a reactionary, pro-business climate with the lowest union density, lowest wages, and most stringent anti-labor laws.

Georgia Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson told the Financial Times last October: “The fact that you have these massive labor stoppages in the traditional union states definitely will benefit Southern states as companies are looking to grow.”
In March, Georgia passed a law forbidding companies that receive state incentives from recognizing a union by any method other than secret ballot elections.
South Carolina Governor Henry McMaster pledged in his State of the State address to “fight [unions] to the gates of hell.”
McMaster and five other Southern governors issued a joint statement contending that the UAW drive “threatens the values we live by” and would “certainly put our states’ jobs in jeopardy.”
Nearly every new foreign-owned auto plant opened in the U.S. since the 1990s has chosen the South for its base of operations.
The Midwest's share of automotive jobs has fallen from around 60 percent to around 45 percent, while the South’s share grew from 15 percent in 1990 to 30 percent today.
The South is the region that 60 percent of Black workers call home, along with a growing population of immigrant workers.
Nearly two-thirds of plant construction investments in Southern states are for manufacturing beyond EVs.
The UAW is committing $40 million over the next two years to organize 13 non-union automakers, largely concentrated in the South.
Workers at Ultium Cells in Lordstown, Ohio, which manufactures batteries for General Motors, recently won a strong agreement providing the UAW’s initial step into the EV industry.
Chattanooga Volkswagen workers won their union vote overwhelmingly in April.
Several weeks later, Daimler Truck North America workers in North Carolina, Georgia, and Tennessee won a record contract.
Workers at Mercedes in Alabama came up short in their May vote on unionizing, but the union continues organizing to build worker committees and shop floor militancy.
Last year, workers in Georgia at Blue Bird, the country’s second-largest bus manufacturer which produces electric buses, won a victory to join the Steel Workers (USW).
Workers at New Flyer in Alabama, another manufacturer of electrified buses, voted yes this year for the Industrial Division of the Communications Workers (IUE-CWA).
The rise of the EV industry presents organizing challenges due to huge plant sizes and looming anti-union Supreme Court decisions.
Localized supply chains and vertically integrated operations where battery, parts, and whole car are made at one site may open opportunities for workers.
Workers can identify key chokepoints in the production process where a small group taking action has outsized power to disrupt operations.
The Southern Workers Assembly, a cross-sector rank-and-file network, recently launched a program aimed at getting workers jobs in strategic sectors to organize.
Ben Carroll lives in Durham, North Carolina and is the organizing coordinator for the Southern Workers Assembly.
source_title: New ‘Battery Belt’ Opens Organizing Front in the South | Labor Notes

facts: Hyundai Motor Group announced $21 billion in U.S. investments at the White House.
Hyundai Motor Group executives traveled to southern Georgia to celebrate electric vehicles being assembled there and future plans.
The EV assembly facility is located in Savannah, Ga.
source_title: Hyundai touts American investment at Georgia EV plant - Automotive News

facts: Hyundai's new $7.6 billion electric vehicle factory in Georgia opened on Wednesday.
The new Hyundai plant is the company's biggest.
The opening of the Georgia facility is a significant step in Hyundai's growth.
Hyundai operates two major automotive plants in the U.S.
One of Hyundai's major U.S. automotive plants is located in Georgia.
The other of Hyundai's major U.S. automotive plants is located in Alabama.
source_title: How many Hyundai plants are in the U.S.?

facts: Hyundai is constructing facilities in Savannah, Georgia, dedicated to the production of electric vehicles (EVs) and batteries.
The new facility will be Hyundai’s first EV-only plant in the United States.
Hyundai anticipates investing $5.5 billion in the new facility.
Suppliers will contribute an additional $1 billion to the project.
The facility is located on a 2,923-acre site.
Construction of the facility began in early 2023.
Hyundai anticipates that production will begin on the site in the first half of 2025.
The EV facility is expected to produce 300,000 vehicles per year.
The project will create approximately 8,100 new jobs.
Hyundai does not identify which specific EV models will be produced at the factory.
Hyundai stated that a “broad range” of vehicles will be produced on Georgia’s assembly lines.
Details about the battery-building plant are limited to it “will be built through a strategic collaboration.”
Hyundai's current electric vehicle lineup includes the Kona Electric, Ioniq 5, and the Nexo powered by a hydrogen fuel cell.
Hyundai-owned Kia offers the all-electric EV6 and Niro.
Hyundai’s luxury Genesis brand offers the GV60, GV70, and GV80 EVs.
In addition to the new Georgia facilities, Hyundai declared it will invest more than $10 billion in the United States by 2025.
The broader U.S. investment will explore diverse technologies such as autonomous driving, robotics, artificial intelligence, and advanced air mobility.
The $5.5 billion set out for the new Georgia EV factory is part of that reserve.
The state of Georgia signed a deal with Rivian earlier this month offering $1.5 billion in tax breaks to entice the company to relocate to the state.
Rivian's planned factories will employ 7,500 people by 2028 and generate 400,000 EVs each year.
Toyota is constructing a $1.29 billion battery factory in North Carolina.
General Motors intends to bring its third EV battery manufacturing plant to Michigan.
Stellantis, the parent company of Jeep, Dodge, and Chrysler, is planning to develop an EV manufacturing plant in the United States, though the location has yet to be determined.
Ford and SK Innovation are bringing new EV-focused plants to Tennessee and Kentucky.
Tesla already has numerous factories in the United States dedicated to making batteries and EVs.
Tesla recently established another factory in Austin, Texas.
source_title: Hyundai is constructing EV and battery manufacturing plants in Georgia

facts: Hyundai Motor Group has committed $12.6 billion to Georgia, making it the largest publicly backed economic development project in state history.
The Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) is located in Bryan County near Ellabell, approximately 25 minutes west of Savannah.
The facility is a next-generation EV manufacturing campus rapidly reshaping the industrial real estate landscape along the Savannah–Interstate 16 corridor.
The Ellabell facility is often described as a “museum of modern manufacturing.”
The plant has a target capacity of 500,000 EVs annually.
The facility projects 8,500 direct employees.
Approximately 7,000 additional satellite workers are expected.
The plant will utilize over 850 robots.
The facility will deploy 300 automated guided vehicles (AGVs).
The plant uses AI-driven logistics and procurement systems.
The plant produces the Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9 models.
The vehicles are built on an advanced 800-volt EV architecture designed for ultrafast charging.
The facility operates as an automation-first, robotics-driven, just-in-time precision production site rather than a labor-intensive legacy factory.
A $4.3 billion joint venture battery facility with LG Energy Solution is located next to the vehicle assembly plant.
The joint venture battery plant will begin manufacturing lithium-ion cells for Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis models in 2026.
Battery manufacturing's energy intensity, material heaviness, logistical complexity, and capital density force supplier proximity and create industrial clusters.
As of early 2026, over 20 Hyundai suppliers have announced facilities in Georgia.
Billions in committed supplier capital are being deployed.
Thousands of supplier jobs have been announced.
Supplier expansion is occurring across multiple counties along the I-16 corridor.
The Savannah Harbor–I-16 Corridor serves as an EV manufacturing spine linking Bryan County, Chatham County, Effingham County, and Bulloch County.
The ecosystem is expanding through logistics optimization, battery integration, port access, and energy infrastructure rather than traditional labor intensity.
The Port of Savannah is one of the fastest-growing container ports in the United States.
The port adjacency enables inbound shipments of battery materials, electronics, and components for the Metaplant.
The port adjacency facilitates outbound exports of finished EVs from the Metaplant.
The combination of port access, battery manufacturing, and vehicle assembly creates a logistics multiplier effect for the corridor.
Strongest land demand for the 0–20 mile radius around Bryan County/Ellabell focuses on 10–40 acre tracts with I-16 access for sequencing and just-in-time suppliers.
Land demand in the 20–60 mile radius (Pooler, Effingham, Statesboro) targets tier 1 and tier 2 suppliers needing power-capable sites and 200K–500K SF pad-ready parcels.
There is a 3–5 year value-creation window for entitled, infrastructure-ready land in the corridor.
The Metaplant's hyper-automation drives demand for Industrial Outdoor Storage (IOS) outside the facility fence.
High-probability IOS applications include trailer drop yards, rack and dunnage storage, battery material staging, contractor laydown yards, and logistics overflow.
Key IOS nodes are located at I-16 interchanges near Ellabell, the I-95/Pooler logistics belt, and parcels adjacent to the battery plant.

Successful brokers in the corridor will track Hyundai supplier announcements, map power capacity and transmission upgrades, identify battery-adjacent zoning, and position themselves as EV corridor specialists.
The supply chain is developing in four waves: the Hyundai anchor, battery integration, tier 1/tier 2 clustering, and logistics/overflow services.
Key risks to monitor include EV demand volatility, federal tax credit shifts, trade policy changes, labor and immigration enforcement disruptions, and power capacity constraints.
The facility's automation-heavy design reduces downside risk compared to traditional labor-driven auto factories.
The emerging ecosystem will feature high capital per square foot, lower labor density, heavy robotics integration, battery-first supply chains, and port-optimized logistics.
The Savannah–Bryan County–Interstate 16 corridor is becoming one of the most advanced EV manufacturing ecosystems in North America.
source_title: How to profit off Hyundai's $12 Billion Metaplant in GA

facts: Bojangles has installed its first electric vehicle charging station.
The new charging hub is located in Savannah, Ga.
The Savannah location was developed in partnership with XLR8 America and Energy and Environmental Design Services.
This installation marks the first milestone in a larger initiative to bring EV charging infrastructure to Bojangles restaurants across the country.
Richard Del Valle, chief information officer at Bojangles, stated that driving trends are changing along with customer expectations.
Del Valle noted that with EV charging, time becomes an asset and the company is turning the stop into a meaningful experience for customers.
The Savannah charging hub serves as the first step in a planned national rollout for the 870-unit Bojangles brand.
Bojangles currently operates locations in 23 states.
Future stations will vary between level two (medium) and level three (high) speed charging efficiency.
The charging stations are designed to maintain over 97% uptime.
source_title: Bojangles Powers Up With First EV Charging Station

facts: Local and state leaders gathered on Thursday, July 18, 2024 to celebrate the grand opening of AJIN Georgia in Bulloch County.
AJIN Georgia is the first Tier 1 supplier announced for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
The facility will produce interior metal and structural parts.
Construction of the facility kicked off in early 2023.
Jamie Calloway serves as the HR General Manager of AJIN Georgia.

Attendees included HMGMA CEO Onchung Gwon, representatives from MOBIS, state and local officials, the Korean consulate, and academic leaders from Georgia Southern University and Ogeechee Technical College.
Representatives from Ajin USA and Wooshin in Alabama were also in attendance.
Mr. Jungho Sea serves as the CEO and President of AJIN Georgia.
Billy Allen is the Chair of the Development Authority of Bulloch County.
The facility is located at Bruce Yawn Commerce Park.
Bulloch County has successfully attracted three to four Hyundai suppliers.
Sydne Smith is the COO of the Georgia Department of Economic Development and conveyed congratulations from Governor Brian Kemp and Commissioner Pat Wilson.
AJIN Georgia will create 630 new jobs.
State Senator Billy Hickman represents District 4.
A Georgia Southern University economic impact study highlights the vital role of manufacturing in the local economy.
State Senator Billy Hickman stated that Ajin Georgia, HMGMA, and other suppliers create career opportunities for local youth to stay or return home after college.
The Council General of the Republic of Korea congratulated AJIN Georgia on its successful site selection.
The Council General of the Republic of Korea praised AJIN Georgia's role in establishing an electric vehicle supply chain in the southeastern U.S.
The event highlighted the economic and cultural partnership between Korea and the U.S.
The plant cost $317 million.
The facility spans 82 acres.
The facility includes hundreds of robots handling most production processes.
Over 200 of the initial 300 employees have been hired.
A hiring push is planned for August to fill the remaining positions.
AJIN Georgia is providing career opportunities for citizens with no production experience.
The company can train an employee in less than a week to perform most production jobs.
The grand opening of AJIN Georgia marks a significant milestone for the community and the broader economic landscape.
source_title: AJIN Georgia grand opening marks major milestone for Bulloch County's economic growth  - Grice Connect

facts: Governor Brian Kemp announced that Joon Georgia, Inc., an automotive parts manufacturer, will create 630 new jobs and invest $317 million in Bulloch County.
Plans for the company’s new manufacturing facility mark it as the first confirmed supplier for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America in Bryan County.
Joon Georgia is part of Ajin USA, a current supplier for multiple Hyundai Motor Group facilities.
The company produces a variety of automotive body parts and electronics for original equipment manufacturers.
Jung Ho Sea, CEO of Ajin USA, stated that Joon Georgia will create approximately 630 new jobs with competitive benefits in Bulloch County and the State of Georgia.
Joon Georgia’s new facility will be located at Bruce Yawn Commerce Park, previously known as the Southern Gateway Commerce Park, near Statesboro and in close proximity to the Port of Savannah.
Operations at the Bruce Yawn Commerce Park are expected to begin in mid-2024.
The company will begin hiring for the new facility in mid-2023.

Billy Allen, Chair of the Development Authority of Bulloch County, noted that education resources including Georgia Southern University and Ogeechee Technical College will provide critical workforce support to help Joon Georgia succeed.

Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project in partnership with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Quick Start, Georgia Ports Authority, and Georgia EMC.
Workforce recruitment for the new facility will be supported by Quick Start, which is described as the top-ranked workforce training program in the nation.

By partnering with the Technical College System of Georgia, Quick Start helps assess workers, train new employees on unique processes, and develop customized job-specific training using the most current techniques and media.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Hyundai Motor Group, its suppliers, and the Savannah area JDA are creating generational opportunities for communities in Georgia.
Since 2020, more than 30 EV-related projects have contributed over $13.3 billion in investment and announced more than 18,100 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Non-affiliated suppliers connected to the Hyundai project are predicted to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around the state.
source_title: First Hyundai Motor Group Supplier to Build Facility in Bulloch County • The Georgia Virtue

facts: Governor Brian Kemp announced that Aspen Aerogels will open a manufacturing plant in Bulloch County, Ga.
The project could add more than 250 jobs and more than $300 million in investment.
Hundreds of new jobs are on the way to Bulloch County.
They will build in the Southern Gateway Commerce Park near the interchange of I-16 and US Hwy 301.
The plant will make thermal barriers that will insulate batteries on electric cars.
Aspen will be the first industry to build at the Southern Gateway Commerce Park.
Transport America is already building a huge convenience center and truck maintenance shop inside the park.

Benjy Thompson, Development Authority of Bulloch County, stated that the project represents advanced manufacturing in the electric vehicle space, noting that the number of jobs, amount of investment, and pay make it a huge project.
source_title: New company bringing jobs to Bulloch Co.

facts: North Carolina is suing automaker VinFast after the Vietnam-based company allegedly failed to meet obligations related to the construction of a proposed electric vehicle and battery manufacturing plant in Chatham County.
Attorney General Jeff Jackson filed the lawsuit on Thursday.

The lawsuit alleges that because VinFast “breached its agreements with the state,” North Carolina is exercising its contractual right to acquire the 1,977-acre site and protect taxpayer money tied to the project.
VinFast said it does not expect the facility to be operational until 2028.
The state argued that a 2028 operational date is two years past the agreed upon construction deadline.
North Carolina awarded a job development investment grant for the project.
North Carolina appropriated up to $450 million for site development and infrastructure improvements in 2022.
Four years ago, VinFast, an all-electric vehicle manufacturer, agreed to invest more than $3 billion in a manufacturing facility in Chatham County.
The original agreement included plans to create 7,500 jobs.
At the time of the announcement, the project was considered the largest economic development announcement in North Carolina history.
The state said there has been “zero progress” on the project since the initial agreement.
The state argued that VinFast had “defaulted” on its agreements.
Attorney General Jeff Jackson stated: “Vinfast agreed to build a factory and create jobs for North Carolinians — it didn’t do either.”
Jackson added: “When North Carolina makes a deal, we build in protection for taxpayers. VinFast broke the deal, so we’re using that protection to find a project for this site that will create jobs.”
According to the state’s complaint, VinFast failed to begin “vertical construction” of its buildings and structures as of January 2024.
VinFast has not provided evidence that the project could be completed on a delayed timeline.
VinFast had requested an extension until July 1, 2028.
VinFast cited slowdowns in the EV market affecting the project and its ability to secure funding as reasons for the extension request.
The state declined VinFast’s request for an extension.
The state argued that VinFast won’t be able to meet its job creation commitments this year.
VinFast failed to file its 2025 annual report.
VinFast failed to respond to state requests for a project update.
VinFast ceased operations on the site in December 2024, according to the complaint.
The complaint states: “VinFast has failed to take any concrete action that shows it can and will fulfill its obligations.”
The complaint notes: “Under the terms of the agreements, the State has a right and a duty to protect taxpayer dollars and ensure a prime industrial site is used for its highest and best purpose.”
In January, North Carolina’s Justice Department notified VinFast that it had defaulted on its agreements.
VinFast argued that it had met its construction deadlines.
VinFast plans to open the facility by 2028.
source_title: North Carolina sues EV maker VinFast over unbuilt factory | Manufacturing Dive

facts: A $4 billion business development project is coming to Chatham County.
VinFast, an electric vehicle and battery maker based in Vietnam, chose Triangle Innovation Point in Moncure for a new production facility.
The site is expected to bring 7,500 local jobs to the area.
Chatham County landed the largest economic development package in North Carolina’s history.
The Chatham Economic Development Corporation (EDC) has been pitching the idea of an auto manufacturer in Chatham for more than 10 years.
Chatham Park, a mixed-use development, is set to open in 2045 with 60,000 residents and 22,000 homes.
The region covers 710 square miles in the heart of North Carolina with over 70,000 residents spanning the towns of Pittsboro, Siler City and Goldston.
A majority of the population lives in rural areas.
VinFast supports North Carolina’s goal of shifting to electric vehicles.

Chatham County Manager Dan LaMontagne stated the investment will benefit the community for decades by employing thousands to build environmentally responsible vehicles that reduce the carbon footprint.
Central Carolina Community College has educational pipelines in place to support the workforce needed for the EV industry.
Chris Ehrenfeld, owner and CEO of BOLD and former chairman, noted Chatham County's growing residential and commercial real estate markets signal a bright economic future.
The 64 corridor running east-west across Chatham is expected to see incredible growth and job creation from this project, Chatham Park, and the eventual development of the CAM site.
source_title: The Economic Future Of Chatham Is Looking Brighter (And More Eco-Friendly) Than Ever

facts: SK Battery America Inc. laid off nearly 1,000 workers at a manufacturing plant northeast of Atlanta.
The layoffs are amid automakers’ changing electrification plans and uncertain consumer demand for EVs.
March 6 marked the last working day for 958 plant employees.
The layoffs represent about 37% of its workforce.
A Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) notice was filed by human resources chief Chuck Moore.
Impacted workers will be paid through May 6.
The plant will continue to employ about 1,600 workers.
SK opened the $2.6 billion battery plant in Commerce, Georgia, in January 2022.
The Korean company notably supplied the Ford F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck.
Ford announced plans to cancel the fully electric version of the truck in December.
The U.S. electric vehicle market is at a standstill amid the Trump administration steering federal support away from electrification.
The Trump administration is favoring more lax automotive emissions policies and a broader agenda supporting the oil and gas industries.
SK Americas spokesperson Joe Guy Collier stated the workforce reduction was made to align operations to market conditions.
Collier said SK Battery America remains committed to Georgia and to building a robust U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing.
SK is pursuing a range of future customers, including in the Battery Energy Storage System arena.
The City of Commerce and the Jackson County commission chair did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
Ford said in December that it would scrap the fully-electric version of its iconic pickup truck and opt for an extended-range version.
A Ford spokesperson said it could not comment on supplier personnel actions.
SK and Ford had together previously invested $11.4 billion in joint battery plants in the U.S.
The battery maker ended the joint venture with Ford in December.
SK is also a supplier to Volkswagen.
Georgia U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff stated that these were battery manufacturing jobs and now they’re gone, blaming Trump’s war on electric vehicles for hurting Georgia’s economy.
Ossoff said Georgians were booming and building new plants before losing their jobs.
SK has invested significantly in Jackson County in Georgia in recent years as automakers shored up plans to spend billions to develop and build EVs.
The federal government under former President Joe Biden supported efforts to build out a domestic EV supply chain.
In June 2020, SK announced plans to pour $940 million to expand its battery manufacturing presence in Atlanta.
At the time, Gov. Brian Kemp’s office said the expansion would create 600 jobs.
SK and Hyundai are still jointly building a $5 billion battery factory near Cartersville, northwest of Atlanta.
Georgia has attracted other massive EV manufacturing investments, including Rivian’s $5 billion factory and Hyundai’s own $7.6 billion factory complex.
Few states benefited more than Georgia from Biden’s signature climate law, the Inflation Reduction Act, which accelerated a rush of green energy projects.
The 33 additional projects announced in Georgia by the end of 2024 were the most nationwide, according to E2, an environmental business group.
Projects in Georgia topped $20 billion, pledging more than 25,000 jobs.
Qcells, a unit of South Korea’s Hanwha Solutions, said that it had resumed normal production.
The company had temporarily reduced hours and pay for some workers last year because U.S. customs officials had been detaining imported components needed to make solar panels.
A spokesperson for Kemp’s office said it is grateful for the presence of workers remaining at SK’s factory.
Georgia’s job market remains robust, with over 219,700 new jobs being announced since 2019.
EV demand has not met automakers’ ambitious expectations in recent years.
EVs accounted for about 8% of new vehicle sales in the U.S. in 2025, much the same as a year earlier.
Automakers have been reevaluating their multibillion-dollar electrification plans as financial losses mount and demand shifts.

Manufacturers including Ford, General Motors, Stellantis and others have reneged on factory, investment and product plans, laid off workers, and pivoted some efforts to hybrid and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles.
Hybrids and more efficient gasoline-powered vehicles are seemingly more palatable for mainstream buyers concerned about EV driving range and charging infrastructure availability.
Under President Donald Trump, Congress has eliminated tax credits of up to $7,500 for consumers’ purchases of new or used EVs.

The administration has announced plans to weaken fuel economy and greenhouse gas emissions rules for automakers, essentially eliminating any federal incentive for auto companies to make their vehicle fleets cleaner.
source_title: SK lays off nearly 1,000 workers at Georgia plant amid cooling automaker EV plans – AgriNews

entity_name: SK Innovation Commerce EV Battery Plant
facts: This project represents one of the largest single investments in a job-creating initiative in Georgia’s history and the largest in more than a decade.
The project is projected to employ nearly 2,000 people by 2025.
Plateau led site development in Phase I construction efforts for the new SK Innovation battery manufacturing plant in Commerce, Georgia.
The facility is located on 283 acres along I-85 about 65 miles northeast of downtown Atlanta inside the Commerce industrial complex.
The 2.4 million-square-foot, $1.67 billion facility is SK Innovation’s first EV battery factory in the U.S.
Work began in spring 2019 with Phase 1 project completion scheduled for spring 2020.
Plateau’s execution and service during the small enabling project at the beginning of this development opened up the remaining phases for the entire site.
Phase 2 began construction in 2020.
Phase 2 consisted of a 1.2 million-square-foot facility located along Phase 1 of the project and included site and utility work, construction of medium voltage banks and cross-country piping racks.
The site development included a unique utility system with different types of effluent produced by the plant, requiring three separate sewer systems.
Plateau constructed an onsite substation directly for Georgia Power.
Some of the project challenges included the close proximity of the jobsite to the active facility as well as an accelerated timeline with substantial completion scheduled for December 2021.
SK, which makes lithium-ion batteries for hybrid and fully electric automobiles, builds batteries for multiple vehicle manufacturers.
SK Innovation will invest a total of $2.6B to complete construction of its first and second electric battery plants (Volkswagen and Ford), creating ~2,600 jobs by the end of 2023.
source_title: SK Battery - Plateau Excavation

facts: Located 65 miles northeast of downtown Atlanta on 283 acres along I-85 inside the Commerce industrial complex.
The manufacturing facility is 2.4 million square feet.
It is SK Innovation’s first EV battery factory in the U.S.
Work began in spring of 2019 with phase 1 of the project.
Phase 1 facilities include multiple roof elevations and mezzanine decks.
Phase 2 began construction in the last quarter of 2020.
Phase 2 consisted of a 1.2 million-square-foot facility located along Phase 1 of the project.
Phase 2 included eight additional buildings totaling 90,000 square feet.
The additional buildings include kitchens, cell discharge areas, administrative office expansion, and a bridge linking the two main buildings.
source_title: SK Innovation Battery - EV Battery Manufacturer | Steel Service

facts: RaceTrac operates a location in Commerce, Georgia that features an EV charging station.
The site offers gas and diesel fuel options alongside the EV charging capability.
Customers can purchase coffee, frozen yogurt, and hot food items at the convenience store.
source_title: Gas Station and Convenience Stores in Commerce, Georgia | RaceTrac

facts: SK Innovation is expanding its investment in its Georgia, US battery plant.
The total investment of KRW 1.9 trillion, based on net foreign capital, is the largest in the history of Georgia.
It is estimated that more than 2,000 jobs will be created.
If further investment is realized, the impact on the state's economy will amount to several tens of trillion won in the coming decades, even considering the backward effect.
SK Innovation will secure a stable supply of batteries to nearby global automakers by having the only large battery plant in Georgia.
From 2022, the plant's production will be supplied to Volkswagen's plant in nearby Chattanooga, Tennessee.
SK Innovation plans to open the era of a full-fledged "3rd generation electric vehicle" by incorporating cutting-edge battery technology into its Georgia battery plant.
The third generation of electric vehicles has a range of more than 500 km on a single charge.
SK Innovation has been producing prototypes for 3rd generation EV batteries and will begin mass production in 2022.
It is the only Korean manufacturer to produce its own separator by securing Lithium-ion Battery Separator (LiBS) technology and production capacity.
It has introduced differentiated methods in cell production by eliminating the gluing process, reducing production steps and giving a technical advantage over others in performance and margin.
SK Innovation produces cells by cutting electrodes in a single sheet and alternately stacking them with separators (Zigzag Stacking).
source_title: SK Innovation expands its investment in its Georgia, US battery plant

facts: A South Korean company plans to build a $72 million factory in Georgia to make parts for electric vehicles, hiring more than 140 workers.
Daesol Ausys announced its plans on Tuesday to build a factory in West Point, making luggage boards and covers for compartments in electric vehicles.
The company supplies parts to Hyundai, Kia and General Motors.
It will be the third factory in the same West Point industrial park for members of the company’s corporate family.
Daehan Solution Co. opened a million factory there in 2008 to make headliners and other sound insulation parts for the nearby Kia plant.
Daesol Material opened a $35 million factory there in 2017 to make automotive fiber padding.
Daehan Solution today employs 350 full-time workers, while Daesol Material employs 50 workers, said Meghan Richardson, an economic developer for the city of West Point.
Daehan Solution also has a plant near Montgomery, Alabama, supplying the Hyundai assembly plant there.

The project is another in a wave of electric vehicle suppliers announcing plants in Georgia after Hyundai said in 2022 that it would build a $5.5 billion plant to assemble electric vehicles and batteries in Ellabell, Georgia, near Savannah.
The Hyundai site could grow to 8,100 employees and is slated to begin producing vehicles in 2025.
Suppliers have pledged to invest nearly $2.2 billion and to hire 5,000 people.

Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp said in a statement: “Our state’s automotive industry employs over 50,000 hardworking Georgians and will continue to grow as e-mobility suppliers choose all corners of the state for the jobs of the future.”
Daesol Ausys is scheduled to begin production in December 2024.
The company would pay $15 to $20 an hour, said Meghan Richardson.
The state will pay to train workers for the facility.
The state gave $500,000 last year to help Harris County clear and grade the site and build a road, funds the county matched using sales tax collections.
The city of West Point and the county are granting eight years of property tax breaks, worth a projected $5.7 million.
Daesol Ausys could qualify for $875,000 in state income tax credits, at $1,250 per job over five years, as long as workers make at least $31,300 a year.
source_title: South Korean auto supplier plans $72 million plant in Georgia to build electric vehicle parts | AP News

facts: A Korean automobile parts supplier announced Wednesday that it will open a factory in Coastal Georgia to support Hyundai Motor Group’s future $5.54 billion electric vehicle plant.
Seoyon E-HWA manufactures car parts such as door trims, bumpers and seats.
Seoyon E-HWA committed to building a $76 million factory in Chatham County according to a news release.
The company promised 740 direct and indirect jobs.
Seoyon’s new facility will be located at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.
Production is expected to begin in October 2024.
Wednesday’s announcement marks the fourth large company to flock to the Savannah area because of Hyundai’s future factory.
Ecoplastic Corporation, Joon Georgia and Hyundai Mobis all announced plants in Bryan County over the past few months.
source_title: Another Hyundai supplier plans plant near future Georgia EV factory

facts: Electric vehicles are steadily making their way toward prevalence on the road and in the market, particularly in Georgia and other parts of the Southeast.
Over the next three years, automakers and equipment producers will have factories in development in and around Georgia.

Richard Franza, Ph.D, professor of management at the Hull College of Business at Augusta University, stated it is not a question of if EV manufacturers are coming to Georgia, but how fast they will arrive.

The Georgia Supreme Court recently rejected an appeal challenging bond agreements between the state and the Joint Development Authority of Jasper, Morgan, Newton and Walton Counties (JDA) for the $5 billion Rivian EV manufacturing plant west of Atlanta.
Construction of Hyundai Motor Group’s EV and battery manufacturing facilities, representing another investment exceeding $5 billion, is still underway west of Savannah.
Kia Corporation, owned by Hyundai, announced an investment of $200 million to build its electric SUV factory in West Point, Georgia.
Augusta Technical College is building an automotive training center along Laney Walker.
Augusta Technical College is also constructing an advanced manufacturing school off Interstate 20.
Copper foil plants by companies Aurubis and Denkai America have put the CSRA on the EV manufacturing map.
Richard Franza noted that building a manufacturing or assembly facility automatically attracts suppliers around it, citing major EV investments by companies like Amazon.
Franza expects logistics companies FedEx and UPS to switch to electric fleets, which will help establish charging networks.
Franza stated that Georgia already had a battery manufacturer before getting vehicle manufacturers, giving the state a good existing network for the automobile industry.
The pace at which EV infrastructure is built is a key factor in determining when EVs may become commonplace.
Franza estimates more charging stations will appear on highways before seeing a significant increase in electric cars on those roads.

In March, the Georgia House of Representatives passed Bill 406, which would charge EV drivers by the kilowatt-hour for electricity to qualify the state for $135 million in federal funding toward public charging stations.

In April, Governor Brian Kemp and the Georgia Department of Natural Resources announced an initiative with Georgia Power to install charging stations at Cloudland Canyon, Fort Yargo State Park, High Falls State Park, Skidaway Island, and Wormsloe Historic Site.

Franza observed that charging stations are already starting to spring up in apartment complexes, gas stations, and other retail locations, providing at least a two-year head start on big manufacturers in Georgia.
The wider public's adaptation to electric vehicles over fossil fuels will largely be determined by how fast the federal government moves on emissions requirements.
Currently, little lobbying is needed for EV transition because the Biden administration is already pushing hard for an expeditious shift.

President Joe Biden’s Federal Sustainability Plan, enacted via Executive Order 14057 in late 2021, commits the federal fleet to transition light-duty vehicles completely to zero-emission vehicles by 2027 and heavy-duty vehicles by 2035.
Franza warned that if administrations change after the 2024 election, more lobbying may be needed to maintain EV transition momentum.
Georgia has not yet manufactured any actual electric vehicles.
The state’s charging infrastructure to support Tesla and other currently traveling EVs remains limited.

While the EV industry taking root in Georgia would not likely speed up manufacturing or use in the state immediately, the combination of automakers and suppliers building a supply chain base helps protect against significant delays in the industry's growth.
Electric vehicles currently account for less than 1% of vehicles on the road.
The strongest indicators of the growing shift to EVs will be the ratio of gas stations versus charging stations, and the production of combustion engines versus electric cars.
Franza advised looking for when these ratios start to change, noting it is not a black-and-white issue.
The main issue in the early years will be how much consumers want EVs compared to traditional combustion engine vehicles.
source_title: Augusta University expert weighs in on emerging electric vehicle market - The Augusta Press

facts: Highland 75 is a 782-acre master-planned corporate/industrial park located a mile east of the I-75 Cassville-White Road exit in Bartow County.
Highland 75 is certified under the Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Program, which puts construction projects on a fast track.

The Bartow-Cartersville Joint Development Authority was formed in 2004 by Cartersville and Bartow County to create the park, splitting expenses and revenues between the city, county, and school systems.
Hyundai Motor Group/SK On announced a joint venture last December to build an electric vehicle battery manufacturing facility on 600 acres several miles west of I-75 between Cartersville and Rome.
The Hyundai Motor Group/SK On joint venture project is valued at nearly $5 billion and will create 3,750 jobs.
Both Hyundai Motor Group/SK On and Qcells began site work for their respective facilities in the spring.
Qcells announced in January it would build a $2.5 billion manufacturing facility on 381 acres in Highland 75, creating 2,500 jobs.
The Qcells facility is expected to open in 2025.
Hanwha Advanced Materials Georgia, Inc. (HAGA) announced in March it would build a $147 million manufacturing facility creating 160 jobs in the park to supply the Qcells facility.
The HAGA plant is projected to open next summer.

About 20 million square feet of logistics/manufacturing space (Class A Industrial) has been built or planned in Adairsville, Emerson, the Cassville White corridor and Cartersville over the last seven or eight years.
Approximately 10,000 jobs have been added in Bartow County over the last 10 years.
The Foxfield Co. paid about $10.6 million for 800-plus acres next to the Anheuser-Busch plant at I-75 and Cassville White Road in 2019.
Anheuser-Busch undertook a $150 million expansion in 2020.
Duluth Trading Company was scheduled to open a facility in Adairsville this summer.
Highland 75 includes Voestalpine Automotive Body Parts Inc., which supplies parts to Mercedes, BMW and Volkswagen.
Highland 75 also hosts Constellium Automotive Structures N.A. and Surya/Artistic Weavers.
The Cartersville-Bartow County Chamber of Commerce hired a workforce development director to work with companies.
The chamber posts information for employers, job seekers, educators and others on its dedicated platform.
The chamber works with Georgia Highlands College on business internships and Chattahoochee Technical College on a Project Purpose program to train high school seniors with technical skills.
Melinda Lemmon serves as executive director of the Cartersville-Bartow County Department of Economic Development.
Peter Olson is the Bartow County Administrator.
Steve Taylor has served as a sole commissioner for Bartow County for 13 years.
Cindy Williams is the CEO of the Cartersville-Bartow County Chamber of Commerce.

facts: Hometown Electric provides home car charging station installation services for residents in the Atlanta Metro Area.
The service focuses on helping Atlanta residents save gas money and help the environment.
A home car charging station requires a dedicated circuit that is isolated and supplied by the right amount of power to function properly.
Charging stations need their own dedicated point of safety, called a breaker, inside the electrical panel.
Creating this special circuit allows control over the amount of power that flows into the vehicle's battery.
Power flow is measured in amperes (amps), which literally means the amount of power moving by a single point during a given second.
A 20 amp outlet typically charges up to 20 miles of driving distance per hour while charging, depending on the vehicle and the type of batteries your vehicle has.
Charging speed can be increased depending on the setup and the vehicle driven.
Hometown Electric determines the proper flow and connections necessary to optimize the charging process for a specific vehicle.
Car charging station installation requires specifying the amperage of the outlet via its dedicated breaker connection for safety.
A general rule is that the amount of amps determines the amount of miles per hour charged, such as a 20 amp outlet providing 20 miles of charge each hour.
Most charging stations are capable of 30 amp connections.
Hometown Electric offers flat rate pricing for its services rather than hourly rates.
The company employs top-level engineers who are background-checked.
Hometown Electric is locally owned and operated in the Atlanta area.
All employees adhere to a zero-tolerance drug policy.
source_title: Home Car Charging Station Installation in Atlanta | Hometown Electric

facts: HSAGP Energy LLC is a joint venture company between Hyundai Motor Group and SK On.
The joint venture has established an electric vehicle battery cell manufacturer.
The manufacturing facility is located in Kingston, Georgia.
The directory page categorizes the entity under 'Manufacturers - General'.
source_title: HSBMA Battery Manufacturing America | Manufacturers - General

facts: PAIH (Polish Investment and Trade Agency) named Ascend Elements’ Project Apex 2 Manufacturing Investment of the Year.
The project is an EU-first battery material refining center to fuel Poland’s.
