DOE described the financing as support for “Project Horizon,” encompassing site development, construction and manufacturing facilities.
DOE Loan Programs Office Director Jigar Shah said investing in American innovation is securing supply chains, strengthening the workforce, and ensuring the U.S. leads the clean transportation future.
Rivian’s Securities and Exchange Commission filings describe the loan as including capitalized interest.
The loan is explicitly intended “for the construction of its next manufacturing facility in Stanton Springs North.”
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp, Rivian CEO RJ Scaringe, and GDED Commissioner Pat Wilson broke ground on Sept. 16.
Early work at the site includes electrical and water utility hookups and road access.
Local media reports indicate early work has since commenced.
ENR has not yet independently verified those reports.
Rivian has not confirmed its pick for general contractor.
The car company temporarily paused the Georgia project in 2024 to conserve cash while launching its R2 line at its Normal, Ill., plant.
Rivian restarted the Georgia build after securing federal financing and reconfirming its long-term strategy.
The factory represents one of the largest single industrial investments in Georgia history.
It adds to a growing cluster of EV and battery plants in the Southeast.
State leaders see the Rivian facility as a keystone for attracting suppliers and bolstering the region’s automotive sector.
State officials project the plant will reach the mandated 7,500-employee target by 2030.
Another 2,000 construction jobs are projected to be created during the build-out.
Wilson highlighted that clawback provisions protect taxpayers “in the extremely unlikely event of company goals not being met.”
source_title: Rivian Breaks Ground on $5B EV Plant in Georgia | Engineering News-Record

facts: Rivian has broken ground on its $5 billion Georgia factory.
The project is contingent on creating 7,500 direct jobs and 8,000 indirect jobs by 2030.
Rivian secured a $6.6 billion federal loan from the U.S. Department of Energy for the project.
Rivian secured a $1.5 billion state incentive package from Georgia for the project.
Construction at the Georgia factory is scheduled to begin in 2026.
The incentives are tied to Rivian's commitment to localize its supply chain amid rising tariffs and geopolitical uncertainty.
Rivian partnered with LG Energy Solution (LGES) to produce 4695 cylindrical battery cells at a new Arizona facility.
The Arizona-made cells will power Rivian's R2 and R3 models.
The new 4695 cells offer six times the capacity of the 2170 cells currently in use, enhancing energy density and safety.
The LGES collaboration aligns with Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) requirements for domestic battery production.
The partnership reduces Rivian's reliance on overseas suppliers while qualifying the company for federal tax credits.
Rivian has proactively stockpiled lithium iron phosphate (LFP) battery cells from Gotion High-Tech in China and Samsung SDI in South Korea.
The stockpile ensures short-term supply chain stability amid tariff risks.
Rivian aims to co-locate suppliers near its Georgia plant to reduce logistics costs and lead times.
This supplier co-location strategy is echoed by traditional automakers like Toyota and Ford in their Southeastern U.S. operations.
The Georgia factory features a two-phase design targeting 400,000 annual vehicles by 2028.
Each phase of the Georgia facility will produce 200,000 units annually.
Rivian currently produces 15,000 units annually at its Illinois plant.
The company burns through $1 billion annually in operating costs.
Rivian has partnered with Georgia's technical colleges and universities to create apprenticeship programs for advanced manufacturing labor.
The Georgia plant is located near I-20 and major ports, providing a logistical edge for distribution to the Eastern U.S. and export markets.
Competitors like Tesla and Ford are scaling production, with Tesla's Texas and Berlin Gigafactories targeting 500,000 units annually by 2025.
Rivian differentiates itself through premium utility vehicles (R1T/R1S) and upcoming mass-market R2 and R3 models aimed at lower price points.
Environmental concerns from local residents regarding the Georgia plant's impact on farmland could delay permits or trigger regulatory scrutiny.
Rivian is addressing environmental concerns through sustainability initiatives including renewable energy integration and modern construction techniques.
The project's success depends on continued progress with LGES and domestic battery producers for supply chain localization.
Meeting the 2028 production targets is essential for Rivian to achieve economies of scale and profitability.
source_title: Rivian's Strategic Momentum: Supply Chain Resilience and Production Scalability Post-Georgia Factory Groundbreaking

facts: The Rivian Georgia plant is a large-scale electric vehicle manufacturing campus selected to expand Rivian’s North American production capacity.
The plant aims to create thousands of jobs and position the company closer to southeastern logistics and supplier networks.
Rivian settled on a multi-thousand-acre site in the greater Atlanta region.
The site was sited to give easy access to the I-75 and I-85 corridors and freight routes that feed both the Atlanta metro and statewide distribution chains.
The site sits within driving distance of Atlanta’s international airport and key rail corridors.
This location reduces inland transit time for high-value parts and finished vehicles destined for ports or regional dealers.
State and local incentives played a major role in the location decision.

Georgia and partner counties offered performance-based tax credits, infrastructure support and workforce training commitments aimed at lowering the plant’s operating cost during the first decade of operations.
Rivian prioritized a location with an available workforce pool skilled in manufacturing trades and automotive assembly.
The workforce includes technicians, electricians and welders supplied by regional community colleges and trade programs.
Land acquisition and pre-construction work included phased purchases, environmental assessments, and site grading.
Early site prep focused on utility extensions, stormwater controls and access roads to meet permitting deadlines.
The Georgia plant links directly to the Port of Savannah via major interstate and rail links.
This gives Rivian one of the shortest overland routes to a high-capacity container port on the U.S. East Coast.
Intermodal rail and dedicated trucking corridors were part of the site’s selection criteria.
These corridors enable inbound shipments of stamped bodies, electronics and battery cells and outbound transport of complete vehicles to regional dealers and export facilities.
Plans include rail spurs or nearby interchange options to connect the campus to mainline freight carriers.
The plans also include designated truck staging zones and route improvements to limit plant-related traffic through residential areas.
Battery logistics received focused planning with designated hazardous materials staging areas.
Specialized trailers for cell transport and controlled-temperature transfers where pack assembly occurs are planned to comply with safety rules.
The initial public announcement occurred in late 2021.
This was followed by land closing, permitting and a formal groundbreaking within the next 12–18 months.
Staged construction runs through site works, foundations, structural steel and enclosure.
Rivian broke the build into major phases: civil infrastructure and utilities; body-shop, paint and final assembly buildings; and commissioning for test cells and quality labs.
Contractor selection emphasized firms with rapid-build experience and integrated project controls to meet an aggressive ramp schedule.
Modular construction techniques and parallel workstreams were used to compress calendar time.

Common timeline variances included supply-chain hold-ups for long-lead mechanical systems, weather-related delays during major earthwork, and iterative permitting requirements tied to utility relocations.

Major permits required include local zoning approvals, stormwater and erosion control permits, wetlands permits where applicable, air emissions permits for paint and solvent operations, and utility connection permits.
The approval sequence typically starts with zoning and site-plan sign-off, then civil permits, followed by building permits and environmental operating permits tied to specific process equipment.
Public comment periods accompanied environmental reviews.
Municipal hearings focused on traffic mitigation, wetlands offsets and stormwater management.
Mitigation commitments were written into development agreements.

Where regulatory delays occurred, solutions included accelerated mitigation work, supplemental studies to address agency questions, and negotiated schedules for phased permitting to allow construction to continue on non-impacted areas.
The plant will produce Rivian’s current consumer models and is configured to accept future models.
Early production focuses on high-volume platforms while leaving room for additional vehicle families or special-purpose units over time.
Rivian designed the assembly flow for multi-platform flexibility so the same lines can handle different vehicle lengths, battery sizes and trim levels with minimal retooling and faster changeovers.
Planned annual production targets scale over several years and are tied to hiring milestones and supplier readiness.
Initial output focuses on ramp stability before moving to full-rate production.
Rivian expects to identify production bottlenecks early, common ones including paint-shop capacity, battery pack test throughput and final-dynamics validation.
The company plans to deploy targeted fixes such as additional test rigs or secondary paint booths for these bottlenecks.
Rivian’s approach places battery pack assembly close to final assembly when possible to reduce inbound transport risk and handling time for high-voltage packs.

Cell-level production remains the realm of specialized cell manufacturers, so the plant relies on external cell suppliers while managing pack integration, testing and thermal-management assembly on-site.
Supplier partnerships for modules, electric motors and power electronics are structured to provide redundancy with multiple Tier-1 vendors supplying critical systems to reduce single-source risk.

Rivian has signaled commitments to end-of-life battery handling, including collection programs, refurbishment for stationary storage and partnerships for raw-material reclamation to limit landfill-bound batteries.
The factory layout groups stamping and metal prep, body shop welding, paint operations, and a flexible final assembly hall designed for mixed-model flow and short takt times.
Automation plays a large role with high-accuracy robots performing welding, adhesive bonding and material handling.
Automated guided vehicles (AGVs) move subassemblies between cells to cut lead time.
Digital manufacturing systems—MES, PLC integration, and real-time quality dashboards—link operations and quality so engineers can spot deviations and correct them inside the shift.
Rivian integrates its skateboard architecture into the flow, allowing chassis-level integration early and reducing complexity later in the line.
Large-scale casting and modular subframes are used where appropriate to reduce part count and assembly steps.
Typical roles include line operators, machine technicians, quality engineers, process engineers, logistics coordinators and OT/IT specialists to support automated cells.
The plant emphasizes a human-plus-robot model where humans handle variability, inspections and final fit/finish while robots handle repetitive, high-precision tasks that improve quality and safety.

Rivian’s hiring strategy phases recruitment with ramp targets: initial skilled trades and supervisors, then production operators as lines reach steady state, supported by continuous training programs.
Training curricula combine classroom work, simulator-based station training and on-the-job mentoring to reduce first-pass defects and keep uptime high during ramp.
Local supplier sourcing focuses on metal stamping, interior trim, seating and logistics services to shorten lead times and create resilience against long-haul disruptions.
Long-distance suppliers supply semiconductors, specialty electronics, and battery cells.
Procurement strategy includes multi-sourcing and long-term contracts to lock capacity for key components.

Inventory strategies blend just-in-time deliveries for high-turn parts with safety stock for long-lead or supply-sensitive items like chips and battery cells to balance working capital and continuity.
Rivian and local economic development teams promote supplier parks and adjacent industrial land to attract Tier-2 and Tier-1 suppliers within truckable distance of the campus.
The project brings thousands of direct manufacturing and engineering jobs plus a larger number of indirect jobs in suppliers, logistics and local services.
Timelines for hiring align with production milestones.

Workforce development relies on partnerships with nearby community colleges and apprenticeship programs to create pipelines for technicians, welders and electricians specific to EV manufacturing needs.
Reskilling initiatives target workers from legacy automotive or industrial employers, offering shortened training tracks to qualify them for production floor or maintenance roles.
Compensation and benefits packages aim to be competitive in the region and include health benefits and apprenticeship pay scales.
Local labor relations vary, and the company typically engages early with community stakeholders on workforce plans.
State and local incentives for major EV plants commonly include tax credits, workforce grants and infrastructure funding.
Those incentives are performance-based and tied to hiring and investment milestones.
Fiscal analysis estimates the project’s multiplier effect through supplier activity, construction spending and household wages.
The public-payback window depends on realized payroll and tax receipts.
Public debate centers on trade-offs: the immediate fiscal cost of incentives versus projected long-term gains in jobs and economic activity.
Transparency in performance metrics is a common request from local watchdogs.
Officials justified incentives by pointing to accelerated regional development, long-term tax base growth and workforce uplift.
Critics push for stronger accountability clauses in agreements.
Construction-phase concerns include soil disturbance, sediment runoff and water use.
Developers implemented stormwater controls, wetlands mitigation and phased grading to limit impacts.

Operational emissions are managed with air permits for paint booths and solvent operations, energy-efficiency measures, and commitments to monitor emissions and report results under regulatory programs.

On-site sustainability plans include energy-efficiency in buildings, investment in onsite renewable generation or off-site renewable procurement, and programs to reduce manufacturing waste and water use.
Community safeguards commonly include continuous environmental monitoring, complaint hotlines and third-party audits to verify compliance with permit conditions.
Large plants change local demand for housing, schools and services.
Regional planners typically model expected population growth and coordinate upgrades to roads, water and sewer capacity ahead of full operations.
Traffic mitigation plans pair plant access roads, dedicated truck routes and signal timing adjustments to limit heavy-vehicle impacts on residential streets during peak shifts.
Community benefit agreements or targeted investments often arise from negotiations, funding local workforce programs, transportation upgrades and sometimes contributions for affordable housing.
Stakeholder sentiment varies: many local businesses welcome the economic lift, while some residents raise concerns about congestion and changes to land use patterns.
Legal risks include challenges tied to permits, land deals or incentive agreements.
Mitigation requires clear documentation, community engagement and adherence to environmental commitments.
Political changes at state or federal levels can alter tax policy, grant availability or regulatory priorities.
This makes robust, legally binding incentive agreements important for project stability.
Reputational risks center on worker safety, environmental performance and transparency.
Proactive community communication and timely reporting reduce the chance of sustained negative coverage.
The Georgia campus expands Rivian’s geographic manufacturing footprint and lowers distribution time to East Coast and southern markets.
This improves responsiveness and lowers logistics spend per vehicle.
Scale at the Georgia plant supports lowering per-unit costs through higher volume, more localized suppliers, and optimized logistics that shorten the supply chain for heavy components.
Regional diversification reduces single-site concentration risk and positions Rivian to meet growing demand while keeping options open for product line expansion and export volumes.
Industry watchers should track near-term production KPIs: weekly and monthly vehicle output, first-pass yield, and the pace of hiring against public milestones.
These metrics show whether ramp assumptions hold.
Supplier announcements and long-term component contracts indicate resilience against bottlenecks in battery cells, semiconductors and stamped body parts.
Regulatory milestones—final permits, air and water approvals, and any outstanding litigation—can swing the plant’s timeline.
Watchers should monitor local council and permitting agency updates.
Jobseekers should monitor Rivian’s careers portal and local workforce boards for hiring events, pre-employment training dates and apprenticeship sign-ups tied to plant milestones.
Suppliers should watch municipal supplier portals and state economic development RFPs; register interest early, demonstrate capacity for volume and provide clear lead-time data.

Residents with concerns should use posted public-comment channels, attend scheduled hearings, and contact designated municipal liaisons and the plant’s community outreach office for updates and mitigation requests.
To follow the project safely, rely on official press releases, municipal announcements and scheduled plant tours rather than attempting unauthorized site access.
Media access rules are typically defined before any public tours.
source_title: Rivian Georgia Plant Jobs, Production, Updates

facts: Kia EV9 production has started at the South Korean automaker’s Georgia assembly plant in West Point.
The Kia EV9 is an all-electric three-row SUV and the first electric vehicle (EV) made in Georgia to be put up for sale in the United States auto market.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that the expansion adds a new product line to vehicles already being built in West Point.
Governor Brian Kemp thanked Kia for choosing Georgia again and noted the state's goal to become the "e-mobility capital of the nation."
Kia’s Georgia plant will assemble the 2025 model year of the EV9.
Since its global debut, the Kia EV9 has won multiple awards.
In April 2024, the Kia EV9 won the “Best of the Best” Red Dot award in the Cars and Motorcycle category.
Earlier this month, the Texas Auto Writers Association named the Kia EV9 the “Best in Class” in several categories, including Performance SUV, mid-size CUV, and electric vehicle.
The Kia EV9 has been recognized for its “Opposites United” design philosophy, specifically its contemporary exterior with strong classic lines.
The Kia EV9 has also been acknowledged for its usability.
According to Eric Watson, Vice President of Sales Operations at Kia America, the Kia EV9 contributed heavily to the automaker’s 151% increase in EV sales in the United States.
SeungKyu (Sean) Yoon, President and CEO of Kia North America and Kia America, stated that today's launch is an example of Kia's commitment to the U.S. market and Georgia.
Through Plan S, Kia aims to be a leader in the e-mobility sector for years to come.
The Kia EV9 has previously won accolades including World Car of the Year, World EV of the Year, and North American Utility Vehicle of the Year.
SeungKyu (Sean) Yoon commented that the EV9 will be assembled by team members at Kia Georgia with a track record for assembling award-winning, world-renowned products.
source_title: Kia EV9 production begins in Georgia

canonical_name: Kia Georgia West Point Assembly Plant
facts: Kia Georgia has started production at its West Point assembly plant in Georgia for the 2025 Kia EV9 electric vehicle (EV) SUV.
The addition of the 2025 EV9 marks a historical milestone as the first EV assembled in the West Point assembly plant.

Governor of Georgia Brian Kemp, James Watson (Kia Georgia’s Vice President of Production), and SeungKyu (Sean) Yoon (President and CEO for Kia North America and Kia America) attended the production celebration.

Governor Brian Kemp praised the milestone, stating, “I want to congratulate the Kia Georgia team members on today’s achievement and thank them for choosing the Peach State yet again as we work to become the e-mobility capital of the nation.”
Prior to the EV9 addition, the Kia Georgia West Point assembly plant only assembled the Telluride, Sorento, and Sportage models.
The transition to adding the EV9 to Kia Georgia’s assembly roster was supported by an investment of over $200 million.
The over $200 million investment led to the creation of around 200 new jobs at the plant.
Production of the new EV9 in Georgia is aimed to be eligible for the $7,500 EV tax credit by leveraging local production incentives.
The Kia EV9 has won the 2024 World Car of the Year and 2024 World Electric Car awards at the New York International Auto Show.
The Kia EV9 also won Best of the Best at the Red Dot Award: Product Design 2024.

SeungKyu Yoon, President and CEO for Kia North America and Kia America, stated, “Having achieved prestigious accolades such as World Car of the Year, World EV of the Year, and North American Utility Vehicle of the Year, EV9 will be in excellent hands with the team members at Kia Georgia and their track record for assembling award-winning, world-renowned products speaks for itself.”
The company anticipates the 2025 Kia EV9 will exceed the 50,000-unit milestone within its first year of launch in the U.S. market due to high demand in global EV markets.
source_title: Kia Georgia Makes History with 2025 EV9 SUV Assembly | EV.com

entity_name: Hyundai-LG EV Battery Plant Joint Venture
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solutions EV Battery Plant Joint Venture
facts: South Korea's Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solutions announced plans late Thursday to spend $4.3 billion in building an electric vehicle (EV) battery plant in southeast Georgia.
The two companies are making efforts to comply with President Joe Biden's clean energy tax law.
Currently, no Hyundai electric vehicles are eligible for the tax credit.
Both companies signed a memorandum of understanding during a ceremony at LG's headquarters in Seoul.
Both companies will 50/50 jointly invest in the EV battery plant to be set up in Bryan County, south of Savannah.

Jaehoon Chang, president and chief executive of Hyundai Motor Company, stated that Hyundai Motor Group is focusing on its electrification efforts to secure a leadership position in the global auto industry.
The plant will be built adjacent to Hyundai's "Metaplant" manufacturing facility in Georgia.
In May 2022, Hyundai announced plans to invest $5.5 billion in a massive EV plant in Ellabell, west of Savannah.
Garrison Douglas, a spokesperson for Republican Gov. Brian Kemp, stated the new plant will foster 3,000 jobs as part of the previously estimated 8,100 roles in the state by the end of 2025.
The Metaplant facility will produce Hyundai, Genesis and Kia electric vehicles.
The Hyundai/LG plant is likely to break ground later this year.
The Hyundai/LG plant is set to begin battery production toward the end of 2025.
The plant will work toward supplying batteries for 300,000 electric vehicles annually.
Battery supply capacity will later be expanded to support 500,000 electric vehicles.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated the project is a milestone in Georgia's path to becoming the EV capital of the nation.
The announcement comes amid an ongoing rush of electric vehicle and battery makers across the country due to the U.S. Inflation Reduction Act.

Under the IRA, EV makers can qualify for a $7,500 tax credit if they assemble vehicles in North America or meet a certain percentage of battery parts and minerals from North America or a U.S. free trade partner.
As of now, no Hyundai electric vehicles, along with its Kia and Genesis models, are eligible for the tax credit because they don't meet domestic manufacturing requirements.
The state of Georgia pledged $1.8 billion in tax breaks and other incentives for an automobile plant, representing the largest subsidy package offered by a U.S. state.
Hyundai is the world's third-largest automaker by vehicle sales.
Hyundai is already assembling electric vehicles at its plant in Montgomery, Alabama.
Hyundai announced it would start assembling its electric Kia EV9 large SUV at the Kia plant in West Point, Georgia.
Last month, Hyundai announced a partnership with SK On to build a huge plant in Cartersville, Bartow County.
The Hyundai/SK On partnership involves a $5 billion investment to create 3,500 jobs.

Through the joint venture, both parties plan to establish an EV battery cell plant in Bartow County, Georgia, close to the Group's U.S. production facilities including Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama, Kia Georgia and Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
The new plant is expected to start manufacturing battery cells in the second half of 2025 with an annual production capacity of 35 GWh.
The 35 GWh capacity is sufficient to support the production of 300,000 EVs.
LG noted it would be its seventh battery plant in operation or under construction in the U.S.
LG's focus is on expanding EV production in the country.
LGES CEO Youngsoo Kwon stated that two strong leaders in the auto and battery industries have joined hands to drive the EV transition in America.
source_title: Hyundai, LG Partner To Build $4.3 Billion EV Battery Plant In Georgia: What To Know

facts: LaGrange sits at the center of West Georgia's I-85 corridor, where manufacturing investment has grown steadily with the Kia Georgia plant in nearby West Point anchoring a regional supplier network.
Troup County's commercial base spans industrial manufacturing, distribution, healthcare, and professional services.
The Kia Georgia plant in West Point created a supplier network that runs through LaGrange and the surrounding area.
Suppliers in the region require cleaning programs built specifically for production floor environments.

Major employers in the Troup County area include Kia Georgia (West Point) - Automotive manufacturing, West Point Stevens area facilities - Textile and industrial, WellStar West Georgia Medical Center - Healthcare, I-85 corridor manufacturers - Supplier network, LaGrange industrial parks - Distribution and light manufacturing.
Key business districts in the region include the I-85 industrial corridor, LaGrange industrial parks, Downtown LaGrange commercial district, and West Point industrial zone.

Millennium Facility Services offers shift-based or nightly janitorial for Troup County manufacturing facilities, including floor sweeping, scrubbing, restroom service, and break room cleaning scaled to plant size and shift schedule.

Industrial warehouse and distribution cleaning covers sweeping, scrubbing, and restroom service for distribution and warehousing operations throughout the LaGrange I-85 corridor, featuring large-format floor programs for concrete facilities with high equipment traffic.

Office and administrative building janitorial provides nightly cleaning for corporate offices, professional services buildings, and administrative spaces in downtown LaGrange and surrounding commercial corridors.

Medical facility cleaning covers healthcare facilities serving Troup County, including medical offices, outpatient clinics, and support facilities, utilizing bloodborne pathogen trained staff with verified completion on every shift.

Supplier and tier manufacturing cleaning targets the Kia Georgia supplier network and related I-85 corridor manufacturers in Troup County, accommodating quality documentation requirements for automotive supplier relationships.
Construction and post-build cleaning handles new manufacturing facilities, warehouses, and commercial buildings in LaGrange before equipment installation or tenant occupancy.
Austin Jones, CEO of Millennium Facility Services, states that GPS verification is foundational to the company's operations and invoicing policy.
LaGrange sits approximately 65 miles southwest of Alpharetta on I-85, at the intersection of the West Georgia industrial corridor and the Alabama state line.
The Kia Georgia manufacturing plant in nearby West Point produces the Telluride, Sportage, and EV6 for the North American market.
Kia Georgia's presence has made Troup County a destination for automotive suppliers and logistics operations.
Millennium Facility Services' documentation platform supports cleanliness verification to meet supplier audit requirements for the automotive network anchored by Kia Georgia.
Industrial accounts in Troup County are served by locally recruited staff to ensure consistent nightly coverage across the West Georgia market.

The company provides a single vendor solution for both manufacturing plant floors and attached administrative spaces to eliminate coordination problems between separate industrial cleaning and office janitorial services.
Austin Jones, CEO, notes that facility managers switching to Millennium typically cite inconsistent attendance from previous providers as their primary complaint rather than price.
An unnamed client states that Millennium built a cleaning program around their production shifts so operations do not pause for maintenance.
The majority of cleaning is self-performed by trained W-2 employees, with third-party specialists only used when necessary and managed under the same quality standards.
Services cover manufacturing plants, distribution warehouses, professional offices, medical facilities, retail spaces, and multi-tenant commercial buildings throughout LaGrange and Troup County.
The company's Alpharetta headquarters is approximately 65 miles from LaGrange via I-85 South, with established crew operations along the I-85 corridor.
For facilities with restricted production areas and access control requirements, Millennium provides crew rosters, background documentation, and operates within facility security protocols.

Cleaning around production equipment, assembly lines, and manufacturing cells follows specific practices that avoid cleaning operational equipment or using water near electrical components, requiring coordination with plant supervisors.
The company routinely covers production floors, warehouses, and administrative buildings under a single industrial campus contract to ensure consistent standards across the entire facility.
Millennium provides GPS-verified completion logs, inspection reports, and product safety data sheets upon request for third-party audits, safety inspections, or customer site visits.
source_title: Commercial Cleaning LaGrange GA | Millennium Facility Services | Millennium

facts: GAM announced the development of “Manufacturing Georgia,” the first-ever digital platform designed to help Georgia manufacturers engage directly with the public.
Manufacturing Georgia is being developed through a multi-year strategic partnership between GAM and Unmudl, a leading Skills-to-Jobs® Marketplace.
The platform will feature dynamic, social media-like capabilities to connect manufacturers with future workers, especially younger audiences.
Key goals include improving public awareness of modern manufacturing and increasing the availability of skilled manufacturing workers within the next five years.
Manufacturing Georgia will be available to all manufacturers in the state.
Lloyd Avram serves as GAM’s President and CEO.
GAM formed a focus group in late 2023 to brainstorm public engagement strategies targeting younger generations.
Unmudl was selected as the development partner following a competitive review process.
Stephanie Scearce is GAM’s Vice President of Workforce Innovation.
Jim Lechleitner serves as Head of Strategy at Unmudl.
The platform will feature real-time job postings, youth apprenticeships, work-based learning opportunities, training programs, and insights into modern manufacturing.
Patrick Lenz, President and COO of Toyo Tires NA Manufacturing, expressed support for the initiative to engage local high schools and sustain future workforce.
Stuart Countess, President and CEO of Kia Georgia in West Point, emphasized combating the stigma that manufacturing is dull, dirty, dangerous, or a dead end.
Brian Cooksey, Director of Workforce Development at Shaw Industries in Dalton, highlighted the platform's role in providing accurate career information to students and families.
Smaller manufacturers comprise approximately 67 percent of Georgia’s manufacturing sector and will benefit from the platform.
John Fluker, President and CEO at Grenzebach, stated that connecting with the next generation is key for manufacturers of all sizes in Georgia.
The platform will launch in phases: a pre-screening event in March, a soft rollout to select manufacturers and education institutions in April, and a public launch in July.
GAM's Manufacturers Education Foundation and contributions from Toyo Tires, Kia Georgia, Shaw Industries, and Grenzebach are funding the initiative.
Manufacturers across Georgia will have free access to the platform in 2025, with an affordable subscription fee starting in 2026.
GAM represents approximately 10,000 manufacturing entities and almost 427,000 workers in Georgia.
GAM has advocated for manufacturing growth and excellence in Georgia for 125 years.
Unmudl is a public benefit company that connects talent and employers through a nationwide network of community and technical colleges.
Unmudl focuses on mid-level ‘hands-on’ technician skills to provide access to high-quality, affordable training for in-demand jobs in key industries including manufacturing.
source_title: Georgia Association of Manufacturers Partners with Unmudl to Launch Innovative Digital Platform: Manufacturing Georgia

facts: SK Battery America (SKBA), the U.S. subsidiary of South Korean battery manufacturer SK On, is expanding its battery production capacity to support Hyundai and Kia’s growing North American electric vehicle lineup.
SKBA plans to begin producing batteries for Hyundai and Kia EVs as early as March on 9 out of 12 production lines across its Georgia U.S. Plant 1 and Plant 2.
This allocation will convert approximately 75% of SKBA’s total U.S. battery production capacity for use by Hyundai and Kia.
The expansion aligns with Hyundai and Kia’s plans to ramp up U.S. electric vehicle manufacturing at their new, highly automated Metaplant America facility.
Hyundai and Kia are preparing to begin full-scale mass production of the Ioniq 5 electric vehicle starting in March.
SKBA’s Georgia plant location enables convenient and timely battery deliveries to Hyundai’s Metaplant facility located in the same state.
SKBA can deliver batteries to Hyundai’s Metaplant within four hours via land transportation.
The local supply arrangement reduces transportation time and logistics costs compared to the previous method of importing batteries from South Korea and Europe.
SKBA’s Georgia plant has a production capacity of approximately 16.5 GWh of batteries per year, which is estimated to power about 200,000 electric vehicles.
Expanding local battery operations will increase benefits from the Advanced Manufacturing Production Credit (AMPC) under the U.S. Inflation Reduction Act (IRA).
In 2024 alone, SK On received approximately $202 million (292.4 billion won) in AMPC tax credit benefits.
source_title: SK Battery America to allocate 75% capacity to Hyundai and Kia - Automotive Technology Insight | Forecasts | Industry News | Supply Chain

facts: SK Battery America Inc. laid off nearly 1,000 workers at a manufacturing plant northeast of Atlanta on Friday amid automakers' changing electrification plans and uncertain consumer demand for EVs.

The company said Friday marked the last working day for 958 plant employees, about 37% of its workforce, according to a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification, or WARN, notice filed by human resources chief Chuck Moore.
Impacted workers will be paid through May 6.
The plant will continue to employ about 1,600 workers.
SK opened the $2.6 billion battery plant in Commerce, Georgia, in January 2022.
The Korean company notably supplied the Ford F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck.
Ford announced plans to cancel the fully electric version of the truck in December.

The news comes as the U.S. electric vehicle market is at a standstill amid the Trump administration steering federal support away from electrification in favor of more lax automotive emissions policies and a broader agenda supporting the oil and gas industries.
SK Americas spokesperson Joe Guy Collier said in a statement that the workforce reduction was made to align operations to market conditions.
Collier said, “SK Battery America remains committed to Georgia and to building a robust U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing.”
“We are pursuing a range of future customers, including the Battery Energy Storage System arena.”
Ford said in December that it would scrap the fully-electric version of its iconic pickup truck and opt for an extended-range version of the vehicle.
A Ford spokesperson said it could not comment on supplier personnel actions.
SK and Ford had together previously invested $11.4 billion in joint battery plants in the U.S.
The battery maker ended the joint venture in December.
SK is also a supplier to Volkswagen.
Georgia U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff, a Democrat, said in a statement, “Let’s be clear: these were battery manufacturing jobs and now they’re gone.”
“As predicted, Trump’s war on electric vehicles is hurting Georgia’s economy. We were booming and building new plants. Now Georgians are losing their jobs.”

SK has invested significantly in Jackson County in Georgia in recent years as automakers shored up plans to spend billions to develop and build EVs and the federal government under former President Joe Biden supported efforts to build out a domestic EV supply chain.
It had also announced in June 2020 plans to pour $940 million to expand its battery manufacturing presence in Atlanta.
At the time, Gov. Brian Kemp’s office said the expansion would create 600 jobs.
SK and Hyundai are still jointly building a $5 billion battery factory near Cartersville, northwest of Atlanta.
The state has also attracted other massive EV manufacturing investments; Rivian’s $5 billion factory and Hyundai’s own $7.6 billion factory complex among them.
Few states benefited more than Georgia from Biden’s signature climate law, the Inflation Reduction Act, which accelerated a rush of green energy projects.
The 33 additional projects announced by the end of 2024 were the most nationwide, according to E2, an environmental business group.
Exact figures differ, but projects in Georgia topped $20 billion, pledging more than 25,000 jobs.
Qcells, a unit of South Korea’s Hanwha Solutions, said Friday that it had resumed normal production.
The company had temporarily reduced hours and pay for some workers last year because U.S. customs officials had been detaining imported components needed to make solar panels.
A spokesperson for Kemp's office said it is grateful for the presence of workers remaining at SK's factory.

“Thankfully, Georgia’s job market remains robust, with over 219,700 new jobs being announced since 2019, affording Georgians the opportunity for gainful employment, no matter their zip code,” the statement said.
EV demand, while still growing, has not met automakers' ambitious expectations in recent years.
EVs accounted for about 8% of new vehicle sales in the U.S. in 2025, much the same as a year earlier.
Automakers have been reevaluating their multibillion-dollar electrification plans as financial losses mount and demand shifts.

Manufacturers including Ford, General Motors, Stellantis and others — along with others across the EV supply chain — have reneged on factory, investment and product plans, laid off workers and, instead, pivoted some of those efforts to hybrid and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles.
Hybrids and more efficient gasoline-powered vehicles are seemingly more palatable for mainstream buyers concerned about EV driving range and charging infrastructure availability.
Under President Donald Trump, meanwhile, Congress has eliminated tax credits of up to $7,500 for consumers' purchases of new or used EVs.

The administration has also announced plans to weaken fuel economy and greenhouse gas emissions rules for automakers, essentially eliminating any federal incentive for auto companies to make their vehicle fleets cleaner.
source_title: SK Lays off Nearly 1,000 Workers at Georgia Plant Amid Cooling Automaker EV Plans

facts: The HL-GA Battery facility is located next to Hyundai’s EV assembly factory along I-16 west of Savannah, Georgia.
From the outside, the electric vehicle battery plant appears ready for operations, but construction remains ongoing inside.
Evidence of ongoing construction includes a dozen or so lifts and hoists sitting outside the doors, plus several yet-to-be paved roads and parking lots.
Work at the site was interrupted last week by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) raid that triggered an international firestorm.
A top official stated that the plant’s opening has been delayed by two to three months following the raid.
Immigration and Customs Enforcement arrested approximately 475 laborers accused of working at the site illegally.
Among those arrested were 316 Koreans and 14 others from various Asian countries.

The detained Korean workers, along with the other Asian detainees, flew back to Korea on Thursday after spending a week at a federal detention center in Folkston, southeast Georgia near the Florida state line.
A Korean government official stated that the detained citizens were technicians and specialists tasked with setting up machinery at the Georgia factory site.
source_title: Opening of Hyundai-LG EV battery plant to be delayed after ICE raid

facts: The federal E-Verify system is supposed to help employers determine whether their workers are legal, but its use isn't always required.
Since 1986, employers have been required to verify workers' eligibility to work, and can be fined or jailed if caught knowingly breaking the law.
Enforcement of employer hiring laws has typically focused on the workers rather than those who hire them.
Trump officials claim some employers exploit desperate workers through lower pay and dangerous working conditions to pocket savings.
Some employers argue there aren't enough willing American workers to do the toughest jobs at the wages they're offering.

Since January, the Trump administration has targeted workers at raids of California car washes and marijuana farms, a Louisiana racetrack, a Florida construction site and a Nebraska meatpacking plant.
Those past enforcement actions have not yet translated into criminal cases or fines against the employers, but significantly disrupted business operations in some cases.

The administration has fined two Colorado cleaning companies millions of dollars for hiring dozens of undocumented workers and won criminal indictments in two other schemes to employ illegal labor in Arizona and Ohio.
Officials declined to comment on an active criminal investigation at the Georgia plant, sparked in part by local labor unions' complaints that foreign workers were filling jobs.
Federal officials on Sept. 4 raided the HL-GA Battery Company project about 30 miles northwest of Savannah, detaining nearly 500 workers.
The majority of the detained employees were South Koreans who lacked permission to work in the United States.
Special Agent in Charge Steven Schrank of Homeland Security Investigations in Georgia and Alabama stated that companies must bring workers in legally if needed for building or other projects.
Schrank added that the operation sends a clear message that those who exploit the system and undermine the workforce will be held accountable.
So far, only the detained workers are facing punishment, as they were taken to immigration detention sites for deportation.
South Korean officials are working with the U.S. State Department to voluntarily repatriate approximately 300 detained South Korean citizens.
South Korean media reported the detained workers could fly home via charter flight as soon as Sept. 10.
The treatment of South Korean detainees contrasts with tens of thousands of Mexicans, Venezuelans and Guatemalans who face months in jail-like detention centers before forced deportation.
Worksite enforcement actions have been rare in recent years, with HSI records showing investigations into employers can take years to bring to trial.
The Trump administration has recently injected new urgency into employer cases by issuing millions of dollars in fines and bringing criminal cases across the country.

Over the past decade, there have been fewer than 200 federal prosecutions of employers for hiring illegal labor, according to Brian Owsley, associate professor of law at the University of North Texas-Dallas College of Law.
The Biden administration largely halted raids on employers, according to Owsley.

Manufacturing experts say the U.S. workforce lacks the technical expertise that South Korean and other international workers have, whose specialized knowledge is required to ramp up production in the country.
The battery plant will serve the adjacent Hyundai Metaplant car factory.
The projects received millions in state and local tax incentives after promising to create thousands of new jobs.

Federal investigators say the raid resulted from a monthslong investigation into employment practices, but court documents show no evidence they knew large numbers of South Koreans were working there.
The only four people listed as specific targets in the search warrant have Hispanic names.
The Department of Homeland Security and ICE did not immediately respond to requests for comment regarding the search warrant or the four target persons.
In a Sept. 7 social media statement, Trump acknowledged that his immigration crackdown complicated his goals of bringing back manufacturing.
Since January, the White House has touted hundreds of billions of dollars in commitments from foreign companies like Hyundai, which co-owns the plant with LG.
Trump called on all foreign companies investing in the U.S. to respect immigration laws and encouraged them to legally bring technical talent while hiring and training American workers.
Border czar Tom Homan warned employers that federal officials are closely watching workplaces and stated they will do more worksite enforcement operations.
Homan told CNN that employers hire illegal aliens because they can work them harder, pay them less, and undercut competition that hires U.S. citizen employees.
The American Immigration Council estimates at least 6% of manufacturing workers nationwide are undocumented, based on U.S. census data analysis.
Undocumented workers represent 7% of the manufacturing workforce in Georgia.

Federal law provides for $3,000 fines and up to six months in jail for employers, but it is only enforced when officials decide an employer committed a "pattern or practice" of routinely hiring unauthorized employees.
While federal law mandates E-Verify to help ascertain work authorization, it is up to states to determine whether to require its use.
Fewer than half of states mandate the use of E-Verify for public and some private employers, according to a World Population Review analysis.
Foreign workers can easily produce high-quality fake documents, making E-Verify not foolproof.
A police department in Maine recently tangled with the White House over hiring an officer cleared by E-Verify who was later found to be in the country illegally.
Assistant Secretary of Homeland Security Tricia McLaughlin stated that using E-Verify does not absolve employers of their failure to conduct basic background checks to verify legal status.
Federal prosecutors have rarely gone after big-name firms for hiring undocumented workers, according to Owsley.
In April, the Trump administration fined two Denver-based cleaning companies $6.2 million and $1 million respectively for hiring dozens of unauthorized workers.

That same month, prosecutors ordered the multimillion-dollar seizure of 14 properties, seven bank accounts and 15 vehicles from a Chinese staffing company accused of laundering money and supplying foreign workers to an Ohio factory.
Owsley noted that workplace enforcement has historically been used against smaller employers rather than major corporations like Hyundai or Tyson.
During a Sept. 5 news conference, Schrank said investigators are still trying to determine who hired the illegal workers at the Georgia construction site.
Schrank explained there was a network of subcontractors and subcontractors for subcontractors on site, with employees working for various companies beyond just the parent company.
Investigators continue to work on determining exactly which workers worked for what companies.
source_title: Georgia battery-factory raid reveals new Trump target

facts: Hyundai broke ground on its $5.5 billion “Metaplant” near Savannah, Georgia.
Kia confirmed that it will have upcoming electric vehicles made at the Hyundai Metaplant facility.
Hyundai announced in May plans to produce up to 300,000 EVs annually at the Georgia plant.
The Georgia plant will produce models from Hyundai and Genesis, as well as Kia.
Batteries are to be manufactured separately at a corresponding battery venture that has not yet been detailed.
Hyundai’s companion parts and components arm, Hyundai Mobis, is reportedly looking for nearby sites.
Kia already operates a facility in West Point, Georgia, which manufactures Telluride, Sorento, and Sportage SUVs, as well as the K5 sedan.
The new Savannah plant will accommodate U.S. EV assembly and potentially qualify the brand’s future EVs under the stricter requirements of the Inflation Reduction Act and its revamped EV tax credit.
Production at the Savannah plant for Kia is scheduled to start in the first half of 2025.
The Hyundai Ioniq 7, previewed by the Seven concept, is one of the prime candidates for manufacture at the new plant.
Kia’s EV9 electric SUV reaches the U.S. in 2023 but might potentially be shifted to the Savannah plant.
Kia is planning an electric pickup truck that would be a likely candidate for production at the facility.
Rivian aims to produce 450,000 electric trucks per year in Georgia, with its plant due to start production in 2024.
According to the Atlanta Journal-Constitution, citing Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America CEO José Muñoz, the Hyundai plant could be expanded to 500,000 units annually.
The expanded Hyundai plant would involve “maybe five or six” upcoming EV models.
Georgia is being cast as a hub for EV manufacturing due to these recent decisions.
Battery maker SK Innovation is building dual battery plants in Georgia that will support 300,000 EVs per year.
SK Innovation is ramping up production to supply Ford and Volkswagen.
source_title: Kia EVs will also be made at Hyundai’s Georgia “Metaplant”

facts: Hyundai MOBIS Savannah (MSV), also known as MOBIS Alabama, LLC, is the largest tier one supplier to the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
The facility supplies three major modules: front and rear chassis modules, cockpit module, and front-end module.
These components are provided on time and in sequence to streamline the assembly process at HMGMA while reducing reliance on warehouse and storage space.
Hyundai MOBIS serves as the parts and service arm for South Korean automakers Hyundai Motor Company, Genesis Motors, and Kia Motors.

The company's product portfolio includes chassis, cockpit, and front-end modules; safety products such as airbags, headlights, anti-lock brake systems, and electronic stability control products; steering parts; multimedia systems; UVO systems; injection-molded plastic parts like instrument panels, carriers, and bumpers; and steel wheel rims and decks.
Hyundai MOBIS also supplies after-sales service parts for vehicles.
The Module Division supplies modules to automakers using a just-in-sequence (JIS) system.

Hyundai MOBIS participates in the initial stages of new vehicle development processes, performing research and development of auto-parts by designing and testing modules for production applications based on specific specifications.

System integration of modularization contributes to increased competitiveness, in-sequence processing, reduced total weight, and a reduced number of parts needed, which allows for easier assembly, effective inventory control, and cost reduction.
The Savannah, Georgia facility offers competitive wages and benefits to its team members.
Benefits include paid vacation and personal time off, 15 paid holidays, a 401(k) with company match, health/dental/vision insurance, life insurance, and disability insurance.

Hyundai MOBIS maintains a Code of Conduct that guides directors, officers, and employees to perform and manage work fairly and responsibly under all circumstances while adhering to applicable rules and regulations.
The company provides a confidential and anonymous Red Flag Reporting avenue for team members, business associates, and community partners to report problems.
MOBIS Alabama embraces five core values of the Hyundai Motor Group to ensure organizational success.
Hyundai MOBIS aims to affect positive changes in society and fulfill dreams through mobility innovation while maintaining transparent and ethical management practices.
source_title: Hyundai MOBIS Savannah (MSV) - Mobis

facts: Hyundai Mobis, a South Korean automotive supplier under the Hyundai Motor Group umbrella, has clinched a major contract with Volkswagen.
The agreement involves the delivery of Battery System Assembly (BSA) for Volkswagen’s forthcoming generation of electric vehicles.
The exact magnitude of the order has not been disclosed by the partnering companies thus far.
Hyundai Mobis has revealed its intention to establish a dedicated manufacturing facility close to Volkswagen’s manufacturing plants in Spain later this year.
This facility will cater to the production of battery systems, following requisite board approvals.
The battery systems are destined for compact electric cars built upon Volkswagen’s MEB Entry platform.
These vehicles include VW ID.2, Cupra Raval, VW ID.2 X, and a Skoda derivative, slated for production at Martorell and Pamplona plants commencing in 2025.
Volkswagen has publicly disclosed its ambition to manufacture three million compact electric vehicles within its Spanish facilities between 2025 and 2030.
Seat, a subsidiary of Volkswagen, is injecting three billion euros into the Martorell plant’s transformation near Barcelona to facilitate this endeavor.
Seat has also allocated 300 million euros for battery assembly activities at the Martorell site.
Hyundai Mobis might exclusively furnish batteries for models originating from the Pamplona plant.
The supply of battery cells is entrusted to PowerCo’s establishment in Sagunt, near Valencia, which has been under construction since March 2023.
The comprehensive battery systems to be delivered by Hyundai Mobis encompass the battery pack, battery management system, and thermal management components.

The press release does not explicitly specify whether this engagement pertains solely to contract production based on Volkswagen’s specifications or if Hyundai Mobis will additionally partake in developmental services.
Hyundai Mobis underscores the significance of this “significant order for electric vehicle components received from a prominent global automaker.”
Hyundai Mobis boasts over a decade of experience in large-scale production of electric vehicle parts.
Hyundai’s supplier division already operates battery system production facilities in South Korea and the Czech Republic.
The Czech plant focuses on assembling batteries for the Hyundai Kona Electric model.
Plans for new battery system manufacturing units in Indonesia and the United States (Georgia, catering to the extensive Hyundai Metaplant) have already been confirmed.
An accompanying map indicates the presence of a Battery System Assembly (BSA) facility in China as well.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis Secures Significant Order from Volkswagen for Battery System Assembly

facts: On Nov. 8, 2022, Hyundai Glovis is entering the fast-growing battery recycling business market.
The company will be reusing used electric vehicle batteries for its energy storage system (ESS) business.
On Nov. 7, 2022, an auto parts manufacturer plans to hire 630 workers at a new factory in southeast Georgia.
The supplier facility is located in STATESBORO, Ga.
The supplier will supply Hyundai Motor Group's first U.S. electric vehicle plant that is under construction nearby.

links.context: Article on Hyundai Glovis entering EV battery recycling
Article on supplier hiring 630 workers near Hyundai's EV plant in Georgia

source_title: Hyundai Glovis recycling EV batteries +

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) held its Grand Opening on March 26.
At the grand opening, Hyundai Motor Company President and CEO José Muñoz stated that Hyundai planned to increase direct jobs to 14,000.
The job commitment update appeared to correlate with an announcement that the metaplant would increase production from 300,000 units to 500,000.
Confusion exists regarding how many direct jobs Hyundai committed to bringing to the Savannah region versus how many are officially connected to on-site affiliate suppliers.
Even officials making statements have had trouble keeping the job and production numbers straight.
source_title: Hyundai officials set record straight on jobs commitment number

facts: Georgia-based companies recruited Mexican college graduates for engineering work but allegedly had them perform assembly line work instead.
Mechanical engineer Luis accepted a quality engineering role in West Point, Georgia, and arrived at Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport in December 2020.
Upon arrival, Luis was told by his new employer that the engineering job he uprooted his life to take did not exist.
For the next 11 months, Luis worked 12-hour shifts on a Georgia auto parts manufacturer factory floor, carrying heavy loads over 100 pounds.

Six Mexican nationals told The Atlanta Journal-Constitution they were brought to the U.S. under false pretenses with engineering job offers from a staffing agency as smoke screens for low-paid assembly-line work.
The visa used by the workers is the Trade NAFTA (TN) visa, which is meant for high-skilled jobs and does not cover assembly line work.
The company that hired the Mexican nationals and supported their TN visa applications is Newnan staffing agency GB2G, Inc., also known as Allswell.
Workers say Allswell hired them to work for several different auto parts manufacturers in the West Point area that supply the Kia auto plant there.
Allswell has not been charged with wrongdoing and claims full compliance with federal, state, and local laws, stating federal agents found no violations during on-site inspections.
Only one Mexican professional filed a formal complaint against Allswell; others preferred to move on due to lengthy legal processes and immigration status risks.
Luis was promised housing but instead shared a house floor with seven other TN workers upon arrival.
The six Mexican nationals arrived between December 2020 and May 2021 during a pandemic-induced labor shortage that made low-wage jobs harder to fill with U.S. workers.
Shelly Anand, executive director of Sur Legal Collaborative, noted employers are seeking more compliant labor forces due to high turnover in certain industries.

Ben Botts, legal director at Centro de los Derechos del Migrante (CDM), noted allegations of TN visa fraud in the West Point auto sector are not unusual and highlighted concerns about sourcing parts from producers engaging in unlawful practices.

In April, a class action lawsuit filed in Atlanta federal court claimed a Gwinnett-based labor recruiter used the TN visa program to hire dozens of Mexican engineers for manual labor in Luverne, Alabama.
Lead plaintiff Jaime Obregon Acosta holds a bachelor's in mechanical engineering and master's in business administration; over 40 hired through the visa joined the suit.
Civil rights litigator Daniel Werner cited cases of visa misclassification aimed at lowering labor costs.
Workers hung on for months due to pandemic difficulties finding jobs in Mexico and needing money, with some feeling ashamed to return home.
Luis said workers were treated like animals and pack mules, while Augusto called the job offer a total lie claiming companies deceive the U.S. government about bringing qualified personnel.
Immigration attorney Charles Kuck stated that if true, the experience could amount to human trafficking; one engineer applied for protection under these laws.
Auto parts manufacturers might not be responsible for TN visa violations since they used a third-party staffing firm to shield themselves from liability and compliance costs.
Charlotte Alexander, associate professor at Georgia State University’s College of Law, noted employers use third-party agencies to avoid compliance costs and liability.
Documentation showed Mexican professionals worked at three parts makers supplying Kia in West Point: Hyundai Mobis, Hyundai Glovis, and Mando.
Allswell's website lists Hyundai Mobis, Hyundai Glovis, and Mando as clients; they are South Korean auto parts manufacturers with facilities in West Point.
Mobis and Mando did not respond to comment requests.
Head of Glovis Georgia HR denied working with Allswell, suggesting Glovis America might have received the workers.
Kia Georgia stated it is committed to equal employment opportunities and expects the same from business partners.
Allswell should not be confused with All’s Well, a healthcare staffing firm with branches in California, South Carolina, Nevada, Florida, and Pennsylvania.
TN visa status is for Canadian or Mexican nationals working temporarily in the U.S. under 60 NAFTA professions requiring at least a bachelor's degree.
In fiscal year 2021, the U.S. issued 24,904 TN visas, up from 16,119 four years prior; Mexico accounted for 98.8% of total TN visas and dependents in 2021.
The State Department operates the TN program for Mexican applicants and claims aggressive vetting, but it does not collect or publish data on TN visas by destination state or employer.
In fall 2020, Heber Zapata saw a LinkedIn posting from Georgia labor recruitment agency SPJ Connect for U.S.-based engineering roles.
Zapata was offered a $44,000 salary as a production engineer with benefits exceeding his Mexican earnings.
Allswell sent a visa support letter stating the job was professional and specialized, requiring complex engineering knowledge.
Charles Kuck noted the $44,000 offer was a red flag; average annual salary for mechanical or industrial engineers in West Point is roughly $80,000.
AJC reviewed six TN visa support letters showing five men were hired at Hyundai Mobis and one at Mando, with salaries ranging from $40,000 to $44,000.
Zapata worked overnight shifts (7 p.m. to 7 a.m.) on the assembly line at the Hyundai Mobis plant.
Zapata earned roughly $580 per week (~$30,000/year), while American workers at the same plant performed similar work for higher hourly wages.
Zapata filed reports with LaGrange Police Department on July 24, 2021, the National Human Trafficking Hotline, and ICE; no action was noted by police.
Eight months after arriving in West Point, Zapata left Allswell in August 2021 and applied for a visa granting legal status to human trafficking victims.
His trafficking allegations are still under investigation.
SPJ Connect continues advertising vacancies via social media videos pitching TN visa benefits to Mexican university students, with executive Yadira Leos noting roughly 500 monthly vacancies.
On April 8, Leos addressed Universidad Tecnológica de Huejotzingo, emphasizing personal and professional growth for workers.
Colleague Elizabeth Cantú stated in a deleted video that everyone goes to the production line the first year regardless of job title.
Allswell is registered in Coweta County; SPJ Connect is based in Hogansville with offices in Mexico.
SPJ Connect and Allswell share the same registered agent address: 124 Big Horn Drive in Newnan, with Youngjin Lee listed as CEO of SPJ Connect and secretary of Allswell.
Georgia's auto industry boom transformed rural areas, but migrant workers might mean Georgians don't reap full economic benefits from locally located jobs.
Leos stated employers prefer TN employees over locals once familiar with Mexican work culture, repeating this message on June 13 Facebook Live.
LaGrange city officials boosted recruitment efforts; two Economic Development Office officers participated in a July 2021 recruiting trip to Mexico.
A consortium of Mexican technical colleges signed a collaboration agreement with SPJ Connect three months prior to the July 2021 event.
LaGrange Mayor James Thornton stated industries must recruit internationally when local jobs cannot be filled locally.
Tension exists on assembly lines; Augusto noted comments about Mexican workers taking jobs and earning more than locals.
Jose, an industrial engineer from Sinaloa, worked as a production supervisor in Tijuana earning ~$850/week before accepting a 2020 position at Hyundai Mobis.
The AJC withheld Jose's last name due to immigration status uncertainty after he left Hyundai Mobis but remained in the U.S.
The story originated from an April 2022 AJC publication about a class action suit, leading six former Allswell employees to contact the newspaper with similar experiences.
source_title: ‘A total lie’: Mexican engineers say they were misled into manual labor in Ga. factories - Human Trafficking Search

facts: LG Energy Solution is telling some Georgia officials that it could build its own factory in the state if rival SK Innovation cannot proceed.

LG Energy Solution CEO Jong Hyun Kim wrote a Wednesday letter to Democratic U.S. Sen Raphael Warnock on Wednesday saying LG “is prepared to do whatever we can to help the people and workers of Georgia.”
If some other entity acquires the SK plant, LG could help run the $2.6 billion electric vehicle battery plant in Commerce.
SK Innovation plans to hire 2,600 workers for the Commerce facility.
source_title: South Korea’s LG Energy Solution Says It Could Build A Factory In Georgia – WABE

facts: LGES is a leader in battery technology.
LGES secured a $4.3 billion LFP battery contract with European automaker Ampere.
LGES formed a $4.3 billion joint venture with Hyundai in Georgia.
The company's strategy leverages low-cost LFP chemistry and IRA-driven North American manufacturing.
Lithium iron phosphate (LFP) batteries offer lower costs, longer lifespans, and reduced reliance on geopolitically sensitive materials like cobalt and nickel.
LGES deploys Cell to Pack (CTP) technology, which eliminates intermediate modules to optimize space and energy density.
The 39 GWh contract with Ampere is set to power 590,000 EVs over five years.
Manufacturing these batteries in Poland reduces exposure to China's dominance in battery materials.
The move aligns with EU battery regulations that mandate sustainability and traceability standards.
The Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) offers a $7,500 tax credit for consumers who purchase eligible vehicles.
IRA eligibility requires that 60% of a battery's components be manufactured in North America, a threshold rising to 80% by 2026.
LGES's joint venture with Hyundai is located in Bryan County, Georgia.
The $4.3 billion Georgia plant has a 30 GWh capacity.
The plant will supply Hyundai's U.S. assembly lines.
The plant is designed to source 100% of battery components domestically.
Vertical integration is supported by on-site battery pack assembly by Hyundai Mobis, which slashes lead times and costs.
Plant alignment with IRA requirements ensures Hyundai's EVs qualify for tax credits.
The partnership targets a market projected to see 60% of EV sales driven by IRA-eligible vehicles by 2030.
The U.S. and EU seek to reduce dependence on Chinese battery supply chains.
LFP's lower material costs reduce price volatility.
LGES has an existing partnership involving a $3 billion investment in U.S. production lines for Toyota.
By 2030, the Georgia plant alone could contribute $1.2 billion in annual revenue, assuming a 30% margin.
LGES stock is currently trading at a forward P/E of 12.4.
Potential risks include tightening IRA thresholds and supply chain bottlenecks delaying production.
source_title: LG Energy Solution: A Strategic Powerhouse in the EV Transition

facts: U.S. immigration authorities carried out a raid at Hyundai Motor Group’s massive electric vehicle complex outside Savannah, Ga.
The raid halted construction at an adjacent battery plant under development.
The information was reported by the Associated Press.
source_title: ICE raids Hyundai EV Metaplant in Georgia, halts battery site - Automotive News

facts: Georgia Ports Authority (GPA) is a key factor for automakers, their suppliers, other manufacturers, and logistics operations deciding to bring jobs to Georgia.
GPA President and CEO Griff Lynch stated that GPA's mission is to drive success across the entire state, not just Savannah or Atlanta.
In partnership with the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD), the state announced 350 projects from potential port users in Fiscal Year 2023.
The FY2023 project announcements will bring more than 33,000 manufacturing and logistics jobs and $21 billion in investment to 96 counties across Georgia.
GPA, GDEcD, and the Georgia Department of Transportation are working together to foster responsible growth that delivers good jobs while preserving quality of life.
Georgia Ports is investing $29 million in an overpass that will route trucks leaving its Ocean Terminal in Savannah directly onto U.S. 17, avoiding community streets.
According to GDEcD's Global Commerce division, Georgia's growing electric vehicle ecosystem has touched every corner of the state.
The growth of Georgia's EV ecosystem is led by announcements associated with Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, which have located in more than 10 counties across the state.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that companies in e-mobility are prioritizing speed-to-market solutions.
Communities can prepare for new opportunities by investing in site development and obtaining "Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development" certification.
Anovion Technologies, a synthetic graphite manufacturer creating 400 jobs in Decatur County, Southwest Georgia, was swayed to locate there by the state's recruitment effort.
Anovion Executive Chairman Chip Dunn cited meetings with Gov. Brian Kemp, GPA's inland port system, and access to waterway and land transport as factors in the company's decision.
GPA's inland port system includes the Appalachian Regional Port in Crandall, Ga., and the Blue Ridge Connector rail yard now under construction near Gainesville, Ga.
The inland port system supports the movement of large, heavy products from centrally located communities to the state's deepwater ports.
In the Savannah area alone, the inventory of industrial property totals more than 124 million square feet.
More than 14 million square feet of industrial property in the Savannah area was under construction at the close of Calendar Year 2023.

Cushman & Wakefield broker Stephen Ezelle stated in a fourth-quarter report that the Savannah regional economy continues to experience expansion and strong absorption in the industrial sector despite a slowing U.S. economy.
Ezelle projected that Savannah's industrial market will eclipse 8 million TEUs of throughput by 2030 due to continued growth at Georgia Ports.
The operational efficiency offered by GPA and the shift in import cargo from the West Coast and NY/NJ are key drivers in the growth of Savannah's industrial market.
Despite national moderation of container trade, Savannah is expected to continue balanced expansion thanks to the Hyundai announcement and GPA's growing national market share.
Ezelle predicted continued absorption of well-located light industrial warehouse space in Savannah.
Savannah's industrial sector ended 2023 with an overall vacancy rate of 7 percent.

GPA Director of economic and industrial development Stacy Watson stated that being within 30 miles of the port is great but not required to be competitive in drayage, lease rates, and cost of doing business.
Watson noted that locations 40 or 50 miles away from Savannah are an easy drive due to the port's location upriver of the population center and GDOT's efforts to reduce truck route congestion.
Direct truck routes from the Port of Savannah connect to Interstates 95 and 16, and on-terminal service is provided by two Class I railroads.
Watson highlighted that available properties in Bryan, Liberty, and McIntosh counties, as well as points west, offer interstate and rail links to the port and inland markets.
Augusta is a short drive from Savannah, and a majority of its manufacturers are port users.

Development Authority of Augusta President Cal Wray stated that Augusta-Richmond County provides an ideal location for port users due to available affordable industrial land, a key base for manufacturers with over 350 already calling it home, and easy rail/highway connections to the port.
Recent announcements in Augusta include Aurubis Richmond, a metal smelting operation bringing 250 jobs and $600 million in investment.
Denkai America, a copper film producer, is providing 250 jobs and $440 million in investment in Augusta.
Purecycle Technologies, which recycles plastic into like-new pellets for reuse, announced 100 jobs and $400 million in investment in Augusta.
Augusta has a stand-out electric vehicle manufacturing ecosystem including EZGo Textron and Club Car with related suppliers in Richmond and Columbia counties.
Metals and chemical manufacturing are two other important industries in the Augusta area.
GPA supports efficient operation of existing industries and local economic development efforts, serving as a key partner in recruiting new industry.
GPA is a resource for existing manufacturers who have invested billions in the region and announced significant further investments in the last five years.
source_title: Georgia Ports highlights site selection opportunities - DredgeWire : DredgeWire

facts: Local, state, federal and German officials welcomed a $340 million copper recycling and smelting plant in Augusta.
The project exemplifies greener industry and Georgia’s economic focus on the growing electric vehicle market.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp said “Welcome to the future” during remarks to about 100 people at Augusta Corporate Park for the ceremonial groundbreaking.
Aurubis CEO Roland Harings stated the 125-employee facility is slated to start production in January 2024.
Three copper smelting facilities already operate in the United States, with two located in Arizona and one in Utah.
The existing U.S. smelting facilities process directly mined copper, whereas Augusta’s Aurubis facility will smelt copper extracted from items such as pipes, wiring, and computers.
The Augusta facility will form the recycled copper into large sheets for manufacturers to use in their finished products.
Traditional liquid-fuel cars typically contain 18-49 pounds of copper, according to the Copper Development Association.
Hybrid electric vehicles contain about 85 pounds of copper, while plug-in hybrid EVs contain 132 pounds, according to the Copper Development Association.

Pat Wilson, commissioner of the Georgia Department of Economic Development, visited Aurubis’ global headquarters in Hamburg, Germany, which helped steer the state’s decision to focus on the electrification of the auto industry.
In the six months prior to the announcement, Georgia announced that EV manufacturers Rivian and Hyundai would open vehicle production plants in the state.

Pat Wilson described Aurubis along with the Rivian and Hyundai investments as long-term strategic investments in the EV supply chain that will pay generational returns to their communities, the state, and the world.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that the recycling and supply chain component of companies like Aurubis is only going to grow, calling it a decades-long start to a successful business model.
The Aurubis Richmond plant will process about 90,000 tons of complex recycling materials annually to extract about 35,000 tons of blister copper, according to Aurubis.
Aurubis Richmond will be the company's second facility in the United States.
Aurubis Buffalo employs about 650 workers at an upstate New York plant that produces copper and brass sheets for products such as roofing copper and brass hose nozzles.
Copper is highly sought after globally due to its malleability, conductive properties, and role as a strengthening component in alloys.
The Copper Alliance states that copper can be recycled repeatedly without any loss of performance and there is no quality difference between mined and recycled copper.
Copper closed at $4.12 per pound in global market trading on the Thursday prior to publication.
source_title: Aurubis Richmond in Augusta will be the nation's first recycled-copper smelter

facts: Aurubis AG is Europe's largest copper producer.
Aurubis is making a $800 million investment to establish a copper recycling facility in Richmond, Georgia.
The facility is scheduled to commence operations in 2025 after a four-year construction period.
This will be the first large-scale copper recycling plant in the region.
The facility has an annual processing capacity of 180,000 metric tons of complex copper scrap.
The facility will produce 70,000 tons of refined copper annually.
The U.S. exported 960,000 tons of copper scrap in 2024.
41% of the 2024 U.S. copper scrap exports were shipped to China.
Aurubis is leveraging evolving Trump's trade policy impact, including potential tariffs on copper imports.
Demand drivers for the facility include data center expansion and artificial intelligence infrastructure.
Copper components in these sectors require rapid turnover every 3–5 years.
The Richmond facility's $800 million capital expenditure underscores Aurubis' commitment to reshaping global copper supply chains.
Primary mining accounts for approximately 60% of global copper production.
Georgia's location taps into the southeastern U.S.'s robust automotive and telecommunications sectors, which generate substantial copper scrap volumes.
CEO Toralf Haag emphasized the facility's role in "closing the loop" for North American manufacturers.
