The facility enables manufacturers to reintegrate recycled copper into production cycles without dependency on international exporters.
The plant's design incorporates advanced mining 4.0 advancements capable of processing complex copper scrap.
Complex copper scrap encompasses mixed-material waste from electronics and industrial equipment that traditional recyclers often deem uneconomical.
Aurubis can recover copper with 99.9% purity, matching the quality of newly mined metal.
The facility simultaneously extracts precious metals like gold and silver from byproducts.
With a 70,000-ton annual output, the facility could meet nearly 5% of U.S. refined copper demand.
This output reduces import reliance by an estimated 15% over the next decade.
Over 80% of U.S. copper scrap has historically been processed by small-scale regional operators lacking capacity for complex materials.
Aurubis is introducing vertically integrated operations that streamline collection, sorting, and refining.
Haag noted, "North America's export-centric scrap economy represents a $12 billion opportunity for localized value addition".
The company projects a 20% reduction in logistics costs for domestic manufacturers by shortening supply chains previously dependent on Asian processors.
The Trump administration launched a 2025 investigation into copper import tariffs, including scrap.
The investigation aims to incentivize domestic production amidst escalating U.S.-China trade tensions.
Potential tariff scenarios could see imported refined copper face 10–25% duties.
Industry analysts estimate that tariffs could raise U.S. copper prices by 8–12%.
China imported 393,600 tons of U.S. copper scrap in 2024, representing 41% of total exports.
China has tightening environmental standards on imported scrap.
Forward contracts have already secured 65% of the plant's initial feedstock from automotive and telecom sectors.
Processing 180,000 tons of scrap annually could prevent 500,000 metric tons of CO2 emissions compared to primary copper production.
This emission reduction is equivalent to removing 108,000 gasoline-powered vehicles from roads.
Copper recycling requires 85% less energy than virgin metal production.
The project potentially qualifies Aurubis for federal clean manufacturing tax credits under the Inflation Reduction Act.
Data centers are projected to consume 1.2 million tons of copper annually by 2030.
Data centers will become a major scrap source due to 3–5 year hardware refresh cycles.
The company is developing proprietary sorting systems to handle server racks and cooling systems.
The target recovery rate for copper from data center waste streams is 95%.
Each data center rack requires up to 5,000 pounds of copper for power and cooling systems.
Tech giants are investing $200 billion in U.S. data center construction through 2030.
The development follows Rio Tinto's copper shift toward supporting clean energy infrastructure.
Aurubis has identified three additional U.S. sites for potential expansion.
Target regions for expansion include areas with high-tech manufacturing clusters in Texas and the Pacific Northwest.
The modular plant design allows for capacity scaling up to 300,000 tons annually.
Capacity scaling is contingent on scrap availability and policy support.
Copper maintains 100% of its physical and chemical properties through the recycling process.
Copper can be infinitely recyclable without quality degradation.
Primary ore grades globally continue to decline below 0.5% copper content.
Recycled copper production requires up to 85% less energy than primary extraction, translating to a carbon footprint reduction of approximately 7.5 tons of CO2 per ton of copper processed.
Recycling avoids the $10,000-15,000 per ton capital expenditure associated with new mine development.
Recycling eliminates the 170 tons of mining waste typically generated for each ton of virgin copper.
Recycling uses just 15% of the water required for conventional extraction and refining.
The Richmond facility incorporates next-generation separation technologies including X-ray fluorescence sorting, eddy current separation, and artificial intelligence-driven optical sorting.
These systems can identify and separate copper alloys with up to 99% accuracy.
The facility employs advanced hydrometallurgical processes that use biological leaching agents rather than harsh chemicals to extract copper from difficult-to-process materials.
Emerging extended producer responsibility (EPR) legislation in several states will require manufacturers to finance end-of-life management for electronic products.
These regulations are expected to increase domestic copper scrap availability by 25-30% by 2030.
The SEC's proposed climate disclosure rules may incentivize manufacturers to source recycled copper.
Recycled copper carries approximately 65% lower embedded carbon than primary metal.
As reported by Reuters, this trend aligns with the broader mining and clean energy transition taking place globally.
source_title: Aurubis to Ramp Up New US Copper Recycling Facility

facts: Global demand is rising for recycled metals, with copper materials and lithium-ion batteries among those most needed.
China dominates the global metal-recycling investment market, accounting for about 80% of investment at metal-recycling facilities worldwide.
The U.S. accounts for nearly 11% of global investment in metal-recycling facilities.
Industrial Info Resources is tracking nearly 500 active and proposed metal-recycling projects worldwide, totaling more than US$45 billion of investment.

China accounts for more than 94% of the US$14.6 billion worth of projects deemed by Industrial Info Resources as having a high likelihood (81% or more) of beginning construction as scheduled within the next 12 months, with the U.S. making up only 3.1%.
Strong demand from the data-center and automotive markets is keeping global copper prices at elevated levels.
Aurubis Richmond LLC, a subsidiary of German firm Aurubis AG, expects to finish construction later this year on an expansion of its copper scrap recycling and processing plant in Augusta, Georgia.
Under construction since 2022, the additional capacity at the Augusta plant is expected to double the facility's output to 70,000 tons per year, with demand fueled by data centers and EVs.

Aurubis stated in a September 2025 press release that American industry alone currently needs about 1.8 million tons of copper per year, and experts estimate that demand will rise nearly 30% in the next five years.
Aurubis noted that as of now, the U.S. imports about half of the copper it processes, and multi-metal recycling can close this gap quickly.

Cirba Solutions, a subsidiary of environmental-services and heavy-construction firm The Heritage Group, has two projects set to be completed this year: a lithium-ion battery-recycling plant in Columbia, South Carolina, and an expansion of its battery-recycling campus in Lancaster, Ohio.
The new South Carolina plant is expected to produce up to 500,000 EV batteries per year.
The Ohio expansion is expected to boost output by more than 250,000 per year.
Ara Partners is proposing an expansion of its lithium-ion battery-recycling plant in Bartlesville, Oklahoma.
The first phase of the Ara Partners facility completed construction in December and can process 14,000 tons per year of battery materials.
The Ara Partners expansion is designed to elevate processing capacity to 50,000 tons per year.
China's copper-recycling market is experiencing a regional shortage of copper scrap raw materials, fueling a sustained rally in copper prices.

State-owned and private companies across China are investing in secondary copper smelting; among them is Beiliu Guitong Equity Investment Partnership Enterprise, preparing to begin construction this summer on its secondary copper smelter in Yulin, Guangxi.
The Beiliu Guitong project is expected to produce anode copper from scrap copper waste.
Industrial Info Resources tracks over 250,000 current and future projects worth $30.2 trillion (USD) across the world.
source_title: Metal Recycling Offers Answer to Rising Copper, Battery Needs

facts: South Korean President Moon Jae-in is scheduled to visit Georgia this weekend to survey progress on the SK Innovation electric-vehicle battery plant.
The visit follows Mr. Moon’s summit with President Joe Biden in Washington on Friday, where economic cooperation was highlighted as a priority amplified by recent developments in Georgia.
A legal feud between Korean battery giants LG Energy Solution and SK had threatened to close the largest-ever foreign investment in Georgia.
The dispute was settled in April when SK agreed to pay $1.8 billion to LG.
At least 2,600 jobs and $2.6 billion were on the line during the dispute.
The clarity provided by the settlement and the announcement of Mr. Moon’s visit triggered a flurry of Korean investment announcements in Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group said it would invest $7.4 billion into mobility initiatives including upgrading facilities to prepare for electrification.
Hyundai Motor Group stopped short of committing those funds specifically to its plants in Alabama and Georgia.
SK and Ford announced a $5.3 billion battery joint venture called BlueOvalSK on Thursday.
The partners indicated they could build two new U.S. battery plants as part of the BlueOvalSK joint venture.
SK supplies batteries to the electric Ford F-150 Lightning, which President Biden drove earlier in the week before its official Wednesday unveiling.
Korean news sources reported that SK Innovation’s chairman left Korea on a flight bound for the U.S. on Wednesday.

Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson welcomed the collaboration, stating it could connect Georgia’s future-facing EV sector with its traditional auto manufacturing heritage.

Commissioner Pat Wilson stated the announcement provides an opportunity to renew a long-held partnership with Ford, which manufactured cars in Georgia from 1947-2006, while rapidly expanding SK’s footprint in the state.
Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that every new commitment by an automotive OEM shifting goals to e-mobility drives the transition for suppliers and accompanying jobs.
Commissioner Pat Wilson stated Georgia is open for business and ready to work together to seize the moment.
Georgia announced a $10 million investment on Thursday by Korean automotive firm Duckyang.
Duckyang supplies SK Battery with modules and energy-storage systems.
Duckyang sells cockpit modules globally to Kia, Hyundai, Renault, and other companies.
source_title: Korean President Set to Visit SK Plant in Georgia This Weekend - Global Atlanta

facts: Korean supplier Duckyang Industrial is set to invest $10 million to open a manufacturing facility in Braselton, Ga.

facts: Seoyon E-HWA is investing $75 million to establish a new facility in Chatham County, Georgia.
Seoyon E-HWA will create 500 new jobs.
Seoyon E-HWA is an automobile manufacturer operating in various countries such as Korea, Europe, and India.
Seoyon E-HWA will supply electric vehicle parts in Chatham County, Georgia.
Seoyon E-HWA aims to contribute to making Savannah a central city for automobile development and production through continuous cooperation with the local government.
This investment marks Seoyon's second Georgia facility, committing to nearly doubling the number of jobs it supports in the state and extending its partnership with the business community.

Seoyon E-HWA will be located in the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center, ideally positioned close to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, the Port of Savannah, Interstates I-16 and I-95, and more.
Green Georgia is investing $59 million to establish its headquarters in Thomaston, Georgia.
Green Georgia will create 170 new jobs in Upson County.

Upson County has successfully grown its manufacturing industry over the last few years.

facts: The Effingham County Industrial Development Authority (ECIDA) announced that Sewon America, a subsidiary of Korean-based Sewon Precision Industry Co., is expanding its manufacturing operations in Rincon.
The expansion project costs $50 million.
The project will add 98,250 square feet to the facility.
The project will create 100 new jobs.
The expansion introduces new capabilities for Sewon America.
This marks the first expansion by a Hyundai supplier since the launch of Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America in late 2024.

Misti Martin, Deputy Commissioner of Global Commerce at the Georgia Department of Economic Development, called the expansion a full-circle moment and incredible milestone for the Metaplant’s supplier network.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development values long-lasting partnerships and seeks partners who share that commitment.
Matt Saxon, Chairman of the ECIDA, stated that Sewon America chose Effingham County for its second U.S. facility.
The expansion underscores Sewon America's confidence in the community, workforce, and strong business climate of Effingham County.
Rincon Mayor Kevin Exley expressed excitement about the opportunity for more quality jobs in the community.
The City of Rincon looks forward to partnering with Sewon to ensure local citizens can take advantage of the new positions.
Robert Vazquez, Director of Human Resources at Sewon America, stated the expansion will allow the company to better serve customers and add new capabilities.
Sewon America plans to grow alongside the community, create new job opportunities, and continue investing in its talented workforce in Effingham County.
Sewon America operates one of the largest automobile body parts manufacturing plants in North America.
The company opened its first Georgia facility in LaGrange in 2008.
The LaGrange facility has since doubled in size.
Sewon America began operations in Rincon in 2025.
With the expansion, Sewon will continue to supply electric vehicle (EV) body parts for original equipment manufacturers (OEMs).
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America is one of the OEMs that receives EV body parts from Sewon.
The project was supported by the Georgia Department of Economic Development, the City of Rincon, and the Effingham County Board of Commissioners.
source_title: Aug. 28 - Sewon America Launches $50 Million Expansion in Rincon | Effingham/Statesboro News | savannahbusinessjournal.com

facts: Sewon America, Inc. operates in manufacturing and warehouse/distribution centers.

canonical_name: Savannah Metropolitan Statistical Area Manufacturing Sector
facts: Savannah MSA manufacturing firms account for 18.5% of the area’s economic output as measured by GDP.
Manufacturing accounted for $6 billion of output in 2023.
As of early 2025, there were 527 manufacturing establishments in the Savannah CSA.
These establishments employed 30,316 workers (+3.2%).
Average weekly wage was $1,905 (+9.7%).
Covered employment in manufacturing in the Savannah MSA increased to 24,261 as of early 2025.
Since the May 2022 Hyundai Metaplant announcement, regional manufacturing base increased 29%.
This represents a gain of 5,442 workers.
An additional $900 million in worker compensation is flowing through the regional economy.
Over 88% of manufacturing growth has been at employers in the transportation equipment sector.
Growth occurred at Gulfstream, Hyundai Metaplant, and supply chain firms for the Metaplant.
Recent activity and announcements point toward another strong year for manufacturing in 2026.
Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority (Savannah JDA) represents Bryan, Bulloch, Chatham, and Effingham counties.
Savannah JDA continued its partnership with Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
In March 2025, HMGMA held its grand opening.
The project is the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history.
Grand opening occurred just two and a half years after breaking ground.
In September 2025, Hyundai Motor Group announced Phase 2 expansion.
Phase 2 will bring capacity to 500,000 vehicles.
Phase 2 will include 3,000 jobs and $2.7 billion in investment.
HMGMA is currently producing the IONIQ 5 and IONIQ 9.
HMGMA is expected to begin production of additional EVs and hybrids in 2026.
Supplier activity surrounding the project expanded with more than $2.5 billion in new capital investment.

Savannah JDA supports site development, infrastructure coordination, and project impact across the four-county region.
Gulfstream Aerospace Corporation is the largest private employer in Savannah.
Gulfstream has a global workforce of 20,000+ workers in production, service, maintenance, engineering, and R&D.
Gulfstream announced the all-new, super-midsize Gulfstream G300 as the latest aircraft to join its next-generation fleet.
The G300 made its first flight in December 2025.
First flight demonstrated significant program maturity and launched its rigorous flight test program.
Gulfstream aircraft surpassed 3 million nautical miles flown on sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) blends in 2025.
First customer delivery of the all-new Gulfstream G800 was completed in 2025.
The G800 is the world’s longest range business aircraft.
G800 entry into service follows FAA and EASA certifications earlier in 2025.
Gulfstream announced its 50th customer delivery of the G700.
The G700 was outfitted at the Savannah completions center.
Since entering service in April 2024, the G700 fleet has accumulated more than 11,700 flight hours.
The G380 fleet completed its 300th customer delivery.
The in-service G280 fleet has accrued more than 575,000 flight hours.
The G280 fleet has completed nearly 358,000 landings.
JCB’s North American headquarters is centered in Savannah.
It is a 500,000-square-foot facility supporting over 115 dealers in the U.S. and Canada.
In August 2025, JCB was awarded a $45 million contract to supply 4CX Backhoe Loaders to the United States armed forces.
In November 2025, JCB won the “Tractor of the Year” award for its all-new high-power Fastrac 6300.
Savannah Economic Development Authority (SEDA) worked with partners to attract new job opportunities and investment to Chatham County.
SEDA facilitated eight new companies or expansions in 2025.
SEDA finalized and adopted a new strategic plan for priorities over the next five to seven years.
SEDA collaborated on industry expansion, workforce needs, and opportunities from major regional investments including Hyundai Metaplant America growth.
World Trade Center Savannah (WTCSav) advanced international engagement through high-impact programs and global outreach.
In partnership with Georgia Power, WTCSav hosted the Southeast Georgia Good News and International Protocol Training for representatives from more than 23 counties.
WTCSav convened the inaugural Global Business Summit, bringing together nearly 130 leaders from Quebec, Washington D.C., Georgia state agencies, and Southeast Georgia.
WTCSav supported the Greater Savannah Ghana Alliance’s first tri-city mission.
This mission facilitated more than 40 business connections and meetings with government, educational, and industry partners.
WTCSav led an outbound mission to Marseille, France for the World Trade Center’s Association’s 55th Global Business Forum.
WTCSav’s TradeBridge program expanded in 2025 with the addition of County Limerick, the first Irish west coast county to join.
WTCSav completed 118 trade service projects in 2025.
WTCSav hosted 20 delegations from eight countries in 2025.
WTCSav delivered cross-continental programs and continued support of 16 Southeast Georgia counties and the City of Savannah as its founding investor.
Regional Industry Support Enterprise (RISE) team supported workforce development in an eight-county region including Bryan, Bulloch, Candler, Chatham, Effingham, Evans, Liberty, and Screven Counties.
RISE focus areas include Education (K-12 and Higher Ed), Military, Childcare, Underrepresented populations, Housing, Marketing, and Transportation.
Key RISE initiatives included a Military/Employer Forum.

RISE grew K-12 engagement with 8th Grade Experience, Regional Educator Externship Program, and Building Your Future: Career and College Showcase.
21 employer forums with 608 total attendees were held throughout the region in 2025.
RISE supported six hiring events across the region, reaching more than 3,400 job seekers.
RISE led a best practices trip to West Georgia with 35 stakeholders from economic development, education, industry, and non-profit partners.
Bryan County manufacturing employment at 40 establishments increased 18%.
Employment rose to 1,900 workers as of early 2025.
As of year-end 2025, employment at Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) EV facility increased to 1,444 workers.
HMGMA employment represents a 31% gain in one year.
By year-end, associated automotive manufacturers of componentry, systems, electronics, plastics, and parts employed an additional 2,633 workers in Bryan County.
Continued employment growth is anticipated in 2026 as production ramps up at the Metaplant and allied firms.
Effingham County’s manufacturing sector saw strong gains in 2025.
Two new manufacturers commenced operation in Effingham County in 2025.
Total employment in Effingham County's manufacturing sector reached 2,600 workers.
Sewon America announced a $50 million expansion.
Expansion includes new electroplating capabilities.
Expansion includes roughly 100,000 square feet of additional space.

Georgia Transformer committed to a major expansion at its Rincon facility.

Facility will bring 120 high-skill jobs.
Facility introduces defense-related manufacturing to the county’s diversified economy.
2025 brought more than $130 million in new capital investment to Effingham County.
Hundreds of new jobs were added to Effingham County in 2025.
Liberty County’s 29 manufacturers employed about 2,200 workers in 2025.
Atlantic Building Components, a truss manufacturing company headquartered in South Carolina, announced an expansion.
Company is establishing operations in Hinesville with an initial workforce of 25 employees.
Company is positioned to serve the growing regional housing market.
Georgia Department of Community Affairs awarded a $2 million grant to support construction of Tradeport West Way.
Road will open 170 acres for development.

Bulloch County’s 58 manufacturing establishments employed 2,400 workers as of early 2025.

Recent success infuses $1.1 billion investment in Bulloch County.
There is limited availability of publicly supported development sites in Bulloch County as a result.

facts: Auto parts manufacturer Sewon America is investing over $300 million toward a second facility in Georgia, according to a Feb. 21 press release.
Sewon’s new facility will be located in Rincon and will produce EV body parts for original equipment manufacturers.
One of the OEM customers for the Rincon facility is the Hyundai Metaplant near Savannah.
Suppliers for the Hyundai Metaplant have now committed over $1.8 billion in investment in Georgia, at least $800 million higher than initial expectations.
Sewon’s investment is expected to create 740 jobs.
South Korea-based Sewon is another manufacturer on the growing list of Hyundai suppliers either establishing or expanding its operations near the incoming $5.5 billion Hyundai Meta plant.
Sewon already has one facility in LaGrange, Georgia, and the second facility will be Rincon’s largest private investment.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp congratulated Chairman Kim on the company’s then-recent expansion during his first international trip as governor to Korea.
Gov. Kemp stated that discussions with Chairman Kim led to this announcement as Sewon America continues to grow its Georgia operations.
In November, Joon Georgia became the first supplier to announce operations, with plans to invest $317 million in a production facility in Bulloch County.
Later that month, Hyundai Mobis became the second supplier to invest in the area, announcing it would spend $926 million on an EV Power Electric system plant in Bryan County.

In January, Ecoplastic Corporation announced it is investing approximately $205 million in a new manufacturing facility in southeast Georgia to supply components to the incoming electric vehicle campus.
Last month, Seoyon E-Hwa announced it will invest almost $76 million toward its second manufacturing facility in Georgia.
Gov. Brian Kemp called the Hyundai Metaplant the “largest economic development project in state history.”
The Hyundai Metaplant is estimated to create 8,100 jobs in Georgia’s coastal region.
Hyundai began recruiting to fill those Metaplant jobs in November, according to a Savannah Harbor-Interstate I-16 Corridor Joint Development Authority press release.
source_title: Another Hyundai supplier to expand near Georgia EV campus | Supply Chain Dive

facts: Hanon Systems is a global leader in automotive thermal management for electrified mobility.
Hanon Systems has achieved a milestone with the development of the world's first 4th generation heat pump system for electric vehicles.
The 4th generation heat pump system has been commercially available since July.
It is the first to employ a parallel heat source recovery method.
The technology utilizes waste heat from the motor and battery, as well as heat from external air, for heating, cooling, and battery temperature management.
The system substantially enhances EV driving range by addressing one of the primary concerns of electric vehicle owners.
Hanon Systems' modular heat pump system incorporates multiple patented or patent-pending technologies.
A key feature is the integration of the Refrigerant Control Module (RCM) and the Coolant Control Module (CCM) into a unified control unit.
This integration allows for comprehensive management of the entire thermal system, optimizing efficiency and performance.
Subu Nagasubramony, co-CEO of Hanon Systems, emphasized the company's commitment to innovation.
Continuous refinement of the heat pump system design has improved efficiency and reduced power consumption requirements.
Reduced power consumption directly contributes to extended EV driving range.
As the world's second-largest thermal management company, Hanon Systems remains dedicated to developing lightweight and high-efficiency components.
Hanon Systems began mass production of heat pump systems with waste heat recovery in 2015.
In 2018, Hanon Systems launched a second generation system that added battery thermal management functions.
The 2018 launch created the first integrated thermal management system to enhance EV driving range.
The third generation system was developed in 2021 and incorporated an 800V electric compressor.
The 800V electric compressor significantly reduced EV charging times.
The 4th generation heat pump system made its debut in the Kia EV3 in South Korea.
Hanon Systems plans to expand production of the heat pump system to the U.S. and Europe.
Yoon Ho Wang, Vice President of AP Innovation Center at Hanon Systems Research Institute, highlighted the importance of close collaboration with clients in developing this technology.
Hanon Systems has introduced a world-first innovation called Thin HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning) technology.
The Thin HVAC technology reduces the overall HVAC system size by approximately 30%.
This reduction minimizes the exposure of the HVAC system area under the cockpit.
The design change increases legroom for passengers without compromising comfort or performance.
source_title: Hanon Systems Enhances Efficiency with Next-gen Heat Pump System for Electric Vehicles - Environment+Energy Leader

facts: Two additional Hyundai suppliers are establishing manufacturing sites in Georgia, following the automaker's decision to operate in the state.
Auto thermal systems manufacturer Hanon Systems will invest over $40 million in a new manufacturing facility.
Hanon Systems' new plant will be located near Statesboro and will assemble automotive thermal solutions.
Hanon Systems aims to open its Georgia site in May 2024, creating 160 jobs.
South Korea-based Woory Industrial Co. is investing $18 million to establish its first U.S. manufacturing facility in Georgia.
Woory Industrial's new plant will be located in Dublin and will produce electrical heaters, control units, and actuators for EV and internal combustion automakers in North America.
The Woory Industrial site will create more than 130 jobs, with operations expected to commence in November.
Woory Industrial CEO Jungwoo Kim stated that the transition to electric vehicles is the future and the U.S. EV market is the key market among all automotive markets.
Jungwoo Kim added that Georgia will be a good opportunity for Woory Industrial to advance as part of the vanguard of EV development.
The two new supplier factories follow Hyundai's announcement of a $5.5 billion manufacturing campus in Ellabell, which is the largest economic development project in Georgia's history.
Georgia officials originally predicted that Hyundai's Metaplant would attract approximately $1 billion to the state via offsite supplier investments.
As of April, officials stated that Hyundai's suppliers have outpaced that estimate with plans to invest nearly $2 billion in the state.
Angela Hendrix, senior vice president of marketing and public relations for the Savannah Economic Development Authority, said more supplier announcements are anticipated.
Hyundai continues to expand its own direct investment in Georgia.
In late May, Hyundai and LG Energy Solution struck a joint venture deal to build a $4.3 billion battery cell plant adjacent to Hyundai's EV campus.
Hyundai and LG Energy Solution will each take a 50% stake in the venture, where Hyundai will assemble battery packs using cells from the plant for its U.S. manufacturing sites.
source_title: Hyundai’s Georgia supplier network expands to 9 manufacturers | WardsAuto

facts: The automotive thermal system market was valued at $42.8 billion in 2020 and is estimated to reach $71.9 billion by 2030.
The market is growing at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 5.5% from 2021 to 2030.
Asia-Pacific is expected to dominate the global automotive thermal system market due to increased electric vehicle sales and surging vehicle production in India and China.
Rising adoption of electric vehicles driven by environmental concerns and stringent emission regulations is a key market growth factor.
Integration of thermal systems in electric vehicles improves operating range, battery performance, and passenger comfort.
Automobile manufacturers globally are focusing on developing electric and hybrid vehicles, including components like electric compressors and battery cooling systems.
In August 2021, Johnson Electric introduced a high-power electric compressor designed to reduce heat produced by batteries during high-rate fast charging of electric vehicles.
Greater demand for luxury vehicles with advanced features and comfort is significantly impacting the growth of the automotive thermal system industry.
The introduction of stringent emission regulations is driving market growth.
Integration of smart thermal management solutions into vehicles is a significant growth factor.
High costs associated with automotive thermal systems are expected to hamper market growth.
Lack of standardization due to variations in emission regulations is expected to hamper market growth.
A surge in demand for electric vehicles is creating remunerative opportunities for the market.
The introduction of lightweight heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC) solutions and eco-friendly refrigerants is creating new market opportunities.
Technological advancements are expected to create remunerative opportunities for the automotive thermal system market during the forecast period.
The COVID-19 crisis caused massive supply chain slowing, falling business confidence, and increased panic among customer segments.
Government lockdowns and temporary industry shutdowns adversely affected overall production and sales of automotive thermal systems during the pandemic.
Pandemic-related supply chain disruptions halted vehicle production and reduced passenger car sales globally.
A global shortage of components caused major production delays and limited vehicle inventory during the pandemic period.
Despite pandemic challenges, growth in electric car sales was observed and is anticipated to contribute to market growth.
Various government initiatives worldwide are promoting electric vehicle adoption, leading to automotive thermal system market growth.
By application, the fluid transport segment is anticipated to exhibit significant growth in the near future.
On the basis of vehicle type, the passenger cars segment is anticipated to exhibit significant growth in the near future.
Depending on propulsion, the electric and hybrid vehicles segment is anticipated to dominate in the near future.
Region-wise, Europe is anticipated to register the highest CAGR during the forecast period.

Key players operating in the global automotive thermal system market include BorgWarner Inc., Continental AG, Dana Incorporated, Denso Corporation, Gentherm Incorporated, Grayson Thermal Systems, Hanon Systems, Mahle GmbH, Robert Bosch GmbH, and Valeo S.A.
Allied Market Research (AMR) is a full-service market research and business-consulting wing of Allied Analytics LLP based in Wilmington, Delaware.
AMR provides global enterprises and medium/small businesses with market research reports and business intelligence solutions to assist clients in making strategic business decisions.
Data presented in AMR reports is extracted through primary interviews with top officials from leading companies in the domain concerned.
AMR's secondary data procurement methodology includes deep online and offline research and discussion with knowledgeable professionals and analysts in the industry.
source_title: Automotive Thermal System Market to Surge to $71.9 Billion by 2030, Expanding at 5.5% CAGR

canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group EV and Battery Metaplant
facts: The Hyundai metaplant is under construction in Bryan County.
Official announcements project 8,100 jobs at the site.
Construction spending is projected to be at least $5.5 billion.
Suppliers plan to hire thousands more employees for the project.
Hyundai Motor Group projects 8,100 new jobs at its site located at Interstate 16 and Georgia 280 in Bryan County once the EV and battery plant is fully operational.
According to 2022 Georgia Department of Labor data, only 11 private companies employ over 1,000 workers across Chatham, Bryan, and Effingham counties.
Gulfstream is the largest private employer in the region with roughly 11,500 workers around Savannah.
The Savannah-Chatham Public School System employs approximately 5,500 staff members.
Approximately 3,500 civilians work at Fort Stewart, and 640 civilians work at Hunter Army Air Field.
About 27,000 people work in the hospitality industry across Chatham, Bryan, and Effingham counties, distributed among roughly 1,600 small businesses.
Hyundai and its adjacent battery plant will offer an average salary of at least $58,105 per job, plus benefits.
The average salary is structured to qualify for state and local grants, tax breaks, and land incentives valued at approximately $228,000 per job.
Supplier Ecoplastic's plant near Register will provide 456 jobs over eight years upon full functionality, manufacturing plastic car parts.
Ecoplastic workers must be paid at least the county's average wage of $21.65 per hour, per a February 2023 agreement with Bulloch County.
A 40-hour workweek at $21.65 per hour equates to approximately $43,300 annually for Ecoplastic workers.
In exchange for building in Bulloch County, Ecoplastic received a $2.3 million discount on its site purchase price and a 15-year county property tax discount.
The property tax discount for Ecoplastic does not apply to school and fire district taxes.
Ecoplastic may also be eligible for a $1.6 million state grant.
Supplier Joon Georgia has a contract with Bulloch County and the state guaranteeing an hourly wage matching the county average for 630 promised jobs at its new facility.
Joon Georgia received a $3.1 million land discount near I-16, a $2.7 million state grant, and a 10-year county property tax break.
The property tax abatement for Joon Georgia does not cover school and fire district rates.
The Hyundai Metaplant site spans more than a mile along Black Creek, approximately three miles upstream from where it joins the Ogeechee River.
Hyundai and its suppliers are locating facilities near interstates and within the Ogeechee watershed.
A new wastewater treatment plant across the interstate from Hyundai will process the facility's wastewater and north Bryan County's sewage before discharging it into the Ogeechee River.
Environmentalists from Ogeechee Riverkeeper are monitoring water quality in Black Creek and the Ogeechee River due to concerns about state pollution monitoring.
Eight Hyundai suppliers will be distributed across Bryan, Bulloch, Chatham, Effingham, and Liberty counties.
None of the supplier factory locations are currently served by public transportation lines.
Hyundai production at the metaplant is scheduled to begin in the first half of 2025.
The metaplant site is located on the south side of I-16, approximately 22 miles northwest of Savannah.
Satellite imagery shows site clearance is underway and reveals industrial development in the area dating back to 2005.
Daniel Defense and ORAFOL facilities are located directly across the interstate from the Hyundai metaplant site.
The bid team that secured Hyundai included state and county leaders plus the newly formed Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority.
The bid team competed against other regional states, anticipating manufacturers would demand billions in subsidies.

Georgia's Department of Economic Development highlighted its "Best State for Business" ranking in its offer letter to Hyundai, citing outstanding logistics, a well-trained workforce, low business costs, a pro-business climate, and high quality of life.
The "Best State for Business" ranking notably omits worker quality of life metrics.
The state is investing tens of millions of dollars into job training programs tailored to the new Hyundai and supplier workforce.
source_title: 5 things: Hyundai development - The Current

facts: An automotive parts company will create 630 new jobs and invest $317 million in building a new manufacturing plant in Bulloch County.
Plans for the facility mark the first confirmed supplier for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America in Bryan County.
Governor Brian Kemp made the announcement on Monday afternoon about Joon Georgia’s plans.
Less than two weeks after the groundbreaking of Hyundai Motor Group’s innovative facility, the regional impact is already being seen.
Joon Georgia will be the first of many companies drawn to the Peach State to support the Metaplant, creating jobs and opportunity for generations of hardworking Georgians.
The new plant will be located at Bruce Yawn Commerce Park, previously known as the Southern Gateway Commerce Park, at the I-16 interchange on U.S. Highway 301.
Operations are expected to begin in mid-2024.
The company will begin hiring mid-2023.
Billy Allen, chair of the Development Authority of Bulloch County, expressed honor and pride at welcoming Joon Georgia to the Statesboro-Bulloch County community.
He believes the site in Bruce Yawn Commerce Park and proximity to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America will serve the company well.
Education resources including Georgia Southern University and Ogeechee Technical College will provide critical workforce support to help Joon Georgia succeed.
Joon Georgia is part of Ajin USA, a current supplier for multiple Hyundai Motor Group facilities.
The South Korean company has a plant in Cusseta, Ala., and produces a variety of automotive body parts and electronics for original equipment manufacturers (OEMs).

Jung Ho Sea, CEO of Ajin USA, expressed excitement to be one of the first Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America automotive suppliers to build an automotive parts manufacturing facility in Bulloch County.
This marks Joon Georgia's first plant in the State of Georgia.
Joon Georgia, Inc. will create approximately 630 new jobs with competitive benefits in Bulloch County and the State of Georgia.

The company aims to be the best parts supplier for automotive and EV companies like Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, as well as the best employer and corporate citizen giving back to Bulloch County and the City of Statesboro.
Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was executed in partnership with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia EMC.
Workforce recruitment for the new facility will be supported by Quick Start, the top-ranked workforce training program in the nation.

By partnering with the Technical College System of Georgia, the program helps assess workers, train new employees on unique processes, and develop customized job-specific training using the most current techniques and media.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson called Joon Georgia’s announcement a landmark moment for driving Georgia’s automotive industry into the future.
Hyundai Motor Group, its suppliers, and the Savannah area JDA are creating generational opportunities for communities in Georgia.
Supporting the jobs of tomorrow is a team effort, and regional collaboration ensures those benefits are shared across county lines.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry according to a release from the governor.
Since 2020, more than 30 EV-related projects have contributed over $13.3 billion in investment and announced more than 18,100 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Non-affiliated suppliers connected to the project are predicted to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around the state.
source_title: First Hyundai Metaplant supplier will build plant in Bulloch - Statesboro Herald

facts: MetLife purchased an industrial building one mile from Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America near Savannah, Georgia, on behalf of a Samsung affiliate.
The purchase set a new benchmark for sales of its property type.
In September, MetLife Investment Management paid $72 million for the DSP I-16 Logistics Center.
The purchase price equated to $131.19 per square foot.
The property is located adjacent to the Interstate 16-U.S. 280 interchange in Ellabell, Georgia.
Hyundai Metaplant plans to build more than 500,000 electric and hybrid Hyundai, Genesis and Kia vehicles.
Samsung wanted to invest in properties with connections to the Hyundai automaker's facility because both are large South Korean conglomerates.
While the building was under contract, Ecoplastic America struck a deal for the remaining 117,692 square feet at the DSP I-16 Logistics Center.
The building is now 100% leased.
Ecoplastic America is a subsidiary of South Korea-based Seco Ecoplastic.
Ecoplastic America manufactures plastic automobile parts including bumpers, consoles and trims for Hyundai and other automakers.
Ecoplastic America has a 400,000-square-foot plant in Bulloch County, Georgia, near the Metaplant.
The sale reset per-square-foot pricing for prime-location properties of more than 500,000 square feet along the I-16 growth corridor.
The deal won a CoStar Impact Award as judged by a panel of local industry professionals.
MetLife executed the acquisition on behalf of its Samsung separate account.
The price paid serves as the record for 36'-clear speculative product in the market.
The transaction validates underwriting centered on rent growth, renewal and expansion probabilities.
Newmark Southeast Research Manager Neil Matthee stated that setting new price-per-square-foot metrics draws attention and can be a catalyst for more institutional capital to the market.
Colliers analyst Andrew Harry said the acquisition enhances the emerging EV supply chain ecosystem, anchored by Hyundai's 17 MSF Metaplant and supported by suppliers such as Ecoplastic America.
Ecoplastic America expanded to 253,807 SF just prior to closing.
The property was initially marketed at 79% occupancy.
Ecoplastic America leased the remaining 117,692 SF after award but before closing, representing an uncommon mid-transaction stabilization that strengthened pricing.
Georgia Southern University professor Michael Toma said the submarket is newly emerging and the transaction highlights the importance of the Hyundai Metaplant on the region's logistics industry.

The JLL team serving as listing brokers included Britton Burdette (senior managing director), Dennis Mitchell (senior managing director), Jim Freeman (director of industrial capital markets), Bobby Norwood (managing director), and Maggie Holmes Dominguez (managing director).
source_title: News | Logistics building deal near Georgia Hyundai Metaplant sets new standard

facts: Ecoplastic Corporation will create 456 new jobs and invest approximately $205 million in a new manufacturing facility in Bulloch County.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp welcomed Ecoplastic to the state's growing ecosystem of manufacturers, logistics professionals, and suppliers.
The new facility in Statesboro will serve the Hyundai Metaplant and benefit from close proximity to Georgia's ports system.
Established in 1984, Ecoplastic Corporation is a Korea-based manufacturer of plastic automobile parts for original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) and other suppliers.
Ecoplastic is a Tier-1 supplier for Hyundai Motor Group (HMG) and Kia.
The company manufactures automotive interior and exterior plastic parts, including bumpers, consoles, trims, and plastic molds.
Han Shang, President and CEO of Ecoplastic, stated the company is honored to establish a production facility for electric vehicle parts in Bulloch County.

Ecoplastic aims to contribute to the economic development of Georgia and Bulloch County by increasing local jobs and supplying high-quality products to Hyundai Motor Group Manufacturing Alabama (HMGMA).
The company expressed appreciation for the support of state and county governments.
The new facility will be located at 4822 Highway 301 South Register near Statesboro.
The plant will be capable of injection molding and painting to produce exterior parts, bumpers, garnishes, interior parts, consoles, and trims for electric vehicles.
The company expects to begin operations by October 2024.
Hiring will include positions in HR, finance, parts development, quality control, facility maintenance, injection, and paint production.
Development Authority of Bulloch County Chair Billy Allen welcomed Ecoplastic to the county.
Local officials expressed confidence that Bulloch County's location, workforce, and quality of life will support the supplier's success.
GDEcD Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the state's Global Commerce team on the project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia EMC.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson highlighted Ecoplastic's decades of experience supplying Kia and Hyundai.
GDEcD is building a supplier network for the Hyundai metaplant in Bryan County to provide well-paying, high-tech manufacturing jobs across the region.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry.
Since 2020, more than 30 EV-related projects have announced over $21 billion in investment and over 26,300 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America broke ground on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility in neighboring Bryan County.
Offsite suppliers connected to the Hyundai project were predicted to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around the state.
Announced projects to date have already exceeded the predicted $1 billion supplier investment amount.
Ecoplastic Corporation was established in June 1984 as Apollo Ind Co., Ltd.
The company established its first Technical Research Center in 1987.
It was publicly listed on the Korean Stock Exchange in March 2000.
As a Tier-1 supplier to Hyundai and Kia, Ecoplastic manufactures automotive interior and exterior plastic parts, including bumpers, console, trims, and plastic molds.
Ecoplastic currently employs more than 770 people.
The company recorded sales revenue of more than $700 million in 2021.

facts: Battery recycler Ascend Elements has filed for bankruptcy.
The company operates a major plant located 35 miles east of Atlanta.
Ascend Elements is the latest firm in the electric vehicle and clean energy supply chain to show signs of struggle under President Donald Trump’s administration.
The company filed for Chapter 11 protection on Thursday in the Southern District of Texas’ bankruptcy court.
CEO Linh Austin revealed in a declaration that the company has $103.5 million in long-term debt.
Austin cited a lack of early, sustained revenue generation as a primary issue.
Construction delays, cost overruns, and production constraints exacerbated the financial situation.
These factors created a near-term liquidity shortfall that limited available alternatives.
source_title: Battery recycler with major Georgia plant files for bankruptcy

facts: Founded in 2015.
Developing a closed-loop system for lithium-ion battery production.
Sources materials from spent lithium-ion batteries and uses them to produce new batteries.
In 2023, announced working with Honda to explore the potential for supplying battery materials for the automaker's electric vehicles manufactured in North America.
Started operations at its first commercial-scale lithium-ion battery recycling facility in Georgia.
The Georgia facility opened in 2023.
Closed the Georgia facility for several months in 2025 to address fire concerns.
In September 2025, produced for the first time lithium carbonate that is more than 99% pure with material recovered from used lithium-ion batteries at its Georgia facility.
Management targets annual lithium carbonate production of 3,000 metric tons in the Georgia facility.
Targets more than 15,000 metric tons in the U.S. and Europe by 2027.
Completed a Series D funding round in February 2024.
Remains a privately held company with no sign of an IPO planned anytime soon.
Has raised a total of $1.7 billion over 14 funding rounds according to Crunchbase.
Generating revenue from processing end-of-life batteries and manufacturing scrap at its Georgia facility, according to a 2022 press release.
Construction on another facility in Kentucky is currently paused.
Hopes the Kentucky facility will open in 2026.
In May 2025, Poland offered the company up to $320 million to construct a facility in Poland.
Has a $1 billion supply agreement with an unnamed customer for battery materials.
The supply agreement started in the first quarter of 2025 and has the potential to expand to $5 billion over several years.
source_title: Can You Buy Ascend Elements Stock Pre-IPO? What to Know in 2026 | The Motley Fool

facts: Ascend Elements recycles electric vehicle battery components in metro Atlanta.
Ascend Elements will mark a huge milestone next year.
Ascend Elements said it will start to market lithium carbonate, a key component in EV batteries.
The company has been able to recycle and refine lithium carbonate at its Covington facility.
This marks the first time a U.S. company has been able to produce the compound on American soil without mining it from the earth.
Producing the compound domestically is a key part of onshoring the EV battery supply chain.

Eric Gratz, co-founder and chief technology officer of Ascend Elements, said in a news release that this new domestic supply of a critical battery material will help U.S. industries meet growing demand while avoiding the possibility of tariffs on imported materials.
source_title: Georgia's Ascend Elements facility finds another use for recycled EV batteries

facts: Ascend Elements announced plans to relocate its corporate headquarters and research & development center from Westborough, Mass. to a 101,000 square foot facility at 39 Jackson Road in the Pathway Devens campus in Devens, Mass.
The new facility is owned and operated by King Street Properties in Boston.
Ascend Elements has experienced over 500% headcount growth in the last 18 months.
Ascend Elements currently employs approximately 350 team members globally, including 140 people in Massachusetts.
The 31-mile corporate relocation is planned for September 2024.
Mike O’Kronley serves as the CEO of Ascend Elements.
Pathway Devens is a 45-acre, purpose-built development in Devens that includes an 8,300 square foot amenity center and taproom scheduled to open in Q1 2024.
Other cleantech and biotech tenants on the Pathway Devens campus include Electric Hydrogen, Commonwealth Fusion Systems, and Azzur Group.
Tyson Reynoso is the Managing Director & Partner at King Street Properties.
Ascend Elements recently closed a $542 million funding round.
Ascend Elements received $480 million of U.S. Department of Energy grant awards to accelerate construction of a $1 billion engineered battery materials manufacturing campus in Hopkinsville, Kentucky.
Ascend Elements operates one of North America’s largest electric vehicle battery recycling facilities in Covington, Georgia.

CEO Mike O’Kronley stated that while the company is expanding in the southeast United States, its technology was invented at Worcester Polytechnic Institute and its first location was in Worcester, maintaining strong ties to central Massachusetts.
source_title: Ascend Elements to Relocate and Expand Corporate Headquarters and R&D Tech Center to Devens, MA - 2023-09-22 | ScrapMonster

facts: Anovion Technologies announced plans for a 1.5 million-square-foot manufacturing facility in Bainbridge, Ga.
The project involves an $800 million investment.
The facility will generate more than 400 jobs in Decatur County.
The new facility will house synthetic graphite anode material production.
Production is scheduled to start in late 2025.
This project marks Anovion Technologies’ first large-scale manufacturing development.
The facility will be located in Downrange Industrial Park, a more than 750-acre master-planned industrial campus.
The site is situated in Decatur County, Ga.
The project will be one of the largest industrial developments of its kind in North America.

The development team includes the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team, together with the Development Authority of Bainbridge and Decatur County, the City of Bainbridge, Georgia EMC, Georgia Quick Start and the University System of Georgia.
Graphite is by far the largest component by volume and mass in electric vehicle batteries.
Georgia became a hub for the electric mobility industry in the past years, with some 40 EV-related projects announced since 2020.
These projects have generated more than $21.9 billion in investments.
These projects have generated 28,000 new jobs.
The country’s current administration is prioritizing climate-friendly technology and offers government incentives to EV companies.
Competition in the EV industry is growing according to CommercialEdge data.
LG Energy Solution announced a $5.5. billion project in suburban Phoenix.
LG Energy Solution plans to build two manufacturing facilities for cylindrical battery manufacturing.
The LG Energy Solution project is expected to break ground in 2023.
The LG Energy Solution project is one of the largest such investments in North America.
source_title: Anovion to Build $800M EV Battery Components Plant - Commercial Property Executive

facts: $260 million in investments gives Port of Brunswick room to grow.
INEOS announced that the Port of Brunswick, Georgia, would be its gateway for the delivery of its new Grenadier off-road vehicle to markets in the U.S. Southeast and Texas.
Nineteen dealers in the region are slated to handle the high-end SUV in Atlanta, Ft. Lauderdale, St. Petersburg, Houston, Dallas, and High Point, N.C.
The Grenadier vehicles are assembled in France and shipped from Belgium to U.S. markets via Wallenius Wilhelmsen Logistics.
The Grenadier is the first foray into auto manufacturing for INEOS, a France-based global chemical company that operates in 29 countries.
Brunswick aims to become the nation’s number one gateway for roll-on/roll-off cargoes.
INEOS is joining two dozen auto manufacturers at Brunswick’s Colonel’s Island terminal.
The top five auto manufacturers at the terminal are Mercedes, Hyundai, Kia, Nissan, and GM.
Machinery producers at the terminal include John Deere, Caterpillar, Wirtgen, and Hamm.
Gabriel Pernia, planning and logistics director at INEOS Automotive Americas, stated Brunswick is their tactical and geographical preferred location to cover their sales distribution network.
Pernia noted the port provides extensive capacity for vehicle storage, processing, and maritime cargo, which helps minimize total transit time and improve customer order deliveries.

Georgia is attracting companies from the electric vehicle ecosystem, including the Hyundai Metaplant, a $9.8 billion manufacturing site that will open later this year in Bryan County, 85 miles north of Brunswick.
Automotive parts supplier DAS Corp. opened a $35 million facility in Candler County, 100 miles north of Brunswick.
Synthetic graphite manufacturer Anovion Technologies opened for business last year in Decatur County, 200 miles west of Brunswick.
All-electric automaker Rivian is building a manufacturing facility in Stanton Springs, Georgia, around 250 miles northeast of Brunswick.
The Port of Brunswick’s capacity has been enhanced by the Georgia Ports Authority’s investments of $262 million in the Colonel’s Island facility and additional surrounding acreage.
The investments went toward 350,000 square feet of on-dock warehousing.
The investments included 108,000 square feet of warehousing on the south end of the island.
The investments included 80 additional acres for ro/ro storage.
Buildings totaling 182,000 square feet are still under construction.
An additional 42 storage acres are still under construction.
A fourth ro/ro berth is in the engineering phase.
A new rail yard is in the planning stage.
The Port of Brunswick handled a record 775,565 units of autos and machinery in 2023.
This volume represents an increase of 15.6% over 2022.
Cliff Pyron, GPA’s chief commercial officer, cited population growth in the Southeast driving increased demand for autos across the region as a factor for the cargo increase.
Pyron noted Brunswick’s central location to Southeastern markets favors Colonel’s Island.
Importers can get their finished goods to dealers quicker due to Brunswick’s proximity, reducing time and costs associated with overland transportation.
GPA expects organic growth and its unmatched ability to take on new trade to boost Brunswick volumes well into the future.
GPA employs a two-pillar strategy where the Port of Savannah focuses on container trade while Brunswick specializes in ro/ro.
Last month, non-containerized cargo at Savannah’s Ocean Terminal completed a transition to Brunswick.

Griff Lynch, GPA’s CEO, told an audience at the Brunswick Chamber of Commerce last fall that concentrating on containers in Savannah and autos/machinery in Brunswick streamlines operations and provides more efficient service as cargo volumes continue to grow.
Several new short-sea services linking Mexico to the Port of Brunswick have been launched recently to facilitate import of vehicles assembled south of the border.
In November 2023, the first shipment of over 1,000 vehicles was delivered to Brunswick by Zim’s Gold Star service on the maiden vessel Sebring.
In July, shipping line CMA-CGM started a new short-sea service carrying vehicles from Mexico to the Port of Brunswick.
NYK and Hoegh Autoliners have both recently added routes from Brunswick to the Middle East.
GPA’s investments are part of an ambitious plan to establish Brunswick as the nation’s number one ro/ro gateway.
Lynch stated that at its current rate of growth, the Port of Brunswick is poised to become the nation’s busiest gateway for ro/ro cargo.
Lynch said they will be ready to serve this growth with capital improvement projects underway and available land to expand to demand.
Lynch added that organic growth, a steady pipeline of new customers, and the ability to take on new trade will boost Brunswick’s ro/ro volumes, which are already expanding at a strong rate.
GPA initiated an aggressive infrastructure plan to strengthen Colonel’s Island for auto and machinery processing.
The planned new railyard on the southside of Colonel’s Island will enter the construction phase next year.
Kent Fountain, GPA Board Chairman, said this rail capacity improvement will extend Brunswick’s reach deeper into interior markets to capture more business.
The port features a new fumigation facility onsite which is required for cars and machinery being shipped to Australia and New Zealand.
It is the largest facility of its size for autos and machinery.
Federally funded maintenance dredging is ongoing in the Brunswick River shipping channel.
The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers is awaiting funding for a project to widen the channel and expand the turning basin at Colonel’s Island.
Colonel’s Island encompasses 1,700 acres.
Fountain noted Brunswick’s gateway port model features four on-site auto processors.
The model includes room for customers to grow their business with three available parcels of land totaling 264 acres.
The model includes direct access to Interstate 95 for car carrier and machinery trucks.
With on-terminal rail, Brunswick offers the fastest East Coast rail connections to inland markets.
source_title: Georgia’s Port of Brunswick in bid for top auto gateway | AJOT.COM

facts: Solvay and Orbia are forming a joint venture in Georgia.
The joint venture will supply PVDF resin for electric vehicles.
The new PVDF resin plant is located in Augusta, Ga.
Electric vehicle market growth is driving Solvay to build the major new plant.
The joint venture will provide materials for more than 5 million electric vehicle batteries.
The announcement was published on November 02, 2023.
source_title: Solvay, Orbia joint venture in Georgia to supply PVDF for EVs - Plastics News

entity_name: SHMP (Savannah Harbor Modification Project) Working Group
canonical_name: Savannah Harbor Modification Project Working Group
facts: Savannah Riverkeeper formed a Working Group with representatives from local governments, nonprofits, businesses, and legal groups.
The group meets monthly to assess how harbor development projects impact the river, nearby communities, and the environment.
New harbor development plans include deepening and widening the river.
New harbor development plans include building a large shipping terminal.
New harbor development plans include either replacing the Talmadge Bridge or constructing a tunnel.
Unfinished promises from past expansions add to the complexity of ensuring community voices are heard regarding these projects.
Ongoing industrial growth in the area includes the Hyundai Metaplant and supporting infrastructure.
Since 2001, Savannah Riverkeeper has worked to protect the lower Savannah River Basin.
source_title: SHMP - Savannah Riverkeeper

facts: In early November 2025, solar panel giant Qcells announced it was temporarily furloughing 1,000 US workers (25% of its workforce) and reducing pay and shifts at its Georgia factories due to supply chain delays caused by US Customs.
On March 6, 2026, Qcells announced that its solar panel output is back to normal production.

Qcells spokesperson Marta Stoepker stated on March 6, 2026, that the company aims to build a complete American solar supply chain and will soon be back on track delivering American-made energy with its Georgia team.
Qcells expects its Cartersville, Georgia, factory to manufacture 3.3 GW of ingots, wafers, and cells by the end of 2026.
Solar module production at Cartersville’s 3.3 GW facility began in April 2025.
Qcells' Dalton, Georgia, factory came online in 2019 and has an output capacity of 5.1 GW.
By the end of 2026, the combined Cartersville and Dalton factories will produce 8.4 GW of solar panels and key components annually with a workforce of nearly 4,000 people.
On November 7, 2025, Qcells reported that supply chain delays hindered its ability to import components, preventing its two Georgia factories from operating at full capacity for several months.
Furloughed workers will retain full benefits and not be laid off, but Qcells will temporarily stop using staffing agency employees in Georgia.
Qcells scaled back production while shipments into the US were delayed in the customs clearance process as it introduced new supply chains for its Georgia facilities.
As supply chain operations normalized, Qcells informed employees that HR actions are necessary to improve operational efficiency until production capacity returns to normal.
Spokesperson Stoepker expects furloughed workers to return “in the coming weeks and months.”

In January 2023, Seoul-headquartered Qcells announced a $2.5 billion investment to build a solar supply chain in Georgia, marking the largest-ever investment in US clean energy manufacturing at the time.

The January 2023 investment plan included expanding the Dalton solar factory and constructing a fully integrated solar supply chain factory in Cartersville to manufacture ingots, wafers, cells, and finished panels.
The Georgia factories' total output of 8.4 GW annually is equivalent to nearly 46,000 panels per day.
The annual solar production capacity will be enough to power approximately 1.3 million homes each year.
source_title: US Customs delays force solar giant Qcells to furlough 1,000 workers [update] | Electrek

facts: Qcells and its parent company Hanwha have invested a total of $17 million to-date to onshore the assembly of solar-plus-storage products used by Qcells customers, including microinverters.
Qcells announced a collaboration with Jabil to offer its first domestically assembled residential energy storage system (ESS) to the U.S. residential solar and energy storage market.
The new battery storage system is a domestic content eligible version of Qcells’ third-generation residential ESS, known as Q.HOME CORE G3.
The product is commercially available and ready to ship to customers nationwide.
This announcement marks a continuation of Qcells’ existing collaboration with Jabil to assemble Qcells microinverters in Michigan for use in Qcells AC module products.

A latest investment totaling $11.6 million in advanced manufacturing operations for Q.HOME CORE G3 and future ESS products is expected to contribute to job growth at Jabil’s Auburn Hills, Michigan factory.

Q.HOME CORE G3 can contribute to unlocking the domestic content adder bonus for eligible projects while helping to drive down household energy costs and support the creation of American manufacturing jobs.
The collaboration enables a single, complete residential solar-plus-storage solution backed by one brand and one warrantor.
In Georgia, Qcells has invested nearly $3 billion in building a U.S. solar supply chain.
Qcells is closing in on completing construction of a one-of-a-kind factory in Cartersville, GA that will build critical solar components including ingots, wafers, cells and modules under one roof.

Il Hyeong No, head of Qcells North America’s Residential Business, stated that pairing solar panels from Georgia with battery systems from Michigan can help unlock significant value for homeowners, particularly when installed in the “Grid Support” configuration.
Q.HOME CORE G3 pairs seamlessly with Qcells’ AC module products Q.PEAK DUO AC and Q.TRON AC, which are both assembled in Georgia.
The integrated microinverters for these systems are manufactured in Michigan.
Combined, the products make up a cohesive AC-coupled system architecture designed to maximize performance, simplify system installation, and reduce energy bills for homeowners.

Q.HOME CORE G3’s “Grid Support” configuration helps homeowners avoid costly utility rates by charging their battery with excess daytime solar or off-peak utility power and dispatching stored energy during on-peak evening rates.
Homeowners impacted by frequent power outages can add battery backup capabilities to the system for extra grid resilience.

Q.HOME CORE G3 integrates with competitive financing products offered by EnFin by Qcells, allowing homeowners to access flexible third-party ownership (TPO) products including Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) and leasing options.
Combining Q.HOME CORE G3 with EnFin financing enables access to affordable solar and storage through a single ecosystem for both installers and homeowners.
Qcells will formally debut its domestic content Q.HOME CORE G3 product at scheduled roadshow events in May 2026.
Qcells is headquartered in Seoul, South Korea, with diverse international manufacturing facilities in the U.S., Malaysia, and South Korea.
Qcells provides renewable electricity retail services and packages to end customers across the world.
source_title: Qcells Announces Michigan-Based Manufacturing Collaboration with Jabil for Domestic Content Eligible Residential Battery Storage - Qcells North America

facts: Event ended on Thursday March 27 2025.
Savannah Industrial market remains in growth mode despite economic and real estate financing headwinds.
Port of Savannah has seen twenty-foot equivalent unit growth of over 20% year-over-year.
Active tenant demand for industrial space is approximately 30M SF.
Hyundai Metaplant grand opening is set for early 2025.
The Hyundai Metaplant project spans nearly 3,000 acres.
Hyundai Metaplant will have a profound additional impact on the real estate landscape in the Coastal Georgia region.
Vacancy has crept up to nearly 10% across the Savannah industrial market due to a rush of deliveries.
Questions have arisen regarding labor availability to service these properties and how vacancy will continue to be filled in the short term.
Panel discussion covers Hyundai and its suppliers impact on labor, land availability and valuations, and the impact the Metaplant will have on area industrial assets.
Panel discussion covers developing, managing and building to compete for today's tenants, including cost effective upgrades and how owners make decisions on where to invest.
Event location is Hyatt Regency at 2 W Bay Street, Savannah, Georgia 31401 in the Harborside Ballroom.
