facts: An advanced materials manufacturer will spend upwards of $147 million to build a new facility in Cartersville, Georgia.
The new facility will supply Qcells’ solar manufacturing plant located next door.
The investment was announced by the governor’s office last week (relative to April 3, 2023).
Hanwha Advanced Materials Georgia, Inc. will be the only company in the U.S. to make solar encapsulant film.
Solar encapsulant film protects cells against environmental stressors.
CEO Inhwan Kim stated that the products they make are an important piece of the supply chain puzzle.
HAGA secured a Georgia site certified for accelerated development.
The facility is expected to come online in summer of 2024.
The investment by Hanwha Advanced Materials is central to Qcells’ $2.5 billion expansion plans in Georgia.
Qcells North America President HG Park stated that Qcells is doubling down on building a complete, domestic solar supply chain and that this recent investment is critical to making this happen.
HG Park added that working with Hanwha Advanced Materials will allow customers to confidently know that the solar they buy from them was made right here in America.
Many solar companies are announcing plans to set up or expand operations in the U.S., sparked by clean energy production incentives in the Inflation Reduction Act.
Solar investments have largely focused on panel assembly, leaving a need for the parts that go into them.
Hanwha, the parent company of HAGA and Qcells, is upping its investment across the solar supply chain.
Hanwha recently became a leading shareholder in REC Silicon.
REC Silicon produces polysilicon, which is a key feedstock for solar.
source_title: Qcells supplier to build $147M Georgia factory | Manufacturing Dive

facts: Qcells is a trusted provider of complete clean energy solutions and a leader in the residential, commercial and utility solar market segments.
On May 22, 2024, Qcells released data showing its historic investments in solar manufacturing are driving massive economic benefits to Georgia.
A Chmura JobsEQ Q4 2023 report provided by the Cartersville-Bartow County Department of Economic Development estimates 3,800 direct jobs in Bartow and Whitfield Counties from Qcells’ operations.
The same report estimates total employment (direct, indirect and induced) in Bartow and Whitfield Counties at 6,755 jobs.
Total potential sales/output created in Bartow and Whitfield Counties is estimated at $2,091,896,919 (i.e., more than $2 billion).
The Chmura JobsEQ sales/output estimate is based on the annual direct, indirect and induced sales generated when new employees become active in the local economy.
‘Indirect’ refers to the jobs created by regional companies supplying goods and services for Qcells’ expansion and new facility.
‘Induced’ refers to the jobs created, and sales generated when new employees increase business at local establishments.
Qcells announced an ambitious $2.5 billion investment in Georgia manufacturing in January 2023 to expand operations in Dalton and build a fully integrated solar supply chain including Cartersville.
Qcells completed its Dalton factory expansion in October 2023 creating 510 additional clean energy jobs.
In April 2024, Qcells kicked off solar panel manufacturing at its facility under construction in Cartersville.
Once the Cartersville facility is completed at the end of 2024, Qcells will have launched a full silicon-based solar supply chain with ingot, wafer, cell and finished panel production.
In Georgia alone, Qcells’ full annual solar production capacity is anticipated to reach 8.4 GW annually.
This capacity equates to nearly 46,000 panels per day, which is enough solar to power nearly 1.3 million homes a year.
Hanwha Solutions, Qcells’ parent company, invested in REC Silicon to help reopen its Moses Lake, Washington, factory and restart polysilicon production.
Qcells’ Cartersville facility will take 100% of the polysilicon produced in Moses Lake to produce American-made solar panels.
Resuming production at Moses Lake will bring around 270 people back to work in solar manufacturing.
Qcells will receive encapsulant film ethyl vinyl acetate (EVA) from Hanwha Advanced Materials Georgia (HAGA), co-located in Cartersville and the only company manufacturing EVA at scale in the U.S.
The jobs created from this HAGA investment will result in around 160 people working in solar manufacturing.

Danny O’Brien, Qcells’ President of Corporate Affairs, stated that Qcells is on its third Dalton expansion in the last four years and launched panel production in Cartersville as part of its $2.5 billion Georgia investment impacting local communities.
Danny O’Brien noted that the Georgia workforce is helping accelerate the country’s transition to clean energy while benefiting from good paying jobs.
Danny O’Brien welcomed the Biden Administration's recent actions signaling support for domestic solar manufacturers facing challenging market conditions.

Danny O’Brien emphasized that policymakers understand the importance of the industry and that strong trade and industrial policies are critical to the long-term success of vital factories like Qcells'.
Danny O’Brien expressed looking forward to continued work with federal officials and Governor Kemp in advancing the U.S. clean energy economy.

U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff stated he is pleased Qcells reached this milestone following through on its commitment to build a fully American solar manufacturing supply chain by investing billions of dollars right here in Georgia.
U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff noted that American solar manufacturing is at a crucial turning point and scaling rapidly as a result of his Solar Energy Manufacturing for America Act.
U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff stated that China is doing everything it can to undermine energy independence and American manufacturing of advanced energy technology.

U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff mentioned that the Biden Administration has taken positive initial steps to strengthen trade enforcement against China’s attempts to destroy U.S. solar manufacturing, but China remains a serious threat to made-in-America solar energy.
U.S. Senator Rev. Raphael Warnock stated Qcells made history with their landmark multi-billion dollar investment in Georgia’s clean energy economy.

U.S. Senator Rev. Raphael Warnock noted that with solar energy production growing across the state, Georgia is continuing to reap the benefits of the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law and the Inflation Reduction Act.

U.S. Senator Rev. Raphael Warnock stated he is aggressively fighting attempts by foreign competitors to dominate the solar market and supports efforts to ensure Georgia is on the cutting edge of the solar market.
U.S. Senator Rev. Raphael Warnock expressed looking forward to continuing to partner with Qcells as Georgia becomes the premier destination for America’s clean energy manufacturers.
Gov. Brian Kemp congratulated Qcells on this milestone and looked forward to the full completion of the expansion that will employ thousands.

Gov. Brian Kemp stated Georgia is the No. 1 state for business, citing its talented workforce, reliable infrastructure, and award-winning state and local economic development teams create an environment where innovative companies like Qcells can thrive.
Gov. Brian Kemp noted that Qcells chose Georgia in 2019 and has continued to expand afterward.

Rep. Barry Loudermilk stated the start of solar panel production at Qcells’ Cartersville plant is a win for the state and a step toward establishing a U.S. made solar supply chain to reduce reliance on overseas-made products.
Rep. Barry Loudermilk expressed pride that Qcells is continuing to invest and expand in Georgia and looks forward to the Cartersville plant being a great source of jobs in the state.

Qcells is one of the world’s leading clean energy companies, recognized for manufacturing high-performance, high-quality solar cells and modules, portfolio of intelligent storage systems, and growing international pipeline of large-scale renewable energy projects.
Qcells provides renewable electricity retail services and packages to end customers across the world.
The company is headquartered in Seoul, South Korea (Global Executive HQ) with diverse international manufacturing facilities in the U.S., Malaysia, and South Korea.
Qcells offers photovoltaic products, storage solutions, renewable electricity contracting and large-scale solar power plants.

Through its global business network spanning North America, Europe, Asia, South America, Africa and the Middle East, Qcells provides services and long-term partnerships to customers in utility, commercial, governmental and residential markets.
source_title: Qcells Investments Boost Georgia’s Clean Energy Economy - Qcells North America

facts: A clean energy company is abandoning a plan to build a giant electric battery factory in Atlanta’s suburbs after it shifted to buy a solar panel plant in Texas.
Freyr Battery told officials in Newnan on Thursday that it would not build a $2.6 billion plant that was supposed to hire more than 700 people.
The company sent a Jan. 21 letter to the Coweta County Development Authority announcing its plans to end the project.
The factory would have built batteries to store electricity produced by renewable sources and release it later.
When announced in 2023, the plant would have been the second-largest battery factory worldwide.
Freyr is a startup founded in 2018 that never began construction on the 368-acre (149-hectare) site.
Freyr moved its corporate headquarters from Norway to Newnan in part to maximize eligibility for U.S. tax benefits of President Joe Biden’s climate law.
The company shifted its focus to a newly opened solar panel factory that it bought last year for $340 million from top Chinese solar panel maker Trina Solar.
Freyr spokesperson Amy Jaick wrote in a statement expressing gratitude for the support and partnership found in Coweta County and throughout Georgia.
The company noted it is focusing at the moment on the solar module manufacturing facility in Texas.
The Newnan Times-Herald first reported the story that Freyr senior vice president of business development Jason Peace met Thursday with local officials.

Peace told Coweta County Development Authority board members that the decision to abandon the project was driven by rising interest rates, falling battery prices, a change in company leadership, and a shift in its goals.
Authority President Sarah Jacobs wrote an email on Friday confirming these details.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development said the state conveyed a $7 million grant to buy a site for Freyr in Newnan.
Newnan is located about 35 miles (55 kilometers) southwest of Atlanta.
Department spokesperson Jessica Atwell said the state and company are “working together” to ensure the money is “repaid expeditiously.”
Freyr may also owe money to Coweta County for incentives or costs.
Georgia’s incentives process protects the Georgia taxpayer, and when a company’s plans change, that process ensures discretionary incentives are repaid.
Jacobs said planning for the project made Coweta County a stronger candidate for future projects.
The company had previously planned to build battery factories in Norway and Finland.
In November, the company said it will try to sell its European business.
The company is terminating its license for technology to make batteries, paying $3 million to the company it was licensed from.

Tom Einar Jensen, then the company’s CEO, told investors in August that it had grown difficult to raise money to make batteries because of a surplus of Chinese batteries being produced at lower costs.
The company is switching its strategy into businesses that would allow it to raise cash, including solar panel manufacturing.
The company saw its cash on hand fall from $253 million at the end of 2023 to $182 million on Sept. 30.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp has targeted recruitment of the electric vehicle industry.
Korean firm SK Innovation built a $2.6 billion battery plant in Commerce, northeast of Atlanta and hired 3,000 workers, but later laid off or furloughed some workers.
Hyundai Motor Group has started production at a $7.6 billion electric vehicle and battery plant near Savannah, with plans to hire 8,500 workers.
Electric truck maker Rivian revived its plans to build a plant east of Atlanta after a $6.6 billion loan from the Biden administration.
source_title: Battery firm abandons plan for a $2.6 billion plant in Georgia | AP News

facts: Growth in the energy storage market, including the transportation sector, has spurred a ramp-up in global battery manufacturing and investment in new technologies.
Passage of the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) in the U.S. has brought a flurry of announcements from foreign companies eager to build manufacturing plants in America.
FREYR, a Norway-based company, late last year announced it had purchased a site in Coweta County, Georgia, for its Giga America battery plant.
The company projected an initial capital spend of $1.7 billion for the Georgia project.
The Georgia project was projected to create some 720 jobs in the region.
South Korea-based LG Energy Solution on March 24 said it will quadruple its initial budget for a battery manufacturing plant in Queen Creek, Arizona, spending $5.5 billion for the project.

LG’s Arizona site will have two main facilities: one for building cylindrical batteries used in electric vehicles (EVs), and another for lithium-iron-phosphate pouch-type batteries for energy storage systems.
LG recently announced construction is underway for a battery-manufacturing project in Ohio in conjunction with Honda.
The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) expects U.S. battery production will increase 20-fold by 2030 compared to 2021 levels.

The DOE stated a wave of new planned electric vehicle battery plants will increase North America’s battery manufacturing capacity from 55 Gigawatt-hours per year in 2021 to nearly 1,000 GWh/year by 2030.
The DOE noted many battery plants will be co-located with automotive plants to optimize supply chain logistics.
Most planned projects in the United States are concentrated along a north-south band from Michigan to Alabama.
Based on current plans, Kentucky, Tennessee, Georgia, and Michigan will see the highest growth in battery manufacturing capacity.
Korea’s Samsung SDI announced plans for multiple U.S. projects, including a $2.5 billion facility in Indiana in partnership with Stellantis.
FREYR’s Georgia factory will likely be a model for that company as well as for other U.S. battery manufacturing facilities.
Tom Jensen, co-founder and CEO, said the Georgia site “underscores FREYR’s ambition to develop a very strong and near-term operational footprint in the United States.”
Expanding into the U.S. has been a foundational aspect of FREYR’s long-term strategy from its inception.
With the recent passage of the Inflation Reduction Act, FREYR expects U.S. demand for ESS, passenger EV and other electric mobility applications to grow rapidly over the next decade.
FREYR is supporting its expansion plans with the opening of a Customer Qualification Plant (CQP) in Norway.
The CQP facility in Mo i Rana in northern Norway came online March 28.
The CQP is energized by hydropower as part of a long-term agreement with Statkraft from that company’s Rana hydropower facility.
The factory features a demonstration production line built at the actual physical size of a normal production line in commercial operation.
The demonstration line allows creation of sample sales for customers and provides capability to test different materials in the manufacturing process.
The building serves as a training facility for FREYR workers.
The training facility enables workers to know the challenges that could be part of operating the company’s planned commercial gigafactories such as the one in Georgia.
Jensen said the Chapter One opening ceremony at the CQP inaugurates the global launch of one industrial-scale production line of the 24M Technologies SemiSolid production platform at GWh scale.
The company planned an investor-focused event at the CQP this summer to demonstrate automated production of clean, next-generation batteries with this ground-breaking technology.
FREYR announced potential development of industrial-scale battery cell production in Vaasa, Finland, as well as the U.S.
The company intends to install 50 GWh of battery cell capacity by 2025.
The company plans for 100 GWh annual capacity by 2028.
The company plans for 200 GWh of annual capacity by 2030.

The company announced it has begun discussions on a potential strategic coalition with Glencore Plc, Caterpillar, Siemens AG, and Nidec Corp. to pursue the scale up of sustainable battery solutions across Europe, North America, and beyond.
Jensen said the announcement represents an important first step for FREYR and its four potential global partners as they launch this commercial initiative.

FREYR’s vision for the coalition is to accelerate the energy transition across key sectors by scaling up clean battery production from gigafactories under development, and to incubate and develop innovative technology solutions together.
Tom Jensen answered questions about the facility and the company’s plans to expand its battery manufacturing and provide innovative technology for the energy storage and e-mobility sector.
FREYR aims to provide industrial-scale clean battery solutions to reduce global emissions and produce green battery cells to accelerate decarbonization of energy and transportation systems globally.
One reason for choosing Mo i Rana in northern Norway is the availability of abundant and low-cost renewable energy.

In May 2022, FREYR signed an agreement with Statkraft, Europe’s largest producer of renewable energy, to secure a supply of renewable power under globally competitive terms for operations in Mo i Rana.
FREYR decided early to build the CQP as a full industrial-scale production line at GWh scale, marking a world first for the SemiSolid technology platform.
The company’s ambition is to install eight of these lines in Giga Arctic in Mo i Rana and replicate them in Giga America across the Atlantic.
Having an actual industrial-scale production line in the CQP will shorten the time to reach targeted yield and uptime in subsequent gigafactories.
FREYR has chosen a U.S. technology licensing partner in the MIT-spinoff 24M Technologies Inc.

The battery cell design is improved through providing substantially larger and thicker electrodes, which implies fewer repeating layers in the cell and increases the amount of energy-carrying material per battery cell, lowering bill of material costs per KWh.
The larger physical structure provides for significant system-level cost savings as it requires fewer modules, packs and Battery Management System-related costs at the final product/system level.

The production process is dramatically simplified and fundamentally improved, projecting substantially lower capex, lower energy consumption, less space, and up to three times as many batteries produced per employee when produced at GWh scale.
FREYR created real option value with parallel project development in Norway and the U.S. catalyzed by CQP and the IRA.
The company is evaluating multiple options to fast-track the start of production at Giga America, targeting start-up in 2025.
Customers value that FREYR can provide products from multiple geographies.
The U.S. is the fastest-growing ESS market in the world, which is a core focus segment for FREYR.
The SemiSolid platform significantly simplifies the production process, reducing the number of production process steps in half.
The technology produces larger and thicker electrodes, making final battery products much more adapted to end products like vehicles and energy storage containers.

The fundamental change in the technology is that the electrolyte is used as the binder and solvent upfront in the production process, eliminating the need for expensive and space-consuming solvent recovery and drying process steps.

FREYR projects the simplified production process will reduce capex per installed GWh unit significantly, reduce energy consumption, and triple productivity per employee through further automation and digitalization.
At the final product level, physically larger products could reduce total costs significantly by reducing the number of repeating structures at the system level.
FREYR’s general strategy is to develop a network of global suppliers while targeting regional and decarbonized supply chains.
The company wants to minimize logistics chains to make them as cost-efficient and carbon-efficient as possible while maintaining global flexibility to be resilient.
A major share of raw materials needed in Europe will be supplied from Europe, and for the U.S., from North America, with some cross-continental supply including Asia.
FREYR has so far been able to secure all raw materials it needs.
A major part of the volatility in underlying raw material costs is covered through pass-through mechanisms in offtake contracts.
To meet growing demand and stay within reasonable climate boundaries, the world will need to install seven times more battery capacity in the next seven years.

The main challenge facing the battery industry is scaling up fast, which requires huge amounts of capital and competence in record-breaking time to ensure high-quality products with impeccable safety and sustainability standards.
source_title: The POWER Interview: FREYR Leads Wave of New Battery Manufacturing

facts: Freyr has completed the acquisition of Chinese firm Trina Solar’s Texas solar manufacturing assets.
The acquisition completes Freyr's transition from a European battery company to a US solar one.
NYSE-listed battery startup Freyr has pivoted strategy and acquired a 5GW solar module facility in Texas, US, from Chinese firm Trina Solar.
The acquisition occurred on the same day that Donald Trump was declared to have won the presidential election (6 November).
CEO Birger Steen discussed the company's recent decision to minimize European investments in light of the Inflation Reduction Act.
Freyr is discussing a potential policy response and its approach to energy storage systems (ESS) and using conventional technologies.
The US Department of Energy (DOE) has earmarked up to US$3.5 billion of new capital for battery manufacturing.
This DOE funding announcement came a week after Freyr announced it would only be scaling in the US for now.
source_title: freyr Archives - Energy-Storage.News

facts: Freyr Battery has cancelled its plans to build a battery manufacturing facility in Coweta County, Georgia.
Initially announced in 2022 as “Giga America,” the first phase of the project was expected to deliver 34 GWh of battery capacity with an investment of $1.7bn.
The multi-phase project was projected to create more than 720 jobs and attract a total investment of $2.6bn by 2029.
Freyr cited rising interest rates, declining battery prices, and a change in leadership as reasons for abandoning the project.

“We made this decision after thorough consideration of market conditions and internal priorities,” said Jason Peace, Freyr’s senior vice president of business development, in a letter to the Coweta County Development Authority.
The company plans to sell the 368-acre site for $50m, with the transaction expected to close in February 2025.
Net proceeds are estimated at $22.5m after repaying state and local grants.
Despite this shift, Freyr remains focused on expanding in the U.S.
The company announced it is relocating its global headquarters to Austin, Texas.
Freyr recently acquired U.S. solar manufacturing assets from Trina Solar for $340m.
These assets include a 5 GW solar module facility in Wilmer, Texas, which began production in late 2024 and will ramp up this year.
Freyr also plans to build a 5 GW solar cell facility, with construction expected to start in Q2 2025 and production beginning in 2026.
Freyr continues constructing its flagship Giga Arctic factory in Norway, which will have a nameplate capacity of 29 GWh.
The company sees these efforts as key steps toward building a vertically integrated manufacturing footprint in the renewable energy sector.
source_title: Freyr cancels Georgia BESS plant, expands in Texas instead

facts: Archer Aviation provided an update on the construction progress of its high-volume manufacturing facility in Covington, GA, reaffirming an expected 2024 completion date.
The facility's first build phase, inclusive of grading and foundation work, was completed earlier this year.
Phase two, the erection of the exterior walls and roof, is nearing completion.
Phase three (interior fit out) and phase four (equipment load in and power on) are on track to be completed in the coming months.
Once complete, the facility will be nearly 400,000 square feet.
The facility will support Archer’s planned production of up to 650 Midnight aircraft annually.
This production volume would make it one of the largest manufacturing facilities by volume in the aviation industry.
Archer’s buildout is taking place in close partnership with Stellantis, with both companies continuing to progress plans to mass-produce the Midnight aircraft together.
The Midnight is an electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) aircraft built around Archer’s proprietary electric powertrain.
The Midnight is designed for back-to-back flights of 20-50 miles with minimal charge time in between.
The Midnight travels at speeds up to 150 mph, turning hour-long ground commutes into minutes in the air.
The Midnight operates at 100x quieter than a helicopter at cruising altitudes, making it nearly inaudible from the streets below.
The Midnight features redundant systems across the aircraft allowing Archer to target similar levels of safety as commercial airliners.
The Midnight is designed to carry a pilot, up to four passengers, and carry-on luggage.
Last week, Archer showcased its Midnight aircraft at Atlantic Aviation’s new terminal at DeKalb–Peachtree Municipal Airport.

Guests at the showcase included government officials from Georgia’s Office of the Governor, Georgia’s Congressional Offices, the City of Atlanta, Georgia’s State Legislature, the Federal Aviation Administration, and other local governments.
source_title: Archer's Electric Aircraft Factory in Georgia Is Nearly Complete | Design and Development Today

facts: Archer Aviation Inc. (NYSE: ACHR) is nearing completion of its high-volume manufacturing facility in Covington, GA, reaffirming an expected 2024 completion date.
Construction of the nearly 400,000 square foot facility will be completed by the end of the year.
The facility will support Archer’s planned production of up to 650 Midnight aircraft annually in partnership with Stellantis.
This output volume would make the Covington site one of the largest manufacturing facilities by volume in the aviation industry.
Archer completed the first build phase earlier this year, which included grading and foundation work.
The company is nearing completion of phase two, involving the erection of exterior walls and roof.
Phase three (interior fit out) and phase four (equipment load in and power on) are on track to be completed in the coming months.

Archer showcased its Midnight aircraft at Atlantic Aviation’s new terminal at DeKalb–Peachtree Municipal Airport to guests from Georgia’s Office of the Governor, Georgia’s Congressional Offices, the City of Atlanta, Georgia’s State Legislature, the Federal Aviation Administration, and other local governments.
The Midnight is Archer’s electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) aircraft, built around Archer’s proprietary electric powertrain.
The Midnight is designed to travel at speeds up to 150 mph, turning hour-long ground commutes into minutes in the air.
The aircraft is designed for back-to-back flights of 20-50 miles with minimal charge time in between.
At cruising altitudes, the Midnight operates 100x quieter than a helicopter, making it nearly inaudible from the streets below.
The aircraft features redundant systems across its design to target safety levels similar to commercial airliners.
The Midnight is designed to carry a pilot, up to four passengers, and carry-on luggage.
Adam Goldstein, CEO and founder of Archer, stated that the company’s goal is to be the first in the eVTOL industry to achieve scaled manufacturing once production ramps up to 650 aircraft a year.
Archer describes itself as a leader in the electrification of aviation, designing and developing key enabling technologies and aircraft to power the next great transportation revolution.
source_title: Energy, Automobile, EV, Renewable News | Archer Aviation Showcases Midnight Aircraft In Atlanta As It Nears Completion Of Its High-Volume Manufacturing Facility In Covington, Georgia

facts: California-based air taxi tech company Archer Aviation will soon manufacture its electric helicopters, which they call “eVTOL’s” — electric vertical take off and landing — in Covington.
Efforts for air taxis to beat the commute are set to begin in 2025 in cities like Los Angeles, San Francisco, New York and Miami, with eventual expansion into metro areas like Atlanta.
Archer believes it can combat wasted time in traffic congestion and create a safer and more sustainable mobility option in major cities with its zero-emission aircraft.
Several airline companies are backing them, with recent announcements from Southwest and $215 million dollars in investments from Stellantis, Boeing and United Airlines.
Columbus-based Synovus Bank announced in October a credit agreement of $65 million with Archer.

Tosha Perkins, chief people & partnerships officer at Archer Aviation, said the company wants to transform inter-city travel, replace the 60-90 minute commute by car that takes over an hour in traffic with a 10-15 minute electric air taxi flight that are safe, sustainable, low noise and cost competitive with ground transportation.
When completely built, the company’s manufacturing plant in Covington plans to produce up to 650 eVTOLs per year.
There is a planned expansion of 550,000 square feet with production of 2,300 aircraft per year.
The $1.1 billion company is hiring 1,000 employees and plans to invest $118 million at the Covington facility over the next 10 years.
In 2022, traffic congestion cost the city of Atlanta $6 billion dollars in wasted time and wasted fuel.
The price tag across 15 major U.S. cities was $224 billion, according to the 2023 Urban Mobility Report made by the Texas Transportation Institute.
The individual passenger in Atlanta wasted 82 hours, about 10 work days, said David Schrank, the senior research scientist of the mobility division and author of the yearly report out of Texas A&M.
They spent $1,953.00 wasting gas and wasting time, according to David Schrank, adding that they get the value of time from the Bureau of Labor Statistics and average a wage number.

Congestion in places like Atlanta, New York or 15 other large cities that the Transportation Institute studies comes from “a ton of causes,” Schrank said. “But the two largest factors are too much demand hitting the roadways at peak hours, and incident delays like crashes, breakdowns and weather disruptions.”
The 300,000 or so vehicles that travel every day in Atlanta produce 23.3 million tons of carbon dioxide a year, according to the institute.

About 811,000 tons of those emissions, or about 3.5%, come directly from congestion and are wasted, Schrank said. That is almost the entire amount of all Muscogee County’s traffic pollution in the year 2023, which was 900,000 tons.
“You have to remove hundreds of thousands of vehicles during the peak period to significantly change those numbers,” Schrank said.
The specific price for an urban air mobility eVTOL ride has not been released by Archer, but the company said the cost will be similar to an Uber Black upon entering the market.
“The costs to decrease to a ‘Uber X’ range when the service scales,” Perkins added.
Georgia Tech professor Laurie Garrow, who studies advanced models of travel demand and travel behavior, called Archer’s air taxis “a niche solution but not a solution for every city.”
The logistics of getting the air taxis to and from the exit and entrance points are difficult, she said.

Garrow said other challenges with electric aircrafts are sourcing the batteries and charging the aircraft. The grid’s source needs to be renewable and clean otherwise it just emits emissions to charge the craft.
“How you source these rare minerals is a challenge,” Garrow said. “If you charge them during peak period and draw energy off the grid–that is not clean or renewable–then you have a negative impact.”
The current prototype at Archer, named Midnight, can carry four passengers and clocks in at about 6,500 pounds.
The aircraft can travel at up to 150 mph.
It’s one of three company prototypes.
Prototypes were built and tested near the company headquarters in San Jose, California.
Midnight has 12 electric engines and six independent battery packs, each powering a pair of diagonally opposite motors.
This design is intended to allow the aircraft to complete a flight even if an engine or battery pack fails.
The aircrafts are currently being charged using advanced fast-charging systems developed by Beta Technologies.
That company also installed the Southeast’s first eVTOL chargers in Augusta’s Regional Airport in March of 2023.
Archer says helicopters haven’t become a rideshare option because they are too noisy, have a bad safety record, and are expensive to operate.
Archer is powering Midnight with a proprietary battery pack through a Taiwanese company named Molicel, which has “40 years of leadership experience,” Perkins said in an email.
“(We will) utilize a cylindrical type-2170 lithium-ion battery…they are the most reliable factor for aerospace with proven safe architecture.”
Unlike helicopters, “electric motors can be scaled down in size effectively which internal combustion engines simply can’t,” Perkins wrote.
The company received FAA certification in June, and is only one of two companies in the world to do so, despite dozens of other eVTOL companies vying for air taxi approval.
Garrow also cautioned on the promised timeline of rolling these helicopters out.

“One of the things we’re seeing is many of the projects are overly optimistic,” she said. “It is a very challenging problem to get the aircraft certified and ensure manufacturing pipelines to ramp up ops quickly.”
Garrow said to check the AAM Reality Index to keep an eye out for the air taxi companies that are constantly pushing production goal
Earlier this month, Southwest Airlines signed a memorandum of understanding to develop operational plans across 14 airports in California.

“Southwest is eager to explore the convenience Archer’s air taxis could provide Customers flying Southwest at airports in busy urban areas,” Paul Cullen, vice president of real estate at Southwest Airlines, said in a press release.
source_title: Electric ‘air taxis’ built in GA could offer a rideshare option in big cities. Here’s when | Georgia Public Broadcasting

facts: Archer Aviation Inc. is designing and developing a fully electric vertical takeoff and landing (eVTOL) aircraft for use in direct-to-consumer Urban Air Mobility (UAM) networks.
The company plans to begin production in 2025.
The Midnight aircraft is designed to carry one pilot and four passengers.
Midnight uses 12 electric propellers, including six tilt propellers for vertical takeoff and forward flight transitions, and six fixed propellers dedicated to takeoff and landing.
The aircraft is powered by six independent battery packs to provide redundancy and reduce single-point failure risk.
Archer is exploring advanced technologies like solid-state batteries to potentially double energy density and extend the aircraft's range.
Midnight can reach speeds of up to 150 mph (240 km/h) with a range of 100 miles.
Archer must obtain three critical FAA certifications: type, production, and operational.
The company has already secured Part 135 Air Carrier and Operator certificates, as well as the Part 145 Maintenance and Repair certificate.
Archer leverages components from established suppliers like Honeywell, Safran, and Garmin that are already certified for existing aircraft to reduce regulatory risks.
Archer has completed a 400,000-square-foot manufacturing facility in Georgia to support certification and production goals.
The Georgia facility is designed to handle the scale of production needed to meet anticipated demand.
Archer plans to produce two aircraft per month by the end of 2025.
Production will eventually scale to 650 units annually by 2030.
United Airlines placed a $10 million deposit for 100 Midnight aircraft, with options for an additional 200 units.
Archer and United Airlines plan to launch the first commercial air taxi route in Chicago.
In Abu Dhabi, Archer was selected as the primary eVTOL supplier for the Emirate's air taxi plans.
The Abu Dhabi partnership is supported by a multi-hundred-million-dollar investment from the Abu Dhabi Investment Office.
Commercial services in Abu Dhabi could begin as early as Q4 2025.
Archer formed a joint venture with Sumitomo Corporation to expand into Japan.
The Japan agreement could result in up to $500 million in aircraft orders.
These deals bring Archer's indicative order book to over $6 billion.
Archer secured a strategic collaboration with Anduril Industries to develop hybrid-propulsion eVTOLs for military applications, including a potential program with the Department of Defense.
Last year alone, Archer incurred over $450 million in expenses, resulting in a negative free cash flow (FCF) of $320 million.
For the first nine months of 2024, FCF reached a negative $322 million.
As of last quarter, Archer had just over $500 million in cash.
The company recently raised $430 million in equity capital through key partners such as Stellantis, United Airlines, and the Abu Dhabi Investment Office.
source_title: Archer Aviation: Unlocking the Skies of Urban Air Mobility | Investing.com Canada

facts: Archer Aviation (NYSE: ACHR) stock was up 8.2% as of 3:30 p.m. ET on Tuesday.
Stellantis made a new $55 million investment in Archer Aviation.
The $55 million investment follows Stellantis' purchase of 8.3 million Archer shares in March.
The $55 million investment follows a $110 million stock and strategic-funding deal made in 2023.
Stellantis is the auto company responsible for brands including Dodge, Chrysler, Fiat, Jeep, and Maserati.
Archer confirmed it is on track to complete its manufacturing facility in Georgia later this year.
The Georgia manufacturing facility should be able to support the production of up to 650 eVTOL aircraft a year.
Archer's Midnight electric-vertical-take-off-and-landing (eVTOL) aircraft recently completed a successful flight test.
Archer Aviation stock is down roughly 39% year to date.
The company posted a net loss of $116.5 million in the first quarter of this year.
Archer closed out the first quarter with cash and equivalents totaling roughly $406 million.
The business remains in a pre-revenue state.
Archer will likely need to sell more stock or take on debt to continue funding its operations.
source_title: Why Archer Aviation Stock Is Soaring Today - AOL

facts: A Korean auto supplier is making plans to invest $10 million in a plant in Braselton.
The company will make parts to accompany electric vehicle batteries.

Duckyang Industrial Co. has announced it will use a 230,000-square-foot building in Braselton to manufacture battery modules and energy storage systems for SK Innovation’s $2.6 billion, 2,600-worker plant in Commerce.
Duckyang plans to hire 285 workers.
According to the Associated Press, the town of Braselton and Jackson County could give property tax breaks.
Duckyang could claim other tax incentives, including an income tax credit allowing the company to deduct $1,250 per job from state income taxes annually, up to 50 percent of its tax liability.
That could save up to $1.8 million over five years, as long as workers make at least $28,000 per year.
Duckyang has not announced when it plans to start production.
source_title: Korean company to occupy Braselton plant, bringing almost 300 jobs to area - NGV News

facts: Georgia is quickly becoming the hub for a completely new automotive industry.
A remarkable and deliberate shift occurred in Georgia’s automotive industry over the past five years and accelerated in the last 24 months.

Since 2020, the state has announced more than 20 EV-related projects, from original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) to suppliers that include lithium-ion battery (LIB) manufacturers and recyclers, green hydrogen producers, providers of EV charging infrastructure solutions, seat manufacturers and engine parts providers.
All told, these projects will invest more than $13.54 billion in the state and create close to 18,000 jobs.
Pat Wilson is commissioner of Georgia’s Department of Economic Development.

Manufacturers are setting ambitious electrification timelines, including Cadillac targeting 100% electric by 2028, Kia introducing a new EV model every year for the next nine years, and Ford launching the electric F-150 Lightning.
The U.S. supply chain generally did not exist to meet these OEM goals.
More than 75% of the nation’s lithium-ion battery supply comes from China, along with more than 80% of anodes and more than 55% of cathodes.
Georgia continually invested in its logistics network (interstates, rail, ports) to create a first-class business location.
The Port of Savannah is the most efficient port in the U.S., allowing quicker transport of raw materials into the port and products out of plants compared to many other places.
Georgia also invested in workforce development, especially the Quick Start program started in 1968.
Workforce availability and training capability are what put businesses over the top; Georgia’s Quick Start program listens to the business community to train Georgians for current and future jobs.
Hyundai Motor Group announced in May that it will open a $5.54 billion facility dedicated to making EV cars and batteries at the 2,923-acre Bryan County megasite.
Combined with non-affiliated Hyundai suppliers expected to invest another $1 billion, 8,100 jobs will be created in coastal Georgia.

In December 2021, EV maker Rivian Inc. revealed plans to invest $5 billion to build a manufacturing facility at the 2,000-acre East Atlanta megasite (Stanton Springs North) developed by the Joint Development Authority (JDA) of Jasper, Morgan, Newton and Walton counties.
The Rivian project will include technology centers to support company R&D.
For years, the JDA hoped for a high-tech or pharmaceutical firm to land at its site along the I-20 corridor.
Jerry Silvio is chair of the JDA.
The Rivian investment means a lot of jobs, estimated at 7,500 direct and about 8,000 indirect jobs from first- and second-tier suppliers to the campus.
Economic modeling projections by the state indicate those 15,000-plus jobs will generate more than $1 billion in labor income annually.

Rivian received nearly $1.5 billion in tax breaks and other incentives including free land and improvements, substantial highway improvements, a Quick Start training center and customized recruitment and training programs.
Many inducements are contingent on the company creating 80% of promised jobs and investment by the end of 2028 and maintaining them through 2047 or facing claw-back provisions.
The project sparked controversy over loss of quality of life and environmental issues, but the deal moved forward.
Local community benefits will begin as early as 2023 via payment in lieu of taxes (PILOT) fees for 25 years, after which the company pays taxes based on real and personal property value.
Rivian’s first PILOT payment of $1.5 million in 2023 is 18 times the existing taxes on that same 2,000 acres.
An intergovernmental agreement between the four counties and Social Circle divides the money based on original development investment.
About 60% of those funds will be redistributed to local school systems.

Burke County experienced benefits from Plant Vogtle construction despite it being six years behind schedule and costs increasing from $14 billion to $30 billion; Southern Company payments funded a bypass road, new schools, and a fire station.
Similar results are expected in the four Rivian counties.
SK Battery America finalized a $1.67 billion deal to build two EV battery production plants in Jackson County in 2019.
The company invested an additional $940 million in 2020 and began production in January 2022.
Timothy Jeong is CEO of SK Battery America.
SK On (headquarters of SK Battery America) is now one of the top five makers of EV batteries in the world.
Georgia’s location, logistics network, and support from technical colleges/universities made it an appealing location for SK.
SK Battery America is in early stages of a research project with Georgia Tech to advance all-solid-state batteries for longer driving ranges in more compact batteries.
Cal Wray is president of Augusta Economic Development Authority.
German-owned Aurubis is building a $340 million multimetal smelter and recycling plant, drawn by the Augusta region workforce.
Augusta Technical College (ATC) has roughly 4,000 students and a manufacturing mindset, contributing to the community of manufacturers in Augusta.
Many metals reclaimed from circuit boards, primarily copper, are used in EV manufacturing.
The Aurubis project is set to create 125 middle-skill jobs in phase 1, with more expected over time.
The company broke ground in mid-June and should be operational in the first quarter of 2024.
Ascend Elements is investing $43 million to open its first commercial-scale battery-recycling plant in Covington, east of Atlanta.
The project has capacity to process 30,000 metric tons of lithium-ion batteries and scrap annually, equivalent to 70,000 vehicle batteries each year.
Batteries will be recycled into "black mass" containing lithium, cobalt, nickel, manganese and graphite to make new batteries.
Roger Lin is vice president of global marketing and government relations at Ascend Elements.

The active materials are the most valuable in the lithium-ion battery supply chain; Ascend's process turns black mass back into active material more efficiently than any other technology currently in operation.
Materials created by Ascend go directly back into lithium-ion battery gigafactories, closing the loop from grave to cradle.
Ascend will process battery scrap from SK Battery and may soon find itself with other customers.
They are talking to all major auto makers and battery manufacturers in the country, especially those in the Southeast around Covington.
All EV investment requires charging infrastructure.
Limited public and private charging infrastructure is available statewide.
In November 2021, Congress passed the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law including $7.5 billion to fund the National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure (NEVI) Formula Program.
These funds assist states in creating a network of EV charging stations along designated "alternative fuel corridors," particularly along the interstate highway system.
Each state must submit an EV infrastructure deployment plan before accessing its share of NEVI funds, with state plans due by August 1, 2022.
Jannine Miller is director of planning for the Georgia Department of Transportation (GDOT) and leader of Georgia’s EV infrastructure deployment plan effort.
Out of a $5 billion allocation, Georgia was allocated about $135 million.

Federal regulations call for charging infrastructure every 50 miles, including four high-powered (150 kilowatt) charging stations capable of operating simultaneously, with stations no more than one mile off the access point or interstate.
Regulations highlight advisability of public-private partnerships.

Planners are unsure how deployment will work but hypothetically could partner with existing exit infrastructure (e.g., at a restaurant or gas station off I-16 in Dublin) that doesn't meet federal guidelines, bringing in a utility partner to upgrade it using the allocated dollars.
Issues ranging from cybersecurity to maintenance of charging infrastructure must be worked out.
Miller’s stakeholder list is 85-deep and they are being consulted as the plan develops; planners expect to know much more five months from now.
Innovation is critical to the EV ecosystem.
The Ray in Newnan and the Curiosity Lab in Peachtree Corners are hubs for electrification research.

The Ray recently released a data-driven analysis of truck movement patterns to assist state DOTs with EV infrastructure plans, using in-cab data from two routes: I-20 from Dallas, Texas, to Atlanta and from Savannah to the Atlanta airport.
Allie Kelly is executive director of The Ray.

They need to plan infrastructure for medium and heavy-duty trucks that will be either battery electric or hydrogen fuel cell, including high-powered EV charging stations and hydrogen infrastructure for fueling stations.
Hydrogen fuel cell trucks run on electricity created by combining hydrogen and oxygen, emitting only warm air and water vapor as zero-emission vehicles.
With 16 available chargers in its town center, Peachtree Corners has the largest DC fast-charging site for electric vehicles in metro Atlanta.
In March, the Curiosity Lab received a $495,000 federal grant to build a mobility hub as a testing location for EV infrastructure.
Brandon Branham is assistant city manager and chief technology officer of Peachtree Corners and director of the Curiosity Lab.
The lab acts as a facilitator of infrastructure to stay ahead of consumer EV adoption and make Peachtree Corners EV friendly.
James Chen is vice president of public policy for Rivian.

He describes a major, once-a-generation shift in transportation technology from evolutionary internal combustion improvements to a revolutionary complete paradigm shift to a completely new power-train structure.

The real benefit to Georgia is being on the forefront of this shift in terms of jobs, being seen as a technology leader, bringing up the economy of the entire state, and bringing high-tech, cutting-edge jobs for this century and beyond.
EV Projects in Georgia since 2020 account for more than $13.54 billion in investment and nearly 18,000 jobs.

Listed projects include: Hyundai Sungwoo Solite, Kubota Manufacturing of America Corp., EnChem Co Ltd, SK Innovation (Now “SK On”), GEDIA, EcoPro, Teklas, Kirchhoff Automotive, Duckyang, LioChem, Rivian, Wonbang Tech, Hyundai TranSys Seating Systems, PLUG POWER, Aurubis Stolberg GmbH & Co. KG, Ascend Elements, Aspen Aerogels, Inc., Hyundai Motor Group.
source_title: Electric Revolution - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: Korean auto supplier Duckyang is set to invest $10 million to put a facility in Jackson County near the massive SK Battery factory.
Korean President Moon Jae-in visited the SK Battery factory last weekend relative to the article's publication date.
Duckyang plans to hire 285 at the site in Braselton.
The Braselton facility will supply battery modules and storage systems to SK.
State officials say Duckyang's move demonstrates how the $2.6 billion SK investment will compound on itself through a parade of suppliers.
SK Battery's plant already promises 2,600 jobs in its own right.
Duckyang already serves Korean automotive manufacturers like Kia, Hyundai, Renault Samsung and Genesis.
Duckyang supplies cockpit modules composed of 130 subcomponents around the drivers’ seat of the vehicle.
The SK deal is bringing Duckyang physically into North America.
Duckyang will pursue additional opportunities with current partners in North America.

CEO Dong-in Son stated that it is expected they will have business expansion opportunities to supply not only electric vehicle parts but also interior parts to global automakers based in the United States.
The interior parts expected to be supplied include the cockpit module, door trim panel module, and other flagship parts.
The Duckyang announcement came just days before the presidential visit.
Hyundai Motor Group announced a $7.4 billion investment in the U.S. to electrify more of its vehicle fleet and add other alternative-energy offerings.
Hyundai Motor Group's investment could include massive investments in Hyundai and Kia factories in Alabama and Georgia, respectively.
The carefully worded release from Hyundai Motor Group did not specify where exactly the funds would flow.
Duckyang Industrial was established in 1977 in Korea.
Duckyang has grown globally through a partnership with Visteon, an automotive supplier spun off from Ford in 1999.
Duckyang focuses heavily on research and development as it aims to expand market share worldwide.
As of 2017 Duckyang's revenues eclipsed $800 million.
Duckyang had two plants in China along with its head office and factory in Ulsan, Korea.
source_title: Korean Supplier Duckyang to Invest $10M in Jackson County, Hiring 285 - Global Atlanta

facts: On Friday, April 1st, 2022, the Korea Trade-Investment Promotion Agency (KOTRA) opened its first Atlanta office.
Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) Commissioner Pat Wilson joined Mr. Soo Deuk Sohn, Senior Executive Vice President of KOTRA, to announce the opening.

Both parties signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) affirming dedication to support each other’s services and networks and promote cooperation in economic relations, trade, and investment between Georgia and Korea.
Commissioner Wilson stated that Korea is an established strategic market for investment, trade, and tourism for the State of Georgia, and partnerships are the foundations of their success.
The decision by KOTRA to open an overseas office in Atlanta will continue to strengthen relationships between the two regions.
Existing connectivity includes daily direct flights between Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport and Incheon International Airport.
Long-standing agreements exist between the Georgia ports of Savannah and Brunswick and five Korean ports, facilitating joint efforts to create an innovative electric mobility ecosystem in Georgia.
On a mission to Korea in early March 2022, Commissioner Wilson met with KOTRA President and CEO Mr. Jeoung Yeol Yu, as well as key business representatives including those from Kia and SK Group.
The mission re-confirmed the State of Georgia’s commitment to its Korean companies and international partners.

Mr. Soo Deuk Sohn stated that KOTRA Atlanta will serve as an outpost to support Korean business in the Southeastern United States, expand cooperation in many industrial fields between Korea and the United States, and support Korean companies’ local investments.

KOTRA helps Korean companies successfully settle in new markets like Georgia by acting as the first point of contact and providing investment and support resources, including information on programs offered by state or local agencies such as GDEcD.
The new KOTRA office is located at 950 East Paces Ferry Road NE, Suite 999 in Atlanta.
The office will host teams dedicated to facilitating trade and investment opportunities between Georgia and Korea.
Atlanta will host the eighth overseas KOTRA office in the United States and the 10th in North America.
The Republic of Korea has maintained a consular presence in Georgia since 1971, with Mr. Yoonjoo Park serving as Consul General since June 2021.
The State of Georgia has maintained continuous representation in Korea since 1985 with an office in Seoul headed by Managing Director Peter Underwood.
The state also has a dedicated Georgia-based director of Korean Investment, Yoonie Kim.
Several Georgia companies including American Megatrends, First Advantage Corporation, InComm, Novelis, Unicoi Systems, and UPS have operations in Korea.
Georgia is home to at least 100 Korean facilities that currently employ more than 9,400 Georgians.
Korean-affiliated companies in Georgia include Kia, SK Battery America, Sangsin Technology America, and Q CELLS Korea.

Since 2020, numerous Korean companies have announced projects in Georgia, including SK Group subsidiary company SKC, which will manufacture glass-based substrates for semiconductor chips in Covington along with several business partners.
SUNGDO ENG USA announced its corporate U.S. headquarters in the state to provide highly technical construction services to many Georgia-based clients.
Electric mobility parts and storage systems supplier for SK Group, Duckyang, is opening a new facility in Jackson County.
In west Georgia, brake pad supplier KB Autosys will open their first U.S. advanced manufacturing facility in Georgia to better serve customers in the region such as GM, Hyundai, and Kia.
The Georgia Ports Authority (GPA) in Savannah has an established relationship with ports of Korea and offers seven regular shipping services to the Port of Busan.
GPA’s Brunswick Port also offers shipping services to Incheon, Masan, Pyongtaek, and Kunsan.
In 2021, Georgia exports to Korea totaled $860.59 million, making Korea the 10th largest export market for Georgia.
Top exports from Georgia to Korea include gas turbines, aircraft, medical instruments, chemical woodpulp, and chemical analysis instruments.
Georgia imports from Korea totaled $8.83 billion in 2021, with Korea ranking third among Georgia’s importer nations.
Top imports from Korea to Georgia include motor vehicles, tractor parts, bulldozers, engines, and fork-lift trucks.
source_title: Korea Trade-Investment Promotion Agency Opens First Atlanta Office - Savannah CEO

facts: GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson met with Senior Vice Chairman of SK Inc. during a mission to Korea in early March.
The mission schedule included a tour of Kia Vision Square with Kia leadership.
The mission also included a meeting with the President of the Korea Trade-Investment Promotion Agency (KOTRA).
In April, Georgia welcomed a delegation of high-ranking officials from the Korean province of Gyeong sang Buk-Do to discuss future business opportunities between Georgia and Korea.
Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp made Korea his first economic development mission as Governor in 2019.
KOTRA celebrated the opening of its Atlanta office on March 31, which serves as its eighth U.S. office and tenth in North America.
KOTRA's overseas offices host teams dedicated to facilitating trade and investment opportunities between host states like Georgia and Korea.
Georgia and KOTRA signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) affirming support for each other’s missions and promoting cooperation.
Georgia has maintained continuous economic representation in South Korea since 1985, including an office in Seoul.
GDEcD maintains a dedicated Director of Korean Investment based in Georgia.
The Republic of Korea established a consular presence in Georgia in 1971, with Mr. Yoonjoo Park serving as Consul General today.
source_title: Connecting Cultures: The State of Georgia and Korea | Georgia

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the fourth round of grants from the Rural Site Development Initiative (RSDI) within the OneGeorgia Authority.
The fourth round of RSDI grants totals $7.8 million for ten local projects across Georgia.
With this latest round, the State of Georgia has invested $28 million into rural site development projects since the program's launch in FY 2025.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that over 64 percent of new jobs created under his administration have gone to parts of the state outside the metro Atlanta region.

RSDI grants are awarded for either site improvement or to assist with the costs of seeking Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) certification on approved projects critical to economic development.
Communities match state funding at various levels based on the program's criteria.
Lt. Governor Burt Jones stated that RSDI has awarded $28 million for critical projects in rural communities across Georgia.
Speaker Jon Burns highlighted House support for grants bringing good-paying jobs, noting special pride in investments in Southeast Georgia navigating forestry industry changes.
Georgia Department of Community Affairs Commissioner Christopher Nunn stated that GRAD sites are important to local strategies for attracting employers and investment in Georgia’s communities.

Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that over the last several years, GRAD sites have helped attract an estimated 10,000 jobs to communities across the state and shovel-ready sites strengthen state competitiveness.

The Effingham County Development Authority was awarded $1.8 million for site readiness improvements, including a sewer pump station, at the GRAD-certified Savannah Portside International Park, with total project costs of $2.5 million.

The Development Authority of Columbus was awarded $2 million for site improvement work, including clearing and grading, at the GRAD-certified Muscogee Technology Park, with total project costs of $10 million.

The McIntosh County Industrial Development Authority was awarded $1.5 million for road, water, and sewer infrastructure improvements at the GRAD-certified Tidewaters Industrial Park, with total project costs of $2.8 million.
This is the second site development award for McIntosh County, which previously received a GRAD-Certification Scholarship for this grant.
McIntosh County’s progression from a scholarship to a site development award demonstrates the importance of project-ready sites within Georgia’s communities.

The Thomaston-Upson County Industrial Development Authority was awarded $2 million to construct water, sewer, and transportation infrastructure at the GRAD-certified Central Georgia Business and Technology Park, with total project costs of $2.3 million.
The Albany-Dougherty Economic Development Commission was awarded $135,000 to certify the Pecan Grove Industrial Park, with total project costs of $180,000.
The Glynn County Development Authority was awarded $107,500 for surveys and research to prepare the Mallard Industrial Park for GRAD certification, with total project costs of $119,500.
The Development Authority of Harris County was awarded $54,400 to certify the largest remaining tract of the Northwest Harris Business Park, with total project costs of $77,500.
The Development Authority of Jeffersonville & Twiggs County was awarded $22,500 for surveys and studies required to certify the Hillandale Tract, with total project costs of $25,000.
The Screven County Development Authority was awarded $121,500 for studies and surveys necessary for the Screven Industrial Park to receive GRAD certification, with total project costs of $135,000.
The Seminole County Development Authority was awarded $47,250 for assessments and surveys to GRAD certify the East Industrial Park, with total project costs of $52,500.
Eligible activities for grant funds include site studies, land grading, and other essential community infrastructure efforts that support site development.
Grantees demonstrated substantive plans for industrial site development while meeting OneGeorgia Authority eligibility requirements.
Use of funds is subject to all applicable state laws and regulations, as well as the policies and requirements of the OneGeorgia Authority and the Department of Community Affairs.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) serves as the state’s sales and marketing arm and the lead agency for attracting new business investment and encouraging industry expansion.

The Georgia Department of Community Affairs (DCA) partners with communities to build strong commercial and industrial areas through economic development, local government assistance, and housing initiatives.
source_title: Gov. Kemp Announces Fourth Round of Rural Site Development Grants | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Rideout Arsenal is investing $22 million in a new manufacturing facility in Thomasville.
The investment will create 120 new jobs over the next several years.
Rideout Arsenal specializes in high-performance, high-end competition pistols and is based in the United States.
The company utilizes a localized supply chain built around trusted domestic partners, suppliers, and skilled labor.
Recent anti-gun legislation in Virginia created significant uncertainty for the company, prompting its relocation to Georgia.
The new Rideout Arsenal facility will be located within the Plantation Oak Industrial Park.
Additional buildings are planned throughout the next few years at the Thomas County site.
Rideout Arsenal is actively hiring for production, engineering, administrative, and management positions.
Thomasville Mayor Scott Chastain welcomed the company to the community, citing new highly skilled jobs, partnerships, careers, and local momentum.
Shelley Zorn, President and CEO of the Thomasville Payroll Development Authority, noted that the arrival of new industry attracts investment, innovation, and opportunity to Thomasville.
Tina Herring, Assistant Director of Existing Industry and Regional Recruitment, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) on the project.
The project was executed in partnership with the Thomasville-Thomas County Payroll Development Authority (PDA) and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson emphasized that Georgia’s workforce training and development for companies of every size is a key reason businesses choose the state.
The investment strengthens integrated supply chains, creating additional opportunities for local businesses, workers, and communities throughout the region.
source_title: Rideout Arsenal Bringing $22M, 120 New Jobs to Thomas Co. | Georgia

entity_name: Bipartisan Infrastructure Law - Battery Materials Processing and Battery Manufacturing Funding Opportunity Announcement
facts: Reviewer Comments were emailed to the Business Point of Contact and the Technical Point of Contact (who are listed in Exchange) on August 24, 2022.
The Reply to Reviewer Comments is ONLY being accepted in Exchange from August 24, 2022, 5:00 PM ET until September 1, 2022, at 5:00 PM ET.
This deadline will not be extended and Reply to Reviewer comments will not be accepted through any other methods.
Your submission must conform to the content and form requirements, including maximum page lengths, described in Section IV.E of the FOA.
It is not a mandatory requirement to provide a Reply to Reviewer comments.
The amendment changes the Letter of Intent from required to strongly encouraged.
The amendment clarifies the content and process for a Letter of Intent.
The amendment clarifies the requirements for AOIs 3, 4, 5, and 10.
The amendment corrects language in Criterion 2a Section V.A.

The Office of Manufacturing and Energy Supply Chains (MESC) and Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy (EERE) are issuing a Funding Opportunity Announcement (FOA) No. DE-FOA-0002678 entitled Bipartisan Infrastructure Law - Battery Materials Processing and Battery Manufacturing Funding Opportunity Announcement.

The FOA supports the Bipartisan Infrastructure Legislation (BIL) goals of delivering more clean energy, create new, good-paying jobs and lowering costs for American families and workers by guiding the Nation towards a one hundred percent carbon pollution-free electricity sector by 2035 and net-zero economy by 2050.
source_title: DOE: CMEI eXCHANGE: Funding Opportunities

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced on May 15, 2026 that Unified Legacy is expanding its existing footprint in Macon-Bibb County.
The company will invest $125 million in a new manufacturing facility in Macon.
The investment will create 500 new jobs over the next several years in Bibb County.
Unified Legacy manufactures complex products and components for the defense, aerospace, data center, and industrial markets.

Through advanced machining, welding, and fabrication capabilities, the company delivers custom solutions including ground support equipment, welded assemblies, generator enclosures, fuel storage tanks, and precision-machined components.
Unified Defense, their existing manufacturing facility, has been operating in Byron, Georgia since 2022.
Eric Williams serves as CEO of United Legacy.
The new manufacturing facility will be located at Barnes Ferry Road in Macon.
Construction is expected to begin in 2026.
The company plans to hire for manufacturing, skilled trades, engineering, logistics, quality control, and administrative roles.
Unified Legacy is headquartered in Macon, Georgia, and is the parent company of Unified Defense and Prince Service & Manufacturing.
Macon-Bibb County Mayor Lester Miller stated that economic development and workforce development are two of the community’s top priorities moving forward.

Candice Scott, Senior Regional Project Manager, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development on this competitive project in partnership with the Macon-Bibb County Industrial Authority and Georgia Power.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that strengthening domestic manufacturing is critical to national security and highlighted Georgia's competitive edge with a highly skilled workforce, world-class logistics, and strong local and state partnerships.
source_title: Georgia-Based Unified Legacy to Create 500 New Jobs in Macon-Bibb Coun | Georgia

facts: The State of Georgia surpassed $53.1 billion in exports in 2024.
Georgia's export growth represented a year-over-year increase of 6.4%, outpacing the national average of 2.3%.
Georgia moved up to rank sixth in the United States for the dollar value of total trade.
The state facilitated more than $198.7 billion in trade with 222 unique countries and territories.
More than 87% of Georgia exporters are small businesses.
In 2024, Georgia exported to 219 unique destinations.
Georgia retained its No. 12 ranking in the United States for the dollar value of exports.
Civilian aircraft and ancillary parts remained Georgia's No. 1 export category.
Other top export categories included motor vehicles, data processing machines (computers), electrical apparatus for line telephony, and medical devices.
Markets with full-time Georgia international trade representatives accounted for 66% of exports and 83% of bilateral trade in 2024.
Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport is the busiest and most efficient airport in the world.
The Port of Savannah is the fastest growing and third-busiest container gateway in the United States.
The Port of Brunswick serves as one of the nation's busiest gateways for Roll-on/Roll-off cargo.
The Georgia Ports Authority reported twelve consecutive months of year-over-year container volume growth.
Georgia has more rail miles than any other state in the Southeast.
Governor Brian P. Kemp stated that strategic investments and commitments to job creators are delivering results for Georgians.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that Georgia offers extensive partnerships, expert guidance on export strategies, and top-tier infrastructure including deepwater ports, railways, highways, and airports.

Deputy Commissioner of Trade Lizann Grupalo highlighted that international representatives provide on-the-ground insights to navigate the global environment for Georgia-based small business exporters.
GDEcD's annual International Trade Report tracks merchandise trade using four-digit Harmonized System (HS) codes and origin of movement via data from Trade Data Monitor.
GDEcD's Trade team includes international representatives located in more than a dozen strategic global markets to assist Georgia companies with expanding sales worldwide.
source_title: Georgia Breaks Export Records, Rises in Total Trade | Georgia

facts: Distributors who sell or use motor fuel are subject to an excise tax of $0.26 per gallon, which may be adjusted based on vehicle efficiencies and the consumer price index.
Motor fuels not commonly sold or measured by the gallon used in public highway vehicles are taxed according to their gasoline gallon equivalent (GGE).
Effective January 1, 2027, a GGE of electricity may not exceed 11 kilowatt-hours.
Effective January 1, 2027, a GGE of hydrogen must be at least 2.2 pounds.
Effective January 1, 2027, a GGE of compressed natural gas (CNG) must be at least 110,000 British thermal units.
Effective January 1, 2027, a GGE of liquefied natural gas (LNG) must be at least 6.06 pounds.
CNG is defined as a mixture of hydrocarbon gases and vapors, consisting principally of methane in gaseous form compressed for use as motor fuel.
LNG is defined as methane or natural gas in cryogenic or refrigerated liquid form for use as motor fuel.
Propane and special fuels sold in bulk to a licensed consumer distributor are exempt from the excise tax.
The excise tax for electricity begins January 1, 2027, and only applies to electricity sold at public electric vehicle (EV) charging stations.
The Georgia Department of Revenue may assess, levy, and collect tax for other motor fuels used on public highways using a GGE rate.
All-electric vehicles are subject to an annual licensing fee in 2025 of $352.56 for commercial vehicles and $234.97 for non-commercial vehicles.
AFV annual fees also apply to plug-in hybrid electric vehicles and flexible fuel vehicles, but only if they have an AFV license plate.
AFV license plates are subject to a one-time manufacturing fee of $25, an annual $20 registration fee, and a $35 special tag fee.
Electric, natural gas, propane, bi-fuel, and dual-fuel vehicles are eligible for an AFV license plate.
Effective January 1, 2027, all public EV charging stations must be capable of accurately measuring and displaying electricity dispensed to actively charging EVs on a per kilowatt-hour (kWh) basis.
The Georgia Department of Revenue has the authority to conduct EV charging station inspections to ensure accurate delivery and display of electricity per kWh.
Violators of EV charging station inspection requirements may be subject to a fine of up to $1,000.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development is authorized to establish the Georgia EV Manufacturing Commission (Commission).
The Commission will seek to advance the state’s interest in developing, marketing, promoting, and recruiting the growth of the EV industry through continued investment and job creation in Georgia.
The Commission will provide recommendations to other State agencies via consulting Georgia’s public and private sectors.
A person or entity that provides EV charging services and is not otherwise subject to the jurisdiction of the Georgia Public Service Commission is not defined as a public utility.

The Joint Study Committee on the Electrification of Transportation (Committee) must study the growth of the EV market in Georgia and address concerns regarding public and business needs for public EV charging infrastructure and economic preparedness.
The Committee submitted a report of their findings to the Georgia Legislature in November 2022.
Bulk sales of propane by licensed distributors are exempt from the state motor fuel excise tax.

Beginning Jan. 1, 2027, the sale of electricity dispensed from EV chargers owned by nonprofit organizations, located on federal interstates, and providing electricity without payment is exempt from the state motor fuel excise tax.
The Georgia Department of Education provides grants to local educational agencies for the purchase of new electric, compressed natural gas, or propane school buses.
School districts must coordinate with a transportation field consultant from the Georgia Department of Education to apply for alternative fuel school bus grants.
Alternative fuel school bus grant amounts vary and are available on a first-come, first-served basis.
An income tax credit is available for businesses for 10% of the cost to convert a vehicle to natural gas, electricity, propane, and hydrogen, up to $2,500 per vehicle.
Converted vehicles must be fueled solely by an alternative fuel and must meet emissions standards for low-emission or zero emission vehicles defined by the Board of Natural Resources.
Unused alternative fuel vehicle conversion tax credits may be carried forward for up to three years.
A business that manufactures alternative energy products for use in battery, biofuel, and electric vehicle enterprises may claim an annual tax credit for five years.
The alternative fuel and advanced vehicle job creation tax credit amount is based on the number of eligible new full-time employee jobs and the county.
Qualified entities for the job creation tax credit must be defined as business enterprises, excluding retail businesses.
Job creation tax credit amounts differ depending on how the county in which the business is located ranks based on unemployment rates and income levels.

The Georgia Environmental Protection Division (EPD) provides U.S. Environmental Protection Agency Diesel Emission Reduction Act funding for eligible projects that replace existing school buses with alternative fuel or zero-emission school buses.
Eligible alternative fuels for diesel emission reduction grants include electricity, natural gas, or propane.
An eligible business enterprise may claim an income tax credit for the purchase or lease and installation of qualified EV charging stations located in Georgia.
The EV charging station tax credit is for 10% of the cost of the station, up to $2,500.
Unused EV charging station tax credits may be carried forward for up to three years.

The U.S. Department of Transportation’s NEVI Formula Program required the Georgia Department of Transportation (GDOT) to submit an annually updated EV Infrastructure Deployment Plan for fiscal years 2022 through 2026.
Georgia's submitted NEVI plans must describe how the state intends to distribute NEVI funds and their physical and cybersecurity strategies, including a Community Engagement Outcomes Report.
Submitted NEVI plans must address updated NEVI Guidance.
source_title: Alternative Fuels Data Center: Electricity Laws and Incentives in Georgia

facts: Evans Tool & Die has been a trusted provider of custom tooling and precision manufacturing services for more than 75 years.
The company specializes in the design, development, and production of high-quality metal stampings.
Evans Tool & Die is located in Conyers, near Atlanta.
The company serves a variety of industries, offering expert solutions for complex tooling and part-manufacturing needs.
CEO and President Dee Barnes serves as a board member of the Georgia Association of Manufacturers (GAM).
Through GAM, the company helps foster innovation and collaboration to support more than 150 companies, suppliers, and professional services firms.

Evans Tool & Die joins other Georgia manufacturers including GE Appliances, Gulfstream Aerospace Corporation, Irving Consumer Products, and Kia Georgia in shaping the future of manufacturing in Georgia.
The company is a multi-generational family business that has grown from its beginnings over 75 years ago.
Evans Tool & Die has expanded its capabilities to include stamping, laser cutting, and metal fabrication alongside its original tool and die business.
Dee Barnes leads the company started by her grandfather, supported by her father and uncles, after returning from time at Georgia College and State University.
The company was recognized as a 2025 Small Business ROCK STAR award recipient.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD), in partnership with the Georgia Economic Developers Association, established the Small Business ROCK STAR awards to recognize innovations, community engagement, and positive impact of state small businesses.
