Another Czech company, SILENT, which produces insulated parts and components for the electrical industry, has plans to invest $1 million in Hall County to create 10 new jobs.
source_title: Economic Development Around the State: Hyundai Motor Group Grand Opening & CNN Center Downtown News - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: Hyundai Motor Group and Georgia Institute of Technology signed a memorandum of understanding creating a transformative partnership focused on sustainable mobility, the hydrogen economy, and workforce development.
Hyundai Motor Group is investing $12.6 billion in new electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facilities in Georgia.
The investment includes a $7.59 billion HMGMA site in Bryan County, creating over 8,500 jobs.
The investment includes a $5 billion battery manufacturing joint venture with SK On in Bartow County, providing employment for around 3,500 people.
The proximity to Georgia Tech was a key factor in choosing the locations for Hyundai's new facilities.
Kia's West Point assembly plant under the leadership of Honorary Chairman Mong-Koo Chung and Executive Chair Euisun Chung started vehicle production in 2009.
Sonny Perdue, now University System of Georgia Chancellor, was Georgia's Governor when the Kia plant opened.
A recent study by the Center for Automotive Research reveals that Hyundai Motor’s automotive operations contributed $20.1 billion and 190,000 jobs to the U.S. economy in 2021 alone.
According to the study, Hyundai’s investments in Georgia are expected to create nearly 40,000 jobs and $4.6 billion in individual earnings every year.
Mr. Ángel Cabrera, Georgia Tech President stated that Hyundai aligns well with their values and they appreciate Hyundai's transformative investments in the state.

Mr. Jay Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company stated that Hyundai Motor Group is committed to a sustainable future and is excited about its long-lasting partnership with Georgia Tech focusing on research in sustainable mobility, hydrogen economy, workforce development, and smart cities.
Mr. Jay Chang stated that this partnership influenced their choice to invest in Georgia for EV projects.

Mr. José Muñoz, President and Global COO of Hyundai Motor Company, and President and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America stated that Hyundai's investments in Georgia are expected to generate tens of thousands of employment and tens of billions in economic effect over the ensuing decades.
Mr. José Muñoz stated that one of the primary reasons Hyundai chose Georgia for its new EV production facility was the state's close proximity to universities like Georgia Tech.
The partnership envisions zero-emission hydrogen vehicles on campus, air mobility to Hartsfield International Airport, and stress-free autonomous commuting on I-75 and I-85.
The collaboration with Georgia Tech will offer student development and cooperative work programs.

Mr. Pat Wilson, GDEcD Commissioner stated that globally there is a historic shift towards sustainable transportation, Georgia is leading this change fostering an ecosystem for sustainable mobility and creating future jobs, and congratulated Georgia Tech, Hyundai Motor Group, and partners in the endeavor.
source_title: Hyundai signs MOU with Georgia Tech | Race Auto India

entity_name: Hyundai Mobility Training Center of Georgia
canonical_name: Hyundai Mobility Training Center of Georgia
facts: Governor Brian Kemp and Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America President and CEO Tony Heo joined state and local leaders to celebrate the grand opening of the Hyundai Mobility Training Center of Georgia on November 6, 2025.
The training center is an 89,000-square-foot state-of-the-art facility designed to train the next generation of electric vehicle (EV) manufacturing professionals.
It is strategically located adjacent to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) in Bryan County, Ellabell, GA.
This marks the first training center in Georgia geared toward EVs.
The flexible facility is designed to accommodate training for hybrid vehicles in the future following HMGMA's announced expansion that will include hybrid vehicles.
Governor Brian Kemp stated the center will give thousands of people over the years the knowledge they need to benefit from this generational project.
José Muñoz, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, said the center represents a commitment to investing in people as they continue to create high-paying American jobs.

As HMGMA expands production at one of the most advanced facilities in the world, a workforce equipped with skills to build next-generation vehicles with next-generation production processes is needed.
Hyundai's partnership with Georgia Quick Start ensures Meta Pros have access to world-class training that will help them succeed and help Georgia lead in advanced manufacturing.
A special thank you was given to Governor Kemp, Commissioner Pat Wilson, and the economic development team in Georgia for their ongoing support.
Hyundai’s project in Georgia will create approximately 8,500 jobs by 2031.
At full capacity, the new facility can train up to 824 individuals at a time.
The center provides customized, immersive education tailored to Hyundai’s advanced EV manufacturing processes.
It is operated by Georgia Quick Start, the state’s renowned workforce development program and part of the Technical College System of Georgia (TCSG).
The center will prepare employees for roles at HMGMA and affiliate companies.

Greg Dozier, Commissioner of TCSG, said the center is about giving Georgians the tools to build their own futures right here at home and reflects Georgia’s commitment to innovation, accessibility, and long-term economic opportunity.
Quick Start’s training professionals collaborated with HMGMA to design and build world-class infrastructure that closely mirrors processes trainees will encounter at the Metaplant.
The facility includes a STEP Line (Safe Transition for Efficient Production) where trainees build timing, awareness, and coordination skills in a controlled, digital environment.

It includes an IMPACT Line (Immersive Manufacturing Process and Conveyor Training), a full-length, moving production line with 53 integrated assembly activities and a built-in quality check system replicating real-world EV production.
It features a High Voltage Battery and Safety Lab for hands-on learning with high-voltage systems critical to EV and hybrid vehicle performance.
It includes a VR Experience Lab, a simulated environment for safety and hazard identification.
An Automation & Mechatronics Lab is focused on programmable logic controllers (PLCs), sensors, motion systems, and industrial automation.
Quick Start Deputy Commissioner Scott McMurray said subject matter experts partnered directly with HMGMA to learn the skills its Meta Pros will need when they hit the production floor.

Additional facilities include a 5,764-square-foot multipurpose “Momentum Room,” six flexible classrooms, a robotics lab, welding lab, paint lab, and a robotic dog that supports advanced automation training.
The training center stands as a core pillar of Georgia’s broader strategy to prepare and train the state’s workforce for success in advanced manufacturing.
Tony Heo stated the grand opening marks a proud milestone for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, the State of Georgia, the Technical College System, and Georgia Quick Start.
Heo added that the training center gives confidence that trained talent will be available to shape the future of mobility and American manufacturing right here in Southeast Georgia.
Georgia Quick Start is a program within TCSG that offers customized workforce training to qualified companies as a discretionary incentive to support investment and job creation in the state.
Georgia Quick Start is the oldest workforce training program in the United States, as well as the most awarded by site selection consultants.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) is Hyundai Motor Group’s first dedicated Electrified Vehicle mass-production plant located in Bryan County, GA.
The HMGMA plant boasts a highly connected, automated, and flexible manufacturing system that organically connects all elements of the EV ecosystem to realize customer value.
The Georgia facility will become an intelligent manufacturing plant where all processes of production — order collection, procurement, logistics and production — are optimized utilizing AI and data.
The innovative manufacturing system helps create a human-centered work environment with robots assisting human workers.
source_title: Georgia Unveils Hyundai Mobility Training Center – TCSG | Technical College System of Georgia

facts: The state of Georgia and local governments are giving $1.8 billion in tax breaks and other incentives to Hyundai Motor Group in exchange for the automaker building its first U.S. plant dedicated to electric vehicles near Savannah.
The deal calls for Hyundai to invest $5.5 billion in its Georgia plant and hire 8,100 workers.
The deal is the largest economic development deal in Georgia's history.
The deal comes just months after Georgia closed another major deal with electric vehicle maker Rivian to build a factory in the state.

State economic development commissioner Pat Wilson stated that the generational projects solidify Georgia's spot at the vanguard of the EV transition and ensure thousands of Georgians will benefit from future jobs.
Hyundai executives and Gov. Brian Kemp announced the deal in May with a champagne toast at the project's 2,900-acre (1,170 hectare) site in Bryan County, west of Savannah.
Hyundai plans to start construction on the plant next year and begin producing up to 300,000 vehicles per year in 2025.
The new factory will also produce vehicle batteries.
Officials declined to reveal the specific incentives promised to Hyundai until after the agreement was signed.
The incentive package is worth roughly $300 million more than incentives previously promised to Rivian.
The package amounts to Georgia and four counties in the Savannah area giving Hyundai about $228,000 per job created.
Georgia officials insist the incentive package is a worthwhile investment.
Pat Wilson stated that Hyundai's payroll at the new plant is expected to reach $4.7 billion over 10 years.
Parts suppliers are expected to create thousands of additional jobs in Georgia.
Greg LeRoy, executive director of Good Jobs First, stated that the $1.8 billion in incentives is easily the largest subsidy package a U.S. state has ever promised for an automotive plant.
Greg LeRoy characterized the deal as inherently super-risky because it bets a huge amount on one company and one facility.
Local governments are giving Hyundai more than $472 million in property tax breaks.
Hyundai will pay more than $357 million in lieu of taxes over a 26-year period starting in 2023.
Hyundai will receive more than $212 million in state income tax credits, calculated at $5,250 per job over five years.
If Hyundai did not owe enough state corporate income tax to cover the credits, Georgia would instead give the company personal income taxes collected from Hyundai workers.
The state and local governments spent $86 million to purchase the plant site.
The state will spend $200 million on road construction and improvements.
The state will spend an additional $50 million to help fund construction, machinery, and equipment.
Sales tax exemptions on construction materials and machinery expenses are estimated to cost $396 million.
The deal requires Hyundai to pay back a portion of the incentives if the company falls below 80% of its promised investment or employment.
Kia, a subsidiary of Hyundai Motor Group, received more than $450 million in incentives for its plant in West Point, southwest of Atlanta.
Georgia has promised SK Innovation $300 million in incentives for a $2.6 billion, 2,600-worker battery plant being built northeast of Atlanta.
source_title: Hyundai gets $1.8B in aid to build electric cars in Georgia | Georgia Public Broadcasting

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) employees, known as Meta Pros, receive specialized training through Georgia's Quick Start program.
The state of Georgia invested heavily in HMGMA, including a dedicated training center and tax incentives, to secure the project and its economic benefits.
Quick Start's training program for HMGMA employees includes corporate culture, safety instruction, and specialized skills training based on job roles.
Georgia's investment in HMGMA is expected to have long-term financial benefits for the region, including a significant increase in Bryan County's tax base.
Bonnie Williams works as a Quality Control Meta Pro within the Body Division at HMGMA, spot-checking machine welding for questionable welds that might need human assistance.

Hyundai held an Ioniq 9 reveal in November at AutoNation Hyundai Savannah, located at 7011 Abercorn St., where Bonnie Williams observed finished vehicles after typically only seeing the "bones of the cars."
The music playing while machines operate was a unique part of Bonnie Williams' experience as a Quality Control Meta Pro.
Antoine Kelly works as a Patrol Team member conducting quality control throughout the entire assembly process at HMGMA.
"Meta Pro" is a term HMGMA uses for its employees to indicate trust in colleagues as an integral part of the transformation of mobility and manufacturing, according to its website.
HMGMA features testimonials from Meta Pros on its YouTube Channel.
Courtney McSwain, a Savannah native and long-time industrial maintenance professional at HMGMA, stated she loves her job because "they actually train you."
Georgia's Quick Start program, a division of the Technical College System of Georgia, partners with HMGMA to provide training paid for by Georgia taxpayers.
Training happens at two separate Quick Start facilities: Port City Logistics at 100 Myrtle Drive, Port Wentworth, and Quick Start Advanced Manufacturing at 1500 Pine Meadow Drive, Pooler.
The Quick Start Advanced Manufacturing facility in Pooler is slated for a $10.6 million state-funded 30,000-square-foot expansion.
Construction is well underway on a HMGMA-dedicated Quick Start Hyundai Mobility Training Center, which is a $62.5 million state-funded facility.
The state of Georgia agreed to cover the new training center's operating costs, with an estimated value of training services at nearly $5 million per year for five years.

The state's agreement with Hyundai notes an "in-kind estimated cost" of $54,818,400 for customized Quick Start workforce recruitment program services for HMGMA affiliate suppliers and the battery joint venture.
Hyundai will receive a 27-year property tax abatement as part of Georgia state and local tax incentives and abatements.
Hyundai is expected to pay leasehold and ad valorem property tax payments starting in 2026 after initial construction is complete.
Those estimated annual tax payments are more than $7 million, which is more than 180 times the property's previous estimated taxes of roughly $39,000 based on 2021 undeveloped land values.
The Ellabell, Georgia mega site's physical configuration and visibility from Interstate-16 were key enticement factors for Hyundai, alongside economic incentives.
An Aug. 23, 2023 Georgia Joint Tax credit subcommittee meeting at the Georgia Southern University Armstrong Campus addressed Hyundai's decision to choose Bryan County.
Ulrich Schmidt, a Hyundai site selection consultant and managing director of KPMG LLP’s Global Location and Expansion Services, spoke at the meeting about the site selection process.
Factors in Hyundai's analysis included labor and utility costs as well as recurring taxes.
Ulrich Schmidt stated that "Incentives are essentially the offset to what that bottom line might look like."
Christopher Smith, HMGMA chief project implementation officer and general counsel, called incentives "absolutely critical" to Hyundai's choice.

Christopher Smith noted that Georgia had not offered the most in tax incentives among competing states, but elements like the Quick Start partnership and the Savannah area's promise of workforce availability helped seal the deal.

Quick Start's Deputy Commissioner Scott McMurray said the institution works with employer-partners across the state to establish training programs that fit employees' needs and align with company missions.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development decides which companies are eligible for the Quick Start incentive, looking at manufacturing job types, number of jobs, and hiring schedules.
The threshold for eligibility is around 90-100 trainees in a short time period, though the department typically assists employers with large hiring needs over longer timeframes.
HMGMA and its onsite affiliate suppliers need to hire and train between 6,800 and 8,500 employees, which certainly met the requirements.
All Meta Pros go through a week-long training as part of the onboarding process.
Depending on an employee's position, they may require additional weeks of training in their skill areas.
HMGMA has denied requests for its quarterly and yearly hiring goals.

Chief Administrative Officer Brent Stubbs stated the hesitancy to share hiring glide paths is because "The auto industry is incredibly competitive and hiring glide paths are signals to production goals."
Scott McMurray said roughly 20-30 Meta Pros a month go through initial hire training, which typically entails corporate culture and safety instruction.
Quick Start adjusts resources like instructors or space if there is a surge of around 100 employees.
The new training center features adaptability with dividing walls that can up- or down-size classrooms and an auditorium that can hold roughly 600 people.
Quick Start has provided similar state-funded training since 2009 at the Kia Georgia Training Center near its Westpoint plant.

Both the Kia and HMGMA training centers are purpose-built facilities with agreement options for potential future purchase, differing from typical Quick Start leases that renovate spaces for a few years.
Kia has opted out of purchasing its training facility when the option was presented.
HMGMA could opt to buy the facility after the initial five years or continue the agreement with Quick Start.

At the August 2023 subcommittee meeting, Savannah Chamber President Bert Brantley stressed that the state conducted in-depth analysis ahead of the HMGMA deal and noted recognition of the cost to the state and local government.
Savannah Economic Development Authority President Trip Tollison said Georgia's investment will have long-lasting financial benefits for the region.
Trip Tollison noted that when the property re-enters the tax roll, it will increase Bryan County's tax digest by 800% in a single day, calling it "absolutely unheard of."
source_title: GA Quick Start provides HMGMA employees state-funded training

facts: Hyundai Motor Group announced a multi-year partnership on hydrogen mobility solutions with the Georgia Institute of Technology.
The partnership includes Hyundai Motor North America donating four NEXO fuel cell electric SUVs exclusively for campus use.
Hyundai Motor Group is donating a hydrogen electrolyzer project exclusively for campus use.
The initiative brings NEXO hydrogen fuel cell SUVs and fueling infrastructure to the Georgia Tech campus.
The initiative advances research, workforce development, and zero-emissions logistics.
The donated NEXO SUVs will support campus operations and interdisciplinary research across engineering, sustainability, energy systems, and public policy.
The partnership expansion was announced on April 29, 2026, in Seoul and Atlanta.

Ken Ramírez, Executive Vice President and Head of Global Energy and Hydrogen Business at Hyundai Motor Group, commented on the collaboration; he is a Georgia Tech alumnus (class of 1991) and currently serves on the Georgia Tech Advisory Board.

Randy Parker, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor North America, stated that donating NEXO fuel cell SUVs will expand the clean mobility footprint on campus and provide real-world experiences with zero-emissions transportation technology.

Ángel Cabrera, President of the Georgia Institute of Technology, stated the expansion advances hydrogen research, reduces campus emissions, and strengthens Georgia's role in the future of clean mobility.
The donated hydrogen electrolyzer project will be installed at Georgia Tech's North Avenue Research Area (NARA).

Key research focus areas include engineering (exploring hydrogen-based systems and mobility solutions), sustainability (assessing environmental benefits of hydrogen technologies), energy systems (understanding integration of hydrogen fuel cells into current infrastructure), and public policy (evaluating regulatory and social implications of hydrogen adoption).
The initiative uses the university as a living laboratory for clean transportation technologies, connecting Georgia Tech's research enterprise with campus operations.
Faculty and students will study the real-world performance of hydrogen technology, infrastructure requirements for large-scale deployment, and environmental impacts of hydrogen energy systems.
Insights gathered from the initiative aim to inform and accelerate the widespread use of hydrogen technology in campuses, fleets, cities, and freight corridors.
The initiative supports Georgia Tech's strategic plan goal of expanding the use of zero-emissions vehicles powered by sustainable energy sources.
Beyond hydrogen energy, the partnership aims to advance innovation in autonomous driving, electric vehicle (EV) batteries, charging infrastructure, materials science, and cybersecurity.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) is located in Georgia.
HTWO Logistics operates as a clean logistics partnership running Hyundai XCIENT fuel cell heavy-duty trucks in logistics operations near Savannah.
The collaboration reinforces the link between education, research, and Hyundai Motor Group's long-term goal of achieving carbon neutrality by 2045.
The partnership aims to generate workforce talent and position Georgia as a national leader in hydrogen and zero-emissions transportation.
Additional announcements regarding the partnership's research projects, educational programs, and vehicle deployment are expected in the coming months.
Georgia Tech conducts more than $1 billion in research annually for government, industry, and society.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group and Georgia Tech Sign MOU to Further Collaborate on Hydrogen Mobility Development

facts: Hyundai celebrated the grand opening of its Metaplant in Bryan County, Georgia.
The facility is known officially as Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
HMGMA is a highly connected, intelligent manufacturing and assembly plant that serves as a centerpiece of Hyundai’s North American manufacturing strategy.
The plant operates under the “Hyundai Way,” the company’s new mid- to long-term strategy focused on agility via a unique flexible response system.
The flexible response system allows Hyundai to quickly produce electric vehicles, hybrids, extended-range EVs, hydrogen fuel cell vehicles, or internal combustion vehicles based on consumer demand.

Hyundai Motor Company President and CEO José Muñoz states that Hyundai is maintaining its long-term vision for electrification while leveraging powertrain technology and manufacturing flexibility to respond to market changes.

The Metaplant features a connected, automated, and flexible manufacturing system where all production processes from order collection to procurement, logistics, and production are optimized using artificial intelligence and data.
The innovative manufacturing system is designed to create a human-centered work environment with robotics assisting human workers.
Hyundai announced plans to invest $21 billion to expand U.S. manufacturing capabilities, advance future technologies, and enhance energy infrastructure over the next three years.
Of the total investment, $9 billion will establish an annual production capacity of 1.2 million vehicles across the Hyundai Motor, Kia, and Genesis brands.
The company plans to invest $6 billion to increase the localization rate of automotive components, including core parts for electric vehicles, to ensure robust supply chains.

An additional $6 billion will be invested to drive innovation and expand strategic partnerships with U.S.-based companies in autonomous driving, robotics, artificial intelligence, and advanced air mobility.
This $6 billion investment also includes energy infrastructure projects to contribute to sustainable energy generation development.
These investments are expected to create 14,000 new direct full-time jobs by 2028.
The investments will also create more than 100,000 direct and indirect job opportunities in related industries.
Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama (HMMA), the automaker’s first U.S. assembly and manufacturing plant, will celebrate its 20th anniversary in May.
HMMA is a 3.4-million-square-foot plant in Montgomery that serves as the largest manufacturer in the region.
The Alabama plant assembles six models: Hyundai Tucson, Santa Fe, Sante Fe Hybrid, Santa Cruz, Genesis GV70, and Electrified GV70.
HMMA’s press factory topped the Harbor Report’s North American Automaker Productivity Survey for six consecutive years.
Its engine and assembly factory topped the same survey for five consecutive years.
Almost 390,000 vehicles roll off the HMMA line each year.
The Alabama plant directly employs 4,200 team members.
HMMA supports 38,000 direct and indirect jobs and generates a $5 billion annual economic impact.
To date, 72 suppliers have located in Alabama and North America to support HMMA operations.
The plant invests $750,000 annually in regional programs covering education, cultural arts, diversity, environment, and health and fitness.
Hyundai’s Metaplant began production in the fourth quarter with the 2025 Hyundai IONIQ 5 electric SUV.
Production recently began on the plant’s second vehicle, the all-electric 2026 IONIQ 9 three-row SUV, which will be the first all-new Hyundai model assembled at the facility.
In addition to Hyundai models, the Metaplant will produce Genesis and Kia vehicles.
At full production, the Georgia plant will build more than 500,000 [BF2] vehicles per year.
Hyundai and its partners are investing $12.6 billion in the assembly plant and two battery joint ventures.
The Metaplant will directly employ 8,500 team members, while the battery plants will employ an additional 3,500.
A recent Center for Automotive Research study projects Hyundai’s Georgia investments will create nearly 40,000 jobs and $4.6 billion in individual earnings annually.
Hyundai has invested in regional educational systems through various STEM initiatives.
The company partnered with the SAE Foundation to deliver the A World in Motion PreK-12 STEM education program in the Bryan County School District.
Hyundai partnered with the H2GP Foundation to implement its Hydrogen STEM program to foster curiosity and innovation in sustainable energy.
Hyundai is partnering with Georgia Southern University and Ogeechee Technical College to address regional workforce needs and bridge academia with industry for skilled talent pipelines.
For the past two years, Hyundai has awarded scholarships to Georgia State University undergraduate students pursuing STEM degrees.

Hyundai recently announced a partnership with Georgia Tech to collaborate on research supporting sustainable mobility, including hydrogen economy development, workforce development, and smart city initiatives.
Hyundai reported an all-time annual sales record in 2024 with total and retail sales up 4%, driven by U.S. production capabilities.
The automaker has shifted its manufacturing focus from sedans to SUVs to meet market demand.
source_title: State of the Brand: Investment in America. Hyundai’s commitment to U.S. manufacturing - Automotive News

facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution announced Thursday they will build a $4.3 billion electric battery plant as part of Hyundai’s new electric vehicle assembly plant in southeast Georgia.
The companies will split the investment, starting production as soon as late 2025.
Hyundai Motor Co. CEO Jaehoon Chang said in a statement that the battery plant would “create a strong foundation to lead the global EV transition.”
Chang explained the company wants to speed up efforts to produce electrified Hyundai and Kia vehicles in North America.
“Hyundai Motor Group is focusing on its electrification efforts to secure a leadership position in the global auto industry,” Chang said.
The South Korean automaker said in 2022 it would invest $5.5 billion to assemble electric vehicles and batteries in Ellabell, just west of Savannah.
The site is supposed to have 8,100 employees and is slated to begin producing vehicles in 2025.
Garrison Douglas, a spokesperson for Republican Gov. Brian Kemp, said the 3,000-job battery plant would be part of the 8,100 overall jobs.
Douglas confirmed the $4.3 billion investment would be part of the previously announced $5.5 billion total.
The Hyundai/LG plant is supposed to be able to supply batteries for 300,000 electric vehicles per year.
This capacity matches the initial projected production of the adjoining vehicle assembly plant.
Hyundai has said the Georgia plant could later expand to build 500,000 vehicles annually.

“This is exactly what we envisioned when Georgia landed the Hyundai Metaplant in May of last year, and this project is the latest milestone in Georgia’s path to becoming the EV capital of the nation,” Kemp said in a statement.
In addition to the assembly and battery plants, auto parts suppliers have pledged to invest more than $2 billion in the region around the Hyundai site.
These auto parts suppliers have also pledged to hire 4,800 people in the region around the Hyundai site.
The announcements are part of an electric vehicle and battery land rush across the United States.
Under the US Inflation Reduction Act, EVs must be assembled in North America to qualify for a full $7,500 EV tax credit.
A certain percentage of their battery parts and minerals must come from North America or a US free trade partner to qualify for the full $7,500 EV tax credit.
Currently, no Hyundai or Kia vehicles are eligible for the tax credit unless they are leased.
Hyundai opposed having foreign-made vehicles excluded, in part because it’s building American factories.
Kemp has supported that position, but Democratic Sen. Jon Ossoff says Hyundai should wait until it is producing vehicles domestically, using American-made batteries.
“As soon as those vehicles are produced in Georgia, they can be eligible for the credits through the incentives in the IRA,” Ossoff told reporters in an online news conference Friday morning.

“These manufacturing incentives are attracting and accelerating billions of dollars of investments in jobs and advanced energy and electric vehicle production capacity here in the state of Georgia,” Ossoff said.
LG said this would be its seventh battery plant in operation or under construction in the US.
LG said it was concentrating efforts to expand production in the country, citing federal incentives as a lure for manufacturers.
This is the second huge electric battery plant that Hyundai is partnering to build in Georgia.
Hyundai and SK On, a unit of South Korea’s SK Group, announced in December they would jointly invest $4 billion to $5 billion to build a new plant northwest of Atlanta.
The Hyundai/SK On plant will supply electric batteries for Hyundai and Kia electric vehicles assembled in the US.
That plant, in Cartersville, is planned to begin production in 2025 and employ a projected 3,500 people.
Hyundai will need batteries for more than just vehicles made in Ellabell.
The company is already assembling electric vehicles at its plant in Montgomery, Alabama.
In April, Hyundai announced it would start assembling its electric Kia EV9 large SUV at the Kia plant in West Point, Georgia.
Partnering with LG and SK also will diversify Hyundai’s supplier base, giving the automaker more than one battery manufacturer from which to buy.
Because the battery plant is part of the overall Hyundai complex, Douglas said no additional incentives would be offered.
The state of Georgia and local governments already have pledged $1.8 billion in tax breaks and other incentives.
It’s the largest subsidy package a US state has ever promised an automotive plant, according to Greg LeRoy, executive director Good Jobs First.
Good Jobs First is a group skeptical of subsidies to private companies.
source_title: Hyundai, LG announce $4.3 billion plant in Georgia for electric vehicle batteries

facts: Hyundai Motor Group plans to begin production at its upcoming Metaplant America facility in Bryan County, GA -- its first dedicated EV mass-production plant -- in the last quarter of this year.
Previously, Hyundai said it would start production in the first quarter of 2025.
The announcement was made during an event celebrating the Korean automaker's impact on Georgia, attended by state officials and Hyundai executives.
The General Assembly passed a resolution recognizing Feb. 26, 2024, as Hyundai Day at the State Capitol.
Hyundai and Genesis electric vehicles, including the IONIQ 5, IONIQ 6, GV60 and electrified GV70, were on display outside of the State Capitol.
The hydrogen-powered XCIENT Fuel Cell Truck will be used for logistics needs between Hyundai’s manufacturing facilities and suppliers.
José Muñoz, president and global COO of Hyundai Motor Company and president and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America, stated that Georgia is like a second home to the company.

Hyundai's investments in Georgia are expected to create tens of thousands of great paying American jobs and bring tens of billions of dollars in economic impact for decades to come, according to José Muñoz.

Gov. Brian Kemp stated that Hyundai Motor Group has been an incredible partner to the state for decades and will help recruit businesses, give back to communities, and work with colleges and universities to connect Georgians with opportunities.
Hyundai Motor Group is investing $7.6 billion to develop the Metaplant America facility.
The Metaplant America facility will create 8,500 direct jobs.
Hyundai and SK On are building a $5 billion EV battery cell plant in Bartow County that will employ 3,500 workers.
According to a recent Center for Automotive Research study, Hyundai’s investments in Georgia are expected to create nearly 40,000 jobs and create $4.6 billion in individual earnings every year.
The Metaplant America facility is continuing its hiring efforts and has developed educational partnerships with area colleges to create a pipeline for future employment.
Hyundai has partnered with four area technical colleges to offer the Electric Vehicle Professional Technical Certificate (EVP TCC).
Students who successfully complete the EVP TCC program receive preferred status when applying for a position at the new plant.
source_title: Hyundai Announces Earlier Production Start Date at Georgia EV Plant - Autobody News

The directory listing was published on April 2, 2019.

facts: Hyundai Motor Company is planning to add production capacity at its sprawling manufacturing site in Southeast Georgia.
The company announced a $2.7 billion investment that promises to create 3,000 more jobs.
The announcement was made on Thursday as part of what Hyundai called its "most ambitious growth strategy" ahead of an investor meeting in New York.
Gov. Brian Kemp congratulated Hyundai on the announcement via social media, writing that it reinforces Hyundai’s "commitment to this project and our state."

Savannah Mayor Van Johnson wrote on social media that the project "will bring new opportunities for our workforce, bolster our infrastructure, and strengthen Savannah’s position as a hub for advanced manufacturing."
The investment announcement comes weeks after a massive immigration raid at the site that rounded up hundreds of workers.
The immigration raid caused diplomatic tension between the U.S. and South Korea.
The raid delayed completion of a Hyundai-LG Energy Solutions battery factory.
Didi Caldwell, president and CEO of Global Location Strategies, discussed potential business fallout from the raid with GPB's Orlando Montoya before the announcement.
South Korea's president stated last week that companies from his country might be "more hesitant" to invest in the U.S. if they fear immigration-related difficulties.
Georgia has key economic ties to Korean firms including Hyundai, SK Battery, and Qcells.

Didi Caldwell noted that investments are made after careful consideration and that the raid at the Hyundai plant outside Savannah undermines trust in the U.S. administration having companies' best interests at heart.

The U.S. faces a shortage of skilled labor and a bureaucratic visa system, leading some overseas firms to send workers on short-term visas that often result in overstays and improperly documented workforces.

Caldwell stated she is unaware of anyone trying to circumvent immigration laws but emphasized the necessity for specialized technicians and engineers to transfer institutional knowledge to the U.S. to replicate successful operations.

Caldwell suggested it would be in everyone's best interest for the U.S. government and companies to collaborate on a path that allows knowledge transfer while employing people efficiently and law-abidingly.

Labor availability and skills vary across Georgia, but the state generally fares well compared to the rest of the U.S., which faces shortages in construction and operations, particularly maintenance and mechatronics.
Caldwell, who has 27 years of experience, noted that Southern labor rates have normalized as the region moves up the value chain.

Georgia's advantages include being a right-to-work state with friendly labor management relations, a strong technical college system, and a state incentive program called Quick Start for recruiting and training workers.
Advanced technology factories in Georgia require highly trained engineers, equipment installers, and construction workers skilled in concrete pouring, carpentry, and pipe fitting.

Approximately 40% of U.S. construction workers are immigrants with varying documentation levels, and fear of ICE detention is exacerbating labor shortages by causing documented and undocumented workers to miss work or flee due to raid rumors.
Companies prioritize certainty in investment and immigration policy, as current chaos surrounding immigration, tariffs, monetary policy, and fiscal policy forces increased scenario planning.
The gap between best-case and worst-case business scenarios has grown by an order of magnitude due to this uncertainty.
source_title: Hyundai announces new investment at Georgia plant, weeks after ICE raid | Georgia Public Broadcasting

facts: Xtract One Technologies announced its SmartGateway security screening solution was selected to protect entrances at Hyundai Transys in West Point, Ga.
The SmartGateway will be deployed at the manufacturing plant to protect workers and staff from weapons entering the buildings and to prevent mass casualty events.
Jinho Kim, manager of GA & EHS at Hyundai Transys Georgia Powertrain Inc., stated they viewed and tested Xtract One’s Gateway at Kia Georgia Inc., which had great success with keeping weapons out.
Jinho Kim noted they were impressed by the Gateway solution's quick and accurate weapon detection and felt its customization would fit seamlessly into their manufacturing facility.
SmartGateway is the latest iteration of Xtract One's Gateway screening solution, designed as a fast, frictionless entry screener for manufacturing plants, casinos, and large arenas.
The system unobtrusively detects guns, knives, and other potential weapons on people entering a facility without requiring them to empty their pockets of personal items.

Peter Evans, CEO of Xtract One, stated auto manufacturers, their supply chains, and other large workplaces are well suited for the Gateway solution due to the need to process hundreds of workers through doors quickly during shift changes.
This selection marks Xtract One's third customer in the auto manufacturing industry.
Peter Evans criticized traditional walk-through metal detector systems as inefficient by modern standards, less secure, and more intrusive than their Gateway system.
SmartGateway uses artificial intelligence-powered scanners to detect concealed guns and knives and alerts security personnel to conduct secondary screening.
If people are not carrying weapons, they simply walk through the system without needing to empty pockets or other personal articles.
Upgrades to the system make it easy to learn and use, requiring fewer personnel to operate relative to other scanning systems.
The SmartGateway is customizable to fit the security needs and requirements of individual facilities, including its look and feel.

Ryan Smith, Program Manager for Security at Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia, Inc., recommended Xtract One to Hyundai Transys after being pleased with how the Gateway solutions looked and worked at their facilities.
Since implementing the solution at Kia Georgia, they have prevented a significant number of weapons from entering their facility.
Personnel screening time was drastically reduced from 21 seconds to 2.5 seconds following implementation at Kia Georgia.
Ryan Smith noted that in an industry based on “just in time” processes, the additional time savings provides production flexibility to maintain work areas during crucial times.
Xtract One Technologies is a technology-driven threat detection and security solution company leveraging AI to provide seamless and secure patron access control experiences.
The company manufactures unobtrusive threat detection systems that enable venue building operators to prioritize improved patron experiences while providing unprecedented safety.
Xtract One’s multi-sensor Gateway product enables companies to covertly screen for weapons at points of entry without disrupting the flow of traffic.
AI-based Xtract Vision allows venue and building operators to identify weapons and other threats inside and outside of facilities.
Xtract One Insights provides valuable intelligence for optimizing operations.
source_title: Xtract One Technologies Selected by Hyundai Transys to Secure Facilities and Keep Weapons Out | Financial Post

facts: Two months after 300 South Korean nationals were detained and left the U.S. following an immigration raid at Hyundai’s electric vehicle manufacturing site in Georgia, some of those workers have returned to America to resume their jobs.
The September raid shut down work on a battery plant under construction at the sprawling site near Savannah where Hyundai Motor Group began producing electric vehicles last year.
Most of the 475 workers detained were South Korean nationals.
U.S. immigration officials said the detained workers entered the U.S. with expired visas or visa waivers that prohibited them from working.
The battery plant’s operator, HL-GA Battery Co., stated that construction has resumed with a mix of new and returning workers.
HL-GA Battery Co. thanked the U.S. and South Korean governments, as well as Georgia officials, for their collaboration in supporting a smooth and timely return.
HL-GA Battery Co. stated it remains on track to start production in the first half of next year and continues to actively hire local positions to operate the facility.
HL-GA Battery spokesperson Mary Beth Kennedy confirmed that some returning workers were among the South Korean nationals detained in September but did not disclose how many have returned.
More than 300 South Korean workers were detained in an immigration raid on Sept. 4 at the sprawling site where Hyundai Motor Group produces electric vehicles near Savannah.
The detained workers spent a week at a Georgia detention center before the South Korean government negotiated their release and flew them home.
The roundup was conducted by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement, which released video showing some detained workers shackled in chains.
The raid sparked outrage and feelings of betrayal in South Korea, a key U.S. ally that has pledged hundreds of billions of dollars in American investments.
The Georgia raid targeted one of the state’s largest and most high-profile manufacturing sites, where Hyundai produces electric vehicles at a $7.6 billion plant.
All 475 people detained worked at the battery plant, which will produce batteries to power Hyundai EVs.
The battery plant is operated by HL-GA Battery, a joint venture between Hyundai and LG Energy Solution.

The detained South Korean nationals were largely engineers and other highly skilled workers who came to the U.S. temporarily to install equipment and perform specialized work to get the battery plant up and running.
It remains unclear how many formerly detained workers are returning.

Metro Atlanta attorney Jongwon Lee stated he has firsthand knowledge of two Korean nationals planning to return after the U.S. State Department confirmed their B-1 business visitor visas were still valid.

Kihwan Kim, president of the Federation of Korean Associations for the Southeast U.S., stated that some workers detained in the raid are hesitant to return to the U.S. due to the humiliation of the event.
Kim noted that the affected workers can seek employment opportunities in other countries instead of returning to the United States.
The South Korean government demanded improvements to the U.S. visa system for skilled Korean workers following the raid.

Last month, the South Korean Foreign Ministry announced that U.S. officials agreed to allow South Korean workers on short-term visas or a visa waiver program to help build industrial sites in America.
U.S. President Donald Trump initially defended the immigration raid in September by stating the detained workers “were here illegally.”
During an October visit to Seoul, Trump stated he was opposed to getting the detained workers out of the country.
source_title: South Korean workers return to Georgia after Hyundai immigration raid | AP News

facts: Hundreds of federal officers raided the Hyundai Metaplant in Ellabell, Georgia.
Federal officers arrested 475 people during the raid.
Officials described the operation as the largest single-site enforcement action in Homeland Security Investigations history.
Most of the detained individuals were Korean nationals.
Other detained individuals included visa overstays and individuals without work authorization.
Some workers attempted to flee by running into a sewage pond during the arrests but were captured.
Hyundai stated that none of the detainees were direct employees of the company.
Some of the detained individuals may have worked for contractors or subcontractors associated with the plant.
Construction at the EV battery plant was halted following the raid.
The Metaplant is located on a sprawling 2,900-acre site.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp has promoted the Metaplant as a major economic driver for the state.
The project includes plans to create 8,500 jobs.
Federal officials stated the raid targeted unlawful employment practices.
Federal officials warned companies that exploit workers will be held accountable.
source_title: Massive Immigration Raid at Hyundai Metaplant » ¡Que Onda Magazine!

entity_name: India Electric Vehicle and Battery Ecosystem
canonical_name: India Electric Vehicle and Battery Ecosystem
facts: Clean energy is revolutionising the way people and goods are transported, forcing the automotive industry to reset its goals.
India has been laying much thrust on promoting technologies based on eco-friendly means.
The move to electro mobility is a priority for the government.

The first phase of Faster Adoption and Manufacturing of Hybrid and EV (FAME) scheme, which was rolled out in 2015, was a policy step in the right direction, offering subsidies for manufacture of electric vehicles and setting up charging stations.

By pushing electric mobility under FAME Phase II and setting an ambitious target of generating 175 gigawatts of power from renewable energy by 2022, India is loud in its intent to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
With FAME Phase II, the demand incentive for electric two-wheelers has been hiked to Rs 15,000 per KWh from the uniform subsidy of Rs 10,000 KWh.
The government is encouraging automakers to manufacture flex-fuel engines.
States like Gujarat announced the 'Gujarat Electric Vehicle Policy 2021' to promote mass use of electric vehicles (EVs).
India is firmly set on the path to becoming a global EV hub through these policy initiatives.
The transport industry makes up 10% of India's carbon emissions.
The government's objective is to convert 15% of the vehicles in the country into electricity-powered by 2030, which will reduce air pollution and cut down crude oil imports.
Electric vehicles will account for 30% of vehicle sales in the country by 2030.
This transition is likely to save crude oil imports worth Rs one lakh crore.
The ongoing transformation will shake the auto parts industry more than ever.
It will lead to more imports from countries such as Germany as both countries are on their way to stepping up collaboration on next-generation technologies.
More and more German original equipment manufacturers have been entering the Indian electric vehicle market.
China will seek to aggressively export EV components to countries like India.
Out of the 250,000 EVs on the road today that use lithium-ion batteries, two-wheelers account for 80%, or 200,000 vehicles.
Three-wheelers account for nearly 10% of lithium-ion battery EVs, and the rest are four-wheelers.
The fast adoption of EVs will mean a rise in the consumption of lithium-ion batteries used in industries including automotive and consumer electronics.
The lithium-ion battery market is expected to grow at a rate of 37.5%.
At present, the EV segment accounts for 35% market share of lithium-ion batteries.
The rest of the lithium-ion battery market is used in the telecom sector, data centres, streetlights and other consumer gadgets.
By 2030, EV batteries will account for 80% market share in the lithium-ion battery market.
Prices of lithium-based products are falling constantly, making it affordable for EV manufacturers.
Most electric vehicles today run on lithium-ion batteries that can be recharged.
The batteries used in these vehicles-from two-wheelers to commercial vehicles and public transport buses-are largely the same.
They are made up of lithium, cobalt, nickel, iron and copper.
As per the size, configuration and purpose, a lithium-ion battery can perform between 500 and 10,000 cycles of charging and discharging.
The life of a battery lasts up to eight years.
It needs replacement when its capacity falls below 80%.
In an aluminum-air battery, electricity is generated when aluminum plates react with oxygen in the air.
Aluminium-air batteries have advantages over lithium-ion counterparts in terms of price, range and safety.
Aluminium-air also has one of the highest energy densities of all batteries.
Drawbacks undermining wide use of aluminum-air batteries in India include high anode cost and byproduct removal when using traditional electrolytes.
Another major disadvantage of aluminium-air batteries is that they cannot be recharged like lithium-ion batteries.
A prerequisite of wide application of aluminium-air battery-based vehicles is availability of a large number of battery swapping stations, which is a constraint in the Indian context today.
Policy initiatives in favour of encouraging production of domestic lithium-ion batteries augurs well for India's interest.
These initiatives will also thwart cheap dumping of batteries and parts by China and other countries to the detriment of the homegrown auto parts industry.

facts: Georgia Governor Brian Kemp joined the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) in announcing that the state surpassed $49.7 billion in exports in 2023.
The state eclipsed the record-setting $47 billion in exports in 2022.
Georgia currently ranks seventh in the U.S. for dollar value of trade.
The state facilitated more than $186 billion in total trade to 222 unique countries and territories.
Georgia ranks 12th in the country for dollar value of exports.
source_title: #GDECD | AM 1180 Radio

facts: Georgia’s 2026 legislative session began on January 12 and runs for 40 legislative days.
Lawmakers are deciding how to use about $14 billion in reserves and surplus funds.
Governor Brian Kemp has proposed another round of surplus-funded rebates, estimated at about $1 billion.
The rebate structure would mirror prior years, with up to $500 for married couples filing jointly and $250 for single filers.
The rebate is built into the amended budget rather than a stand-alone bill.
Republicans control both the House and the Senate, and all 236 seats in the Georgia General Assembly are on the ballot this year.
Georgia’s economy is still relatively strong, with low unemployment, but growth has slowed compared to the last few years.
The Senate has passed two major income tax bills and sent them to the House to lower income taxes.

Lawmakers are revisiting a homestead exemption Georgia voters approved in November 2024 (HB 581) that limits how quickly property taxes can rise on a primary residence by tying annual increases in taxable value to inflation.
Under current law, cities, counties, and school systems can opt out of the cap, but SB 382 would remove that option and make the cap apply statewide.
SB 382 has already passed the Senate and now awaits action in the House.
Data center construction in Georgia has surged in recent years, helped by generous sales tax breaks.
Two bills (SB 408 and HB 559) would sunset the current sales tax exemption for data center equipment at the end of 2026.
SB 410 would keep the exemption for existing projects but prevent new data centers from qualifying for it.
Another bill is on the table that would pause all new data center construction until March 1, 2027.
Supporters say the state needs time to study how data centers affect power demand, utility bills, and local budgets.
Industry groups say scaling back tax breaks and pausing projects could push future investments to other states.
The governor still must sign any final budget and tax bills.
source_title: 2026 Georgia Tax Breaks | 2026 Rebate Proposal | Atlanta CPA

canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group Georgia EV Plant
facts: Immigration authorities detained 475 people during an immigration raid in Georgia on Friday.
Most of the detained individuals were South Korean nationals.
The raid targeted Hyundai's sprawling manufacturing site in Ellabell, Ga., where the automaker makes electric vehicles.
Steven Schrank, lead Georgia agent of Homeland Security Investigations (HSI), said the raid resulted from a monthslong investigation into allegations of illegal hiring at the site.
Schrank called it the “largest single site enforcement operation” in HSI’s two-decade history.
The Thursday raid targeted one of Georgia’s largest and most high-profile manufacturing sites.
Hyundai Motor Group began manufacturing electric vehicles at a $7.6 billion plant there a year ago.
The site employs about 1,200 people in an area about 25 miles (40 kilometers) west of Savannah.
Gov. Brian Kemp and other officials have touted the project as the state’s largest economic development project.
Agents focused their operation on an adjacent plant still under construction where Hyundai partnered with LG Energy Solution to produce EV batteries.
Court records indicated prosecutors do not know who hired the “hundreds of illegal aliens.”
The U.S. Attorney’s Office wrote that the identity of the “actual company or contractor hiring the illegal aliens is currently unknown.”
The South Korean government expressed “concern and regret” over the operation targeting its citizens.
Only 46 Koreans were deported during the 12-month period ending Sept. 30, 2024, out of more than 270,000 removals for all nationalities, according to ICE.

South Korean Foreign Ministry spokesperson Lee Jaewoong said in a televised statement from Seoul that “The business activities of our investors and the rights of our nationals must not be unjustly infringed in the process of U.S. law enforcement.”
Lee said the ministry is dispatching diplomats from its embassy in Washington and consulate in Atlanta to the site, and planning to form an on-site response team.
Immigration attorney Charles Kuck said two of his detained clients arrived from South Korea under a visa waiver program allowing stays of 90 days or less without a visa.
One client had been in the U.S. for a couple of weeks, while the other had been there about 45 days.
The attorney did not provide details on their work but said they planned to go home soon.
Schrank told reporters that some detained workers illegally crossed the border, while others entered legally with expired visas or visa waivers prohibiting work.
Some detained individuals worked for the battery manufacturer, while others were employed by contractors and subcontractors at the construction site.
Schrank said he didn’t know precisely how many of the 475 detained were Korean nationals but that they made up a majority.
No one has yet been charged with any crimes, and the investigation is ongoing.
South Korea’s Foreign Minister Cho Hyun said Saturday that more than 300 South Koreans were among the 475 people detained.

Schrank explained it was not a simple roundup but a multimonth criminal investigation where evidence was developed, interviews conducted, documents gathered, and presented to a court for a judicial search warrant.
Most detainees were taken to an immigration detention center in Folkston, Georgia, near the Florida state line.
President Donald Trump’s administration has undertaken sweeping ICE operations as part of a mass deportation agenda.
Immigration officers have raided farms, construction sites, restaurants, and auto repair shops.
The Pew Research Center cited preliminary Census Bureau data saying the U.S. labor force lost more than 1.2 million immigrants from January through July.
The Democratic Party of Georgia condemned the raid on Friday.

DPAG chair Charlie Bailey called the raids “politically-motivated fear tactics designed to terrorize people who work hard for a living, power our economy and contribute to the communities across Georgia that they have made their homes.”
Kemp and other Georgia Republican officials issued statements saying all employers in the state were expected to follow the law.
The Hyundai site sits on 3,000 acres (1,214 hectares) in a largely rural area of Bryan County.
The site draws workers from several surrounding counties and communities including Savannah.
Sammie Rentz opened the Viet Huong Supermarket less than 3 miles (4.8 kilometers) from the Hyundai site six months ago.
The store sells American and Asian products, but Rentz mostly relies on Hyundai workers seeking foods imported from Korea.
Rentz said “Koreans keep this store running” and worried business might not bounce back after falling off sharply since the raid.
Ellabell resident Tanya Cox lives less than a mile from the site and had no ill feelings toward Korean nationals or immigrant workers.
Cox noted few neighbors were employed there and felt more construction jobs at the battery plant should have gone to local residents.
Cox said “I don’t see how it’s brought a lot of jobs to our community or nearby communities” and noted noise replaced bird chirping when the plant operates at night.
Hyundai began producing electric vehicles at the site last September.

Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chairman Euisun Chung credited President Trump with the company’s decision to create more American jobs by building an EV factory in Georgia during a White House appearance.
Chung said at the March event: “Our decision to invest in Savannah, Georgia, creating more than 8,500 American jobs, was initiated during my meeting with President Trump in Seoul in 2019.”
The battery plant operated by HL-GA Battery Co., a joint venture by Hyundai and LG Energy Solution, is slated to open next year.

Search warrant affidavits indicated agents wanted employment records, personnel files, payroll info, bank account info, timecards, video/photos of workers, immigration documents, Social Security cards, visas, passports, birth certificates, and records about ownership/management of construction companies/contractors.
Documents included names and photos of four people identified as “target persons.”
LG stated it was “closely monitoring the situation and gathering all relevant details” and couldn’t immediately confirm how many of its employees or Hyundai workers had been detained.
Plant spokesperson Bianca Johnson said operations at Hyundai’s EV manufacturing plant weren’t interrupted by the raid.
Hyundai Motor Company stated none of its employees had been detained as far as it knew and would review practices to ensure suppliers/subcontractors follow U.S. employment laws.
Hyundai’s statement said: “Hyundai has zero tolerance for those who don’t follow the law.”
HL-GA Battery Co. did not immediately respond to a request for comment but previously stated it’s “cooperating fully with the appropriate authorities.”
Those arrested who fight deportation may be detained as cases wind through immigration court.
The number of people in ICE custody topped 60,000 in August, an all-time high.
source_title: 475 people detained in Hyundai plant immigration raid in Georgia, Homeland security says | AP News

facts: An immigration raid occurred at a Hyundai and LG Energy Solution battery plant site in Georgia on Sept. 4.
The raid unearthed an open secret in the automotive industry.

facts: President Trump told reporters Sunday that “You don’t have people in this country who know about batteries.”
Trump suggested, “Maybe we should help them along and let some people come in and train our people.”
South Korean officials are trying to secure the release of 300 of its citizens detained at a construction site for a Hyundai Motor Co.-LG Energy ... project.
The raid and detention occurred at a construction site in Georgia for the Hyundai Motor Co.-LG Energy joint venture project.

facts: Kia Motors plans to invest $1.6 billion on new machinery, tools, equipment and other production-enhancing technology over the next 16 years at its West Point, Ga. auto assembly plant.
The investment aims to "satisfy demand" for Kia's vehicles in the U.S.
West Point Mayor Drew Ferguson stated the investment gives Kia long-term stability and the ability to grow its supplier network in the area, which is expected to drive new job creation.
Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia operates as the South Korean automaker's only U.S. assembly plant.
The investment will improve the surrounding area while keeping Kia on the cutting edge of the auto industry.
Georgia Gov. Nathan Deal stated Kia has an exceptional track record of growth in the state and that its economic impact goes far beyond the 10,000-plus jobs created by the company and its suppliers.

The deal took nine months to negotiate between Kia, the city of West Point, the West Point Development Authority, the Troup County Industrial Development Authority, the Georgia Department of Economic Development, and Georgia Quick Start.
The financial package includes $1 billion in bonds to be issued by the West Point Development Authority.
An additional $600 million will be provided by the Troup County Industrial Development Authority.
The city of West Point will receive $800,000 annually for the first five years and about $181,000 for years 6 through 16.
The city's development authority will receive $650,000 earmarked for economic development.

Mayor Ferguson noted the $181,000 annual payment offsets public safety and service costs, while a $4 million front-end payment assists with community development goals like better housing, education, and commercial/retail development.
Troup County will receive $450,000 on top of a $3 million grant to seed a new career academy in advanced manufacturing that is currently in planning stages with the county's school system.
Troup County Commission Chairman Ricky Wolfe stated plans to complete community assessment and curriculum definition by August of the following year, aligning it with the local school system.
In exchange for cash payments to the city and county, Kia will receive 100 percent tax abatements on any capital improvements over the 16-year period.
The county school board will abate 50 percent of taxes for the first eight years and 25 percent for the final eight years.
Chairman Wolfe stated the $1.6 billion investment surpasses Kia's original $1.1 billion investment to construct the plant.
The mother facility currently employs just over 3,000 people.
Twenty percent of Kia workers live in Troup County, and 15 to 16 percent live in Muscogee County.
More than 450 Kia workers commute to West Point from Columbus.
Chairman Wolfe highlighted that Kia has consistently blown through sales and profit numbers almost every month, providing a way to competitively expand and gain market share.
Prior to Kia's arrival near Interstate 85 on the Georgia-Alabama border, West Point's textile industry had collapsed, leaving downtown quiet and declining.
Kia announced plans in 2006 to build a manufacturing plant on 2,200 acres in West Point and Troup County, located about 45 minutes north of Columbus.
Production at the plant launched in November 2009.
U.S. sales of the Sorento crossover utility vehicle and the Optima four-door sedan have fueled Kia's rapid hiring and production growth.
The West Point plant also manufactures the Hyundai Santa Fe sport utility.
In February of the current year, Kia assembled its 500,000th vehicle at the facility.
The plant has a manufacturing capacity for 360,000 vehicles each year.
Kia Motors spokeswoman Corinne Hodges confirmed there are no current plans to expand the size of the assembly plant.
Mayor Ferguson noted that Kia's growth has injected confidence into local businesses and helped land nearly 40 new downtown businesses and Point University in the community of about 3,600 people.
Ferguson stated the city now has a strategic plan and funding stream to execute its core city redevelopment plan, expressing excitement over Kia's long-term presence.
source_title: Kia Motors to invest $1.6 billion in West Point auto assembly plant | Columbus Ledger-Enquirer

Headquarters located at 7000 Kia Pkwy, West Point, GA 31833, USA.

Employee breakdown includes 2 C-Level executives, 4 VP-Level executives, 22 Directors, 215 Managers, and 430 Non-Managers.

Hyundai Motor Group is listed as a founder/parent entity.

Listed acquisitions include Boston, PT Paramitra Multifinance, Home Mortgage Company of America, and Hyundai Motor UK.

facts: Kia Georgia recently celebrated three significant milestones, including the official start of production for the highly anticipated 2027 Kia Telluride.
The next-generation 2027 Telluride has officially begun rolling off the assembly line in West Point, Georgia.
Since its original debut, the Telluride has become one of Kia’s most awarded and sought-after SUVs, known for its bold styling, spacious three-row design, and family-focused versatility.
With the 2027 model, Kia is expanding its powertrain offerings at the Georgia plant to include hybrid technology.
The 2027 Telluride marks the first hybrid electric vehicle assembled at Kia’s Georgia plant.
Expanding hybrid production locally represents a major step forward for Kia’s electrification strategy and automotive manufacturing in Georgia.
By expanding hybrid production locally, Kia is reinforcing its long-term investment in advanced technology and sustainable mobility.
The start of 2027 Telluride production coincided with the plant’s 5 millionth vehicle built since operations began in 2009.
In just over a decade, Kia Georgia has grown into one of the region’s most important manufacturing facilities, supporting thousands of jobs and contributing significantly to the local economy.
The milestone event was attended by company leaders and state officials, including Brian P. Kemp, who recognized the plant’s impact on economic growth and workforce development throughout Georgia.
Having the Telluride built in Georgia highlights Kia’s commitment to quality, innovation, and American manufacturing for drivers in Kennesaw and the greater Atlanta area.
As production ramps up, the 2027 Telluride will offer premium comfort, advanced safety features, confident capability, and expanded efficiency options.
source_title: Georgia Kia Milestones & 2027 Telluride Launch | Jim Ellis Kia of Kennesaw

facts: Kia Georgia currently employs approximately 3,000 team members and produces upwards of 350,000 vehicles annually at its manufacturing site in West Point, Georgia.
Earlier this year, Kia introduced the EV9, which is the company’s first U.S.-assembled electric vehicle (EV).
As Kia Georgia’s West Point manufacturing site grows and evolves, the company is converting and diversifying the skillsets of its existing workforce to meet the unique demands of EV production.
Kia Georgia is employing creative tactics and new partnerships to attract and retain new talent in a challenging labor market.
Kia Georgia is investing in workforce development through education partnerships around new curriculums for EV manufacturing, focusing on unique components and safety issues.
These curriculum and educational resource partnerships are helping Kia Georgia upskill current employees and create a pipeline of new workers ready for future EV manufacturing positions.
Kia Georgia has partnered with high schools in Troup County, including THINC College & Career Academy, to create work-based learning opportunities exposing students to manufacturing.
Through the THINC partnership, students can participate in plant internships across production, engineering, or administrative areas to experience a manufacturing environment.

Kia Georgia supports an interactive summer camp called Students Learning About Manufacturing (SLAM), which invites students to tour local industries, manufacturers, and suppliers, and participate in team robotic competitions.

Kia Georgia supports the LaGrange Industrial Fellowship for Teachers (LIFT), a program designed to expose teachers and guidance counselors to manufacturing careers to help them educate students about manufacturing pathways.
MaKara Rumley, an environmental justice expert and founder of the Hummingbird Firm, moderated the Drawdown Georgia panel on advancing equity and economic opportunities in the state.
Latesa Bailey, senior manager and head of department for human resources at Kia Georgia, provided background on Kia’s presence and workforce investments in Georgia.
Garry Harris, managing director of the Center for Sustainable Communities, outlined current clean energy investment and job growth statistics for Georgia.
Georgia ranked 14th in the country for solar jobs in 2022, with solar-related jobs in the state expected to increase by 10.6% in 2023.
In 2022, there were 80,710 jobs in Georgia that could be aligned with clean energy opportunities.
Since 2018, electric vehicle manufacturers and suppliers have invested $22.7 billion in more than 40 projects in Georgia, creating 28,400 jobs.
The Bipartisan Infrastructure Law is providing more than $800 million for workforce development in growing sectors like clean energy.

Since the introduction of the Inflation Reduction Act, the energy sector has added nearly 300,000 jobs nationwide, with 114,000 of them in clean energy technologies like renewables and zero-emission vehicles.
Georgia is the state with the second highest number of new private sector clean energy investments, featuring 22 projects that are creating 16,678 jobs.
Garry Harris stated: “It's a good time to be in Georgia. And it's a good time to be part of the renewable energy movement.”
The convergence of clean energy, workforce development, sustainable industries, and equitable access to fair wages and benefits is positioned to pave the way for lasting economic success in Georgia.
source_title: Georgia's Journey Towards a Just, Green Economy

entity_name: Georgia Clean Economy: Opportunity Rising Report
facts: A report titled “Georgia Clean Economy: Opportunity Rising” was released at the Georgia Clean Energy Summit in Atlanta.
The report was produced for Environmental Entrepreneurs (E2) and features economic analysis and predictive models by BW Research.
Predictions include 403,000 new jobs nationwide, with 100,000 being permanent positions.
Predictions include $156 billion added to U.S. GDP from clean energy investments.
Predictions include $111 billion in new wages for workers across the U.S.
Predictions include more than $32 billion generated in tax revenue for federal, state, and local governments.
Researchers tracked 19 clean energy projects in Georgia launched between August 2022 and August 2023.
The report predicts 38,600 jobs will be created over a five-year construction period for these 19 Georgia projects.
An additional 6,800 jobs per year are predicted to be created annually after the construction phases for these projects.
$13.9 billion is predicted to be added to Georgia’s Gross State Product (GSP) during the construction phase of the tracked projects.
$926.5 million is predicted to be added to Georgia’s GSP annually during the operational life of the projects.
Economic impact was measured using direct, indirect, and induced effects.
Billions were poured into Georgia from the Biden administration’s Inflation Reduction Act (IRA), which was signed into law in 2022.
EVs accounted for 49 percent of capital investments among the major clean energy categories tracked.
Battery storage accounted for 27 percent of capital investments.
Solar accounted for 24 percent of capital investments.
Wind, electric transmission & distribution, and clean fuels were negligible on this scale.
Stuart Countess, president and CEO of Kia Georgia, served as the keynote speaker at the summit.
Kia plans to build the Kia EV9 in Georgia.
Kia hopes to have the EV9 hitting the road by summer of 2024.
Bob Keefe, executive director of E2, stated that clean energy technology has finally caught up with environmental problems, making deployment economically feasible.
E2’s motto is “Good for the economy. Good for the environment.”
New administrations or policy rollbacks could negatively impact Georgia’s clean energy investment direction and opportunities.
Bob Keefe noted that creating jobs and driving economic growth were historically bipartisan goals in Georgia.
E2 has operated for 23 years and utilizes conservative models and estimates for its economic projections.
An estimated $14 billion will be added to Georgia’s GSP during the construction phase of the 19 tracked projects.
Nearly $1 billion is expected to be added to Georgia’s GSP during the operational phase.
An estimated $2.6 billion in tax revenue is projected during the construction phase of these projects.
Georgia’s state budget for the year is $36 billion.
source_title: New analysis shows Georgia clean energy investments are paying off for workforce and economy - SaportaReport

facts: Kia will invest more than $200 million at its West Point plant.
The investment will create nearly 200 jobs at the West Point plant.
The project is to produce the Korean automaker’s new electric vehicle sports-utility model, the EV9.
Gov. Brian Kemp announced the project on Wednesday.
The EV9 will become the fifth model assembled at the Georgia plant since it opened in 2009.
Other models assembled at the plant include the Telluride, Sorento and Sportage SUVs, and the K5 mid-size sedan.
More than 40% of Kia vehicles sold in the U.S. are manufactured in West Point.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that Georgia’s longstanding partnership with Kia has led to generational job creation and growth for the West Point area.
The project aims to bring more opportunity to Georgians and help propel the state toward its goal of becoming the e-mobility capital.
Sean Yoon, president & CEO of Kia Motors North America and Kia Motors America, stated that the EV9 has the potential to be another change catalyst for Kia.
Sean Yoon described the EV9 as the most innovative vehicle Kia has ever built and a standout in the EV market.
To date, Kia has invested more than $1.9 billion in Georgia.
Kia Georgia is responsible for more than 14,000 plant and supplier jobs in the region.
Hyundai is building a $5.5 billion EV plant west of Savannah that will generate 8,100 jobs.
Rivian has under construction a $5 billion EV plant in the Covington area expected to create 7,500 jobs.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team worked on the Kia EV project with the West Point Development Authority, the city of West Point, Troup County, the Georgia Center of Innovation’s manufacturing and energy teams, and the Technical College System of Georgia’s Quick Start program.
The EV9 plant is expected to begin operations in the second quarter of next year.
source_title: Kia to invest $200 million and bring jobs at West Point plant | Columbus Ledger-Enquirer

facts: Kia Georgia celebrated the start of production for the all-new 2027 Kia Telluride at its West Point facility on Feb. 24, 2026.

The ceremonial vehicle represented three major milestones: the start of production for the 2027 Kia Telluride, the first hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) assembled in Georgia, and the 5 millionth vehicle produced at Kia Georgia.
Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp attended the ceremony alongside team members and state/community leaders.
Sean Yoon, President and CEO of Kia North America and Kia America, stated that Kia Georgia is the only plant in the world that builds the Telluride.
The 2027 Telluride launch marks Kia's first US-assembled HEV.
Since beginning production in 2009, Kia Georgia has grown into one of the region’s largest employers and a cornerstone of automotive manufacturing in the Southeast.
The Telluride has been exclusively manufactured in Georgia since its introduction in 2019.
Stuart Countess, Kia Georgia’s President and CEO, emphasized that producing world-class gasoline-powered vehicles, EVs, and HEVs demonstrates the versatility and talent of the plant's team.
Kia Georgia, Inc. is the first manufacturing site in North America for Seoul-based Kia Corporation.
Kia Georgia has an annual production capacity of 350,000 units.
The facility is located on 2,200 acres in West Point, Ga.
Kia Georgia began mass production on Nov. 16, 2009.
Kia Georgia currently produces the Telluride, Sorento (excludes HEV and PHEV models), Sportage (excludes HEV and PHEV models) SUVs, and the all-electric EV9 and EV6.

facts: Kia Georgia, Inc. is the first manufacturing site in North America for Kia Corporation based in Seoul, Korea.
The facility has an annual capacity of 350,000 units.
Kia Georgia is located on 2,200 acres in West Point, Ga.
The facility address is 7777 Kia Parkway, West Point, GA 31833, United States.

Kia Georgia is home to the Telluride, Sorento, and Sportage SUVs.
Kia Georgia is also home to the all-electric EV9 and EV6 SUVs.
The facility represents a total investment of $3.2 billion.
The plant employs 3,200 team members.
Manufacturing processes utilize high-tech stamping, weld shop advanced robotics, and highly automated assembly processes.
The plant produces a finished vehicle every 51 seconds.
Operations run on three full shifts operating at the plant 24 hours a day/five days per week.
