facts: Rivian Automotive announced it is building a $5 billion battery and assembly plant east of Atlanta.
The plant is projected to employ 7,500 workers.
Rivian is based in Irvine, California.
Rivian is a startup manufacturer of electric trucks and commercial delivery vans.
Rivian challenges established automakers like Ford and General Motors and EV leader Tesla.
The plant could grow to as many as 10,000 workers.
It would be among the largest auto assembly complexes in the United States.
It is rivaled by BMW's 11,000-worker complex in Spartanburg, South Carolina.
It is rivaled by Ford Motor Co.'s 8,600-worker plant in Louisville, Kentucky.
Rivian will be the largest industrial announcement in Georgia history.
It surpasses the 4,400-worker Kia complex that opened in West Point in 2009.
Georgia has had a number of failed auto plant recruitments.
Rivian will give Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp another point to bolster his claims that he has been an excellent steward of Georgia’s economy.
The city of Fort Worth, Texas, offered Rivian a $440 million incentive package to lure the plant during the summer.
Published reports indicate Rivian also considered sites in Arizona and Michigan.
Rivian told Fort Worth its plant would make 200,000 vehicles per year.
Workers at the Fort Worth proposal would be paid at least $56,000 a year.
Founder and CEO Robert Scaringe told Bloomberg Television in November that the ability to recruit good workers was the most important factor in the decision.
Rivian’s existing plant in Normal, Illinois, like most auto plants that have opened in recent decades, is not unionized.
Georgia officials are unlikely to detail the full incentive package offered to Rivian on Thursday.
The company could max out what Georgia calls its "mega project tax credit," for companies that hire at least 1,800 people or invest at least $450 million.
That credit could be worth $118 million in state income tax credits.
Local governments are likely to abate property taxes.
Kia got more than $450 million in incentives for its plant southwest of Atlanta.
Georgia has promised $300 million of incentives to the $2.6 billion, 2,600-worker battery plant being built by SK Innovation in Commerce, Georgia, northeast of Atlanta.
Rivian, like Tesla, deals with its customers directly instead of through dealers.
Its arrival in Georgia could prompt the state to loosen a law that mostly prohibits manufacturers from selling directly.
Rivian currently plans two models for consumers — the R1T pickup with a base price of $67,500 and the R1S SUV, with a base price of $70,000.
The truck was recently named Motor Trend’s 2022 truck of the year.
Amazon, which owns a slice of the company, has ordered 100,000 delivery vans, launching Rivian into the commercial vehicle business.
Rivian is flush with cash following a $11.9 billion stock offering Nov. 10 that took the company public, allowing it to finance the new plant.
Its market value is nearly $95 billion, more than either General Motors or Ford.
The company argues that electric vehicle adoption is at the "tipping point" and it is well positioned for success because trucks and SUVs have long been the most profitable vehicles sold.
Some analysts question whether it needs another plant besides the former Mitsubishi plant in Illinois, that Rivian bought for $16 million in 2017.
Rivian says it has a yearly capacity of 150,000 vehicles at the Illinois plant, but is looking to expand there as well.
Sam Abuelsamid, principal mobility analyst for Guidehouse Insights, said it might be premature to invest that much in another large plant just yet.
Abuelsamid said Rivian also may be looking for a manufacturing site in Europe.
Given the size of the Georgia plant, Abuelsamid expects it to make other parts for the vehicles, similar to electric vehicle leader Tesla making seats at its factory in Fremont, California.
Abuelsamid said Rivian has cash in the bank but will burn through that quickly unless it starts delivering vehicles and generating cash, jeopardizing its market darling status.
The company needs more retail customers and commercial vehicle buyers other than Amazon.
Abuelsamid said if actual deliveries don’t start picking up, the markets could turn against them as they have with some other EV startups not called Tesla.
Unlike Tesla, Rivian will be facing serious competition in a much shorter time frame than the near-decade head start that Tesla had.
Ford and GM plan to start selling their own electric pickup trucks in the next year or two.
Tesla also plans a new pickup.
That’s part of a wave of electric vehicle investment, with SK’s $5 billion battery plant northeast of Atlanta just one example.
Ford announced in September it would spend $11.4 billion to build three battery factories and an assembly plant in Kentucky and Tennessee.
The LMC Automotive consulting firm expects U.S. sales of new fully electric vehicles to hit nearly 400,000 this year, nearly double last year’s figures.
They still make up only about 2.6% of sales.
The firm expects sales to grow to more than 730,000 next year and more than 2 million by 2025.
Even at 2 million, EV sales still would be only about 12% of U.S. new vehicle sales.
source_title: Rivian to build $5B electric truck plant in Georgia | FOX 5 Atlanta

facts: Valeo inked a contract worth “almost one billion US dollars” with Indian car manufacturer Mahindra.
The deal involves supplying electric drives integral to Mahindra’s “Born Electric” SUVs.
The partnership signals a shift away from Mahindra's initially planned collaboration with Volkswagen.
The commissioned electric drivetrain encompasses an electric motor, inverter, transmission, and bidirectional power electronics.
Specific details regarding the system remain undisclosed.
Production will take place in close proximity to Mahindra’s facility in Pune, Maharashtra.
Mahindra's decision to partner with Valeo stems from anticipated delays in the supply of Modular Electric Drive Kit (MEB) components from Volkswagen.
The original collaboration was set to equip Mahindra’s Inglo electric vehicle platform.
In August, Mahindra opted for Valeo's agile and reliable solutions to meet its electrification goals.

Xavier Dupont, President of the Valeo Powertrain Systems Business Group, emphasized that locally developed solutions would enable higher voltage and longer-range electric vehicles for the Indian market.
The partnership is positioned as a significant stride for Valeo in the burgeoning Indian electric vehicle (EV) sector.
Valeo has plans for investments to localize production in India.
Mahindra’s “Born Electric” vehicles will rely on Valeo’s electric drives, while Mahindra retains responsibility for chassis, bodywork, interior, and infotainment.
The collaboration is aligned with Mahindra’s commitment to providing sustainable and innovative mobility solutions globally.
The shift away from Volkswagen involves Mahindra sourcing battery cells from BYD and electric motors from Valeo.
Delays in Volkswagen’s delivery of battery cells and electric motors are now projected for 2026 or 2027.
The first set of electric SUVs under Mahindra’s “Born Electric” initiative, including models XUV.e8 and XUV.e9, is slated for launch between 2024 and 2026.
These vehicles will address both domestic and international markets.
source_title: Valeo Secures Nearly $1 Billion Contract to Supply Electric Drives for Mahindra's "Born Electric" SUVs

facts: Vale published its 2025 Annual Report, a document that brings together the company’s financial, operational, environmental, social, and governance information for the period from January 1 to December 31, 2025.
The report reflects the evolution of the former Integrated Report and reinforces a unified view of performance, risk management, and long-term value creation.
Among the highlights of 2025, Vale recorded USD 38.4 billion in net revenue, USD 15.5 billion in adjusted EBITDA, and USD 4.8 billion in recurring free cash flow.
Iron ore production reached 336 million tons, the highest level since 2018.
The Base Metals business saw significant growth, particularly in copper and nickel, strategic minerals for the energy transition.
Vale completed the implementation of the Global Industry Standard for Tailings Management (GISTM) at all dams and ended the year with no structures at emergency level 3.
The company achieved 63% completion of the upstream dam decharacterization program and fulfilled 81% of obligations under the Brumadinho Comprehensive Remediation Agreement.
Vale expanded its circular mining initiatives, producing 26 million tons of ore from circular sources.
Approximately 60,000 people participated in initiatives to combat extreme poverty in 2025.
The company exceeded diversity targets, with women making up 28% of the workforce, above the commitment set for 2026.

Vale voluntarily published the first edition of its ISSB Report in 2025, disclosing financial information related to sustainability, risks, and opportunities prepared in accordance with International Sustainability Standards Board standards.
Grazielle Parenti is Vale’s Executive Vice President of Sustainability.
source_title: Vale Releases 2025 Annual Report highlighting progress in safety, sustainability, and value creation - Vale

facts: Georgia’s economy is projected to grow by 2.4% in 2025, a slowdown from the 3.1% the state saw in 2024.
The state’s GDP is projected to grow faster than the nation’s, which forecasters project to grow by 1.6% in 2025.
The major driver of Georgia’s economic slowdown is the Federal Reserve’s 2022 move to constrain lending to control inflation.
Inflation successfully shrunk from 8% in 2022 to 3% in 2024 and is projected at 3% again in 2025.
The Federal Reserve began reducing monetary policy restraint by cutting policy interest rates in September, with additional rate cuts expected in 2025.
Quantitative tightening is expected to end as credit and money supply expand moderately.
The probability Georgia will slip into recession is 25%.
The number of jobs in Georgia will rise by 1% in 2025, slower than the 1.5% gain estimated for 2024 but higher than the national job growth rate of 0.6%.
Georgia’s unemployment rate is poised to increase from 3.7% to 4%, lower than the 4.3% expected at the U.S. level, due to slowed hiring rather than layoffs.
Health care and housing will lead Georgia’s economic growth in 2025.
Homebuilders will ramp up construction of single-family homes in 2025 by 9% due to slightly lower mortgage rates, favorable demographic trends, and a post-pandemic housing shortage.
Home prices, which have increased 65% since before the COVID-19 pandemic, are expected to hold steady.
Georgia is likely to see job losses in retail and information jobs due to advances in technology and competition from online retailers.
Georgia has broken records in the number of new jobs and industries attracted to the state, though this will slow this year due to the national and global economic slowdown.
With the build-out of existing projects, Georgia is poised to see thousands of new jobs created over the next few years.

Georgia’s advantages for economic development include a favorable mix of industries, supportive demographics, excellent transportation and logistics infrastructure, a competitive tax climate, and low costs of living and doing business.
source_title: Georgia economy expected to grow next year, but at a slower rate – Albany Herald

facts: Georgia Gov. Brian P. Kemp announced the fourth round of grants from the Rural Site Development Initiative (RSDI) within the OneGeorgia Authority.
The fourth round of RSDI grants totals $7.8 million for ten local projects across the state.
With this latest round, the State of Georgia has invested $28 million into rural site development projects since the program's launch in FY 2025.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that over 64 percent of new jobs created during his tenure have gone to parts of the state outside the metro Atlanta region.
The Rural Site Development Initiative is an important part of a broader effort to bring opportunity to all parts of Georgia.
Lt. Governor Burt Jones commended Gov. Kemp for leading the charge to fund and promote the program, which has awarded $28 million for critical projects in rural communities across Georgia.
House Speaker Jon Burns stated that rural communities are the lifeblood of Georgia’s economy and expressed pride in supporting grants that bring good-paying jobs to hardworking Georgians.
Speaker Jon Burns noted he is especially proud of investments being made in Southeast Georgia as the region navigates unprecedented changes in the forestry industry.

RSDI grants are awarded for either site improvement or to assist with the costs of seeking Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) certification on approved projects critical to economic development.
Communities match state funding at various levels based on the program's criteria.
Georgia Department of Community Affairs Commissioner Christopher Nunn stated that GRAD sites are important to local strategies for attracting employers and investment in Georgia’s communities.
Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that over the last several years, GRAD sites have helped attract an estimated 10,000 jobs to communities across the state.
Proactive economic development planning, including through shovel-ready sites, strengthens the state's portfolio and competitiveness as a state.

The Effingham County Development Authority in coastal Georgia was awarded $1.8 million for site readiness improvements, including the construction of a sewer pump station, at the GRAD-certified Savannah Portside International Park.
Total project costs for the Effingham County site readiness improvements are $2.5 million.
The Development Authority of Columbus in west Georgia was awarded $2 million for site improvement work, including clearing and grading, at the GRAD-certified Muscogee Technology Park.
Total project costs for the Columbus site improvement work are $10 million.

The McIntosh County Industrial Development Authority near coastal Georgia was awarded $1.5 million for road, water, and sewer infrastructure improvements at the GRAD-certified Tidewaters Industrial Park.
Total project costs for the McIntosh County infrastructure improvements are $2.8 million.
This is the second site development award for McIntosh County, which previously received a GRAD-Certification Scholarship for this grant.

The Thomaston-Upson County Industrial Development Authority in west-central Georgia was awarded $2 million to construct water, sewer, and transportation infrastructure at the GRAD-certified Central Georgia Business and Technology Park.
Total project costs for the Thomaston-Upson County infrastructure construction are $2.3 million.

links.context: Program information and OneGeorgia Authority details on the Department of Community Affairs website
Application process and eligibility on the Georgia Department of Economic Development webpage

source_title: GA: Gov. Kemp Announces Fourth Round of Rural Site Development Grants | Trade and Industry Development

entity_name: Public Finance Research Cluster’s Fiscal Research Center (FRC)
canonical_name: Georgia State Public Finance Research Cluster
facts: The Public Finance Research Cluster’s Fiscal Research Center (FRC) visited the Georgia State Capitol to brief legislators and staff on tax incentive evaluations completed in 2025.
FRC answered questions from committee members, legislative staff, and other stakeholders as the 2026 session gets underway.
Six evaluations were completed under contract with the Georgia Department of Audits and Accounts (DOAA).

The evaluations reflect Georgia’s ongoing tax incentive evaluation process, which gives lawmakers and the public independent analysis of whether selected credits, deductions, and exclusions are meeting their intended goals.
The evaluations include estimates of fiscal impact and other outcomes for state tax preferences.
The 2025 evaluations were requested by the Senate Finance Committee and the House Ways and Means Committee under the state’s tax preference evaluation framework.
source_title: PFRC Research Team Briefs Georgia Lawmakers on Tax Incentive Evaluations - Georgia State Public Finance Research Cluster

facts: The Abu Dhabi Department of Economic Development (ADDED) partnered with Valeo to establish a technology center in Abu Dhabi for manufacturing components for autonomous and electric vehicles.
The new center will develop industrial solutions to support local electric vehicle start-ups in Abu Dhabi and collaborate with local EV startup companies.
Valeo plans to explore new development areas by combining its advanced technologies with a sustainable vision of green cities.

The agreement aims to improve smart and sustainable mobility and contribute to the transport sector's development, which is targeted by the Abu Dhabi Industrial Strategy (ADIS) as one of its sub-sectors.
The agreement was signed by Arafat Al Yafei, Executive Director of ADDED’s Industrial Development Agency, and Tamer Aly, Vice President of Group Technology Center and General Manager of Valeo Egypt.
The signing occurred in the presence of Rashed Abdulkarim Al Blooshi, General Manager of Valeo Egypt, and the Deputy Minister of ADDED.

Arafat Al Yafei noted that the collaboration to develop a smart mobility center for electric and autonomous vehicle components aligns with Abu Dhabi's initiative to ensure growth, sustainability, and advanced technology go hand in hand across all aspects of economic development.

Tamer Aly stated: “I am very pleased with the signing of this MoU, which enables Valeo, a market leader in the design and production of high-tech automotive components for autonomous and electric vehicles, to have a presence in Abu Dhabi, Utilize their components and technology for Abu Dhabi’s nascent automotive industry and transfer this experience to Abu Dhabi’s high-tech and advanced education society.”
Valeo is a French global automotive supplier headquartered in France that offers a wide range of products to automakers and the aftermarket.
Valeo employs 113,600 people across 33 countries worldwide.
The Valeo group operates 186 production plants, 59 R&D centers, and 15 distribution platforms globally.
source_title: Valeo to build electric car manufacturing plant in Abu Dhabi - UAE Times - Gulf News, breaking news uae, Business, Tech & Lifestyle

facts: A €45 million investment transforms the historic French plant into a European competence center for complex optics and heavy-duty vehicle lighting.
The Valeo industrial site in Angers finalized its €45 million modernization plan, rolled out between 2023 and 2026.
The transformation aligns with the Valeo Elevate 2028 strategic plan to meet aggressive growth targets in the global automotive lighting market.
The facility entirely overhauled its workshops and deployed new optical technologies to satisfy increasing demand from European manufacturers for complex bi-color optical lenses.
Thermoplastic injection workshops were upgraded with new high-performance automated injection press lines.
The high-tech facility operates with 25 injection presses, five varnishing lines, 11 assembly lines, and 181 robots.
Manufacturing processes are optimized through the integration of digital tools and Artificial Intelligence for predictive simulation and real-time system validation.
The site operates with over 1,200 highly qualified employees across Angers and Blois.
Valeo's regional industrial operations supply 9,000 headlights and 22,000 complex modules daily to the world’s leading manufacturers.
The local R&D center currently has 24 projects under development, with six major initiatives specifically allocated to the Angers plant.
Jean-Luc Terrasse, CEO of Valeo’s Light Division, stated that lighting is at the heart of mobility challenges regarding safety, design, and intelligent interaction with the environment.

The €45 million investment allows the factory to consolidate its industrial excellence and demonstrates that a French factory can design and produce systems of unprecedented optical complexity at the forefront of European Industry 4.0.
Over the last three years, the site completed 168 new hires, including 15 specialized technicians joining the workshops in 2026 to configure newly installed presses.
The company fosters long-term employability through an apprenticeship policy covering 6.3% of the workforce.
The Valeo Lighting Academy provides over 3,150 hours of continuous training annually to maintain team excellence.
Two major client launches are planned for 2027 and 2028.
Production volume related to heavy-duty vehicles will double from 20% to 40%.
By 2030, six out of seven European road transport leaders will utilize headlights developed in Angers.
The Angers site is officially cemented as the Group's European Product and Process Competence Center for Heavy-Duty Vehicle front headlights.
source_title: Valeo Angers Completes Modernization for Next-Generation Lighting Systems | Auto-Innovations International

facts: Vanguard National Trailer Corporation will soon bring 400 new jobs to the area.
County leaders say the new plant is a boon for the entire county.
Dade County, Georgia has been preparing for the last few years to attract a major employer.
The Industrial Development Authority purchased 260 to 280 acres of property adjoining the old industrial park in Dade County, Georgia.
Vanguard National Trailer Corporation made several trips to Trenton to look over the property.
The site benefits from proximity to Interstate 59 and a major railroad line for transportation options.
Ted Rumley serves as Chairman of the Dade County Commission.
Peter Cervelli serves as Executive Director of the Industrial Development Authority.
The Industrial Development Authority provided 10 years of tax-free status for the company.
Significant financial incentives were provided for infrastructure by the Industrial Development Authority and the state of Georgia.
The State of Georgia is providing over a million dollars for infrastructure work for the plant.
The new facility will be 350-thousand square feet.
Vanguard National Trailer Corporation aims to have the plant up and running by the end of April next year.
Job applications will be taken next year.
Vanguard National Trailer Corporation manufactures dry freight and refrigerated semi-trailer vans.
Vanguard National Trailer Corporation has 47-thousand employees world-wide.
The Shaw plant in the area closed back in 2008, leaving 420 people without jobs.
source_title: Vanguard National to Build Plant in Dade County - WDEF

facts: James Wilkerson supervises workers building semitrailers from iron sheets and frames for hauling freight at a factory in Monon, Indiana.
Vanguard National Trailer Corporation is a wholly owned subsidiary of Shenzhen-based China International Marine Containers (CIMC).
HPA Monon previously occupied the site, employing over 400 workers in the late 1980s and early 1990s before slashing its workforce to less than 100 and closing in May 2002.
Vanguard came into the picture in 2003 with new equipment, investment, and management, gradually recovering the factory to a healthy operation.
Vanguard now employs over 400 people, making it Monon's largest employer and contributing some 80 percent to the local gross domestic product.
Randy Mitchell, president of White County Economic Development, stated that Vanguard is so important to the town's health that probably every family has had a member work there now or previously.
The two semitrailer companies in White County, Vanguard and Wabash, together produce most of the trailers in the United States right now.
Vanguard CEO Charlie Mudd stated that hundreds of jobs were created in Monon thanks to the CIMC investment.
Mudd noted that CIMC vehicle group companies across the globe share ideas, information, resources, suppliers, and strategy in ways no other trailer company can.
Vanguard is expanding by opening another factory in Trenton, Georgia, creating about 200 jobs.
Mudd commented that the best success comes from combining Chinese knowledge and capabilities with American knowledge and opportunities, describing it as 'True collaboration.'
source_title:
Chinese investment gives wheelpower to small US town
 - Business - Chinadaily.com.cn

facts: Velocity Trailer Centers, a division of Velocity Vehicle Group, has expanded its Vanguard trailer distribution network across Tennessee, Alabama, and Georgia, effective February 16, 2026.

The expansion strengthens the company’s ability to support fleets and owner-operators throughout the Southeastern United States, with greater access to Vanguard dry van and refrigerated trailers, along with customizable factory-built options.

Velocity Trailer Centers is the dedicated trailer sales and support division of Velocity Vehicle Group, providing customers with access to new and used trailer inventory, equipment financing, and aftermarket support through the broader Velocity commercial vehicle network.
The Southeast is one of the fastest-growing freight markets in the U.S., driven by continued expansion in warehouse infrastructure, port activity, and distribution center development.

These factors have strengthened the region’s role as a key logistics gateway within the U.S. freight network while increasing demand for reliable trailer equipment across major transportation corridors.

Robert Ramey, General Manager of Velocity Trailer Centers, stated that expanding Vanguard trailer availability into Tennessee, Alabama, and Georgia allows the company to better support fleets and owner-operators across one of the most dynamic freight regions in the U.S.

By combining Vanguard’s proven trailer engineering with Velocity’s growing service network, the company is helping customers access reliable equipment faster while supporting the continued growth of transportation and logistics across the Southeast.

Customers in the region will now have access to a range of Vanguard dry van and refrigerated trailer configurations, including the latest 2026 and 2027 VXP dry van trailers and R8000B refrigerated trailers.
These models are engineered for durability and operational efficiency in demanding freight environments and can be configured with a range of specifications to meet fleet requirements.

Available configurations include trailers equipped with Thermo King S-600 refrigeration units, stainless steel swing doors, Hendrickson suspension systems, and multiple logistics post configurations, allowing operators to align trailer specifications with their freight applications.

Velocity Trailer Centers now offers Vanguard trailers across an expanded North American footprint, including Canada: British Columbia; United States: California, Nevada, Oregon, Utah, Washington, Georgia, Alabama, and Tennessee; Mexico: Nationwide coverage.

This growing network enables Velocity Trailer Centers to support fleets operating across key freight corridors throughout North America, providing consistent trailer availability and knowledgeable sales support across multiple regions.
The Southeast expansion will be supported by dedicated regional representatives who will assist customers with trailer specifications, inventory availability, and factory production orders.

links.context: Parent company website for more information

source_title: Velocity Trailer Centers Expands Vanguard Trailer Availability | Supply Post - Canada's #1 Heavy Construction Equipment & Commercial Truck Newspaper

facts: Vanguard National Trailer Corp. is a subsidiary of the CIMC Vehicle Group.
CIMC Vehicle Group is the world’s leading manufacturer of commercial transport dry and refrigerated container and trailer equipment with subsidiaries across the globe.
Vanguard has grown to be a premier manufacturer of dry and refrigerated semi-trailers for the North American market.
Vanguard offers superior corrosion protection, high strength and long-life specifications and advanced safety components as standard features on every trailer.
The company serves the Automotive and Transportation industries.
Available equipment includes container chassis, converter dollies, dry freight van trailers, flatbed trailers, and refrigerated trailers.
Container chassis is listed under NAPCS Code 671150101.
Converter dollies are listed under NAPCS Code 671150101.
Dry freight van trailers are listed under NAPCS Code 31101010101.
Flatbed trailers are listed under NAPCS Code 31101010101.
Refrigerated trailers are listed under NAPCS Code 31101010101.
Vanguard National Trailer Corp. is located at 289 Water Tower Dr, Monon, IN 47959 in White County.
Jim Wilkerson holds the title of Director, Manufacturing.
Craig Czupka holds the title of Director, Purchasing & General Service Parts Manager.
source_title: The Supply Chain Marketplace

facts: Vanguard National Trailer Corp. has reduced its operations from two shifts to one.
Vanguard National Trailer Corp. did not cut workers during the shift reduction.
Vanguard National Trailer Corp. joined other truck-trailer makers responding to a slowdown in the market.
Vanguard National is a unit of China International Marine Containers Co. Ltd.
Vanguard National's operations are located in the northwestern Indiana city of Monon.
Wabash National Corp. of Lafayette cut 150 workers due to a decline in orders.
The decline in orders is spreading across the trailer market.
Great Dane Trailers of Savannah, Ga., laid off about 260 workers in the past month.
Great Dane Trailers' layoffs occurred at plants in Brazil and Terre Haute.
source_title: Slowing orders hit another trailer maker – Indianapolis Business Journal

facts: Companies from China and Taiwan formed the biggest foreign contingent at the Mid-America Trucking Show in Louisville, Ky., for the second year in a row to enter the U.S. market.

Some Chinese and Taiwanese companies expressed disappointment that their booths attracted fewer visitors than expected, citing the recession or their location in the Kentucky Exhibition Center as possible causes.
Most Chinese exhibitors had small booths in the exhibit hall's vast West Wing because none came to sell major components or complete vehicles.
Double Coin Tires is an option on International Trucks and displayed in the main halls.
CIMC Vehicles Group Co., owner of Vanguard National Trailer Corp., displayed in the main halls where most major U.S. truck industry suppliers were located.

Peipei Chang, export manager of Cheetah Automotive Products Co. Ltd. in Taichung, Taiwan, attended her company's first year at the show and expressed interest in the U.S. market despite lower-than-expected traffic.
Yuhui Liu, project manager for truck manufacturer China FAW Group Import and Export Corp., attended MATS for the first time to find distributors and enter the U.S. truck market.
FAW Group Corp. is a state-owned company that says it is the largest truck manufacturer in China.
FAW sold 1.2 million vehicles in China in the first nine months of 2008, including 142,388 medium- and heavy-duty trucks, which was up 15% from 2007.
FAW sells only to distributors or manufacturers, so its representatives went to other sections of the show to talk directly to exhibitors in their target market.
Toby Young, president of Exhibit Management Associates Inc., organizes MATS and noted that companies are located according to how they are booked.
Different Chinese agencies ordered blocks of booths and asked that they be grouped together.
Fifty-four companies from China and Taiwan were at the show this year, making them the largest foreign contingent, while Canada had the next largest with 52 companies.

Cherrie Cai, export manager for privately owned Fuhe Automobile Industrial Fittings Co. Ltd. in Foshan, China, stated her company makes trailer axles and is looking for a way to open up a path to the American market.

Fuhe representatives first heard about MATS three years ago, studied truck shows for two years before deciding to attend, and thought it was unfortunate to attend during a difficult economy where not many businesses are seeking new products.

Kim Tsai, president of Mobiletron Electronics Co., stated his company is based in Taiwan with a manufacturing plant in China, makes spare parts for trucks, tire pressure monitoring systems, and side-view mirrors, and employs 1,500 people.
Mobiletron Electronics currently sells mainly to Taiwan and China but wants to sell to the U.S. market and needs distributors.
Frith Zhou, sales manager for Yuhua Industrial Group in Ningbo, China, stated his company produces a wide variety of metal parts and joints for truck engines and frames.
Yuhua Industrial Group already supplies original equipment manufacturers of trucks in Europe and wants to enter the U.S., attending its first time at MATS to find a business partner to supply.
Zhou noted there were a lot of people attending the show but not a single buyer, though he made contacts by visiting other parts of the show.
source_title: Foreign Firms Use MATS as Way to Enter U.S. Market - TT

facts: In 1935, Vernay founder Sergius Vernet invented the wax expansion element that revolutionized the automotive thermostat.
The original wax element thermostat, created more than seven decades ago, is still used in every car on the road.
Vernay has developed into a trusted innovative fluid solutions provider to leading OEMs and emerging companies in the Automotive, Medical, Mobility, Specialty, Printer and Small Engine industries.
Vernay engineers and chemists are recognized throughout the industry for their tenacious problem-solving drive and for providing co-designed, custom fluid control solutions.
Vernay continues to expand the capabilities of its in-house, world-class R&D laboratories in the U.S., Europe, and Asia to develop proprietary material formulations and designs for customers.
source_title: Innovative Flow Solutions - Vernay

facts: Rivian is a world-class electric sport utility vehicle and pickup company.
Rivian was named the 2022 MotorTrend Truck of the Year.
Rivian selected Georgia for a nearly 13 million square foot plant for vehicle and battery operations along Interstate 20 near Social Circle.
The project brings an estimated 7,500 jobs and $5 billion investment to Georgia.
Georgia Power joined with Governor Brian Kemp and the Georgia Department of Economic Development to welcome Rivian to the state.

The Electric Mobility Innovation Alliance (EMIA) was launched in July by Governor Kemp to unite public/private leaders, researchers, and utilities for EV industry recruitment and workforce development.
Chris Womack serves as president, chairman and CEO of Georgia Power.
Georgia currently has a network of nearly 1,000 public charging stations from multiple partners available across the state.
Georgia Power supports the EV needs of several OEMs in the Southeast region including Kia, Mercedes-Benz, BMW, and Volkswagen.

Other EV industry leaders that have moved to or expanded in Georgia include battery producer SK Innovation and EnChem; modules and storage systems producer Duckyang; charging station leader Heliox; and all-electric bus manufacturer Blue Bird.
Georgia is home to more than 200 automotive-related facilities that contribute more than $3 billion annually to the state's economy.
In 2021, Georgia had 977,000 workers supporting its automotive operations and facilities.
Before Rivian's announcement, Georgia's EV and battery production industry created 5,700 new jobs and $3 billion in new capital investment.
Georgia ranks sixth in the nation for EV charging infrastructure and is currently the top state in the Southeast for EV customer adoption.

Georgia Power partnered with the University of Georgia's College of Engineering to create an e-Mobility Certificate program covering engineering, public policy, public health, and business acumen to prepare the workforce for electrified transportation.
Georgia Power is working closely with design students at Georgia Tech to evaluate the environmental benefits and future workforce opportunities of transportation electrification.
Georgia Power joined Southern Company and utilities across the South, Midwest, Gulf Coast, Central Plains, and mid-Atlantic to enable seamless EV travel through a coordinated charging network.
The Electric Highway Coalition will provide increased charging options along major highway routes from Texas to Washington, DC.
Georgia Power is the largest electric subsidiary of Southern Company (NYSE: SO) and serves 2.6 million customers in all but four of Georgia's 159 counties.
Georgia Power maintains a generation mix that includes nuclear, coal, natural gas, solar, hydroelectric, and wind energy.
source_title: Georgia Power welcomes Rivian to Georgia PR Newswire

facts: Georgia was named the No. 1 state for business by Area Development magazine, marking its ninth consecutive year receiving this recognition and making it the only state to do so.
Georgia earned the top ranking in seven categories from Area Development magazine, including for its Quick Start workforce development program.
Chicago-based NewCold Advanced Cold Storage Logistics is investing $333 million to build a temperature-controlled warehousing and distribution facility in Henry County.
The NewCold Advanced Cold Storage Logistics investment in Henry County is the largest made by a single business in the county’s history and will create 170 new jobs.
Belgium-based Solvay Specialty Polymers received a $178 million federal grant to build a companion facility to its existing plant in Augusta.
The new Solvay facility in Augusta will produce battery components for electric vehicles.
The Solvay Augusta project will create up to 500 construction jobs and 100 manufacturing jobs.
New York-based Wheels Up Experience Inc., one of the largest private aviation companies in the country, is building a 34,000-square-foot operation center in Atlanta.
The Wheels Up facility will bring 350 jobs to Atlanta.
The Wheels Up facility is scheduled to open by spring 2023.
Iowa-based animal proteins producer Essentia Protein Solutions is investing $100 million to open a broth factory in Dalton.
The Essentia Protein Solutions facility will create 80 jobs.
Clean Space recently opened a 60,000-square-foot manufacturing plant and warehouse in Flowery Branch.
The Philadelphia-based Clean Space specializes in climate and humidity control for products like pharmaceuticals and electronics.
Clean Space will initially employ 25 people at its Flowery Branch facility.
Convenience store and gas station chain Wawa will open its first location in the state in 2024, with up to 20 more stores planned.
Taiwanese EV software and charging-station company Noodoe recently broke ground on several charging stations for the city of Peachtree Corners.
The Noodoe charging station project is the result of Peachtree Corners’ Curiosity Lab innovation center’s partnership with Taiwanese incubator Startup Terrace.
source_title: Economic Development Around The State - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: Vista Metals completed the construction of a seventh aluminum casthouse at its facility in Adairsville, Georgia.
This milestone represents the second phase of the company’s ongoing $100 million long-range capital plan.
The new Georgia casthouse includes the installation of melting furnaces, homogenizing ovens, and support equipment.
President Kelly Thomas stated that Vista’s investment will further support growth in core markets including outer space, commercial aviation, defense, and industrial applications.
The company is completing final equipment installation in a new world-class finishing facility, which is a critical part of this expansion phase.
Vista Metals announced a $60 million investment in a new location to serve the aerospace industry with specialty aluminum products.
The new Kentucky facility will be located in Bowling Green, Kentucky, and expands upon existing capabilities in California and Georgia.
CEO Andy Primack stated the company plans to bring over 50 new jobs to the Bowling Green area.

Bowling Green is described as a growing city with a strong industrial business base, a proven track record of attracting qualified manufacturing talent, close interaction between business and education, and a long-term community development plan.
Initial site preparation is now underway at the Kentucky location.
Vista Metals will commence a phased build-out to support growth in its core markets.
The new Kentucky site will primarily support downstream processing and value-added products serving aerospace extrusion, forging, and rolling markets.
In addition to commercial aviation, the Kentucky location will support defense, automotive, and general industrial applications.
President Kelly Thomas stated that the opening of the Kentucky facility underscores a commitment to the aerospace industry and delivering robust supply chains.
OEMs are looking to more than double commercial aircraft build rates over the next two decades.
The company intends to invest in a manner that enables customers to test and qualify the facility ahead of the projected growth surge.
Ongoing investments in people, processes, and equipment at existing facilities will continue alongside the expansion.
source_title: Vista Metals Completes Aluminum Casthouse Expansion in Georgia - Light Metal Age Magazine

facts: Jack Lifton, Co-Chair of the Critical Minerals Institute (CMI), states that governments can subsidize mines but OEMs decide where materials are sourced, which technologies are adopted, who earns long-term contracts, and ultimately which critical minerals projects survive.

Successful OEMs—including automobile manufacturers, aerospace companies, defense contractors, and industrial motor producers—decide supply chain viability by asking who can reliably supply qualified materials at globally competitive prices year after year, rather than searching for rare earth mines.
Federal agencies currently measure critical minerals success using political metrics such as counting permitted mines, announced processing plants, grants awarded, and billions of dollars committed.

OEMs measure supply chain success by asking if suppliers can consistently meet specifications, deliver on schedule, expand production as demand grows, remain in business for ten years, and maintain globally competitive prices without permanent government support.

The critical minerals supply chain must be built backward from the OEM rather than forward from the ore body, with customer specifications determining required alloys, metal purity, separation chemistry, concentrate requirements, and finally the type of economically viable ore body.

The current investment community process reverses industrial planning by having junior explorers discover deposits, followed by investment banker presentations, government strategic importance announcements, and consultant market studies before asking if any OEM intends to purchase the products.

The “mine-to-magnet” narrative is criticized for ignoring how OEM procurement actually works, as building every supply chain stage under one corporate roof does not align with actual purchasing decisions.

OEMs rarely qualify suppliers based on mine ownership; instead, they qualify suppliers that consistently deliver products meeting demanding technical, commercial, and logistical requirements to reduce risk.

An automotive company purchasing permanent magnets is buying manufacturing reliability rather than geology, while a defense contractor qualifying specialty alloys is purchasing confidence in consistent shipment performance.

Investors should evaluate which companies understand OEM qualification procedures, which management teams have successfully supplied industrial manufacturers before, which design businesses around customer requirements rather than government funding opportunities, and which projects can survive after subsidies disappear.

America needs more domestic customers willing to sign long-term purchase agreements based on confidence in supplier performance, as this confidence—not government grants or press releases—transforms mineral resources into industrial assets.
Every successful supply chain begins with a purchase order rather than a drill hole.
source_title: Why OEMs, Not Governments, Will Decide America's Critical Minerals Future - InvestorNews

facts: Economic development professionals with Parker Poe Adams & Bernstein LLP helped voestalpine Group develop its 17th production site in the United States.

canonical_name: voestalpine Metal Forming Division Facility (Cartersville, GA)
facts: voestalpine is a global steel-based technology and capital goods group supplying high-performance metal solutions to automotive and industrial markets.

The Georgia-based voestalpine Metal Forming Division facility in Cartersville manufactures ultra-high-strength automotive body-in-white components using precision metal forming, stamping, and assembly processes.
The operation supports just-in-time delivery to automotive OEMs.
The facility operates as a Tier 1 manufacturing environment focused on metal processing, operational performance, and manufacturing systems.
Visitors observe roll forming, stamping, press operations, and die handling in action as high-strength steels are transformed into automotive structural components.
Embedded quality checkpoints, measurement systems, and inspection protocols are used to validate parts against tight automotive specifications.
Material flow, inventory staging, and takt-based work sequences are structured to support continuous throughput with minimal non-value-added time.

The event targets Continuous Improvement/Lean Leaders, Operations/Plant Leadership, Manufacturing Engineers, Quality and Production Leaders, Supply Chain/Material Flow Analysts, and Metals Processing and Industrial Engineering Professionals.
Practical examples of standardized work in a mixed-model press and forming environment are demonstrated as lean takeaways.
Visual controls and error proofing are utilized as part of quality assurance routines.
Flow sequencing is tied to production scheduling and Just-In-Time delivery.
Techniques for integrating inspection into cell-level work without disrupting throughput are showcased.

The tour agenda schedules registration and networking from 10:00–10:20 am, a host presentation and safety briefing from 10:20–10:40 am, the plant tour from 10:40–11:40 am, and a wrap-up from 11:40 am–12:00 pm.
Attendance is limited to one ticket for non-manufacturing companies and two tickets for manufacturing companies.
Steel closed-toed/flat-bottom shoes are mandatory, with high heels prohibited.
Long pants only are required for attendees.
Photos are not allowed during the tour.
Lunch was sponsored by pct Janitorial.

GDEcD Commissioner Chris Cummiskey stated that the project is a significant win for Georgia's logistics network, workforce, and economic development partners.

facts: Volvo Cars is increasing its financial investment into its South Carolina manufacturing operation, signaling confidence in American-built vehicles and growing U.S. demand for luxury SUVs.
The automaker has already invested $1.3 billion into its Ridgeville plant outside Charleston over the past decade.
Volvo plans to continue funding the facility to reach “full utilization” in the coming years.
The South Carolina facility currently has an annual production capacity of 150,000 vehicles.
The Ridgeville plant currently manufactures the all-electric EX90 SUV and the Polestar 3 for both domestic and export markets.
Volvo announced it will begin producing its best-selling XC60 SUV at the South Carolina facility starting in late 2026.
Volvo sold 27,000 XC60s through the first eight months of 2025.
The 2025 XC60 sales figure represents a nearly 20% increase over the same period in 2024.
CEO Håkan Samuelsson stated that Volvo’s investment plans reinforce its long-term commitment to the U.S. market and South Carolina manufacturing operations.
The company is celebrating 70 years of selling cars in America, with over five million vehicles delivered to U.S. customers to date.
Volvo plans to introduce a brand-new hybrid model to the South Carolina production line before 2030, specifically designed to meet American customer preferences.

The expansion aligns with Volvo’s broader strategy of manufacturing vehicles closer to their sales markets in response to the new U.S. tariff environment.
Luis Rezende, Volvo’s president of Americas, stated that the Charleston plant is foundational to the company’s strategic growth plan for the U.S.
Luis Rezende added that adding another model to local production will take a significant step toward realizing the full potential of Volvo’s local manufacturing investments in South Carolina.

facts: Volvo Cars said it will cut approximately 125 jobs, or 5% of the workforce, at its Charleston, South Carolina factory.
The job cuts are being made as the company adjusts to shifting market conditions and evolving trade policies.
The announcement was made on Wednesday as part of a broader effort to realign operations and sharpen its U.S. strategy.
The production changes affect Volvo’s only American plant.
The Charleston facility currently employs about 2,500 workers.
The facility builds the EX90 electric SUV and Polestar’s Model 3.
The plant has a capacity to produce 150,000 vehicles annually.
Only 1,316 EX90s have been sold in the U.S. so far in 2024.
Volvo is majority-owned by China’s Geely Holding.
Volvo emphasized its long-term commitment to U.S. manufacturing.
Volvo remains on track to create 4,000 jobs in South Carolina.
Volvo plans to expand output at the Charleston site in the future.
The Charleston job cuts are separate from broader global cost-saving measures announced last week in Volvo's Q1 earnings report.
The global cost-saving plan includes a plan to reduce expenses by 18 billion Swedish crowns, approximately $1.88 billion.
Volvo has not specified when it will share more details about the job cuts or which specific roles will be affected.
Volvo has nearly 43,000 employees worldwide, with 29,000 in Europe, 10,000 in Asia, and 3,000 in the Americas.
Volvo says the U.S. remains a core market for the company.
Volvo reiterated that its focus remains on strengthening its American product lineup and manufacturing footprint.
The Volvo S60 will end production in the United States at the end of June.
The S60 is currently built at Volvo's factory in Ridgeville, South Carolina.
The new 2025 Volvo EX90 SUV is also now being built at the same Ridgeville, South Carolina factory.
While Volvo will continue to build the sedan at its Daqing factory in China, a spokesperson told Car and Driver there are no plans to import S60s into the U.S.
After this month, the number of sedans sold in the U.S. will decrease by one, as the Volvo S60 is slated to end production at the end of June.

While the S60 lineup will soon be dead in the U.S., it will continue to be sold in some other markets because Volvo's sedan is also built at the company's Daqing factory in China.
A spokesperson told Car and Driver that there currently aren't any plans to import S60s from China into the U.S.
The S60 is still being produced currently as a MY25 vehicle and is available at Volvo retailers across the country.
Customers interested in the S60 are encouraged to reach out to their local retailer.
The current generation of the S60 debuted for the 2019 model year, and its exterior and interior designs are virtually unchanged since then.
For 2025, the lineup offers a nonhybrid model that features a 247-hp turbo four.

facts: Geely-owned Volvo received a waiver for America’s connected car rule.

The US Department of Commerce finalized a rule under the waning days of the Biden Administration to prohibit the sale and import of connected vehicle hardware, software systems, and completed connected vehicles from China and Russia.
The United States justified the rule under national security concerns regarding China’s cyber espionage and intrusion operations threatening US critical infrastructure and public safety.
The White House stated the rule would prohibit the import or sale of certain connected vehicle systems designed, developed, manufactured, or supplied by entities with ties to the PRC or Russia.
Restricted connectivity systems and components include Bluetooth, cellular, satellite, and Wi-Fi modules as well as automated driving systems.
Software restrictions under the rule were set to go into effect for the 2027 model year.
Hardware restrictions under the rule were set to follow for the 2030 model year.

Volvo was granted a "specific authorization" from the Office of Information and Communications Technology and Services that acts as a waiver preventing their vehicles and technology from being banned under the Securing the Information and Communications Technology and Services Supply Chain: Connected Vehicles rule.
The waiver decision followed constructive discussions with the US Department of Commerce and other US officials regarding Volvo Cars’ governance, technology, and data security.
The automaker is owned by Geely and has faced testy relations between Beijing and Washington.
Volvo stated the authorization enables them to continue their growth plans in the United States.
America is one of Volvo's largest markets.
The brand's massive US plant employs over 2,000 people.
The brand's massive US plant has received over $1.3 billion of investment.
The Charleston, South Carolina plant started production in 2017.
The Charleston, South Carolina plant currently builds the Volvo EX90 and Polestar 3.
Two more vehicles will be built at the Charleston plant by 2030.
The XC60 is scheduled to roll off the assembly line starting later this year (2026).

facts: Volvo Cars announced plans to produce a new hybrid model in the US.
The automaker aims to add a next-generation hybrid model to the production line at its Ridgeville, South Carolina plant near Charleston before 2030.
Luis Rezende, president of Volvo Cars Americas, stated that the Charleston plant is foundational to the company's strategic growth plan for the US.

Rezende added that adding another model will take a big step toward realizing the full potential of local manufacturing investments and workforce, accelerating progress toward ambitious commercial targets in the coming years.
Like other global automakers, Volvo Cars has been scrambling to rearrange its supply chain to avoid the brunt of President Trump’s tariffs.
The White House struck a trade deal with the 27-nation European Union in July that will slash tariffs on foreign cars to 15%.
In the meantime, imported vehicles are facing a 27.5% duty.
After Trump unveiled sweeping tariffs in April, Volvo Cars announced plans to ramp up its vehicle production in the US.
In July, Volvo said it will start producing its popular XC60 mid-size SUV at the South Carolina plant starting late 2026.
Currently, the Ridgeville factory only produces Volvo’s fully electric EX90 SUV and the luxury electric Polestar 3.
Volvo emerged as a major name in the EV industry with plans to phase out all non-electric models by 2030.
The company reversed course on its phase-out plan last year and pledged to keep hybrid models among its lineup.

facts: Volvo Cars will begin producing a new hybrid model in the United States by the end of this decade.
The new model is designed specifically for the U.S. market.
Details of the new hybrid model have yet to be released.
The new model will help Volvo boost capacity utilization at its South Carolina plant.
Volvo Cars CEO Hakan Samuelsson stated: "It will take time until America is electric everywhere."
Samuelsson described the larger hybrid model as "more an electric car, but with a Plan B. When the battery is flat, you have a combustion engine kicking in, but normally you’d drive it electric."
Auto makers worldwide are scrambling to rethink production strategies to contend with President Trump’s duties on foreign cars.
Volvo Cars’ CEO said in April that the company would produce more vehicles in the U.S.
The group announced in July that it plans to start producing its popular XC60 mid-size SUV at the South Carolina plant from late 2026.
The South Carolina factory currently builds only Volvo’s fully electric EX90 SUV and the Polestar 3.
Production data shows these current models take up only a small fraction of the site’s annual capacity of 150,000 vehicles.
News of the new hybrid model coincided with Volvo’s 70th anniversary in the United States.
Volvo was long positioned as an EV trailblazer with the aim of phasing out all non-electric models by 2030.
Last year, Volvo reversed course and said hybrids would remain part of its lineup.

Luis Rezende, president of Volvo Cars’ Americas operation, stated: "By adding another model into production here, we’ll take a big step toward realising the full potential of our local manufacturing investments and workforce."
A trade deal struck in July with the European Union cut U.S. tariffs on European cars to 15%.
The EU trade agreement has yet to take effect, with vehicles continuing to face a 27.5% levy.
Samuelsson said the 10% duties eliminated on auto imports from the U.S. into Europe will make selling its U.S.-produced EX90 vehicle abroad more attractive.
Samuelsson stated regarding the 10% reduction: "With this 10% reduction, we have the possibility to grow."

Volvo Cars has plans to produce its XC60 mid-size SUV at the South Carolina plant by late 2026.
The South Carolina factory currently builds only Volvo's fully electric EX90 SUV and the Polestar 3.
Production data shows that the EX90 and Polestar 3 models take up only a small fraction of the site's annual capacity of 150,000 vehicles.
Volvo Cars CEO Hakan Samuelsson stated, "It will take time until America is electric everywhere."
Samuelsson described the larger hybrid model as "more an electric car, but with a Plan B," explaining that it normally drives electrically but kicks in a combustion engine when the battery is flat.
Auto makers worldwide are scrambling to rethink production strategies to contend with President Donald Trump's duties on foreign cars as part of his wider policy to boost U.S. manufacturing.
Volvo Cars announced in July that it plans to start producing its popular XC60 mid-size SUV at the South Carolina plant from late 2026.
The news coincided with Volvo's 70th anniversary in the United States.
Volvo, long positioned as an EV trailblazer with the aim of phasing out all non-electric models by 2030, reversed course last year and said hybrids would remain part of its lineup.
A trade deal struck in July with the European Union cut U.S. tariffs on European cars to 15%, but the agreement has yet to take effect, with vehicles continuing to face a 27.5% levy.

Volvo Cars Americas president Luis Rezende said adding another model into production there will be "a big step toward realising the full potential of our local manufacturing investments and workforce."
Samuelsson noted that the 10% duties eliminated on auto imports from the U.S. into Europe will make selling its U.S.-produced EX90 vehicle abroad more attractive.
Samuelsson stated regarding the tariff reduction, "With this 10% reduction, we have the possibility to grow," referring to Volvo's operations in Europe.
source_title: Volvo Cars to produce new US model amid tariff pressure — TradingView News

facts: Headquartered in Mahwah, New Jersey.
Volvo Car USA LLC (VCUSA) is a subsidiary of Volvo Car Group of Gothenburg, Sweden (Nasdaq Stockholm VOLCAR B).
Provides sales, marketing, parts, service, technology, and training support to Volvo automobile retailers in the United States since the 1950s.
Operations include a manufacturing campus in Ridgeville, SC that underlines the company’s long-term commitment to the United States as a key market.
Maintains offices in California and parts distribution and training centers across the country.
For the full year 2024, Volvo Car Group recorded a record-breaking core operating profit of SEK 27 billion.
Revenue in 2024 amounted to an all-time high of SEK 400.2 billion.
Global sales reached a record 763,389 cars in 2024.
Volvo Cars was founded in 1927 and is one of the most well-known and respected car brands in the world with sales to customers in more than 100 countries.
Listed on the Nasdaq Stockholm exchange under ticker “VOLCAR B”.
Company purpose is "For life. To give people the freedom to move in a personal, sustainable and safe way."
Ambition to become a fully electric car maker.
Committed to an ongoing reduction of its carbon footprint, with ambition to achieve net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2040.
As of December 2024, Volvo Cars employed approximately 42,600 full-time employees.
Head office, product development, marketing and administration functions are mainly located in Gothenburg, Sweden.
Production plants are located in Gothenburg, Ghent (Belgium), South Carolina (US), Chengdu, Daqing and Taizhou (China).
R&D and design centers are located in Gothenburg and Shanghai (China).
source_title: Volvo Car USA

facts: Volvo Group announced a $41 million investment in North Carolina’s Piedmont Triad region.
The investment will purchase and expand the building housing its U.S. Uptime Center.
The expanded facility will now also serve as the global headquarters for Volvo Financial Services (VFS).
Martin Weissburg, chairman of Volvo Group North America and president of Mack Trucks, described the Uptime Center as a strategic site for Volvo Group North America.
Weissburg stated the center plays a critical role in keeping customers and their vehicles on the road, delivering goods, transporting people, and servicing communities throughout the year.
The purchase and expansion of the facility reinforces Volvo Group's commitment to the great communities and residents of the Piedmont Triad.
Volvo Group’s investment includes the acquisition of the Uptime Center building, which opened in 2014 and was previously leased by the company.
The investment also funds a 62,000-square-foot expansion of the Uptime Center to house Volvo Financial Services’ global headquarters.
VFS is the captive finance arm of the Volvo Group.
VFS provides financial services for customers of Mack Trucks, Volvo Trucks, Volvo Construction Equipment, Nova buses, Prevost coaches, and Volvo buses in North America.
VFS also provides financial services for other Volvo Group brands around the world.
VFS operates in 50 countries globally with approximately 1,700 colleagues worldwide.
source_title: Volvo Group Invests $41 Million in North Carolina - TT

entity_name: Volvo Group North America’s Hagerstown powertrain facility
facts: Volvo Group North America’s Hagerstown, Maryland powertrain facility was recently toured by the U.S. Department of Energy’s Better Buildings Better Plants Summit.
Pierre Jenny, vice president and plant manager of the Hagerstown facility, stated that the DOE visit provided an opportunity to spotlight the plant's efficiencies.
The company retrofitted crankshaft grinders with Computer Numerically Controlled grinding machines, replacing 13 machines with three.
The new energy-efficient machines process more crankshafts per day while reducing electricity consumption by 56%.
The new machines reduce water consumption by 53% and coolant chemicals by 60%.
The new grinding machines generate less waste, enabling the facility to divert 68 tons from the landfill.
The project was completed in 2014 as part of the ISO 50001 EnMS and SEP certification.
Achieved savings from the project helped the facility achieve platinum level Superior Energy Performance certification.
The company met its first Better Buildings, Better Plants goal of a 25% reduction in energy consumption at U.S. facilities five years ahead of schedule.
The company set a new goal of achieving 25% more savings by 2024.
In the first four years of the new challenge, the Volvo Group reduced energy consumption by 17.5% compared with a 2014 baseline.
Three of the company’s manufacturing sites—Lehigh Valley Operations, New River Valley, and Hagerstown—are platinum-level partners in the DOE’s Superior Energy Performance program.
source_title: Volvo’s Maryland Facility Highlighted by DOE for Efficiency | Heavy Duty Trucking

facts: Volvo Trucks North America has officially started series production of the all-new Volvo VNL, a completely reimagined Class 8 truck.
The all-new Volvo VNL features sleek aerodynamics and striking visual details.
The New River Valley Assembly Plant in Dublin, Virginia is Volvo Group’s largest assembly facility in the world.
The New River Valley Assembly Plant sits on 566 acres.
The plant has over 2.3 million square feet of space.
The commercial launch of the all-new Volvo VNL serves as a christening of the state-of-the-art cab plant.
The cab plant launch is part of a $400 million dollar investment by the Volvo Group in North America.
Peter Voorhoeve, president of Volvo Trucks North America, expressed excitement about rolling out the all-new VNL to dealer partners and customers.
The truck combines state-of-the-art technology with unparalleled fuel efficiency, driver comfort, and safety.
Throughout the summer, more than 1,800 customers, dealers, and sales staff participated in immersive events at the Volvo Trucks Customer Center.
These events provided hands-on experience with the all-new Volvo VNL.
The truck brings industry-first packaging to simplify the spec’ing process for customers and dealers.
Dealers’ service representatives have access to extensive competency development through e-learning and in-person, instructor-led courses from the Volvo Trucks Academy.
Over 400 all-new Volvo VNLs will start arriving at dealership locations across North America with the series production start.
Customers will have opportunities to get behind the wheel for a test drive.
The all-new Volvo VNL features four exterior and interior trim levels: Core, Edge, Edge Black, and Ultimate.
The truck offers six cab configurations designed to fit customers’ preferences, brand identity, and operational needs.
The initial 400 trucks will include both sleepers and day cab models.
The initial delivery includes a variety of packages, trim levels, and powertrain offerings.
The all-new Volvo VNL boasts advanced aerodynamics for improved fuel efficiency.
It includes safety technologies like collision avoidance systems.
It features best-in-class connectivity solutions.
Designed with superior driver comfort in mind, the truck features ergonomic seating, advanced climate control, and noise reduction features.
Volvo Trucks North America is headquartered in Greensboro, North Carolina.
The company operates a network of nearly 400 authorized dealers across North America.
The 24/7 Volvo Trucks Uptime Center delivers the Uptime Services commitment.
Every Volvo truck is assembled in the Volvo Trucks New River Valley manufacturing facility in Dublin, Virginia.
The Dublin, Virginia facility meets the ISO 9001 standard for quality.
The facility meets the ISO 14001 standard for environmental care.
The facility holds a dual ISO 50001/Superior Energy Performance certification at the platinum level for energy management.
Volvo Trucks North America provides complete transport solutions, offering a full range of diesel, alternative-fuel, and all-electric vehicles.
Volvo Trucks is part of the Volvo Group global organization.
Volvo Trucks supplies medium- and heavy-duty trucks to professional customers via a global network of dealers with 2,200 service points in about 130 countries.
Volvo trucks are assembled in 12 countries across the globe.
In 2025 approximately 120,000 Volvo trucks were delivered worldwide.
The Volvo Group also provides complete solutions for financing and service.
Volvo Trucks’ work is based on the core values of quality, safety, and environmental care.
source_title: Volvo Trucks Redefines the Trucking Industry in North America with Start of Series Production of the All-New Volvo VNL

facts: Volvo Group plans to lay off as many as 800 workers at three US facilities over the next three months due to market uncertainty and demand concerns in the face of President Donald Trump's tariffs.

Volvo Group North America told employees it plans to lay off 550-800 people across three US facilities: its Mack Trucks site in Macungie, Pennsylvania; two Volvo Group facilities in Dublin, Virginia; and a facility in Hagerstown, Maryland.
The company, part of Sweden's AB Volvo, employs nearly 20,000 people in North America.
President Donald Trump has upended the global trading system that has been in place for over 75 years with a plan for tariffs on products from across the world.
Trump's vacillating trade policy has undermined consumer and business confidence and caused economists to raise their forecasts for a U.S. recession.

Volvo Group's layoffs are the latest response from the car and truck industry that is reeling from the Republican president's tariffs on certain parts, which are expected to increase the cost of manufacturing vehicles.

A spokesperson for Volvo Group North America stated that heavy-duty truck orders continue to be negatively affected by market uncertainty about freight rates and demand, possible regulatory changes, and the impact of tariffs.
A spokesperson for Volvo Group North America stated, "We regret having to take this action, but we need to align production with reduced demand for our vehicles."
source_title: Trump tariffs trigger more layoffs, Volvo Group to fire 800 workers over market uncertainty | Today News

facts: The Volvo Group will invest $37.7 million to open a new 115,000-square-foot, state-of-the-art Regional Distribution Center (RDC) in Tacoma, Washington.
The facility is currently undergoing renovations and is expected to open in the first quarter of 2027.
The new facility will serve as the primary RDC for approximately 240 dealers.
Centralizing order distribution at the new facility is expected to help the carmaker significantly reduce its CO2 emissions.

Peter Andell, vice president of service market logistics for Volvo Group North America, stated that the facility represents a significant expansion to the Volvo Group’s parts distribution capabilities, allowing them to reduce transportation lead times and improve reliability, especially for dealers in more remote areas of Western Canada.
The new facility is located at 7323 Valley Ave. E. and will support several brands.
The property is situated near the Port of Tacoma and is approximately 30 miles from the Port of Seattle.
source_title: Volvo to Open $38M Distribution Hub in Washington - Connect CRE

facts: Sweden's Volvo Group is proceeding with its planned $700m heavy-truck factory in Monterrey, Mexico.
CEO Martin Lundstedt confirmed the plan despite President-elect Donald Trump threatening to impose US tariffs on imports of vehicles and other goods.
The maker of Volvo and Mack semi-trucks has targeted growth in North America as a strategic priority, with North America accounting for about 30% of company revenue.
The Monterrey plant is expected to start operations in 2026.
The factory will deliver vehicles to the US, Mexico, Canada, and Latin America.

President-elect Donald Trump plans to sign documents immediately after his January 20 inauguration to begin charging a 25% tariff on imports from Mexico and Canada unless those countries clamp down on illegal immigration and fentanyl trafficking.
The Mexico project is at an 'early stage that is continuing,' according to Lundstedt in a Reuters NEXT Newsmaker interview on the sidelines of CES 2025 in Las Vegas.
Volvo has options to use the plant for sales outside the US.
Lundstedt stated, 'This is not a replacement of our American facilities.'
Depending on US tariff outcomes, the Mexico factory may or may not send trucks to the US.
Volvo's US factories currently make 100% of the trucks it sells in the US.
The company has been investing in plants in Virginia, Maryland, and Pennsylvania to expand that capacity.

Erik Smith, a director in the automotive and industrial practice at AlixPartners, noted that Volvo, Daimler Trucks North America, PACCAR, and TRATON supply nearly all heavy-duty trucks in North America.
Smith stated Volvo is the only one among those competitors without a footprint in Mexico, which is likely why they are looking to establish one.

Trump's tariff proposals have sent a shockwave through the auto manufacturing sector, affecting passenger car makers like BMW, Toyota, and General Motors who have invested in Mexican factories for lower labor costs and business-friendly policies.
Volvo has agreements to provide electric semi-trucks to customers including Amazon.com and Danish logistics firm DSV.
Lundstedt said Volvo expects high-volume production at a planned battery plant in Sweden to begin a couple of years later than the original target of 2029-2030.
In September, Volvo announced it would delay construction of the Swedish battery plant by one or two years, leaving uncertainty about the manufacturing start date.
Volvo wants to be at the forefront of green investments, including zero-emissions trucking, but is refining its strategy to adjust for market factors.
Lundstedt said Volvo does not want to be 'fighting all the windmills alone, like Don Quixote and Sancho Panza.'
source_title: Volvo CEO says plan for Mexico truck factory on track

facts: Volvo Group will invest $38.1 million at the New River Valley (NRV) assembly facility in Pulaski County.
All Volvo Trucks models for North America are assembled at the NRV facility.
The investment will allow Volvo to make several plant upgrades and build a 36,000-square-foot Volvo Trucks Customer Experience Center on the NRV grounds.
Volvo will add 32 new jobs at NRV.
Governor Terry McAuliffe announced that growing the advanced manufacturing industry is a key part of efforts to create jobs, diversify and build a new Virginia economy.
The NRV facility is described as the largest Volvo truck manufacturing facility in the world.
Secretary of Commerce and Trade Maurice Jones stated the announcement testifies to the great workforce and best-in-class business environment in Pulaski County and the Commonwealth.
Volvo Trucks provides complete transport solutions for professional customers, offering a full range of medium to heavy-duty trucks.
Customer support is secured via a global network of 2,200 dealers and workshops in more than 120 countries.
Volvo trucks are assembled in 16 countries across the globe.
In 2014, more than 117,000 Volvo trucks were delivered worldwide.
Volvo Trucks is part of the Volvo Group, one of the world’s leading manufacturers of trucks, buses and construction equipment, and drive systems for marine and industrial applications.
Gӧran Nyberg, president of Volvo Trucks North America, stated the investment demonstrates commitment to employees and the Pulaski County community.
The Virginia Economic Development Partnership (VEDP) worked with Pulaski County to secure the project for Virginia.
Governor McAuliffe approved a $100,000 grant from the Commonwealth’s Opportunity Fund to assist Pulaski with the project.
Governor McAuliffe approved a $2 million performance-based grant from the Virginia Investment Partnership program, an incentive available to existing Virginia companies.
The company is eligible to receive state benefits from the Virginia Enterprise Zone Program, administered by the Virginia Department of Housing and Community Development.
Additional funding and services to support employee training activities will be provided through the Virginia Jobs Investment Program, administered by VEDP.
Joe Sheffey, Chairman of the Pulaski County Board of Supervisors, expressed pride in what Volvo has accomplished over the past 40 years.
Delegate Nick Rush stated that Volvo Trucks is one of the best manufacturers in the world and its commitment testifies to employee hard work and county efforts.
Senator Ben Chafin noted the Volvo Group has been a valuable community partner in Pulaski County and the New River Valley Region.
Since the beginning of the McAuliffe administration, 471 economic development deals have been closed in Virginia with more than $7.799 billion in capital investment.
