facts: Archer Aviation Inc. is constructing a high-volume manufacturing facility in Covington, GA, adjacent to the Covington Municipal Airport.
The facility will be nearly 400,000 square feet.
Construction is on track to be completed by the end of 2024.
The first build phase, inclusive of grading and foundation work, was completed earlier this year.
Phase two, involving the erection of exterior walls and roof, is nearing completion.
Phase three (interior fit out) and phase four (equipment load in and power on) are on track to be completed in the coming months.
The facility will support Archer’s planned production of up to 650 Midnight aircraft annually in partnership with Stellantis.
This production volume would make it one of the largest manufacturing facilities by volume in the aviation industry.
Archer showcased its Midnight aircraft at Atlantic Aviation’s new terminal at DeKalb–Peachtree Municipal Airport.

Guests at the showcase included government officials from Georgia’s Office of the Governor, Georgia’s Congressional Offices, the City of Atlanta, Georgia’s State Legislature, the Federal Aviation Administration, and other local governments.
CEO and founder Adam Goldstein stated that Archer is tracking towards completing the manufacturing facility by the end of the year.
Archer's goal is to be the first in the eVTOL industry to achieve scaled manufacturing once production ramps up to 650 aircraft a year.
The Midnight aircraft is an electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) aircraft built around Archer’s proprietary electric powertrain.
The Midnight is designed for back-to-back flights of 20-50 miles with minimal charge time in between.
The Midnight travels at speeds up to 150 mph, turning hour-long ground commutes into minutes in the air.
The Midnight is 100x quieter than a helicopter at cruising altitudes and nearly inaudible from the streets below.
The Midnight features redundant systems targeting safety levels similar to commercial airliners.
The Midnight is designed to carry a pilot, up to four passengers, and carry-on luggage.
Archer is designing and developing key enabling technologies and aircraft to power the electrification of aviation for congested cities.

facts: Archer Aviation and rival Wisk Aero jointly announced an agreement to settle a yearslong trade secrets dispute on undisclosed terms.

Archer announced a $215 million investment from Stellantis, United Airlines, and ARK Investment Management.

The funding includes Boeing’s money as well as a $70 million acceleration from Stellantis, part of an exclusive manufacturing partnership signed in January.
Archer revealed the FAA has greenlit its Midnight aircraft for initial test flights.

The first delivery of Midnight aircraft to an Air Force base is on schedule under the firm’s $142 million AFWERX contract.
The announcements came just over a month before Archer and Wisk’s legal dispute was set to head to trial.
In May 2021, Wisk sued Archer for the “brazen theft” of over 50 trade secrets, alleging a former employee downloaded sensitive information before departing for Archer.
Archer escalated the dispute in August with a $1 billion countersuit for defamation, calling Wisk’s claims an “extra-judicial smear campaign.”
A motion for injunction by Wisk was denied, allowing Archer to continue developing its Maker aircraft.
Judge William Orrick III blocked Archer's attempt to dismiss the case, ruling Wisk’s allegations were plausible.
Federal prosecutors declined to charge former Wisk engineer Jing Xue in February 2022.
Archer and Wisk entered a second round of mediation in March but reached no resolution before settling.
Archer’s net loss widened significantly in Q2 as it prepares to ramp up manufacturing and flight testing.

Archer has agreements with United Airlines to fly eVTOLs in Chicago and New York City.
Archer will compete in the emerging advanced air mobility (AAM) market, flying passengers on short trips to and from airfields.

facts: Panelists at the CES trade show in Las Vegas discussed that air taxi adoption may be delayed as manufacturers ramp up production and public officials figure out regulation and planning.

Air taxi companies and government officials are shifting focus from approving eVTOL aircraft and vertiport guidelines to implementation challenges including route planning, manufacturing and distribution, and workforce development.

Archer Aviation Chief Operating Officer Tom Muniz stated that original equipment manufacturers will be supply constrained for a long time and must be thoughtful about the best markets to introduce these new products.
Advanced air mobility has the potential to transform the transportation sector because eVTOLs may allow movement of people or goods currently underserved.

Unlike electric vehicles that largely replace existing gas- and diesel-powered cars and trucks, eVTOLs could enable longer commutes to work, improve access to emergency medical services in rural areas, and directly connect urban downtowns to major airports.
Aircraft manufacturers might need to produce tens of thousands of eVTOLs but currently lack the capability, as commercial jet manufacturers make hundreds of aircraft per year.
There is not much precedent for producing tens of thousands of aircraft in aerospace manufacturing outside of World War Two.
Bob Brock, Kansas director of aviation and advanced air mobility, noted that many regions lack the supply chains, distribution networks, and other resources needed to build and deliver eVTOLs.
Brock recommended that air taxi manufacturers establish operations in locations that can help them achieve the economies of scale needed to succeed.
Auto manufacturer Stellantis announced it would help Archer Aviation produce its flagship Midnight electric air taxi.

Archer Aviation CEO Adam Goldstein explained that Archer and Stellantis began working together in 2020 on engineering projects, including ways to optimize the vehicle around high-volume manufacturing.
Goldstein likened the Archer-Stellantis relationship to Apple and Foxconn, where Archer designs the product and Stellantis manufactures it at scale.

During the ITS World Congress meeting in Los Angeles last September, local government officials, transportation researchers, and urban planning consultants said cities must start planning for air taxis to prevent them from wreaking havoc on urban transportation systems due to their complexity and need for greater coordination.
source_title: Air taxi supply shortage may slow eVTOL adoption, Archer Aviation exec says at CES | Smart Cities Dive

entity_name: Georgia Economic Development & Supply Chain Updates
facts: Georgia surpassed $60.2 billion in exports in calendar year 2025, marking the fifth consecutive year of increases and a 68.9% increase over the past decade.
Georgia goods are exported to 213 unique countries and territories, with Canada, Mexico, Singapore, Germany, and the Netherlands as top destinations.

Civilian aircraft and ancillary parts remain the state’s No. 1 export category, followed by computers, hardware and storage units; motor vehicles; network communications equipment; and medical devices.
Yamaha Motor Co. Ltd. is relocating its U.S. headquarters from California to Kennesaw, with the shift beginning in 2026 and continuing through 2028.
Yamaha produces motorized products including ATVs, motorcycles, boat engines, and personal watercraft, and already employs more than 2,300 people across several facilities throughout Georgia.
The Georgia Ports Authority opened a new $134 million inland port in Gainesville with an annual capacity of 200,000 containers.

The Gainesville inland port will feature direct rail service five days a week between Northeast Georgia and Savannah, providing an alternative to the 600-mile roundtrip truck route and reducing the number of trucks on Georgia’s highways.
Swiss company PFIFFNER Group is investing $18.5 million to open a manufacturing facility in Heard County, creating up to 60 new jobs over 10 years.
The PFIFFNER facility will be operational in 2027 and will supply instrument transformers for high-voltage substations and transformer bushings for manufacturers and utilities.
source_title: Economic Development Around the State - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: Aurubis, a German metals supplier, is investing almost $700 million in a new secondary smelter for complex recycling materials in Richmond County, Georgia.
The new facility is considered the first of its kind in North America.
The smelter will be commissioned in the second half of 2024.
An expansion to the plant is already in progress.
The expansion will increase the facility’s total processing capacity to 180,000tpa of complex recycling materials.
Aurubis aims to establish a reliable and sustainable supply chain to recover more value from recyclable materials.
The company is committing to investing more than $200,000 in the local community for scholarships and job training programs for young workers.
The facility will create long-term, secure jobs for more than 200 people.
The metals recovered from the recycling process are essential to the mobility and energy transition and contribute to the electric vehicle ecosystem.
The project aligns with Georgia’s Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance initiative.
Inge Hofkens, Aurubis multimetal recycling chief operations officer, stated that the plant is strategically important to the US industry.
source_title: Aurubis Investing Nearly $700M on US Smelter in Georgia

facts: Aurubis AG's Supervisory Board approved an additional comprehensive investment package of around €530 million.
The investment package includes accelerated expansion of a modular recycling system and doubling of processing capacity at the Aurubis Richmond site in Georgia, USA.
A second recycling module with an investment volume of around €250 million is slated to be added to the Aurubis Richmond plant currently under construction in Georgia.
An additional €90 million was approved for a capital expenditure adjustment covering infrastructure requirements and inflation for the current project.
Production at the expanded Aurubis Richmond site is scheduled to begin in 2026.
Recycling in the USA is growing by 5% every year, resulting in rapid increases in the availability of complex recycling materials.
The new expansion stage will double throughput volume from the previously planned 90,000 tons of complex recycling materials to 180,000 tons per year.
The additional plant at the Richmond site will be built in accordance with the highest environmental standards for environmentally friendly recycling of input materials.
Aurubis anticipates creating around 80 additional jobs in the region due to the expansion.
Once both recycling modules are completed, Aurubis anticipates employing around 200 people at the Richmond site.
The expansion leverages a high level of synergy and limited need for additional space by using existing infrastructure designed for expansion.
Starting in 2025/26, Aurubis anticipates additional EBITDA totaling around €90 million per year from the approved expansion phase.
The Richmond plant will benefit from attractive local electricity and natural gas prices that will positively impact cost efficiency.
Construction is already underway on the first plant at the Richmond site.
Together with the planned expansion, Aurubis anticipates total investments of €640 million for the Richmond site.
The expanded Richmond site is projected to deliver an additional contribution to EBITDA of around €170 million starting in fiscal year 2026/27.
Another part of the investment package involves securing the core business with an investment of around €190 million in the Complex Recycling Hamburg (CRH) project at the Hamburg site.
The CRH project will increase capacity to process around 30,000 more tons of recycling material and internal complex smelter intermediary products annually on a larger scale.
The CRH process was developed in-house and considerably broadens metallurgical capabilities in the Aurubis smelter network while optimizing utilized capacity for existing plants.
The CRH project will improve the processing of precious metals with an even shorter process run time.
Once the CRH project is ramped up in 2025, the company forecasts an annual earnings contribution of around €40 million.
The third approved project is the expansion of the existing photovoltaic plant at the Pirdop site in Bulgaria by an additional 14 MW.
An initial investment volume of around €12 million will be used to increase the Pirdop solar park's output from 14 MW to 24 MW.
Solar panels for future enlargement of the Pirdop solar park will also be purchased for €8 million.
The current expansion employs stationary, double-sided modules that produce more than conventional PV modules.
The expanded Pirdop solar plant is scheduled to be commissioned as early as 2024.
Once complete, the expanded solar park will generate enough electricity to power 9,000 households or a small city every year.
Compared to coal-fired power generation, the expanded solar park will save 34,000 tons of CO2 emissions per year, or over 500,000 tons for its planned 15-year operating period.
The in-house solar park expansion advances Aurubis' sustainability goals calling for a 50% reduction of Scope 1 + 2 emissions by 2030.
The three strategic projects are projected to deliver an annual earnings contribution of around €130 million (EBITDA) in total.
Aurubis plans to finance the approved projects primarily out of current cash flow with no need for a capital increase.
The Supervisory Board and Executive Board will recommend a dividend of €1.80 per share, which is the highest recommended dividend since the company's IPO.
The previous year's dividend was €1.60 per share.
Aurubis reported an outstanding 2021/22 operating EBT of €532 million, compared to a previous year adjusted figure of €381 million.
For the current fiscal year 2022/23, the company projects an operating EBT of between €400 and €500 million.
The Supervisory Board also agreed to move forward with a comprehensive modernization project of the company’s IT landscape, including migration to the S/4HANA SAP platform.
CEO Roland Harings stated that the new resolutions show the company is delivering on its “Driving Sustainable Growth” strategy and accelerating growth in recycling at its US plant.

COO Heiko Arnold noted that the CRH project retains much more value creation in-house, highlights research and development expertise, and creates a foundation for additional strategic projects at the Hamburg plant.
source_title: Aurubis adopts € 530 million investment package to accelerate growth and sustainability

facts: Aurubis Richmond in Georgia is the first recycling plant specialized in multimetal recycling in the US.
Full operations have started at Aurubis Richmond LLC in Augusta, Georgia, with more than 160 people currently at work.
Around 300 guests attended the symbolic Ribbon-Cutting Ceremony on September 20, 2024.
Guests at the ribbon-cutting included Governor of Georgia Brian Kemp and Mayor of Augusta Garnett L. Johnson.
The plant is a central building block in the Aurubis Metals for Progress: Driving Sustainable Growth company strategy.
The US was identified as an attractive growth market for recycling during the development of the company strategy.
Aurubis Richmond contributes to sustainability and supply chain security in the US economy by recovering valuable raw materials, primarily copper, which is on the Critical Materials List in the US.
After the second stage is completed, Aurubis Richmond will be able to process more than 180,000 t of e-waste and other complex recycling materials.
Inge Hofkens serves as COO Multimetal Recycling and closely supervised the project.
Aurubis Richmond is the first greenfield project in 110 years of company history.
Project Manager Hans Rosenstock emphasized the complete support from the Executive Board, Supervisory Board, and local contacts shaped the entire project.
In the first half of 2020, a small core team started developing the technical concept and defining equipment needs for supplier bids.
Site selection criteria included logistics for suppliers, a close port to link to Europe, secure energy supply at competitive prices, and availability of sufficient recycling materials.
David Schultheis serves as President & Managing Director Aurubis Richmond.
The COVID-19 lockdown presented challenges for potential site tours and establishing relations on the ground.

American decision-makers showed keen interest in sustainable industrial value creation, economic growth, job creation, educational opportunities, raw material security, and climate protection, particularly in Richmond County, Georgia.
The plans for Aurubis Richmond were presented to the Supervisory Board for approval on November 10, 2021.
The company was officially founded as Aurubis Richmond LLC (limited liability company) with framework conditions negotiated with local authorities.
Contracts were signed in Hamburg immediately after the Supervisory Board meeting, with equipment orders following a few days later in cooperation with manufacturer SMS.
Groundbreaking occurred in June 2022 for construction on a greenfield site roughly 20 km southeast of Augusta.
A completely new organization was established covering Occupational Safety, Plant Security, Finance, Purchasing, and Sustainability.
The plant offers valuable educational opportunities and scholarships to train experts of tomorrow.
A community benefit agreement was concluded with Augusta Technical College, Aiken Technical College, and the Richmond County School system for scholarships and vocational training programs.
A recruiting event held in spring attracted 350 participants.
The Supervisory Board approved the second stage in December 2022, initiating expansion planning while the first stage was under construction.
In July 2023, project and operations were separated, with Dirk Wouters from Beerse managing construction and David Schultheis overseeing the operating team.
The shredder facility and smelter furnace were commissioned along with the first deliveries of recycling materials.
US First Lady Dr. Jill Biden visited the new plant in November 2023, and the topping out was celebrated.
The plant is outfitted with cutting-edge technology complying with environmental standards set by the State of Georgia and federal authorities.
Aurubis Richmond was planned as the first zero-discharge plant, capturing, cleaning, and returning all process water and rainwater to the cycle.
By February 2024, the workforce grew from five employees to more than 100 in one year, increasing to 160 by the September ribbon-cutting.
The company fosters a company culture through in-house and external employee events, team and charity events, blood drives, local Christmas parade participation, and college events.
Aurubis CEO Dr. Toralf Haag stated the new site recovers strategically important metals for the American market, bolstering the independence of local supply chains.
Smelting operations will gradually come online in a ramp-up curve following the commissioning of equipment in Module 1.
While ramping up the plant step by step, the expansion Module 2 is being built.
The site's unique scalability allows production to adjust to market demand, with additional components that can be added for a custom fit.
The investment in Augusta amounts to around €740 million.
Aurubis is targeting becoming carbon-neutral well before 2050 and produces blister copper.
Inge Hofkens noted there is a growing market in the US for recovering valuable metals for the circular economy.
David Schultheis highlighted that megatrends like electric vehicles and sustainability are highly relevant to the team's purpose.
With the new site, Aurubis is now the largest multimetal recycling provider in the United States.
The top blown rotary converter (TBRC) is the core plant technology used for processing complex recycling materials and blister copper.
Aurubis expects an increase in electric and hybrid vehicle batteries to drive a growth market in recycling over the long term.
Aurubis developed and tested a patented process for recovering metals from black mass, which contains lithium, nickel, cobalt, and manganese from disassembled end-of-life batteries.
In its smelter network, Aurubis recovers around 95% of battery metals from black mass, surpassing EU guidelines for minimum recycling efficiencies.
A demonstration plant for testing a subprocess on an industrial scale was set up at the Hamburg site in calendar year 2024, with its main unit 50 times larger than the pilot plant.
Aurubis entered into a partnership with Talga Group Ltd., an Australian battery material and technology company, to extend Talga technology to all Aurubis graphite products.
The hydrometallurgical recycling technique for black mass is innovative and patented, using ozone to process black mass without nickel by recovering lithium first.
The hydrometallurgical process allows flexible use of raw materials, separation of graphite as an intermediate, and modularity compatible with other refining steps.
source_title: We pursue growth - Aurubis Annual Report 2023/24

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Aurubis will invest $340 million (€300 million) in a state-of-the-art recycling and secondary smelting facility for multi-metal recycling in Augusta.
The new facility will be the very first of its kind in the U.S.
The facility will create 125 jobs in the Augusta area.
Governor Kemp stated that the investment marks the single-largest investment made by a German company in the State of Georgia.
Aurubis will join more than 500 German companies with operations in Georgia.
Aurubis’ commitment to sustainable development and raw material security will play a role in building Georgia’s electric mobility value chain.
From the Augusta facility, the company plans to produce materials found in lithium-ion batteries and other electronics.
Roland Harings, CEO of Aurubis AG, stated that the company will become a forerunner for multi-metal recycling in the U.S., a role it already holds in Europe.
Aurubis will build its facility on 150 acres at Augusta Corporate Park, which is a “Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified” site.
Operations are expected to begin by the first half of 2024.
The company plans to begin hiring for positions as soon as summer 2022.
Steven Kendrick, Chairman of the Development Authority of Augusta, stated that Aurubis will build a world-class advanced manufacturing facility and bring skilled jobs to Augusta.
The Augusta Economic Development Authority stands ready to provide a foundation for helping Aurubis succeed over the next several decades.
GPA Executive Director Griff Lynch stated that the Georgia Ports Authority is playing a role in bringing jobs and investment to Georgia.
Exports produced by the Aurubis recycling operation will strengthen the state’s global position via Savannah, which is already one of the nation’s most balanced ports in terms of import-export trade.

Senior Project Manager Fernanda Kirchner represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce division on this project in partnership with the Augusta Economic Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, and Georgia Power.
Managing Director for the State of Georgia’s Europe Office Sérgio Domingues played a supporting role in attracting Aurubis to Georgia.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Aurubis will build the first multi-metal recycling plant in the United States in Richmond County.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson thanked partners in Augusta and state leadership for supporting one of the largest German investments in Georgia.
Augusta Corporate Park has completed due diligence and other specific certification requirements prior to visits by prospective businesses as a GRAD Certified park.
Receiving a GRAD certification with GDEcD is a proactive way for community planners and landowners to help catalyze economic growth and industrial development.
Thousands of Georgia jobs have been created on GRAD sites since the program’s inception in 2008.
source_title: Aurubis to Open State-of-the-Art Recycling and Copper Smelting Facility in Augusta • The Georgia Virtue

facts: Europe's biggest copper smelter Aurubis will invest around $700 million to build a smelter for complex recycling materials in North America.
The commissioning of the secondary smelter in Augusta, Georgia, is planned for the second half of 2024.
U.S. first lady Jill Biden toured the facility on Wednesday.
An expansion of the plant is already under way.
The expanded site will process 180,000 tons of complex recycling materials from 2026.
The complex recycling materials to be processed include printed circuit boards and insulated copper cable.
Aurubis COO of multimetal recycling Inge Hofkens stated the company is creating long-term, secure jobs for more than 200 people in an industry that is strategically important to the U.S.
source_title: Aurubis to invest about $700 million to build smelter in US

facts: Aurubis AG is a leading global provider of non-ferrous metals and one of the largest copper recyclers worldwide.
First Lady Jill Biden visited Aurubis Richmond's new site in Augusta (Richmond County), Georgia.
The company is investing around US$ 700 million to build the first secondary smelter for complex recycling materials in North America.
Commissioning of the secondary smelter is planned for the second half of the coming year.
An expansion of the plant is already under construction.
The expanded plant will allow Aurubis Richmond to process a total of 180,000 tons of complex recycling materials per year as of 2026.
Complex recycling materials processed include printed circuit boards and insulated copper cable.
Dr. Biden visited as part of the Biden-Harris Administration’s Investing in America agenda.
Augusta is one of five focus regions – known as Workforce Hubs – where a large number of jobs for skilled workers are to be created as a result of significant federal and private-sector investments.
Aurubis COO Multimetal Recycling Inge Hofkens stated the plant creates long-term, secure jobs for more than 200 people in an industry strategically important to the US.
The metals returned to circulation are essential to the mobility and energy transition and an important contribution to the electrical vehicle ecosystem.
This project fits perfectly with Georgia’s Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance initiative.
The Workforce Hub initiative supports Aurubis's commitment to training workers for the challenging tasks in its secondary smelter.
Aurubis has pledged a community benefit agreement with the Technical Colleges in Augusta and Aiken (South Carolina) as well as with the Richmond County School System.
As part of this agreement, Aurubis will invest more than US$ 200,000 in scholarships and job training programs for young workers over the next five years.
The company actively promotes women in the metal industry with its international Women4Metals initiative.
Georgia offers ideal conditions for tapping the great growth potential of the U.S. recycling market.
Increasing awareness for sustainability and a significant jump in collection volumes are generating roughly 6 million tons of recyclable, metal-bearing materials in the U.S. every year.
This volume is a main driver for Aurubis's investment decision.
The goal is to establish a reliable and sustainable supply chain by returning more of these recycling materials to local cycles.
Experts estimate that the recycling volume in North America will increase by an average of 5% annually in the coming years.
Georgia offers a pro-business climate, excellent infrastructure including logistics, and a stable energy supply at competitive prices.
Aurubis CEO Roland Harings emphasized support from Georgia State officials and opportunities to contribute expertise and become a multi-metal recycling pioneer in the U.S.
The Inflation Reduction Act offers great potential to accelerate Aurubis's future U.S. growth and investment, helping position the Southeast as a leading ecosystem for EV and multi-metal recycling.
Aurubis is in a strong financial position and ready to invest more in the U.S. in keeping with its 'Metals for Progress: Driving Sustainable Growth' strategy.
source_title: First Lady of the United States visits Aurubis Richmond, the first secondary smelter for complex recycling materials in North America

facts: Aurubis launched a new recycling plant in the U.S. state of Georgia last month.
The Georgia recycling plant will take until the 2026/27 fiscal year to reach full production.
Full production at the Georgia facility will yield 70,000 metric tons a year of blister copper.
Aurubis is executing a 1.7 billion euro (USD 1.9 billion) capital expenditure programme that includes the new recycling plant in the United States.
The company plans to focus on internal projects and its major capex programme for the next two years before considering further expansion through M&A or building additional recycling plants.
Aurubis CEO Toralf Haag stated the company will shun takeovers and prioritize rebuilding stakeholder trust following a scandal involving stolen copper and warnings of weaker profits next fiscal year.
Major shareholder Salzgitter holds a 29.9% stake in Aurubis, but there are no new discussions or developments regarding acquiring more shares or taking over the company.
Aurubis expects an improvement in spot treatment charges during the course of next year, though it anticipates being impacted by low treatment charges for calendar year 2025.
Despite expected low treatment charges, Aurubis will not face difficulty sourcing raw materials due to existing long-term supply deals.
source_title: Copper producer Aurubis to shun takeovers, rebuild trust, says new CEO, ETAuto

facts: Aurubis AG, one of the largest copper recyclers worldwide, announced the opening of its Aurubis Richmond (Augusta, GA) facility September 24, 2025.
The facility is the first U.S. multimetal recycling and smelter plant of its kind.
The facility is already in operation and is producing key strategic metals such as copper, nickel, tin, and precious metals for American manufacturers.
Naveed Moghadam, Commercial Director at Aurubis, stated at ReMA’s September 2025 Copper Roundtable that the company wants to be a first mover against its competition.
Moghadam stated, “We see the U.S. as a key and growing market where development is happening.”
Moghadam added, “We invested in the site because we wanted to be one of the first players to support manufacturing being reshored to North America.”
A company press release noted that the demand for strategic metals, such as copper, is rising steadily worldwide.
American industry alone currently needs about 1.8 million tons of copper per year.
Experts estimate demand will rise nearly 30% in the next five years.
As of now, the US imports about half of the copper it processes.

Multimetal recycling can close this gap quickly, and as the first and most technologically advanced secondary smelter, Aurubis Richmond reinforces the independence of American supply chains by processing complex recycling materials.
The new facility is the nation’s largest and most advanced secondary smelter and will provide metals for AI and data centers, energy infrastructure, electrification, and defense.
The company invested $800M in building the new site.
The company has added 240 high-quality industrial jobs to the local area.
ReMA Copper Roundtable participants agreed that copper is a critical material for the future and that demand is great.
A January 26, 2025 copper update noted that AI and data centers are two main drivers of copper demand.
The International Energy Agency (IEA) projects that electricity consumption from data centers is expected to double by 2026.
David Brooks, Global Head of Metals at Argus Media, said while EV sales in the U.S. increased 7.3% in 2024, consumers have been slower to adopt these vehicles than the rest of the world.
According to the IEA’s “Trends in electric car markets” report, global electric car sales exceeded 17 million in 2024, totaling 20% of new car market.
Global EV sales were 25% higher in 2024 than 2023.
Approximately 1.3 million EVs were sold in the U.S. during 2024, a 7.3 increase over the previous year and roughly 12% of global sales.
On June 30, the White House announced tariffs on all copper products, excluding copper cathodes, which the market needs.
David Brooks stated that the tariff on copper products is designed to encourage new mills in the U.S. and more domestic production.
source_title: Aurubis Opens New Smelter in Augusta, GA | K2 Castings

facts: German firm Aurubis AG announced plans to construct a new metals recycling plant in the U.S.
Aurubis AG will invest €300 million in the new facility located in Georgia.
The facility will be the first ever secondary smelter for multimetal recycling in the United States.
The plant will process circuit boards, copper cable, and other recycling materials containing metals into 35,000 t of blister copper annually.
Aurubis will further process the intermediate products into various industrial and precious metals at its European smelter sites to a great extent, but also sell them directly in the US market.
The facility will have an annual capacity of 90,000 tons of complex recycling materials.
Aurubis plans to begin construction on the plant in 2022.
Aurubis plans to commission the plant in the first half of 2024.
source_title: This Morning in Metals: Aurubis AG to invest €300M in U.S. metals recycling plant - MetalMiner

facts: Aurubis is setting a course to capitalize on megatrends through its revised strategy, "Aurubis Performance 2030. Forging resilience. Leading in multimetal."
The company is strengthening safety and plant security while prioritizing its core business and leveraging efficiencies in its smelter network.
Aurubis has a strong footprint in Europe and North America with a clear focus on responsible concentrate processing and recycling.
Electrification, energy infrastructure, defense, artificial intelligence, and data centers require high volumes of copper and other metals produced by Aurubis.
By 2035, more than 200,000 new wind turbines will have been built with up to 40 t of copper each.
Over 1,000 new hyperscale data centers are expected to require as much as 30,000 t of copper apiece for electricity and cooling by 2035.
Rising defense spending worldwide will flow into industries that rely on Aurubis's metals.
Market analysts anticipate a demand increase over the next ten years of around 26% for gold, 40% for tin, and 82% for tellurium.
Demand for copper is expected to double by 2050.
Aurubis has already commissioned key strategic investment projects, with others following in the current fiscal year and next.
The company deployed more than 75% of the around €1.7 billion planned for concrete projects by the end of September.
Aurubis is systematically developing all sites in its smelter network while continuously optimizing material streams and investing in state-of-the-art technology to maximize metal recovery.
Aurubis's new site in the United States stands out in terms of investment volume, providing unique capacities with the first secondary smelter for complex recycling materials.
The US needs about two million t of copper per year, half of which is imported, creating high dependence on foreign countries, especially South America.
Aurubis's new site in the United States is positioned to reduce this import dependence and benefit from the attractive US market.
An innovative recycling plant will be commissioned in Hamburg in 2026 to process significantly more complex recycling materials.
Aurubis has expertise in recovering 20 metals and elements, developed over nearly 160 years of handling complex raw materials.
The company aims to achieve the highest recovery rates to convert all input materials into marketable products, ensuring exceptional product quality and supply security for customers.
Aurubis's copper production has a carbon footprint more than 60% below the global average of all copper smelters.
Aurubis invested in plant facilities, processes, and know-how, including carrying out the largest scheduled maintenance shutdowns in its history at its Hamburg and Bulgaria sites.
New anode furnaces in Hamburg are hydrogen-ready for the first time and built for the hydrogen age.
The company consistently eliminates bottlenecks in production steps and uses state-of-the-art technologies such as digital twins to optimize production control and planning.

Aurubis has more than doubled the number of cameras at its Hamburg site, increased security staffing levels, intensified employee training, relies on drones with infrared cameras for perimeter security, and invested in a state-of-the-art automated sampling system.
These security measures are being rolled out to other sites.
Aurubis invests extensively in decarbonization and environmental protection, having spent more than €1 billion since 2000.
Recycled content in Aurubis's cathodes is high, at around 45%.
Nearly all of Aurubis's sites are certified by the Copper Mark.

Aurubis halted further investment in battery recycling because the market is developing much more slowly than expected with stark regional differences, despite recovery rates for valuable battery metals like lithium being at about 95%.
The company decided to concentrate its resources on processing primary and secondary materials and producing copper and other metals of the future.
Demand for copper products is good, with momentum seen in the copper premium.
Aurubis is forecasting an earnings corridor between €300 and €400 million for the fiscal year, on par with the previous year's level.
The company is targeting positive free cash flow.
Aurubis aims to continue growing strategically while allowing shareholders to participate in its success.
For the 2025/26 fiscal year, Aurubis targets a dividend payout amounting to 30% of adjusted consolidated earnings.
The company has a strong balance sheet with very low debt and an equity ratio of over 50%, which opens the door on possible acquisitions, though that is not the current focus.
source_title: A conversation with our Executive Board - Aurubis Annual Report 2024/25

facts: Hamburg-based Aurubis AG announced plans for a $347 million (300 million euro) smelter in Georgia.
The facility will hire 125 workers.
Construction on the Augusta smelter is scheduled to start next year.
The plant is expected to begin operations in 2024.
Aurubis stated that much copper is currently sent from the United States to Asia for recycling, making local recycling a sound business decision.

Aurubis CEO Roland Harings stated in a statement: "The need for modern, sustainable, local processing capacities is very strong. Today, these aren't at all sufficient to cover the high demand, so large volumes of valuable feed materials have to be exported."
Georgia Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson called it "one of the largest German investments in our state."
Company spokesperson Meino Hauschildt said the plant will recycle circuit boards, copper cable, and other materials including nickel, tin, zinc, gold, silver, and platinum.
The company expects up to 6.6 million tons (6 million metric tons) of metal-bearing materials to be collected for recycling in the U.S. in coming years.
The amount of metal-bearing materials available for recycling is expected to grow at a rate of 5% annually.
Aurubis said the smelter supports environmental goals by reducing the need for new copper to be mined and allows recovery of other materials combined with copper.
The smelter will be powered with electricity and natural gas.
The plant will be designed to the latest environmental standards with water reused on site.
Dust and air emissions from the plant will be low.
The company expects the plant to add about $93 million (80 million euros) to its profits before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization beginning in the 2025-2026 fiscal year.
Aurubis could claim state tax breaks including an income tax credit allowing it to annually deduct $4,000 per job from state income taxes.
The income tax credit is capped at up to $2.5 million over five years.
To qualify for the income tax credit, workers must make at least $28,000 a year.
The state of Georgia will pay for job training for the new hires.
Company officials expect to receive property tax abatements and aid with infrastructure for the project.
source_title: German company plans $347 million smelter in Georgia, hiring 125, ETHRWorld

facts: The State of Georgia has become a center of international business, with Atlanta and Savannah serving as primary global gateways for people and products through air, sea, and rail.
Decades of strategic decisions by state leaders are delivering generational investments that keep Georgia at the forefront of global trends and stretching into new markets.

Fifty years ago in 1973, state leadership under then-Governor Jimmy Carter began establishing Georgia’s physical presence abroad to kickstart its trajectory as a global hub for business and innovation.
Coryn Marsik serves as Director of International Investment at the Georgia Department of Economic Development.

Coryn Marsik stated that Georgia having international representation for over 50 years has helped give its economy remarkable resiliency, noting that these offices recruit diverse investments to create jobs and help companies reach new global markets.
Georgia established its first international office in Tokyo in 1973 to provide direct access to rapidly developing markets in East Asia.
Japanese companies continue to be the top international employer in Georgia to this day.
The state maintains active ties with Japan-Georgia organizations including the Japan-America Society of Georgia and the Japan External Trade Organization.
Georgia serves as a charter member in the Southeast U.S.-Japan Alliance.
The State of Georgia’s Europe Office was established shortly after the Tokyo office, with professionals serving the European market from Munich, Germany.
Germany is one of Georgia’s top investor nations and has a robust mobility industry that anchors Georgia’s mission to attract innovative technologies from Europe.
In 2020, Governor Brian Kemp and a State of Georgia delegation met with automotive companies headquartered in Germany, where electrification and alternative fuels were repeatedly discussed.
Following the 2020 meetings, the state adopted a new goal to identify opportunities at every step of the mobility and battery supply chain.
Just three years after the 2020 meetings, the mobility and battery supply chain in Georgia announced more than $25.5 billion in investments and an estimated 30,700 new jobs over the next few years.
European-based companies enhancing Georgia’s mobility ecosystem include GEDIA Automotive, Aurubis AG, and Heliox.
The success of Georgia’s mobility and clean tech industries led to representation at the 2023 World Economic Forum in Davos-Klosters, Switzerland.
Governor Brian Kemp served on a panel at the 2023 World Economic Forum, stating that one can count on stability and a great economy and business environment in Georgia.
Ellen Kraft serves as Managing Director of the Europe Office.

Ellen Kraft stated that the European market is full of opportunity for attracting innovative companies, helping Georgia businesses connect with new customers, and establishing new ties, noting that relationships are expanding with examples like the North Rhine-Westphalia (NRW) office in Atlanta.
International businesses operating in Georgia tap into university-backed research, innovative ecosystems, and solutions-oriented partnerships to stay up-to-date on business trends.
Commissioner Pat Wilson explains how Georgia is reaping the benefits of strategic leadership decisions made decades ago.

links.context: Commissioner Pat Wilson's explanation of Georgia's success

source_title: GDEcD: 50 Years of International Presence - Metro Atlanta CEO

The new facility will be the very first of its kind in the U.S.
The facility will create 125 jobs in the Augusta area.
Governor Kemp called the project the single-largest investment made by a German company in the State of Georgia.
Aurubis will join more than 500 German companies with operations in Georgia.
Aurubis' commitment to sustainable development and raw material security will play a role in building Georgia's electric mobility value chain.
From Augusta, the company plans to produce materials found in lithium-ion batteries and other electronics.

Roland Harings, CEO of Aurubis AG, stated the investment sets an example of sustainable growth and makes the company a forerunner for multi-metal recycling in the U.S., a role it already holds in Europe.
Aurubis will build its facility on 150 acres at Augusta Corporate Park.
Augusta Corporate Park is a "Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified" site.
Operations are expected to begin by the first half of 2024.
The company plans to begin hiring for positions as soon as summer 2022.
Steven Kendrick, Chairman of the Development Authority of Augusta, welcomed Aurubis and noted they will build a world-class advanced manufacturing facility bringing skilled jobs to Augusta.
The Augusta Economic Development Authority stands ready to provide a foundation for helping Aurubis succeed over the next several decades.
Griff Lynch, Executive Director of the Georgia Ports Authority (GPA), stated exports from the Aurubis recycling operation will strengthen Georgia's global position via Savannah.
Savannah is already one of the nation's most balanced ports in terms of import-export trade.
Fernanda Kirchner, Senior Project Manager for GDEcD's Global Commerce division, represented the department on this project.
The project involved partnerships with the Augusta Economic Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, and Georgia Power.
Sérgio Domingues, Managing Director for the State of Georgia's Europe Office, played a supporting role in attracting Aurubis to Georgia.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson welcomed Aurubis and noted it will be the first multi-metal recycling plant in the United States in Richmond County.
Pat Wilson thanked partners in Augusta and leadership for supporting one of the largest German investments in the state.
Augusta Corporate Park completed due diligence and specific certification requirements prior to visits by prospective businesses as part of GRAD certification.
Receiving GRAD certification with GDEcD helps catalyze economic growth and industrial development for regions served.
Thousands of Georgia jobs have been created on GRAD sites since the program's inception in 2008.
Aurubis AG is a leading global provider of non-ferrous metals and one of the largest copper recyclers worldwide.
The company processes complex metal concentrates, scrap metals, organic and inorganic metal-bearing recycling materials, and industrial residues into high-quality metals.
Aurubis produces more than 1 million tons of copper cathodes annually.
From copper cathodes, Aurubis makes wire rod, continuous cast shapes, profiles, and flat rolled products made of copper and copper alloys.
Aurubis also produces precious metals, selenium, lead, nickel, tin, and zinc.
The company's portfolio includes additional products such as sulfuric acid and iron silicate.
source_title: Georgia Chron - Georgia: Gov. Kemp: Aurubis to Open State-of-the-Art Recycling and Copper Smelting Facility in Augusta - 10148299

facts: Hyundai plans to invest more than $10 billion toward accelerating electrification and autonomous vehicle technology in the U.S. by 2025.
Hyundai has earmarked $5.5 billion for a new EV plant and battery manufacturing facility in Georgia.
Non-affiliated suppliers are contributing another $1 billion into that project, pushing the total cost to $6.5 billion.
The remaining $4.5 billion of Hyundai’s total U.S. investment will go into what the automaker has identified as “key future businesses.”
In Hyundai’s view, key future businesses translates to robotics, AI technologies, advanced air mobility and autonomous driving capability.
Hyundai said it will invest its funds through joint ventures and subsidiaries.
The investment will also help Hyundai upgrade R&D operations for its Kia and Genesis brands.
The new EV factory is the largest economic development deal recruited by Georgia, according to officials.
The 2,923-acre site is slated to break ground early next year and begin commercial production in the first half of 2025 with an annual capacity of 300,000 units.
The operations will help Hyundai toward its goal of becoming a top-three EV provider in the U.S. by 2026.
Mainly powered by renewable energy sources, the plant will use a highly connected, automated and flexible manufacturing system where robots will assist human workers.
Hyundai said the plant will produce at least some of the 23 EVs it plans to roll out by 2025.
Its battery manufacturing facility will help Hyundai establish a stable supply chain.
In 2020, Hyundai purchased from SoftBank an 80% stake in Boston Dynamics, in a deal that valued the mobile robotics firm at $1.1 billion.
Part of its $10 billion investment announced Sunday will go toward creating a robotics value chain, from component manufacturing to logistics.
Boston Dynamics has launched its first two commercial robots, Spot and Stretch, to work in a variety of industries.
Spot is being tapped for use in the power utilities, construction, manufacturing, oil and gas and mining industries.
Stretch, which is a robot arm that moves boxes, is being marketed for use in warehouse facilities and distribution centers.

The money will also support the Hyundai subsidiary New Horizons Studio, which works on Ultimate Mobility Vehicles such as the Tiger, an autonomous cargo carrier equivalent in size to a carry-on suitcase.
In 2019, Hyundai established a $4 billion joint venture with Aptiv, a global supplier developing AV technology.
Motional, the resulting AV technology company, is currently piloting autonomous driving technology on the Lyft and Via ride-share apps in Las Vegas.
Hyundai said it will “actively support Motional,” meaning the company intends to invest more money into the venture as it progresses toward commercial operations.
Motional will put Hyundai robotaxis, based on the brand’s Ioniq 5 EV, on Lyft’s network in Las Vegas next year.
The cars will come with human safety operators but should be able to navigate hotel drop-off and pickup maneuvers autonomously.
Hyundai launched Washington D.C.-based Supernal last year to figure out how to integrate air mobility into existing networks for intermodal travel.
Supernal is developing an app for customers to plan their journey using a mix of cars, trains, advanced air mobility, eVTOLs and e-scooters.
Supernal aims to begin commercial service in 2028.
source_title: Hyundai to spend $10B in US by 2025 on EVs, AVs and robotics

facts: Chamber of Progress published a new report forecasting the number and types of jobs that will be created as a result of autonomous vehicle deployment in the United States.

The report forecasts that more than 114,000 workers — and up to 455,000 workers in an optimistic scenario – will be employed over the next 15 years to meet AV production, distribution, maintenance, upgrades, and repair needs.
For every 1,000 autonomous vehicles produced and deployed annually, approximately 190 workers will be needed for manufacturing and servicing these vehicles.

The report was developed by Steer, a consultancy that combines commercial, economic, technical, and planning expertise, and Fourth Economy, a national strategy firm focused on community and economic development.
No previous studies have looked in detail at the types of jobs, wages, or location of jobs created by AVs.

The report found that AV-related jobs will be high-paying and accessible to workers without a college degree, with 82% of AV workers overall and 59% of those without a college degree earning more than the U.S. median wage.
The report examines which states are best-positioned to recruit AV workers in the future.
Chamber of Progress CEO Adam Kovacevich stated that the deployment of autonomous vehicles will mean hundreds of thousands of jobs created here in the US.
Kovacevich noted that with smart public policy, AVs are likely to bring good-paying jobs for a whole range of workers with different educational backgrounds.
He added that ultimately this sector could be an economic engine for the US if the country is able to maintain a lead in designing and producing these cars.
In a moderate economic scenario, the report anticipates deployment of 9 million autonomous vehicles over the next 15 years, with 114,000 jobs created.
In an optimistic scenario, the report forecasts deployment of 36 million AVs deployed over the next 15 years with 455,000 jobs created.
AV industry jobs typically pay above U.S. median wage, with analysis showing that 82 percent of AV workers make more than the US median wage.
AVs can support the resiliency of the US auto industry where it is strong today and create new centers for AV-related jobs in places with strong tech sectors.
To take advantage of AV economic and job opportunities, the US must encourage further technology development, increase capital investment, and establish a favorable regulatory framework.
Chamber of Progress is a center-left tech industry policy coalition promoting technology’s progressive future.
The organization works to ensure that everyone benefits from technological leaps, and that the tech industry operates responsibly and fairly.
Corporate partners do not have a vote on or veto over Chamber of Progress positions.
Chamber of Progress does not speak for individual partner companies and remains true to its stated principles even when its partners disagree.
source_title: New Study: Autonomous Vehicle Jobs to Exceed 110k in U.S.: Every additional 1000 autonomous vehicles will mean 190 jobs - Chamber of Progress

facts: AWS plans to invest at least $11 billion in Georgia to expand its infrastructure to support various cloud computing and AI technologies.
AWS estimates that its investment will create roughly 550 jobs in the state of Georgia.
AWS stated in a press release that it is proud to expand operations in Georgia to help drive the next generation of cutting-edge technologies such as AI.
AWS expressed gratitude to state and local leaders who have partnered with the company and looks forward to keeping Georgia at the leading edge of the digital age.
AWS' investment infusion comes about eight months after the company said it would pour $11 billion into data centers in Indiana.
The Indiana data center deal will create at least 1,000 jobs, according to an AWS pledge.
Data center construction in the Atlanta market increased 76% year-over-year by midyear 2024.
Georgia's cheap electricity, existing fiber-optic infrastructure, and state tax incentives are attractive to Big Tech companies.
Google, Meta, Elon Musk’s X, and Microsoft are other major data center infrastructure spenders in the region.
In 2023, Microsoft purchased hundreds of acres of land to build a data center expected to cost around $1.8 billion.
Locals oppose the data center developments, arguing they compete with housing and other more pressing real estate needs.
Last September, Atlanta’s city council banned new data centers from opening in neighborhoods near transit and the popular Beltline walking trail.
Data centers tend to have high power requirements.
Utility Georgia Power is leaning partly on fossil fuels to generate enough capacity to power current and future data centers.
Environmental advocates have criticized Georgia Power's move to rely on fossil fuels for data center power generation.
By 2028, the Atlanta metro’s data center inventory could grow to a power load of over 4,000 megawatts.
The projected 2028 power load will be more than 30 times the metro’s data center load in 2012.
The AI boom is responsible for driving many current data center expansion projects because AI requires lots of computing power to develop and run.
Goldman Sachs estimates that AI will represent about 19% of data center power demand by 2028.
Microsoft recently stated it was on track to invest $80 billion in AI data centers this year.
A McKinsey report estimates capital spending on procurement and installation of mechanical and electrical systems for data centers could eclipse $250 billion in the next five years.
source_title: AWS says it'll invest 'at least' $11B to expand data center infrastructure in Georgia | TechCrunch

facts: Unit 3 at Plant Vogtle, located southeast of Augusta, Georgia, has completed testing and entered commercial operation.
Unit 3 is the first American nuclear reactor to be built from scratch in decades.
Construction of Unit 3 was seven years late and $17 billion over budget.
At full output, Unit 3 generates 1,100 megawatts of electricity.
The power from Unit 3 can supply 500,000 homes and businesses.
Electricity from the unit is distributed to utilities in Georgia, Florida, and Alabama, as well as Southern Co. subsidiary Georgia Power's 2.7 million customers.
Chris Womack serves as CEO of Atlanta-based Southern Co.
A fourth reactor at Plant Vogtle is nearing completion.
The Nuclear Regulatory Commission approved loading radioactive fuel into Unit 4 before the end of September.
Unit 4 is scheduled to enter commercial operation by March.
The third and fourth reactors were originally budgeted at $14 billion but are now on track to cost their owners $31 billion.
Original contractor Westinghouse paid $3.7 billion to the owners to walk away from the project.
Total spending on the Vogtle project is almost $35 billion.
Construction for the third reactor began in 2009, with power generation originally slated for 2016.
Government officials and utilities are looking to nuclear power to alleviate climate change by generating electricity without burning natural gas, coal, and oil.
Most U.S. focus is currently on smaller nuclear reactors to avoid the cost and schedule overruns that plagued Vogtle.
Southern Co. does not plan to add any more reactors to its fleet.
Georgia Power owns a 45.7% share of the reactors.
Oglethorpe Power Corp., which provides electricity to member-owned cooperatives, holds a smaller ownership share.
The Municipal Electric Authority of Georgia and the city of Dalton hold smaller ownership shares.
Oglethorpe and MEAG plan to sell power to cooperatives and municipal utilities across Georgia, Jacksonville, Florida, parts of Alabama, and the Florida Panhandle.
Almost every electric customer in Georgia will pay for Vogtle through an ongoing finance charge.
Residential customers are projected to pay more than $926 apiece as part of the finance charge.
Elected public service commissioners approved a rate increase that adds $4 per month to residential bills starting as soon as the third unit begins generating power, potentially in August.
This follows a previous $16-a-month rate increase for higher fuel costs.
Experts testified that high construction costs have wiped out any future benefit from low nuclear fuel costs.
Tom Newsome, director of utility finance for the commission, testified that cost increases and schedule delays completely eliminated any life-cycle cost benefit.
Opponents argue that power generated from solar and wind would be cheaper than Vogtle.

Southern Environmental Law Center staff attorney Bob Sherrier stated that capital-intensive projects benefit Georgia Power's shareholders but are not the least-cost option for Georgians facing repeated bill increases.
Commissioners will later decide who pays for the remainder of Vogtle costs, including the fourth reactor.
Customers will pay for spending deemed prudent, while the company and its shareholders will pay for spending deemed wasteful.
Georgia Power CEO Kim Greene stated the company has not made a final determination on how much to ask customers to pay pending a prudence filing.
source_title: The first US nuclear reactor built from scratch in decades enters commercial operation in Georgia | AP News

facts: Rivian is increasing its initial production capacity to 300,000 vehicles annually for its future manufacturing facility in Stanton Springs North, Georgia.
The increased capacity represents a 50 percent increase over the initially planned capacity of 200,000 units.
The capacity boost will facilitate a lower cost per unit and provide significant room for future expansion in later phases.
Rivian is partnering with the U.S. Department of Energy’s (DOE) Office of Energy Dominance Financing (EDF) to update its DOE loan.
The updated DOE loan aims to bolster American manufacturing and construction jobs.
Vertical construction at the Georgia plant is expected to begin in spring 2026.
Rivian expects to first draw on the DOE loan by early 2027.
Vehicle production at the facility is expected to begin in late 2028.
The total DOE loan for the initial phase is aligned with the updated facility design and roadmap at up to $4.5 billion, consisting of $4,006 million principal and $494 million capitalized interest.

Preparations are underway for the development of the stamping press area, which is one of the most capital-intensive and technically demanding projects within the plant from a construction perspective.
Progress on the facility's primary buildings is expected to accelerate this summer as they begin to take shape.
Rivian recently announced a partnership with Uber.
The company is expected to build up to 50,000 robotaxis at its Georgia plant beginning in late 2028.
Rivian Founder and CEO RJ Scaringe stated the company is excited to partner with the DOE to grow its manufacturing footprint in Georgia.
RJ Scaringe noted that the R2 vehicle model dramatically expands Rivian's market opportunity.
RJ Scaringe emphasized that thousands of dedicated people will soon work in the Georgia plant and will be instrumental to Rivian's growth as it scales American manufacturing.
The project aims to ensure that the U.S. retains its leadership in innovation and technology on the global stage.
Rivian is an American automotive manufacturer and technology company that develops and manufactures electric vehicles and vertically integrated technologies and services.
Rivian vehicles are manufactured in the United States and sold directly to consumer and commercial customers.
source_title: Rivian Announces Optimized Capacity Plan for Georgia Plant | Nasdaq

entity_name: University of Michigan Electric Vehicle Center
canonical_name: University of Michigan Electric Vehicle Center
facts: In an effort to cultivate a robust electric vehicle ecosystem in the state where the modern auto industry was born, the new University of Michigan Electric Vehicle Center launched on April 27, 2023.
The center will focus on accelerating collaborative research and development.
The center will focus on developing a highly skilled workforce.
The center will focus on establishing advanced campus infrastructure and facilities to support research and education.
U-M and the state of Michigan have finalized the contract for the $130 million center.
The legislature approved funding for it in the fiscal year 2022–23 budget.
The University of Michigan is in the process of building on-campus solar installations with a capacity of 25 megawatts across the Dearborn, Flint and Ann Arbor campuses.
The university aims to have resulting solar facilities fully operational by the end of 2025.
The total amount of electricity that would be generated by the installations is estimated to equal the power consumed by approximately 3,000 homes annually.
Once operational, the solar installations will help U-M eliminate direct greenhouse gas emissions on campus by 2040.
The university also is on pace to achieve net-zero emissions from purchased electricity by 2025.
source_title: Home | 2023 Annual Report

facts: Bain Capital Private Equity completed the acquisition of Big Tex from H.I.G. Capital on December 21, 2015.
Financial terms of the private transaction were not disclosed.
Big Tex is headquartered in Mt. Pleasant, Texas.
Big Tex manufactures professional-grade open utility, equipment, horse, livestock, and cargo trailers and truck beds under brands including Big Tex, CM, S&H, and Circle J.
The company was founded in 1982 and serves dealers and customers across North America.
Curt Howell is the Chief Executive Officer of Big Tex and will continue to lead the business with the existing management team.
Todd Cook is a Managing Director at Bain Capital.
Kirkland & Ellis LLP served as legal counsel for Bain Capital Private Equity.
PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP served as accounting advisor to Bain Capital Private Equity.
Piper Jaffray acted as Big Tex’s exclusive financial advisor.
McDermott Will & Emery LLP provided legal advice to Big Tex and H.I.G. Capital.
Big Tex sells its family of trailer brands through a diversified network of over 600 dealers nationwide.
The company maintains manufacturing and assembly facilities in Texas, Oklahoma, Georgia, and Idaho.
The company’s Tex Trail division operates as a leading parts distributor with 12 locations.
Bain Capital has approximately $75 billion under management across private equity, venture capital, public equity, and credit products.
Bain Capital was founded in 1984 and employs more than 400 professionals.

Bain Capital has deep experience in industrial manufacturing and distribution, with investments in companies including TI Automotive, Sensata Technologies, FTE Automotive, Trinseo SA, ASIMCO, and Hero MotoCorp Ltd.
