facts: The Alternative Fuels Data Center provides a Medium- and Heavy-Duty Vehicle Search tool to find and compare alternative fuel vehicles, engines, and power sources.
Peterbilt 548 is available in Class 8 with CNG or LNG options, featuring Cummins L9N 8.9L Near Zero or Cummins B6.7N Near Zero power sources.
The Peterbilt 548EV electric variant offers an electric-only range of 135 to 250 miles and a battery capacity of 250 to 375 kWh.
The Peterbilt 548EV uses an automatic transmission and is powered by a PACCAR ePowertrain available in 470 HP, 540 HP, or 605 HP configurations.
According to the manufacturer, the Peterbilt 548EV has a gross combined weight rating (GCWR) of 82,000 and supports a maximum charging speed of 350 kW for up to an 80% charge in 80 minutes.
The Peterbilt 567EV electric variant offers an electric-only range of 100 to 250 miles and a battery capacity of 250 to 625 kWh.
The Peterbilt 567EV uses an automatic transmission and is powered by a PACCAR ePowertrain available in 470 HP, 540 HP, or 605 HP configurations.
According to the manufacturer, the Peterbilt 567EV has a gross combined weight rating (GCWR) of 82,000 and supports a maximum charging speed of 350 kW for up to an 80% charge in 80-120 minutes.
The Peterbilt 579EV electric variant offers an electric-only range of 200 miles and a battery capacity of 250 to 500 kWh.
The Peterbilt 579EV uses an automatic transmission and is powered by a PACCAR ePowertrain available in 470 HP, 540 HP, or 605 HP configurations.
According to the manufacturer, the Peterbilt 579EV has a gross combined weight rating (GCWR) of 82,000 and supports a maximum charging speed of 350 kW for up to an 80% charge in 90 minutes.
RAM 2500/3500 HD and RAM 3500/4500/5500 heavy-duty trucks are available with Biodiesel (B20) fuel options.
The RAM heavy-duty truck variants utilize a Cummins ISB6.7 power source paired with a RAM 8-Speed TorqueFlite HD automatic transmission.
Users can select up to four items from the search results to compare vehicle specifications side-by-side.
source_title: Alternative Fuels Data Center: Vehicle Search

entity_name: 2026 Transportation Technologies Office Annual Merit Review
canonical_name: 2026 Transportation Technologies Office Annual Merit Review
facts: The U.S. Department of Energy Transportation Technologies Office (formerly the Vehicle Technologies Office) held its 2026 Annual Merit Review (AMR) on June 1-4, 2026.
The event was held at the Hyatt Regency Crystal City hotel in Arlington, Virginia.
The purpose of the AMR is to evaluate TTO-funded projects for their contributions to DOE’s mission and goals.
During the TTO AMR, advanced vehicle technologies projects funded by TTO are presented and reviewed for their merit.
The event provides an opportunity for lab experts, industry stakeholders, and DOE staff to meet and exchange ideas and perspectives on related technologies.
Reviewers come from a variety of backgrounds, including current and former members of the vehicles industry, academia, national laboratories, and government.
Each reviewer evaluates a set of projects based on how much they contribute to or advance the Department of Energy’s missions and goals.
Reviewers consider each project’s breadth, depth, appropriateness, accomplishments, and potential.
The final reviews are described in TTO’s Annual Merit Review Results Reports.
Presentations from 2009–2025 are available in the Merit Review Presentations Database.
The 2025 TTO Annual Merit Review plenary session was held on June 2, 2025.
Invited speakers and TTO staff set the stage for the AMR technical presentations.
Reports from 2008-2025 and other subprogram reports are available on the Annual Progress Reports page.
source_title: 2026 Transportation Technologies Office Annual Merit Review | Department of Energy

facts: The U.S. Department of Transportation’s NEVI Formula Program required the Alabama Department of Transportation to submit an annually updated EV Infrastructure Deployment Plan for fiscal years 2022 through 2026 describing how the state intended to distribute NEVI funds and their physical and cybersecurity strategies, including a Community Engagement Outcomes Report.
The submitted NEVI plans must address updated NEVI Guidance.

The Alabama Department of Economic and Community Affairs offers grants for light-duty EV chargers and the replacement of qualified medium- and heavy-duty diesel vehicles with new diesel or alternative fuel vehicles.

Rebate grants are available for EV chargers; medium- and heavy-duty trucks; school, shuttle, and transit buses; freight switchers; airport ground support equipment; and forklifts and port cargo handling equipment.
Vehicles must meet model year requirements to qualify for the rebate grants.
Funding amounts for the rebates are based on vehicle type, fuel type, and applicant type.
The rebate grants are funded by Alabama’s portion of the Volkswagen Environmental Mitigation Trust.

The Alabama Department of Economic and Community Affairs administers the EV Charging Infrastructure Program allowing government and non-government entities to receive funding for installing publicly accessible direct current fast chargers and Level 2 EV chargers along non-interstate corridors.
Additional terms and conditions apply to the EV Charging Infrastructure Program grants.

The Alabama Department of Transportation is authorized to administer the Electric Transportation Infrastructure Grant Program to distribute grants for EV charging infrastructure expansion under Code of Alabama 40-12-242.
EV owners must pay an annual fee in addition to standard registration fees.
All-electric vehicle owners in Alabama must pay an annual fee of $203.
Plug-in hybrid electric vehicle owners in Alabama must pay an annual fee of $103.
Beginning in 2023, EV fees in Alabama increase by $3 every four years.
A portion of the EV fees contributes to the Electric Transportation Infrastructure Grant Program, which provides grants for EV charging stations.

The Alabama Legislature established a Green Fleets Review Committee and Green Fleets Policy outlining a procurement procedure for state vehicles based on criteria that includes fuel economy and life cycle costing.
State fleet managers must classify their vehicle inventory for compliance with the policy and submit annual plans for procuring fuel-efficient vehicles.

Annual procurement plans must reflect a 4% annual increase in average fleet fuel economy for light-duty vehicles, a 3% annual increase for medium-duty vehicles, and a 2% annual increase for heavy-duty vehicles per fiscal year.

Government entities must manage and operate their fleets in a manner that is energy efficient, minimizes emissions, and reduces petroleum dependency by using specified proven technology identified by the Committee.
An entity that owns, operates, leases, or controls electric vehicle charging stations is not defined as a public utility under Alabama Public Service Commission Docket No. 32694.

links.context: Alabama Department of Economic and Community Affairs Electric Vehicle Charging Infrastructure Program website
ADECA EV Charging Infrastructure Program website

source_title: Alternative Fuels Data Center: Electricity Laws and Incentives in Alabama

entity_name: U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) Rare Earth Elements and Critical Minerals Award Program
facts: The U.S. Department of Energy awarded $19 million for 13 projects in traditionally fossil fuel-producing communities across the country to support production of rare earth elements and critical minerals vital to battery, magnet, and clean energy component manufacturing.
The U.S. faces persistent shortages in domestic supply of these materials, forcing reliance on imports and leaving clean energy technology production at greater risk of disruption.

Secretary of Energy Jennifer M. Granholm stated fossil fuel communities can be at the forefront of the clean energy economy by producing critical minerals needed to build electric vehicles, wind turbines, and other products.

Granholm noted building clean energy products domestically secures the supply chain for innovative solutions needed to reach net-zero carbon emissions by 2050 while creating good-paying jobs across America.
The funding awards align with recommendations from the White House Interagency Working Group on Coal and Power Plant Communities and Economic Revitalization.

The working group's national roadmap aims to partner with local communities to ensure the shift to a clean energy economy creates good-paying union jobs, spurs economic revitalization, and supports workers.

U.S. Senator Joe Manchin highlighted DOE's investment in West Virginia University’s Mid-Appalachian Carbon Ore, Rare Earth and Critical Minerals Initiative to expand and transition coal resources to high-value products.
Manchin stated the project will create innovative solutions for a cleaner energy future, spur economic growth, and create good-paying jobs in West Virginia.

As Chairman of the Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee, Manchin pledged to continue advocating for funding and resources to support research projects in West Virginia and across the U.S. that help coal communities.

U.S. Senator Bob Casey commended Penn State for receiving a grant, noting its world-class research distinguishes itself in the clean energy field and will contribute to the nation’s development of clean energy.

U.S. Senator Mark Warner expressed pleasure that Virginia Tech’s Center for Coal and Energy Research received federal funding to advance research on natural resources in Virginia and discover innovative uses for them.
Warner stated the project will increase environmental sustainability, boost economic opportunity, and create jobs in the region.
U.S. Senator John Hoeven noted North Dakota provides a vast array of critical resources for the U.S.

Hoeven stated the grant supports the University of North Dakota’s Energy & Environmental Research Center’s efforts to advance production of rare-earth elements, critical minerals, and other carbon products from North Dakota lignite coal.
He emphasized this is vital for adding value to lignite coal resources, enhancing domestic production to reduce reliance on foreign imports, and supporting regional economic growth.

U.S. Senator Shelley Moore Capito stated West Virginia has a great opportunity to become a leader in rare earth element and critical mineral production using new technology to extract elements from acid mine drainage.
Capito noted efforts like this will revitalize localities while cleaning up abandoned mine sites.

She mentioned authoring language in the National Defense Authorization Act last year to encourage the Department of Defense to submit a report to Congress on the domestic supply chain and expansion of stockpiling these elements.
Capito also helped introduce legislation to improve the quality and timeliness of critical mineral production permitting on federal land.
She expressed confidence that WVU will continue to expand understanding and operations in rare earth element and critical mineral extraction.
U.S. Senator Mitt Romney stated China’s near monopoly over rare earth metals makes the rest of the world dependent on them.
Romney noted this funding allows Utah to play a vital role in U.S. production of rare earth metals and critical minerals, helping rebuild the supply chain and decrease dependence on China.
He emphasized the projects will create new jobs and spur economic growth for communities in the Uintah Basin.

U.S. Representative Morgan Griffith stated work at the Virginia Center for Coal and Energy Research has led to breakthroughs in tapping rare earth minerals integral to modern technology, used in advanced batteries to smartphones.
Griffith noted DOE’s award advances research that could support jobs in Appalachia and shore up supply chains vital to national security and economic growth.
Selected projects fall under 12 areas of interest corresponding to selected U.S. basins with potential to produce rare earth elements and critical minerals.

Appalachian Basin — North (Kentucky, Maryland, Ohio, Pennsylvania and West Virginia): Pennsylvania State University (University Park, Pennsylvania) aims to assess and catalog Northern Appalachian Basin rare earth elements and critical minerals resources and waste streams, develop strategies to recover minerals from these streams, and assess infrastructure, industries and businesses in the Northern Appalachian Basin to determine supply chain gaps. DOE Funding: $1,204,129

Appalachian Basin — Central (Kentucky, Tennessee, Virginia and West Virginia): Virginia Polytechnic Institute and State University (Blacksburg, Virginia). The Virginia Center for Coal and Energy Research at Virginia Tech plans to promote regional economic growth and foster new job creation by accelerating extraction and processing of rare earth elements and critical minerals resources from coal, coal sediments, coal ash, coal refuse and impoundments, acid mine drainage and other basin-specific resources in the Central Appalachian region. DOE Funding: $1,499,999

Appalachian Basin — South (Alabama, Georgia and Tennessee): Collaborative Composite Solutions Corporation (Knoxville, Tennessee) aims to develop and deploy new technologies for manufacturing rare earth elements, critical minerals and valuable non-fuel, carbon-based products from coal and/or coal waste in the South Appalachian Basin, thus revitalizing distressed South Appalachian coal communities, reducing reliance on foreign imports, and creating advanced manufacturing jobs producing coal-derived products. DOE Funding: $1,499,997

San Juan River-Raton-Black Mesa Basin (Arizona, Colorado and New Mexico): New Mexico Institute of Mining and Technology (Socorro, New Mexico) plans to determine the rare earth elements and critical minerals resource potential in coal and related stratigraphic units in the San Juan and Raton basins in New Mexico. DOE Funding: $1,483,787

Illinois Basin (Kentucky, Illinois, Indiana and Tennessee): Board of Trustees of the University of Illinois (Champaign, Illinois) aims to lead a project to evaluate domestic occurrence of strategic elements in coal, coal-based resources and waste streams from coal use. The project will assess mining techniques and state-of-the-art separation and mineral extraction technologies. DOE Funding: $1,499,987

Williston Basin (Montana, North Dakota and South Dakota): University of North Dakota (Grand Forks, North Dakota) plans to lead a coalition to drive expansion and transformation of coal and coal-based resource usage within the Williston Basin to extract rare earth elements and critical minerals and produce nonfuel carbon-based products. DOE Funding: $1,500,000

Powder River Basin (Montana and Wyoming): University of Wyoming (Laramie, Wyoming) aims to provide an economic benefit to the Powder River Basin of Wyoming and Montana by stimulating new resource development around the nation’s largest coal mines. DOE Funding: $1,499,817

Uinta River Basin (Colorado and Utah): University of Utah (Salt Lake City, Utah) plans to quantify, assess, and plan to enable transformation of Uinta Basin earth resources such as coal, oil shale, resin, rare earth elements and critical materials into high value metal, mineral and carbon-based products that can be used in advanced products such as carbon fiber composites in aircraft and high powered magnets and batteries in electric vehicles. DOE Funding: $1,500,000

Green River — Wind River Basin (Colorado and Wyoming): University of Wyoming (Laramie, Wyoming) aims to develop and catalyze regional economic growth, job creation, and technology innovation in the Greater Green River Basin/Wind River Basin of Wyoming and Colorado by increasing supply of rare earth elements and critical minerals to manufacturers of non-fuel carbon based products and products reliant upon critical minerals. DOE Funding: $1,499,647

Gulf Coast Basin (Alabama, Arkansas, Georgia, Louisiana, Mississippi and Texas): The University of Texas at Austin (Austin, Texas) plans to quantify coal from mines and coal ash from power plant resources and refuse as feedstocks for rare earth elements and critical minerals within the U.S. Gulf Coast Basin. DOE Funding: $1,497,376

Alaska (Alaska): University of Alaska Fairbanks (Fairbanks, Alaska) aims to reduce nation’s reliance on imported rare earth elements and critical minerals by establishing Alaska’s resources as competitive sources of supply. DOE Funding: $1,499,808

Other: University of Kansas Center for Research Inc. (Lawrence, Kansas) plans to study feasibility of recovering critical minerals from coal and associated strata in the Cherokee-Forest City Basin encompassing Kansas, Iowa, Missouri, Nebraska, Oklahoma and the Osage Nation. DOE Funding: $1,500,000

Other: West Virginia University Research Corporation (Morgantown, West Virginia) aims to focus on expansion and transformation of use of coal and coal-based resources, including waste streams, to produce products of high value to the 21st century energy and manufacturing ecosystem. DOE Funding: $1,500,000
Projects will be managed by DOE’s Office of Fossil Energy’s National Energy Technology Laboratory (NETL).
The Office of Fossil Energy funds research and development projects to reduce risk and cost of advanced fossil energy technologies and further sustainable use of nation’s fossil energy resources.
source_title: DOE Awards $19 Million for Initiatives to Produce Rare Earth Elements and Critical Minerals | Department of Energy

facts: Neaton Rome Inc. is an automobile parts manufacturer based in Rome, Georgia.
A U.S. Department of Labor's Wage and Hour Division (WHD) investigation found that Neaton Rome terminated an employee in violation of the Family and Medical Leave Act (FMLA).

The employer failed to provide required notification about whether the employee's absence would qualify for leave after she requested time off for an FMLA-qualifying health condition and provided supporting documentation.
The employer erroneously failed to designate the leave as FMLA-protected before terminating the employee wrongfully after assessing disciplinary attendance points for missed work.
Neaton Rome Inc. will reinstate the employee and pay her $13,133 in lost wages.
The company will reinstate the employee's health benefits dating back to the day of termination.
The employer will grant the employee 160 hours of vacation time she would have earned on her work anniversary date.
The company will increase the employee's hourly wage to reflect a raise she would have received on her anniversary.
Neaton Rome Inc. will remove disciplinary attendance points erroneously assessed due to the employee's FMLA-covered absence.
Wage and Hour Division District Director Eric Williams stated that many Americans rely on the Family and Medical Leave Act for critically needed workplace flexibility.

Eric Williams noted that the U.S. Department of Labor is committed to ensuring employees' rights under the FMLA are protected and encouraged employers to review tools and resources regarding their rights and obligations under the law.
WHD's mission is to promote and achieve compliance with labor standards to protect and enhance the welfare of the Nation's workforce.
WHD enforces Federal minimum wage, overtime pay, recordkeeping, and child labor requirements of the Fair Labor Standards Act.

WHD also enforces the Migrant and Seasonal Agricultural Worker Protection Act, the Employee Polygraph Protection Act, the Family and Medical Leave Act, wage garnishment provisions of the Consumer Credit Protection Act, and employment standards in immigration statutes.
Additionally, WHD administers and enforces prevailing wage requirements of the Davis Bacon Act and the Service Contract Act for Federal construction and goods/services contracts.

The Department of Labor's mission is to foster, promote, and develop the welfare of wage earners, job seekers, and retirees; improve working conditions; advance opportunities for profitable employment; and assure work-related benefits and rights.

links.context: Wage and Hour Division toll-free helpline

source_title: U.S. Department of Labor Investigation Results in Auto Parts Manufacturer Reinstating, Paying Employee for Family and Medical Leave Act Violations | U.S. Department of Labor

entity_name: Advanced Materials and Manufacturing Technologies Office (AMMTO)
canonical_name: U.S. Department of Energy Advanced Materials and Manufacturing Technologies Office
facts: On December 19, 2024, the U.S. Department of Energy’s Advanced Materials and Manufacturing Technologies Office (AMMTO) announced 11 funding selections through its Platform Technologies for Transformative Battery Manufacturing program.
The total federal funding amount for the 11 selected projects is $25,540,000.

The program's goal is to create platform materials and technologies for manufacturing sodium-ion batteries, flow batteries, and nanolayered films, along with systems frameworks to leverage smart manufacturing technologies in battery production.
Selected projects will support flexible, scalable, and highly controllable battery manufacturing processes.
The 11 projects directly support the growth of domestic battery manufacturing capabilities and the DOE’s Energy Storage Grand Challenge and Long-Duration Storage Shot goals.
AM Batteries, Inc. received $2,790,000 to develop a novel dry electrode manufacturing process for sodium-ion batteries in Billerica, Massachusetts.
AM Batteries partners with Unigrid and The Laboratory for Energy Storage and Conversion at The University of Chicago.

AM Batteries' project will develop solvent-free electrode coating technology to enable advanced sodium-ion chemistry, potentially improving energy density, reducing manufacturing costs and energy use, enabling scalability, and fostering collaboration.
Argonne National Laboratory received $1,490,000 for a pilot continuous hydrothermal manufacturing process for hard carbon production from domestic petroleum coke feedstocks in Lemont, Illinois.
Argonne partners with ACT-ion Battery Technologies.

The hydrothermal process uses various feedstocks to produce fine-tuned high-performance hard carbon via rapid carbonization and condensation at low temperatures, offering a lower-cost alternative with promising performance for a competitive U.S. sodium-ion battery supply chain.
Clean Republic SODO LLC d/b/a Dakota Lithium Materials received $1,990,000 to manufacture high-performance NaFePO4-based cathode materials in Seattle, Washington & Grand Forks, North Dakota.
Clean Republic partners with Boise State University and Savannah River National Laboratory.

The project leverages an eco-friendly pilot-scale lithium iron phosphate process to produce low-cost cathode materials for long-duration sodium-ion batteries, using dry mixing of feedstocks like sodium carbonate and iron phosphate with bio-additives like glucose to decrease energy consumption and eliminate water/solvents.
Quino Energy, Inc. received $2,590,000 to develop low-cost, large tank form factor redox flow batteries leveraging existing fuel storage infrastructure in San Leandro, California.
Quino Energy partners with CellCube, Richardson Electronics, South Coast Terminals, and the University of Houston.

The project will develop aqueous organic quinone redox flow battery (QRFB) technology in large carbon steel tanks to reduce balance of plant costs and installation, gaining acceptance by site owners and insurers.
Arkema Inc. received $2,140,000 for high performance non-perfluorosulfonic acid membranes for next generation redox flow batteries in King of Prussia, Pennsylvania.
Arkema partners with Tetramer Technologies, Stryten Energy, & Pacific Northwest National Laboratory.

The project will build a scalable platform to tailor non-PFSA ion exchange membranes for flow batteries operating under low/neutral pH, manufacturing more conductive, selective, and durable membranes at lower cost than current state-of-the-art.
University of Akron received $1,590,000 for scalable manufacturing of anion exchange membranes with polymers of intrinsic microporosity from commodity polymers in Akron, Ohio.
University of Akron partners with Pacific Northwest National Laboratory, Parker Hannifin Corp. & Schaeffler Group USA.

The project will develop polymers tailored for RFB membranes to enhance ionic conductivity, selectivity, and stability, plus optimize scalable manufacturing processes for large-area membranes efficiently and cost-effectively.
University of Maryland: College Park received $2,590,000 for microfabricated nanolayers for solid-state microbatteries and ionic capacitors in College Park, Maryland.
University of Maryland partners with Forge Nano.
The project aims to strengthen manufacturable battery technology by transferring atomic layer deposition processes from lab-scale to manufacturing scale tools for commercial applications.

Illinois Institute of Technology and Spraying System Co. received $2,590,000 for novel spray deposition for large-scale production of multilayered dielectric capacitors and rechargeable batteries in Chicago, Illinois and Glendale Heights, Illinois.
The project will develop a dry electrostatic spray process and prototype machine to fabricate sandwich-structured capacitors and battery electrodes with uniform thickness over large areas.
High-energy density dielectric capacitors and rechargeable batteries from this project will greatly improve energy harvesting during regenerative braking of electric vehicles.
Charge CCCV received $2,590,000 for advancing lithium-ion battery electrode manufacturing through machine-learning enabled in-situ quality control in Vestal, New York.
Charge CCCV partners with FastCap Systems Corp d/b/a Nanoramic Laboratories & Argonne National Laboratory.

The project will develop a manufacturing-scale smart digital platform to utilize in-line data and make predictions via APIs, using datasets from national lab/industry partners to create models for in-line production decision making.
This will enable progress in physics-driven simulation at cell/factory levels, improve digital twin capabilities, reduce R&D overheads, and eliminate costly wasted trials.
American Lithium Energy Corporation received $2,590,000 for innovative non-invasive sensorics for process efficiency, control, and traceability in Carlsbad, California.
American Lithium Energy partners with Siemens Corporation, ReElement Technologies, & Voltaiq.

The project will take a holistic approach to smart manufacturing by incorporating continuous in-process improvement loops via sensor integration and data-driven insights across cell/pack buildup and factory floor optimization.
It will evaluate an ecosystem approach for on-site recycling of manufacturing scrap for zero-waste capability and adopt digital thread/tools for improved battery cell performance metrics.
Titan Advanced Energy Solutions received $2,590,000 for smart battery manufacturing enabled by real-time high resolution cell morphology monitoring and control in Salem, Massachusetts.
Titan partners with Harris Hill Automation & Bureau Veritas.

The project will develop a cell inspection platform using data-driven digital techniques to reduce manufacturing quality costs, focusing on defect detection and cell uniformity to support predictive analytics and integrate into factory data pipelines.
This intelligent inspection technology will accelerate time to market and improve scalability for U.S. battery cell manufacturers, raising domestic competitiveness.
source_title: Funding Selections: Platform Technologies for Transformative Battery Manufacturing | Department of Energy

facts: Novelis Inc. is a leading sustainable aluminum solutions provider and the world leader in aluminum rolling and recycling.

Novelis announced it is proposing to offer $750 million aggregate principal amount of its senior unsecured notes due 2033 in a private offering exempt from registration under the Securities Act of 1933.

The issuer intends to use net proceeds from the proposed offering, together with cash on hand, to purchase all outstanding $750 million aggregate principal amount of its 3.250% Senior Notes due November 2026 that are validly tendered and not withdrawn pursuant to a previously announced tender offer.

To the extent there are remaining proceeds, the issuer intends but is not obligated to use them to redeem the remaining 2026 Notes not validly tendered at the applicable optional redemption price plus accrued interest.
Novelis partners with customers in the aerospace, automotive, beverage packaging, and specialties industries throughout North America, Europe, Asia, and South America.
Novelis had net sales of $17.1 billion in fiscal year 2025.

Novelis is a subsidiary of Hindalco Industries Limited, an industry leader in aluminum and copper, and the metals flagship company of the Aditya Birla Group, a multinational conglomerate based in Mumbai.
source_title: Novelis Announces Senior Notes Offering

entity_name: Panasonic Automotive Systems Company of America
canonical_name: Panasonic Automotive Systems Company of America
facts: Panasonic Automotive Systems Company of America, a tier one automotive supplier and division of Panasonic Corporation of North America, announced at CES 2023 the launch of a unique modular audio system designed specifically for electric vehicles.
The company is headquartered in Peachtree City, Georgia, with sales, marketing, and engineering operations located in Farmington Hills, Michigan.
The patent-pending audio system features a modular and scalable design that utilizes the vehicle's cabin environment for sound calibration.

It includes a power-saving EV audio option that reconfigures speaker placement to optimize sound and directs audio directly to the ears of the driver and passenger instead of saturating the cabin with traditional door-mounted speakers.

Andrew Poliak, CTO of Panasonic Automotive Systems Company of America, stated that the design lowers the cost of operating an EV by reducing half of the power output found in traditional systems without compromising sound quality.

The system's modular, space-saving speaker design maps the base audio across the cabin to drastically simplify the number of speakers needed, eliminating bulky door speakers to free up driver reach zone space and reduce vehicular weight.
Rigorous technical testing showed the application of bass delivery, speaker location, and sound tuning meets or exceeds performance thresholds of larger systems.
Testing demonstrated an up to 67% reduction in power consumed by the new system design, conserving energy that can be redirected to add up to more miles on the road for the average driver.

The sustainable design features a simplified bill of materials requiring reduced gauge wiring that weighs less, takes up less space, and relies less on costly metals like copper compared to traditional wiring.

Panasonic developed this EV audio system to improve vehicle space, weight, power, and cost aspects while supporting its GREEN IMPACT initiative aimed at achieving virtually net-zero CO2 emissions by 2030 and 300 million tons in avoided emissions by 2050.
A recent AAA consumer survey suggests electric vehicle sales are poised for significant growth, with 30 percent of Millennial buyers planning to choose an EV for their next purchase.
source_title: Panasonic Automotive Unveils EV Audio System for Electric Vehicles that Offers High Quality, Low Power Listening Experience

facts: Propex Operating Company, LLC will expand its carpet backings manufacturing facility in Hazlehurst, GA.
The expansion involves adding jobs and investing capital to increase production capacity.
The investment specifically targets increased capacity to produce Isis®, a new innovative platform of backings used in commercial carpet tile manufacturing.
Isis is the first recycled polyester woven backing for carpet tile.
Propex introduced Isis in 2011 after years of development.
Prior to this expansion, Isis was developed and manufactured by Propex at their facility in Gronau, Germany.
Increasing global demand for Isis necessitates further capacity investment to better serve customers.

facts: Stryten Energy LLC is a U.S.-based energy storage solutions provider headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia.
The company will feature its Enhanced Flooded Battery (EFB) technology along with its full lineup of energy storage solutions for the automotive aftermarket at AAPEX 2023.
AAPEX 2023 takes place October 31 to November 2 at the Venetian Expo in Las Vegas, Nevada.
According to the EPA, half of new vehicles produced in 2021 included start-stop technology.
Nearly 30% of new vehicles sold in the U.S. with start-stop technology now come equipped with EFB, a trend projected to increase year over year.
EFB technology provides energy to run modern vehicles' electrical load demands repeatedly without significantly impacting the battery’s performance.
For start-stop systems, the battery provides the electrical energy to support vehicle functions during the stop portion of the drive cycle.
The battery must be resilient during partial state of charge cycling and have an enhanced charge acceptance capability for this system to work properly.

Bill Nonnamaker, Vice President of Sales, Transportation for Stryten Energy, stated that batteries need more energy to support start-stop technology alongside conveniences and advanced safety features in vehicles today.
As vehicle power demands continue to evolve, choosing the right battery will become even more important, according to Nonnamaker.
The Stryten EFB is specifically designed to resist cycling-related failures associated with increased electrical demands of modern vehicles.
Independent third-party testing found that Stryten Energy’s EFB offers three times the cycle life and twice the charge acceptance compared to standard flooded batteries.
Additional tests showed Stryten Energy’s EFB yields more than two times the cycle life of a competing premium flooded battery.
The EFB contains a more robust alloy for higher heat tolerance at a lower grid thickness.
The EFB has an average capacity output 50% greater than Absorbed Glass Matt (AGM) for an equally sized battery.
The EFB outperforms AGM in under-hood applications for vehicles equipped with a flooded battery from the factory due to greater energy availability, increased battery life, and high heat tolerance.
Stryten Energy will make a $10 donation (up to $10,000 total) to the University of the Aftermarket Foundation for every selfie taken with the Stryten Jeep at its AAPEX booth.
The University of the Aftermarket Foundation funds scholarships and educational grants to help talented individuals maximize their career potential in the automotive aftermarket industry.

Bob Egan, MAAP, chairman of the University of the Aftermarket Foundation, praised Stryten Energy's support and emphasized that education and training are critical for developing and servicing new technologies.
As part of its sustainability pledge for AAPEX 2023, Stryten Energy implemented LED lighting and will power down the booth during non-show hours.
QR Codes will be used in place of printed literature to minimize paper waste, and branded plastic water bottles have been replaced with a water cooler and recycled cups at the booth.
Stryten Energy provides energy storage solutions across the Essential Power, Motive Power, Transportation, Military, and Government sectors.
The company partners with recognized global companies to meet demand for reliable and sustainable energy storage capacity.
Stryten's stored energy technologies power submarines to subcompacts, microgrids, warehouses, distribution centers, cars, trains, and trucks.
Stryten's technology portfolio includes advanced lead, lithium, and vanadium redox flow batteries, intelligent chargers, and energy performance management software.
The independent third-party battery testing studies evaluated EFB performance against other technologies in the North American replacement market using standard industry test protocols.

facts: Stryten Energy LLC is a U.S.-based energy storage solutions provider headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia.
The company released new research comparing today's automotive battery technologies on June 07, 2022.

An independent third-party research company conducted an in-depth analysis evaluating the performance of the Stryten Energy Enhanced Flooded Battery (EFB) compared to the Stryten Energy SLI and a competing premium flooded battery.

Bill Nonnamaker, Vice President of Sales, Transportation, stated that today’s vehicles require advanced battery technology to power key systems for safety and fuel efficiency, and that Stryten’s EFB offers high performance and battery life to meet these demands.
The tested batteries were all L3/H6 type.
Stryten EFB specifications are 750 CCA / 120 RC / 74 AH.
Stryten SLI specifications are 750 CCA / 133 RC / 70 AH.
The competing premium flooded battery specifications are 730 CCA / 120 RC.
Three samples of each battery type were subjected to the SAE j2801 cycle life test and the EN50342-6 17.5% depth of discharge (DoD) cycle life test.
The SAE j2801 test is the industry standard high temperature (75°C) life cycle test for SLI batteries designed to simulate battery usage in a standard internal combustion vehicle application.
The EN50342-6 / 17.5% DoD test is an industry-standard partial state of charge and micro cycling life cycle test focused on Start/Stop and vehicles with additional power demands.
Including the partial state of charge testing was intended to better address the changing power needs of modern vehicles.
Test results show that Stryten EFB has superior performance over competing premium flooded batteries.
The Stryten EFB meets the additional depth of discharge requirements for vehicles with Start-Stop functionality.
The Stryten EFB yields higher heat tolerance with a unique grid design.
On the 17.5% DoD partial state of charge test, the Stryten EFB yielded 2X+ the cycle life of either the competing premium flooded battery or the Stryten SLI.
The Stryten EFB was equal to the competing premium flooded battery on the high-temperature j2801 cycle life test.
Both the Stryten EFB and competing premium battery performed 25% greater than the Stryten SLI on the high-temperature j2801 test.
Further teardown indicates the competing premium battery utilizes a relatively thick grid compared to their standard product to achieve improved SAE j2801 performance.
The Stryten grid is also thicker than standard but pairs this with a more robust grid alloy to provide even more heat protection on a grid thickness basis.
While the competing premium flooded battery addresses high-temperature life compared to standard SLI designs, it does not address cycling failure modes or Start-Stop functionality.
The Stryten EFB provides a solution for these issues and is designed from the ground up to combat the fastest-growing causes of premature battery failure.
Stryten Energy helps solve energy challenges across the Essential Power, Motive Power, Transportation, Military, and Government sectors.
The company partners with recognized global companies to meet growing demand for reliable and sustainable energy storage capacity.
Stryten powers applications ranging from submarines to subcompacts, microgrids, warehouses, distribution centers, cars, trains, and trucks.
Their stored energy technologies include advanced lead, lithium, and vanadium redox flow batteries, intelligent chargers, and energy performance management software.
The company is an industry leader backed by more than a century of expertise.
source_title: Stryten Energy Releases New Research Comparing Today's

facts: The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) reached a settlement agreement with Decostar Industries, Inc. (Decostar) to address violations of the Resource Conservation and Recovery Act.

Decostar will take steps to control levels of hazardous air emissions from their facility in Carrollton, Ga., in order to protect workers and the surrounding community and improve overall air quality.
As part of the settlement, Decostar will implement a leak detection and repair program.
Decostar will perform a supplemental environmental project valued at over $2.7 million.
Decostar will pay a $377,900 civil penalty.
EPA Region 4 Administrator Trey Glenn stated that the agreement will reduce hazardous air emissions at the Carrollton facility, protect worker health, and improve air quality in the surrounding area.
The injunctive relief addresses specific violations concerning leak detection and repair by instituting a program to ensure compliance.
The leak detection and repair program involves specifying the roles and responsibilities of all employees and contractor personnel assigned to monitoring.
The program includes a tracking system that ensures new pieces of equipment added to the facility are integrated into the equipment program.
The program requires removing pieces of equipment that are taken out of service from the program.
Decostar will replace existing robots and paint applicators with devices that increase transfer efficiency as part of the supplemental environmental project.
The replacement project is expected to reduce hazardous waste generated at the facility by 4,130 pounds per year.
The project is expected to reduce hazardous air pollution emissions by 1,385 pounds per year.
The project is expected to reduce emissions of volatile organic compounds by 1,972 pounds per year.
source_title: EPA Reaches Agreement with Decostar Industries, Inc. in Carrollton, Georgia to Reduce Hazardous Air Emissions | U.S. EPA News Releases | US EPA

Section 173.185 regulates lithium-ion cells and batteries based on the amount of lithium in the anodes of fully charged cells and batteries.
If lithium-ion cells and batteries meet the conditions for the exception provided in § 173.185(b), they are not subject to the HMR.
If not excepted, the other provisions of § 173.185 apply.

facts: EV adoption is beginning to take off, meaning more people will be purchasing EVs now and in the future.
Scientists and policymakers want to know how the battery supply chain will accommodate and reinforce increasing demand.
A new wave of investment in battery development and manufacturing of next-generation EV battery packs in the U.S. will help America hit the goal of roughly 10 million new EVs sold per year by 2030.

Argonne researchers have studied trends in EV manufacturing and adoption, analyzed the supply chain, and determined the impacts of more widespread EVs in terms of displaced petroleum consumption and greenhouse gas emissions.

Dave Gohlke, an Argonne energy and environmental analyst, stated that EVs are beginning to gain a broader foothold in the market and this trend will only accelerate throughout the remainder of the decade.
Gohlke noted that increased investment and adoption of EVs will create thousands of new American jobs, help decarbonize the country, combat climate change, and shape the economy and environment.

Joann Zhou, interim director of Argonne's System Assessment Center and manager of its mobility and deployment group, stated that the 2022 Inflation Reduction Act provides tax incentives for domestically produced EVs to promote an American EV manufacturing pipeline.
Zhou said the Inflation Reduction Act marks an important milestone for getting more EVs produced domestically.
Argonne researchers track EV sales and new investment announcements in battery and EV manufacturing, asking who made the battery and where, and who assembled the car and where.

Gohlke reported that since last year, there has been roughly one new announcement of a battery development or EV manufacturing project every few days, a dramatic increase even since the beginning of the pandemic.
Xinyi Wu, an Argonne postdoctoral researcher, noted that many of these announcements are substantial in terms of allocated dollars and planned jobs.

Argonne's chief goal is to supply DOE and others with information about the impacts of accelerated EV manufacture and adoption, focusing on job creation numbers and quantitative analysis of potential gasoline and greenhouse gas reductions.
Gohlke stated that tracking and analyzing this data is essential for helping DOE and the Biden Administration meet their decarbonization goals.
To reduce EV production costs, companies are tending to locate battery manufacturing facilities in states that contain vehicle assembly plants.
Zhou explained that major investments in EV technology have led the automotive community to efficiently build vehicles, resulting in an emerging 'battery belt'.

Argonne research identified Michigan and Kentucky as key states for EV battery production through 2030, with each supplying between 97 and 136 gigawatt-hours of battery capacity by the end of the decade.
Georgia, North Carolina, Ohio, Tennessee, and Kansas would also be important contributors to domestic EV battery capacity.
Domestic EV battery manufacturing capacity will increase by almost 20 times between 2021 and 2030, according to a November 2022 Argonne report.
A map on the DOE website based on Argonne's work shows the locations of new U.S. battery manufacturing and supply chain investments.
Zhou pointed to battery recycling as a way to obtain critical materials in the U.S. needed for new batteries, noting Argonne's long history in the field could help secure the supply chain.
The ReCell Center, a multilab center funded by DOE and led by Argonne, is exploring battery recycling technologies to establish a circular battery ecosystem.
Argonne National Laboratory is the nation's first national laboratory, conducting leading-edge basic and applied scientific research in virtually every scientific discipline.
Argonne researchers work closely with hundreds of companies, universities, and federal, state, and municipal agencies to solve specific problems and advance America's scientific leadership.
Argonne is managed by UChicago Argonne, LLC for the U.S. Department of Energy’s Office of Science.
The U.S. Department of Energy’s Office of Science is the single largest supporter of basic research in the physical sciences in the United States.
source_title: A new look at the electric vehicle supply chain as battery-powered cars hit the roads en masse | Argonne National Laboratory

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp, Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung, company officials, and Savannah JDA representatives officially broke ground on Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America’s new manufacturing plant at the Bryan County Megasite.

Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority Chairman Carter Infinger noted that beyond the investment and jobs, Hyundai is already impacting the community through nonprofit contributions.

Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Hyundai placed a bet on Georgia sixteen years ago and has since become a Georgia company that changes lives across the state.
The Bryan County facility will directly create future jobs for the Savannah area and support opportunities in the EV revolution statewide.
The Bryan County Megasite was selected partly due to its close proximity to the Port of Savannah.
Under Griff Lynch and the Georgia Ports Authority, the Ports of Savannah and Brunswick have expanded to handle record cargo volumes.
Hyundai will access global markets quickly via rail and highway connections to the port.
Non-affiliated HMGMA suppliers connected to the project will invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group is a global enterprise with about 250,000 employees worldwide and mobility brands including Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis.

facts: Inalfa Roof Systems is laying off 127 employees in Georgia — nearly half of its workforce at the facility.

The company supplies major automakers worldwide and operates multiple manufacturing facilities.

A workforce reduction of nearly 50% suggests the company is facing serious operational or market pressures that are forcing a fundamental restructuring of its Georgia operations.
Automotive suppliers have been under significant pressure due to changing market conditions, shifts in vehicle production, and evolving automotive technology.
Federal WARN Act typically requires private-sector employers cutting 100+ workers at a single site to provide 60 days' advance notice or pay in lieu of notice.
Georgia is an at-will employment state, meaning employers can generally terminate employment without cause.
Under the Older Workers Benefit Protection Act (OWBPA), employees over 40 in group layoffs are entitled to 45 days to review a severance agreement, while individual terminations require 21 days.

Concerns about shareholder dilution and margin compression remain powerful considerations shaping future returns.

facts: Georgia was named the No. 1 state for business for the 11th year in a row by Area Development magazine, with workforce development being a major contributor.
Georgia earned a No. 1 ranking for the 15th year in a row for its nationally recognized workforce development program, Georgia Quick Start.
The state’s economic development efforts in fiscal year 2024 resulted in $20.3 billion in investments and plans for 26,900 new jobs statewide.

Governor Brian P. Kemp delivered the keynote address at the second annual Governor’s Workforce Summit, during which he announced that Georgia has been named the No. 1 state for business for the 11th consecutive year by Area Development magazine.
Governor Kemp provided an update on the state of Georgia MATCH and other state efforts to strengthen workforce development while putting forth the goal of making Georgia the Top State for Talent.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that the recognition underscores their pro-business approach to economic development creating opportunities for Georgians in every corner of the state.

Governor Kemp thanked the state economic development team and community partners, noting continued efforts through Georgia MATCH which has already seen a notable increase in enrollment at both USG and TCSG institutions.
Leaders from across the public and private sector remain engaged in solving the need for more workers, as observed at today’s summit.
No other state has earned the distinction of Top State for Business for so many years.
Area Development’s annual poll considers approximately 50 leading site consulting firms from across the U.S. and evaluates 14 different factors to determine the ranking.
Governor Kemp hosted the second annual Governor’s Workforce Summit at the Georgia World Congress Center.
The summit brought together leaders from business, education, and public policy to align these sectors in preparing Georgians for available jobs statewide.
The highlight of last year’s summit was the announcement of Georgia MATCH, the largest direct college admission program in the nation.
Georgia MATCH sent a letter to every high school senior in the state informing them of spots at Georgia’s University System and Technical College System institutions.
Following last year’s rollout, both systems saw a preliminary year-over-year increase in enrollment this fall of 9.3% for TCSG and 6.2% for USG.
This enrollment increase occurred despite challenges created by the federal government’s mishandling of this year’s FAFSA rollout.

Dennis Shea, Publisher of Area Development, stated that Georgia has maintained top 10 rankings in each survey subcategory for over a decade, including available workforce, cost of doing business, cooperative and responsive state government, energy availability and costs, logistics and infrastructure, and site readiness programs.
Site selectors recognize that Georgia provides the whole package for companies choosing to grow or expand in the state.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Georgia does economic development differently by prioritizing partnerships and investments in infrastructure, education, and workforce development.

Between July 1, 2023, and June 30, 2024, GDEcD’s Global Commerce team supported 429 facility expansions and new locations totaling more than $20.3 billion in investment, resulting in plans for 26,900 new jobs statewide.

Area Development is considered the leading executive magazine covering corporate site selection and relocation, founded in 1965, publishing quarterly with a website receiving over one million visitor sessions annually.
source_title: Gov. Kemp Celebrates Top State for Business Ranking at Workforce Summit - Location Georgia

facts: JTEKT North America announced it is expanding its manufacturing operation in Greenville County and creating 87 new jobs.
JTEKT North America manufactures a broad range of products under the brands JTEKT Automotive Systems, Koyo Bearings and Toyoda Machine Tools.
The company maintains its regional headquarters in Greenville, South Carolina.
JTEKT North America operates 12 manufacturing sites, three technical centers and five distribution centers throughout North America.
The expansion adds seven new machine lines and two new assembly lines to the existing facility located at 1866 Old Grove Road in Piedmont.
Additional processes will be added to machine components internally at the site.
JTEKT North America will have the capability to produce front drive shafts at its expanded facility.
Operations are expected to be online in the third quarter of 2025.
source_title: JTEKT North America’s new expansion to create dozens of new jobs in Greenville Co.

facts: Automotive parts manufacturer JTEKT announced plans to invest $48.4 million in its Piedmont factory, creating 87 new jobs in Greenville County.
Part of JTEKT Corporation, JTEKT North America manufactures a broad range of products under the brands JTEKT Automotive Systems, Koyo Bearings and Toyoda Machine Tools.
In addition to the company's Piedmont operation, it has 12 manufacturing sites, three technical centers and five distribution centers across North America.

JTEKT Americas CEO Gary Bourque stated in a press release that the investment reflects absolute confidence in current and future associates, state leadership, and delivering next-generation solutions to customers and trusted brands.
The $48.4 million investment will fund seven new machine lines, two new assembly lines, and additional processes for machine components.
With these improvements, JTEKT North America will gain the capability to produce front drive shafts.
source_title: JTEKT adds 87 jobs to Greenville Co., Harris Teeter opening

facts: JTEKT North America's estimated annual revenue is currently $2.6B per year.
JTEKT North America's estimated revenue per employee is $462,000.
JTEKT North America has 5,623 employees.
JTEKT North America grew its employee count by 4% last year.
JTEKT North America was established in 2013, bringing together all of JTEKT’s global products under one regional umbrella.
The companies that make up JTEKT North America have been doing business since 1958.
JTEKT North America operates 14 manufacturing facilities.
JTEKT North America supplies bearings, driveline components, steering systems, and machine tools.
JTEKT North America's regional scope includes markets such as North America, Europe, Latin America, Asia-Pacific, and the Middle East.

Key competitors for JTEKT North America include JTEKT Corporation, Hyundai WIA, Okuma Corporation, Mitsubishi Heavy Industries Machine Tool Co., SKF, Schaeffler AG, NTN Corporation, Iljin Bearing Co., Dalian Machine Tool Corporation, DMG Mori Co., Ltd, Doosan Machine Tools, and Haas Automotive Inc.

JTEKT North America's leadership structure includes roles such as Chief Leader, VP of Sales, VP Manufacturing, Steering Segment Director, Technical Center Director, Segment Director, Director of Driveline Segment, Director of Production Administration, Executive Director, and Supply Chain Planning Manager.

facts: JTEKT North America has sent a WARN notice announcing 136 workers losing their jobs at its Telford plant in Washington County, Tennessee.
The shutdown timeline begins at the end of April next year and concludes in August.
A change in automotive design with conventional engines being used less is one factor in closing the plant, according to the company.
A decline in the use of their parts is another factor cited by the company for the closure.

facts: Lund International Holding Company is a portfolio company of Highlander Partners, L.P.
On March 11, 2013, Lund announced the acquisition of the Prestige Fender Flare assets from Empower Group, Inc.
Prestige Fender Flares is a leading designer and producer of premium quality fender flares sold throughout the United States.
Co-founders Anthony Liu and Robert Chen successfully built Prestige into a leading provider of fender flares in the automotive aftermarket over six years.
Anthony Liu and Robert Chen will join Lund in a management capacity focusing on new product development.
Prestige has developed a thorough and complete offering of fender flares for light trucks and SUVs.

Co-founders Anthony Liu and Robert Chen stated they are excited to complete the transaction with Lund and look forward to leveraging its extensive, multi-channel distribution capabilities to continue growing in the fender flare market and other new product categories.
Mitch Fogle serves as President of Lund.
Mitch Fogle stated that adding Prestige’s fender flares to the Lund family of products solidifies Lund's leadership in the automotive aftermarket accessories industry.
Mitch Fogle expressed looking forward to working with Anthony Liu and Robert Chen to maximize the potential of their product offering and exploit combined new product ideas.
Prestige is the first add-on acquisition completed by Lund under Highlander’s ownership.
Jeff L. Hull serves as Chairman of Lund International and Managing Partner of Highlander.
Jeff L. Hull stated the acquisition validates Lund's desire to be a leader across all product categories in the exterior aftermarket automotive accessories market.
Jeff L. Hull noted the acquisition confirms Lund's ongoing interest in utilizing an acquisition-based strategy to complement organic growth efforts.
Jeff L. Hull stated Lund continues to evaluate numerous other potential acquisitions and is always on the lookout for new opportunities.
Lund International Holding Company is a leading designer, manufacturer, and marketer of branded automotive aftermarket accessories for passenger cars, light trucks, and heavy trucks.
Lund's product portfolio includes ventvisors, hood shields, heavy-duty floor mats, tonneau covers, storage boxes, and running boards.
Lund offers the industry’s broadest range of functional body and appearance products that allow customers to customize and personalize their vehicles.
Highlander Partners, L.P. is a Dallas-based private investment firm.
Highlander Partners' partners have committed over $500 million of principal funds for direct private equity investments in North America and Central Europe.

Highlander Partners focuses on making investments in middle market businesses in targeted industries where principals have significant operating and investing experience, including basic manufacturing, food, building materials, and others.
Highlander Partners uses a “buy and build” investment approach to create value by helping companies grow organically and through acquisitions.
source_title: Lund International, a Portfolio Company of Highlander Partners, Acquires Prestige Fender Flares – Highlander Partners

facts: Truck Hero Inc. is set to acquire Lund International Holding Co., a portfolio company of Highlander Partners.
The terms of the deal have not been disclosed.
Headquartered in Ann Arbor, Michigan, Truck Hero, Inc. is a fast-growing designer and manufacturer of accessories for pickup trucks with some movement into the Jeep market as well.
Truck Hero provides pickup truck owners a wide variety of truck bed covers and accessory options including truck caps and bed liners.

Existing brands include: Advantage, A.R.E., BACKRACK, BAK, BedRug, Extang, Husky Liners, N-FAB, Omix-ADA, Retrax, Rugged Liner, Rugged Ridge, Superlift Suspension, TruXedo, Undercover and online retailer RealTruck.
This new pending transaction marks Truck Hero’s 15th acquisition since forming in 2007.

The company stated the acquisition is well-aligned with its growth strategy of acquiring businesses within the vehicle accessory market that further diversify its product portfolio into attractive, complementary, high growth segments.
Buford, Georgia-based Lund was founded in 1965 in Anoka, Minnesota with the mission of leading the industry in meeting customer needs for innovative automotive accessories.

Lund’s accessories span 30 product categories from brands that include AMP Research, AVS, Belmor, Bushwacker, LUND, Rampage Products, RoadWorks Manufacturing, Roll-N-Lock, Stampede Automotive Accessories and TonnoPro.
Lund is led by president and CEO Mitch Fogle.

Bill Reminder, president and CEO of Truck Hero, stated: This is an opportune time to bring Truck Hero and Lund together and that the Lund team has built an impressive collection of great companies which will be a perfect match to Truck Hero.
The acquisition provides a complementary product portfolio that further diversifies Truck Hero’s product category mix and helps increase its exposure to a larger market of vehicles.

Mitch Fogle stated: We believe the merger of these two great companies is a winning combination and together we manufacture and commercialize market leading brands that encompass the broadest product offering of vehicle accessories.

Joe Scharfenberger, managing director at CCMP Capital, stated: The highly talented Lund and Highlander teams have built a very attractive, profitable and scalable portfolio of functional automotive accessories brands.

Jeff L. Hull, President and CEO of Highlander Partners added that combining Lund into Truck Hero was an outstanding opportunity to further enhance an already successful investment and that Highlander will be a significant shareholder in the combined company.
source_title: Truck Hero Set to Make Strategic Acquisition of Lund International | Modern Jeeping News & Education

facts: The US is ramping up efforts to onshore the lithium-ion battery supply chain.
Global lithium-ion battery demand across EVs and stationary storage came in at around 950 gigawatt hours last year.
Global battery manufacturing capacity was more than twice demand, at close to 2,600 GWh.
China’s battery production in 2023 alone was similar to global demand.
Canada is matching US incentives, while Europe, India and others are awarding subsidies to grow their battery industries.
The oversupply picture is going to get worse before it gets better.
BNEF is tracking 7.9 TWh of annual battery manufacturing capacity announced for the end of 2025.
Demand projections are 1.6 TWh, assuming steady EV demand growth and very rapid growth in batteries for storage applications.
Even half the total announced capacity would be enough to equip almost every car sold in the world next year with a 50 kWh battery pack.
Much of the announced capacity will either be behind schedule or outright cancelled as scaling realities become clearer and contenders thin out.

Other parts of the supply chain cannot deliver at that level, and EV batteries are not yet a commodity product due to different formats, chemistries, automaker preferences, and planned model launches.
Plants typically do not run at their intended capacity, and utilization rates have been falling for the last few years.
The market is heading for even more serious oversupply even with a hefty de-rating factor for actual online capacity.
Prices will fall and margins will get squeezed, already evidenced by a 14% dip in average battery pack prices in 2023.
China’s CATL expects to be able to sell battery cells at the equivalent of less than $60 per kWh this year.
Average capacity utilization of existing plants will continue to fall after dropping in 2023.
Battery prices are converging across sectors as manufacturers look for new markets for their products.
The price gap between commercial-vehicle batteries and passenger car batteries has narrowed as the market swung in favor of buyers and should continue to close.
Low prices will make it difficult for new entrants to compete, even with generous support provided by the US Inflation Reduction Act and other means.
The production phase involves challenges including cutting down scrappage rates, which typically start out high and gradually come down over time.
There is a scenario that battery makers receiving generous support in the US and Europe are undercut by their Chinese rivals.
China’s BYD and CATL both continue to invest billions in R&D, are steadily launching new and better products, and behave more like scrappy startups than bloated corporate fiefdoms.
Lower prices will benefit consumers with cheaper EV models planned for launch in 2025 and 2026.
The recently released Xiaomi SU7 EV uses CATL’s new battery technology, boasting up to 830 kilometers (516 miles) of range for the equivalent of $34,000 in China.
The stationary storage market is already growing very rapidly and will also benefit from lower battery prices.
Battery supply dynamics are acting as a moderating factor for EV demand: when EV demand slows, battery prices fall, leading to lower EV prices and helping spur a recovery in demand.

Some current low battery prices are driven by lower raw material costs for oversupplied materials like lithium, which may not hold long since it is easier to build a battery plant than bring on new mining or refining capacity.
Some price cuts are likely due to established players looking to protect market share and force smaller ones out.
There is definitely an element of technology and manufacturing improvement at play in the price cuts.
Overcapacity is a boon for potential EV buyers but marks a challenging time ahead for new entrants to the battery industry.
source_title: China Already Makes as Many Batteries as the Entire World Wants | BloombergNEF

facts: The State of Georgia surpassed export records with almost $50 billion in 2023 for the third straight year.
Governor Brian P. Kemp joined the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) to announce that the state surpassed $49.7 billion in exports in 2023.
The 2023 export figure eclipsed the record-setting $47 billion recorded in 2022.
Georgia currently ranks seventh in the U.S. for dollar value of trade.
The state facilitates more than $186 billion in total trade to 222 unique countries and territories.
Georgia ranks 12th in the country for dollar value of exports.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that for the third year in a row, Georgia has broken every record when it comes to exports, bringing billions of dollars to communities across the state.
An estimated 87 percent of those exports come from small businesses.
International trade touches every county in the state, supporting jobs in logistics, manufacturing, agriculture, and more.
Top exports for 2023 included civilian aircraft products, motor vehicles, turbojets and turbines, poultry, and chemical woodpulp products.
Canada, Mexico, China, Germany, and Singapore were the top destinations for Georgia exports.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that international representatives in overseas markets have helped find homes for Georgia products and goods for decades.
These representatives leverage their unique understanding of overseas markets to support small businesses and increase the state’s overall exports.
Representatives work with Georgia-based export specialists to ensure Georgia businesses have an advantage and the economy remains strong.
Support from the General Assembly and local leadership helps Georgia identify new opportunities in the global economy.
In 2023, the state’s top five bilateral trading partners were Mexico, South Korea, Canada, China, and Germany.
There was notable year-over-year growth in total trade between the State of Georgia and South Korea.
Markets where Georgia maintains full-time representation accounted for 67 percent of exports and 71 percent of bilateral trade in 2023.
Lizann Grupalo, GDEcD Deputy Commissioner of International Trade, stated that Georgia punches above its weight in exports due to the hard work and dedication of Georgia-based businesses.
Trade builds a more resilient economy, allowing businesses to shift strategies as market opportunities evolve.
There is an estimated one in nine jobs in Georgia tied to the logistics industry.
Georgia is home to Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport, described as the busiest and most efficient airport in the world.
Georgia has the fastest growing and third-busiest container gateway in the U.S. at the Port of Savannah.
Georgia has one of the nation’s busiest gateways for Roll-on/Roll-off cargo at the Port of Brunswick.
Georgia offers a robust rail and highway infrastructure, with more rail miles than any other state in the Southeast at 5,000 miles of rail.
Georgia has 20,000 miles of federal and state highways.
GDEcD’s International Trade team works to bolster Georgia exports and brand the state as a competitive source of quality products and services.
The team includes International Representatives located in 12 strategic global markets.
The International Trade office provides Georgia businesses with Global Insight and Global Connections to successfully diversify their international customer base.

source_title: Georgia breaks export records for third year

facts: Mack trucks like the Anthem, Granite, LR, and Pinnacle are heavyweights in the trucking industry, serving an array of heavy-duty applications across the construction, transport, and vocational segments.
With roots dating back to 1890, the Mack Trucks brand has a long history of motor vehicle innovation.
Mack’s first successful vehicle was the Mack bus, a 40-horsepower (30-kilowatt) vehicle with a 20-passenger capacity introduced in 1900.

After converting the Mack bus into a truck, the company found a new path as a leading innovator of truck technology, introducing modern truck staples like advanced air cleaners and oil filters, power brakes, and shock absorbers.
In 2017, Mack introduced the Anthem, which replaced the Pinnacle axle back model.
The Anthem is a Class 8, on-highway truck designed with aerodynamics in mind, featuring roof fairings, hood, bumper, cover tow loops, air dam, and close-out flange to reduce wind resistance.
Mack equips the Anthem with features designed to improve fuel efficiency, such as its Predictive Cruise Control with Econo-Roll and the HE+ fuel efficiency package.

The HE+ fuel efficiency package works with the powerful Mack MP8HE engine and Mack’s Energy Recovery Technology to capture waste energy from the engine’s exhaust and convert it into additional torque.

The Granite is engineered for construction and vocational truck markets, with buyers able to select day cab trucks pre-fitted with standard mixer, bridge mixer, lift axle dump, conventional dump, rear loader, or hydraulic roll off hoist.
Mack Granite mixers can include an 11LMP7 or 13LMP8 engine ranging from 325 to 505 HP (242 to 376 kW).
Mack’s LR is built for the refuse and recycling industries, featuring a large windshield and windows in the door to reduce blind spots.
The LR Electric is a fully electric Class 8 vehicle designed to help refuse customers meet sustainability goals and provide quieter operation.
The LR Electric can be fitted with equipment bodies from numerous manufacturers based on customer needs.
The LR Electric has four lithium-ion batteries and two motors that produce up to 448 HP (334 kW) of continuous power and 4,051 pounds-foot (5,492 newton-meters) of torque.
Mack introduced the Pinnacle in 2006, replacing the Vision as its leading option for hauling applications.
The Anthem replaced the axle-back Pinnacle variant in 2017, but the axle-forward Pinnacle continues to be a reliable platform for regional and bulk hauling.
The Super-Liner was produced in North America from 1977 to 1993, and models can still be found today.
Mack Trucks Australia continues to manufacture the Super-Liner.

Mack Trucks outlined a two-pronged tractor strategy built around the new Pioneer and a redesigned Anthem at its Experience Center testing grounds.
U.S. Class 8 retail sales continue to face buyer reticence despite improving market conditions and growing optimism in May.

International Motors Director of Engineering Navtej Singh stated at ACT Expo that service intervals are improving to reduce headaches going into 2027.
OEMs welcomed the prospect of IIHS ratings for larger vehicles at the 2026 Advanced Clean Transportation Expo.
Mack Trucks unveiled an MP13 engine compliant with EPA's upcoming 2027 nitrogen oxide emissions regulations at the 2026 ACT Expo on May 5.
ACT Expo 2026 signaled momentum shifts in the clean transportation movement, growing acceptance of connected vehicle technology, AI, and autonomous driving as subsidies fade.

facts: HL Mando America Corp. was recognized for its support of Auburn University, the Samuel Ginn College of Engineering, and the Department of Mechanical Engineering.
HL Mando committed $200,000 to support up to four graduate students in an accelerated bachelor’s and master’s degree program in mechanical engineering at Auburn University.
The funded graduate students must have a special interest in automotive technologies.
HL Mando is based in Seoul, South Korea, and operates as an electric vehicle and autonomous driving solutions company specializing in core components for vehicle safety.
HL Mando is a global leader in key enabling technologies for future mobility, including redundancy concept and by-wire technology.
HL Mando is strengthening eco-friendly vehicle solutions such as e-Drive and hydrogen fuel cell converters.
HL Mando America is the US-based subsidiary of HL Mando.
HL Mando America has manufacturing operations in Opelika and Hogansville, Georgia.
HL Mando America produces brake calipers, electric steering systems, and suspension systems.
HL Mando America executives CEO Bruce Kim and Head of Americas Region Yun Lee attended a reception on Monday, August 5, hosted by Mario Eden, dean of engineering.
During the reception, HL Mando presented Auburn with a ceremonial check for its educational support commitment.

Yun Lee stated that HL Mando considers it a blessing to have Auburn University in their vicinity and has had great experiences partnering with the Department of Mechanical Engineering and the Office of International Programs.
Yun Lee expressed hope that HL Mando’s contributions will help prepare tomorrow’s leaders in the automotive industry, both locally and globally.
HL Mando has established a long-standing relationship with Song-Yul Choe, the William B. and Elizabeth Reed Professor in Auburn’s Department of Mechanical Engineering.
Song-Yul Choe’s research interests include modeling/control of power sources, piezoelectric devices, high powered connectors, solenoid valves, and tires.
Mario Eden, dean of engineering, stated that industry support from leaders like HL Mando is vital for preparing graduate students to enter the workforce as leaders.
