facts: SK Battery America is an electric vehicle battery manufacturer with a factory in north Georgia.
A fire destroyed Metro Site Inc.'s material recovery facility in Commerce, Georgia, on July 14, 2023.
The blaze started after lithium-ion battery scraps exploded at the facility.

Metro Site Inc., a waste and recycling company not authorized to process batteries, filed a trespass lawsuit against SK Battery America in Jackson County State Court in Georgia (Case number: 23SV271).
Investigators traced the lithium-ion batteries found in the debris back to SK Battery America, a subsidiary of South Korea’s SK Group.
Post-fire inspections revealed over 600 burnt and charred SK batteries at the site.
Expert findings confirmed the shipped batteries underwent thermal runaway, causing the fire.
SK Battery America had sent defective lithium-ion batteries to Metro Site at least twelve times despite knowing they had caused previous fires there.
The Plaintiff alleged that SK Battery America knew Metro Site was not licensed or equipped to handle hazardous materials like lithium-ion batteries.
SK Battery America was accused of destroying key evidence during litigation by deleting video footage from inside its plant, which the court ruled constituted evidence spoliation.

Following a December 22, 2022 incident where battery cells delivered by Roll-Off Systems caused a fire at Metro Site, SK Battery America updated its policies and provided additional employee training to prevent future occurrences.
Jinsu Kim issued revised instructions to the company’s logistics and module teams to prevent further battery cell deliveries to Metro Site.

After implementing the updated policies, no incidents occurred between December 2022 and July 14, 2023, during which Roll-Off Systems transported approximately 2,700 loads of waste from SK Battery America to Metro Site without issue.
The defense argued that SK Battery America took previous incidents seriously and implemented internal measures, including policy revisions and employee training.
Metro Site Inc. and SK Battery America reached a $31 million settlement less than two weeks before the scheduled trial.
Plaintiff attorney Jackson McDonald stated the settlement was driven by a demand for a public disclosure clause to highlight SK Battery’s alleged misconduct and deter similar actions by corporations.
The agreement avoided a second spoliation hearing and ended one of several legal disputes surrounding the fire.
McDonald predicted increased litigation against manufacturers related to lithium-ion batteries and electric vehicle technology, emphasizing the significant risks posed by emerging industries.
An SK Battery spokesperson confirmed the settlement and highlighted procedural improvements to prevent similar incidents.
The company maintained that it had not intentionally sent hazardous materials to Metro Site or destroyed evidence, expressing commitment to community safety and a desire to move beyond the dispute.
Plaintiff attorneys planned to introduce evidence of recurring fires at the SK Battery America plant based on an open records search of 911 service calls revealing numerous internal factory fires.
source_title: $31M Settlement for Improper Disposal of Waste Leading to Fire | Jurimatic by Exlitem | Jury Verdicts & Settlements | Exlitem

facts: Nearly 1,000 workers at SK Battery America’s Commerce, Georgia, plant lost their jobs on March 6.
The layoffs were the direct consequence of a Ford cancellation, vanishing federal incentives, and a domestic EV market that has stalled well short of industry projections.
The layoffs are among the most visible signs yet that the U.S. battery supply chain built during the Biden era is now contracting under a very different policy environment.
SK Battery America filed a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification covering 958 employees, roughly 37% of its Commerce workforce.
The affected workers’ last day was March 6, with pay and benefits continuing through May 6.
Ford announced in December that it was dissolving the BlueOval SK battery joint venture and canceling the fully electric F-150 Lightning due to sluggish sales.
The dissolution is expected to generate a $19.5 billion write-down for Ford.
SK Battery America opened the $2.6 billion Commerce plant in January 2022.
As recently as 2023, SK Battery America was expanding its workforce by several hundred employees to keep pace with anticipated EV demand.
Congress has eliminated consumer EV tax credits of up to $7,500.
The Trump administration has moved toward more relaxed automotive emissions standards, creating significant uncertainty across the EV supply chain.
EVs represented approximately 8% of U.S. new vehicle sales in 2025, roughly flat compared to the prior year.
Major OEMs including Ford, GM, and Stellantis have all pulled back on pure BEV timelines, redirecting investment toward hybrids and more affordable entry-level EVs.
SK and Hyundai are still jointly building a $5 billion battery factory near Cartersville, Georgia.
Rivian’s $5 billion facility and Hyundai’s $7.6 billion manufacturing complex represent ongoing state investments that have not been pulled back.
A company spokesperson said SK Battery America is pursuing new customers in the Battery Energy Storage System arena.
The pivot toward stationary grid storage means demand drivers are less dependent on consumer purchase behavior and federal EV policy.
A law firm has announced an investigation into whether SK Battery complied with WARN Act notification requirements, adding legal uncertainty to the transition.
source_title: SK Battery Cuts 37% of Georgia Workforce - The EV Report

facts: SK Battery America (SKBA) has exceeded its hiring goal of 2,600 employees two years ahead of schedule for its two new manufacturing facilities in Commerce, Georgia.
The hiring milestone was reported according to a press release from Georgia Governor Brian Kemp’s office.
SK Battery America met its initial hiring goal at the end of 2022.
The company is further expanding its workforce to approximately 3,000 employees over the next year to meet growing demand for electric vehicles.
SK Battery America announced its first project in Georgia in 2018 with an initial planned investment of $1.6 billion.
Since 2019, SK Battery America has invested $2.6 billion to build two battery manufacturing facilities in Jackson County.
The Jackson County facilities are built to supply electric vehicles, including the Ford F-150 Lightning and the Volkswagen ID.4.
SK Battery America started mass production at its Georgia facilities in early 2022.
Governor Brian Kemp described SK Battery America as one of the first companies to put Georgia on the e-mobility map.
A week prior to this announcement, SK Battery America announced plans to open a new regional IT hub in Roswell, Georgia.
The new regional IT hub in Roswell, Georgia, will create 200 tech jobs.
The new regional IT hub in Roswell, Georgia, will invest about $19 million over the next few years.
Governor Brian Kemp referred to Georgia as the No. 1 state for business while praising SK Battery America’s continued growth.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that SK Battery America brings jobs of the future benefiting Georgians in manufacturing and will tap into tech talent from top-ranking universities.
source_title: SK Battery Exceeds Hiring Goal In Georgia Two Years Ahead Of Schedule | Doc and the Block

facts: SK Battery is threatening to stop work on its promised factory in Jackson County and leave the country unless President Joe Biden sides with them.
President Biden has until April 11 to reverse a decision punishing SK over allegations it stole trade secrets from another Korean competitor to build the plant.
The Jackson County plant investment is valued at $2.6 billion and is expected to provide an estimated 2,600 green jobs for Georgia.
State and local leaders provided one of the biggest tax breaks in state history to secure the plant: $300 million in tax abatements and free land.
The plant was scheduled to start producing batteries for electric vehicles in 2022.
In February, SK Battery's parent company SK Innovation faced a ruling from the United States International Trade Commission (USITC).
The USITC ruled that SK tried to destroy evidence it stole 22 trade secrets from rival Korean battery manufacturer LG, now called LG Energy Solutions.
The punishment includes a 10-year ban on the battery technology SK is accused of stealing, which would essentially shut down the Jackson County operation.
SK denied wrongdoing and urged the White House to veto the USITC ruling.
An SK spokesperson told Reuters that if Biden does not side with them, the company is reviewing options to move its US battery production to Europe or China.
Federal authorities report catching Korean nationals trying to sneak into the country to help build the plant, allegedly taking jobs from American workers.
Many neighborhoods surrounding the plant have homes occupied by multiple overseas workers shuttled back and forth to the site.
SK stated that around 250 Americans currently work at the plant, with many more assisting with construction.
Many Georgia politicians are lobbying on behalf of SK, fearing that punishing the company would force the United States to rely more on Chinese-made batteries for the EV market.
LG Energy Solutions took out a full-page ad in the Atlanta Journal-Constitution highlighting the possibility of Georgia receiving one of its two new US battery plants.

Denise Gray, president of LG Energy Solutions Michigan Tech Center, stated her company will not leave Georgia without jobs and is ready to provide batteries SK was supposed to build for the Ford F-150 electric pickup and VW ID.4 SUV.
The government ruling grants SK a four-year grace period to fulfill the Ford contract and a two-year grace period for the VW contract.
SK and LG have attempted to negotiate a settlement regarding the trade secret dispute.
Korean press reports indicate LG is asking for 3 trillion won (approximately $2.3 billion), close to the plant's total value, while SK countered with 1 trillion won (around $888 million).
Gray stated she cannot confirm the accuracy of the reported settlement numbers but emphasized her company's desire for a settlement to secure jobs in the US and Georgia.
SK was granted a four-year exemption to build batteries for the new electric Ford F-150.
Commerce city manager James Wascher emailed FOX 5 expressing hope that the Biden Administration will use its authority to overturn the ITC decision and support job creation commitments.
Wascher noted the dispute should be settled in federal court, where damages can be awarded if wrongdoing is proven at trial.
A settlement between SK and LG is currently viewed as the only way to ensure the future of the Jackson County plant.
Gray agreed that a presidential thumbs-up could speed up a settlement, while a thumbs-down could delay it indefinitely.
source_title: SK Battery threatens to abandon Georgia plant if Biden doesn't side with them | FOX 5 Atlanta

facts: SK Battery America (SKBA) is opening a new regional IT hub in Roswell, Georgia.
The new facility will create 200 tech jobs.
SKBA will invest about $19 million in the next few years for the new IT hub.
The new IT center will serve as an integrated IT management center for the company’s battery manufacturing facilities across the United States, including its Georgia facilities.
The facility is located at 1110 Sanctuary Parkway in Roswell.
Operations at the new IT hub are expected to begin in January.
SKBA will be hiring IT experts for battery manufacturing execution system development and operations as well as data analysts.
SK Battery America is investing nearly $2.6 billion in two lithium-ion battery manufacturing facilities in Jackson County.
The Jackson County facilities will supply electric vehicles such as the Ford F-150 Lightning and Volkswagen ID.4.
This Roswell IT hub is one of SK Group’s three major investments in the State of Georgia.
SK Group was founded in 1976 and is the second-largest conglomerate in South Korea.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp announced the opening of the facility on a Thursday.
Governor Kemp stated that the company continues to bring jobs of the future that will benefit hardworking Georgians in manufacturing and tap into tech talent from top-ranking universities.
Jason Choi, Head of Information Technology at SK Battery America, stated the decision to open the IT regional center in Roswell aims to strengthen SK’s position as a leader in the battery industry.
The new center will help build and operate an advanced manufacturing system in accordance with further expansion of battery production bases in the United States.
SKBA plans to expand its presence and engage with the community and surrounding region through this center.
source_title: SK Battery America Opening New Facility In Georgia, Investing $19 Million | Doc and the Block

facts: SK Battery America, a Korean electric vehicle battery manufacturer with major operations in Georgia, laid off 958 employees, or more than a third of its workforce, at its Commerce plant.
The company notified state officials on Friday of the layoffs at its Commerce plant via a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN).
The Commerce plant is located approximately 70 miles northeast of Atlanta.
Georgia's manufacturing sector maintained steady job growth, adding 2,600 jobs year-over-year despite the U.S. losing nearly 300,000 manufacturing jobs over the past two years.
The 2% year-over-year job growth in Georgia's manufacturing sector was reported by Rajeev Dhawan of the Economic Forecasting Center at Georgia State University’s J. Mack Robinson College of Business.
A February U.S. Department of Labor jobs report indicated the nation lost about 92,000 jobs in January, with Georgia also experiencing job losses.
source_title: Georgia Trend Daily - March 9, 2026 - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: President Joe Biden campaigned on the promise of electric vehicles and green jobs, and Georgia voters responded by electing him to the White House and handing the state’s two Senate seats and control of Congress to Democrats.
A U.S. International Trade Commission (ITC) ruling threatens to shut down a giant electric vehicle battery factory set to employ upward of 2,600 workers in the suburbs north of Athens, Georgia.
Georgia’s Republican lawmakers have emerged as the project’s most vocal defenders in an unusual partisan twist.
President Biden has just 19 days left to veto trade authorities’ ruling.
The ITC ruled last month in favor of South Korean battery giant LG Chem, finding rival SK Innovation guilty in a trade secrets case.
The ITC banned SK Innovation from importing what it needs to run its planned Georgia factory for the next decade.
There has been no sign from the White House that it will block the decision so far.
SK Innovation has hired consultants to draft a plan to shut down its $2.6 billion plant in Georgia.
The move sent Peach State officials scrambling to save one of the largest economic development projects in the state’s history.
Pat Wilson, GOP commissioner of Georgia’s Department of Economic Development, said: “There’s nothing partisan about the jobs of the future.”
Georgia’s Republican leaders have been very public in their call for Biden to act to save the Commerce, Georgia, plant.
None of the six Democrats representing Georgia in the House of Representatives responded to repeated requests for comment.
Five emails to Democratic Sen. Jon Ossoff’s office went unanswered, and former gubernatorial candidate Stacey Abrams declined an interview request.

Sen. Raphael Warnock (D-Ga.) declined an interview but his spokesman provided a link to a March 4 confirmation hearing video where Warnock pressed Polly Trottenberg, Biden’s pick for the No. 2 job at the Department of Transportation, on whether the agency would “provide to President Biden an analysis of” the ITC ruling’s effect on U.S. battery supplies.
Warnock said during the hearing: “It’s not a good position for U.S. automakers if they’re only relying on almost one cell supplier.”

Warnock stated the adverse ITC ruling “threatens seriously the future of that project, whether it’ll happen at all,” calling it “one of the largest economic investments in Georgia’s history and a severe punch in the gut... for President Biden’s own goals.”

Conservative Atlanta-based pundit Erick Erickson called the ITC’s decision “a bad day for all of us here in Georgia” and compared potential job losses to Keystone XL oil pipeline construction worker layoffs.

Erickson wrote that if Warnock and Ossoff lack political clout to reverse the decision, they will “help contribute to the loss of over 2,000 jobs in Georgia,” questioning if Warnock wants that going into 2022.

Carol Browner, former Clinton-era EPA administrator, served as an expert witness for SK Innovation, testifying before the ITC that eliminating its Georgia plant put the U.S. electric vehicle supply chain at risk.

Sally Yates, former Obama-era deputy attorney general, told The New York Times’ Dealbook the case could “seriously undermine critical progress on the climate crisis” and risked “killing jobs and setting back the Georgia economy.”

Andrew Young, civil rights leader and former UN ambassador during the Carter administration, warned in an opinion piece that the ITC ruling “imperils thousands of direct and indirect Georgia jobs and postpones the future of the new era auto industry” over a technicality.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp (R) repeatedly urged Biden to “support thousands of hardworking Georgians and their communities.”

On March 12, Kemp sent a letter to Biden asking him to “exercise the authority granted to you under law to disapprove the ITC ruling on grounds that it is contrary to the public interest and will seriously jeopardize your administration’s environmental and economic goals.”
Kemp wrote: “Simply put: the livelihoods of thousands of Georgians are now in your hands,” and “This critical opportunity requires us to work together for the good of my state and our country.”
GOP Rep. Buddy Carter hoped Biden was helping broker an agreement between LG Chem and SK Innovation to allow manufacturing plans to proceed.

Carter said: “We don’t want to lose those jobs in Georgia and we don’t want to lose those jobs in the electric vehicle industry,” and expressed hope that presidential staff are engaged in brokering a deal.

White House U.S. Trade Representative spokesman Adam Hodge stated the administration is “currently reviewing the policy implications of the ITC determination” and will complete the review within the 60-day federal law deadline.
The dispute began in 2017 when SK Innovation poached engineers in Seoul, prompting LG to file lawsuits accusing them of violating noncompete contracts.
In 2019, LG’s battery subsidiary, LG Energy Solutions, lodged a complaint with the ITC.
Around the time SK Innovation announced plans to open its massive Georgia factory to supply Ford Motor Company and Volkswagen, LG turned to the ITC.
LG filed a separate lawsuit in Delaware federal courts for monetary damages, pending conclusion of the ITC case.
On Feb. 10, the ITC found SK Innovation guilty of destroying documents allegedly detailing what it learned from its LG hires.
SK Innovation argued that South Korean laws do not require companies to retain the same documents as U.S. statutes.
The commission ruled unanimously to ban SK Innovation from importing raw materials for the next 10 years, which LG said would take to develop batteries independently.
The ITC granted a carve-out allowing SK Innovation to build batteries for four years for Ford’s electric F-150 pickup truck and two years for Volkswagen’s Crossover series.
The commission determined the carve-outs should give automakers time to find alternative sources or for SK Innovation to reach a settlement with LG.
Tesla is the largest battery maker in the U.S., but its Nevada Gigafactory only produces packs for its own vehicles.
LG operates Midwest projects that will produce roughly 30 gigawatt-hours per year when completed, providing batteries to General Motors and other automakers.
SK Innovation’s Georgia plant would add another 22 gigawatt-hours of annual capacity.
The rest of the battery market is made up of much smaller companies.

LG sent a letter on March 10 to Warnock vowing to “do whatever we can to help the people and workers of Georgia,” hinting it was prepared to make investments in the state to offset the closure, according to The Atlanta Journal-Constitution.
David Callahan, lawyer for LG, said the company was preparing to invest $4.5 billion in the U.S., resulting in at least two more factories, one of which could be in Georgia.
SK Innovation stated LG is “using the ITC to try to muscle SK out of the US EV battery market, period.”
A SK Innovation spokesperson said: “SK has always been open to good-faith settlement talks with LG,” but criticized LG as “inflexible and unreasonable” and noted “LG doesn’t want competition.”
James Frith, analyst at BloombergNEF, said allowing LG to dominate poses risks to producing EVs at the speed and affordability the Biden administration wants.

Frith stated: “Competition within the battery industry has been one of the key factors that helped to drive down cell and pack prices over the past decade,” and warned it’s not a good position for U.S. automakers relying on almost one cell supplier.
Frith noted other manufacturers could fill SK Innovation’s void, including Japan’s Panasonic Corporation, South Korea’s Samsung, and China’s CATL or BYD.
Callahan denied LG was seeking a monopoly and accused SK Innovation of bluffing about shuttering the plant to stave off a costly settlement.

Callahan said: “If SK Innovation intends to be in this hugely growing market in the long haul... it would be suicide to leave Ford and Volkswagen in the lurch,” and added, “They’re threatening Georgia. If I were Georgia, I would be mad.”
SK Innovation already started hiring hundreds of workers, and it could take years for another company to replace the plant or increase supply to electrify the U.S. auto fleet.

Wilson said: “We need batteries in order to keep up with the demand, this was not only an investment into Georgia but an investment into electric vehicles in the United States and a diversification of the supply chain outside of Asia,” adding, “We saw this as a win-win for the state and the country.”
source_title: The Georgia GOP Is Begging Biden To Act On Battery Plant, While Dems Stay Silent | HuffPost Latest News

facts: NextStar plans to use up to 900 foreign workers to install equipment at its electric-vehicle battery plant in Windsor, Ontario.
Canada's Building Trades Unions (CBTU) describes NextStar's plan to hire as many as 900 foreign workers as an "insult" to Canada's tradespeople.
Sean Strickland is executive director of CBTU, which represents more than 500,000 skilled trades workers in 14 unions.
Strickland stated the installation work should go to trained Canadians, specifically workers in southwestern Ontario.
Strickland called the plan "absolutely unconscionable" and urged resolving the situation to maximize Canadian jobs on the project.
CBTU noted its members have performed similar state-of-the-art technology installations in the automotive sector for 100 years, including battery plants across North America.
NextStar's requirement of 900 foreign workers with original equipment manufacturer (OEM) experience is beyond industry standards seen by CBTU.
NextStar confirmed it will hire about 1,600 technicians from outside suppliers to assemble, install and test equipment.
The hiring includes up to 900 "temporary specialized global supplier staff," mainly from South Korea.
NextStar stated these workers have specific knowledge of the equipment from building and disassembling it for shipping and will see the installation through.
The Windsor plant is a partnership between Stellantis and South Korean company LG Energy Solution.
The facility is slated to open in 2025 and received $15 billion in federal and provincial subsidies.
Strickland noted it is too difficult to determine an acceptable number of foreign workers until the trades union secures a meeting with the companies.
Construction at the plant includes a mostly completed building base, ongoing concrete pouring, and expected equipment installation in the coming weeks.
The Construction Employers Coordinating Council of Ontario and Provincial Building and Construction Trades Council of Ontario spoke out against NextStar's plans.
These two groups stated their workers have been involved in auto, EV and battery sector construction over the last decade and called the need for foreign permits a "false narrative."
They asserted there isn't a project in Ontario their contractors and skilled trades workforce cannot build and urged dialogue with industry.
Vic Fedeli, Ontario's minister of economic, job development and trade, spoke with NextStar CEO Danies Lee about workers coming to Windsor.
Fedeli stated the temporary workers will assist with training 700 Ontario workers to equip them with net new skills for future projects as the industry transitions to building cars of the future.
Fedeli clarified these temporary workers will perform highly specialized work, upskill Ontario workers, and then return home.
François-Philippe Champagne, federal minister of industry, said he spoke to building trade unions Friday and they are "very much on the same page."
Champagne stated he wants to sit down with the company and unions to determine the minimum required amount of people to transfer technology so Canadians can operate and build the plant.
Industry experts noted bringing foreign workers to set up new projects is expected, especially in partnerships with foreign entities in the electrification space.
Joe McCabe, president of Auto Forecast Solutions, stated foreign representation at project kickoffs is standard and must be accepted as a short-term necessity.
NextStar said temporary staff will work on site anywhere from three to 18 months before returning home.
A NextStar spokesperson clarified these are not permanent full-time jobs.
source_title: Plan to use 900 foreign workers at Windsor EV battery plant called an 'insult' to Canadian labourers | CBC News

facts: SK Battery America Inc. laid off nearly 1,000 workers at its manufacturing plant northeast of Atlanta on Friday amid automakers’ changing electrification plans and uncertain consumer demand for EVs.
The last working day for 958 impacted plant employees was Friday, according to a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) notice filed by human resources chief Chuck Moore.
Impacted workers will be paid through May 6.
SK opened its $2.6 billion battery manufacturing plant in Commerce, Georgia, in January 2022.
The Korean company previously supplied batteries for the Ford F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck.
Ford announced plans to cancel the fully electric version of the F-150 Lightning in December.

The layoffs occur as the U.S. electric vehicle market faces a standstill due to the Trump administration steering federal support away from electrification toward laxer automotive emissions policies and oil/gas industry support.
SK Americas spokesperson Joe Guy Collier stated the workforce reduction was made to align operations with market conditions.
Collier stated, “SK Battery America remains committed to Georgia and to building a robust U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing.”
Collier added that the company is pursuing future customers in the Battery Electric Storage System arena.
The City of Commerce and Jackson County commission chair did not immediately respond to requests for comment regarding the layoffs.
Ford decided in December to scrap the fully-electric F-150 pickup truck in favor of an extended-range version.
A Ford spokesperson declined to comment on supplier personnel actions related to the plant.
SK and Ford previously invested $11.4 billion together in joint battery plants across the U.S.
SK ended its joint venture with Ford in December.
SK has made significant investments in Jackson County, Georgia, as automakers developed EV plans supported by the former Biden administration's domestic supply chain efforts.
In June 2020, SK announced plans to invest $940 million to expand its battery manufacturing presence in Atlanta.
Governor Brian Kemp’s office stated that the June 2020 expansion would create 600 jobs.
SK and Hyundai are jointly building a $5 billion battery factory near Cartersville, northwest of Atlanta.
Georgia has attracted other major EV manufacturing investments, including Rivian’s $5 billion factory and Hyundai’s $7.6 billion factory complex.
U.S. EV demand has not met automakers' expectations despite still growing, with EVs accounting for about 8% of new vehicle sales in the U.S. in 2025, matching the prior year.
Automakers are reevaluating multibillion-dollar electrification plans due to mounting financial losses and shifting consumer demand.

Manufacturers including Ford, General Motors, Stellantis, and others have reneged on factory and investment plans, laid off workers, and pivoted some efforts toward hybrid and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles.
Hybrids and more efficient gasoline-powered vehicles are gaining preference among mainstream buyers concerned about EV driving range and charging infrastructure availability.
Under President Donald Trump, Congress eliminated consumer tax credits of up to $7,500 for purchases of new or used EVs.
The Trump administration announced plans to weaken fuel economy and greenhouse gas emissions rules for automakers, effectively removing federal incentives for cleaner vehicle fleets.
source_title: SK lays off nearly 1,000 workers at Georgia plant amid cooling automaker EV plans - Loonie Politics

facts: SK Battery is a North Georgia EV battery maker.
SK Battery paid $31 million to Metro Site, Inc., a nearby recycling business that went up in flames last year.
The fire at Metro Site was allegedly caused because the factory illegally dumped hazardous scrap batteries there.
SK Battery admitted no liability in the settlement of the civil lawsuit filed by Metro Site.
The settlement had no confidentiality clause.
Scott Ledford is the owner of Metro Site, an automated industrial recycling center near Commerce, Ga., which he spent five years building.
The fire at Metro Site started July 14, 2023.
The fire took four days and three million gallons of water to finally extinguish.
Court records show SK Battery America accepted a settlement offer from Metro Site last month.
Ledford’s legal team made the $31 million sum public in a press release that publicly accused the company of destroying evidence in the case.
A press release stated that during the litigation, SK Battery destroyed critical evidence that it had been on notice to preserve.
State and local governments wooed SK Battery to Commerce at the end of the last decade with $300 million in tax abatements, grants and land.
SK Battery's $2.6 billion factory employs more than 3,000 workers.
The factory has made batteries for the Ford F-150 Lightning and the Volkswagen ID.4.
Four years ago, SK Battery faced allegations of deleting emails in a trade secrets dispute with a rival battery maker.
The U.S. International Trade Commission ruled in the other company’s favor in that dispute, with SK settling for $1.8 billion.
SK Battery denied destroying evidence in that case in a statement to FOX 5.
In the Metro Site case, Ledford’s attorneys asked for sanctions, with SK Battery pushing back saying everything requested was provided.
The judge never made a ruling on the allegations regarding evidence destruction.
Allyn Stockton is a Clayton attorney and part of Scott Ledford's legal team.
Stockton told the FOX 5 I-Team that SK Battery destroyed videos from inside and outside the plant.
An affidavit filed in the case was signed by Mike Ramirez, a former safety manager at the plant.
The affidavit elaborated on accusations that surveillance cameras should have captured scrap batteries being moved from within the plant to the outside.
Ramirez left behind logbooks when his employment ended, which did not come back in discovery along with the videos.
Stockton said those videos might have shown the batteries going into the roll-off dumpster bound for Metro Site.
Ledford’s attorneys described other troubling things learned during the case about the company’s scrap batteries, as well as more fires.
One record filed in the case shows 62 internal fires at SK.
The factory has its own internal firefighting team with their own fire truck, confirmed by the company.
The Ramirez affidavit alleges fires started because the plant ramped up production to unsafe levels, creating high volumes of unusable batteries called NGs or "no good" that often combusted.
The affidavit stated fires were occurring inside the plant routinely and hundreds upon hundreds of NG pallets were stored in four warehouses in Jefferson, Georgia.
A fire occurred in Jefferson and was caused by NG cells.
Ledford and his attorneys said there would be no confidentiality clause so people need to know what happened.
Stockton described this as part of the culture of that corporation.
Some of Ledford’s attorneys are involved in a separate ongoing lawsuit over the fire representing Banks County government.
The lawsuit accused SK Battery of illegally endangering government property and employees, including the fire station next door.
SK declined to talk on camera about the Metro Site case but said by email through a spokesman that they settled because both parties were ready to move on.
SK added they are not sure why Metro Site and its attorneys continue to argue the case.

On the 62 fires, SK said they occurred over three years, with many of the events triggered only by a smoke alarm without any fire, and none resulting in significant damage or burn injuries to employees.
SK's written statement described stringent procedures for disposing of production material.
A photo from a 2020 news release shows SK's EV battery cells.
SK Battery America stated it values its partnership with the Georgia community and is committed to having a positive impact on the area.
SK added that more than a year ago, they added an extra layer of oversight to further strengthen procedures before any waste material leaves their site.
The Georgia Environmental Protection Division fined the company $33,000 over the fire.
The agreement ending the recycling center’s lawsuit provides a generous settlement for Metro Site to rebuild and relaunch its business, according to SK.
Ledford built his business from the ground up only to watch it burn to the ground in July 2023.
Ledford said the company had sent him lithium-ion batteries about a half dozen times before, mixed in with recycling materials.
Fires had broken out previously but nothing severe.
Ledford said SK acknowledged they were not licensed nor permitted to handle lithium-ion battery packs or any type of hazardous waste.
Every time Ledford met with them, they made promises that they had their problem fixed on their end.
A pallet full of batteries was put in a dumpster illegally and shipped over to Metro Site.
When hundreds were put together, there was a chain reaction that lit everything in the remote vicinity to it on fire.
SK Battery also faces a lawsuit from Banks County over the July 2023 fire at Metro Site, which is next door to a fire station.
The fire took four days and three million gallons of water to put out.
Ledford alleged that found among the embers were more than 600 charred batteries made by SK.
He filed his lawsuit a few weeks later.
Ledford said despite how it looks, the settlement did not make him rich.
He said the $31 million would be sucked up by debt associated with the facility, rebuild costs of the facility, and equipment that was lost, leaving him to about break even.
One of Ledford’s attorneys is Bo Hatchett, who is also a Republican state senator from Cornelia.
Asked if tax incentives for SK Battery in retrospect were a mistake, Hatchett said the company brought a lot of jobs to the area.
Hatchett believed that since the fire occurred, SK has made some significant changes with their safety.
He noted it was a small business run by a local family that burned to the ground.
source_title: Destroyed recycling center settles with SK Battery for $31M, then blasts the company | FOX 5 Atlanta

facts: The U.S. Department of Labor cited SK Battery America for seven health and safety violations at its Commerce, Georgia, facility.
The Department of Labor proposed $75,449 in penalties against the battery maker.
SK Battery America employs about 3,100 people at its two battery manufacturing plants in Commerce.
SK Battery America is a subsidiary of South Korea-based electric vehicle battery manufacturer SK On.
OSHA inspectors investigated the Commerce facility between June 26 and Nov. 7, 2023.
Inspectors found that the Commerce facility exposed workers to respiratory hazards when working with cobalt, nickel and manganese.
Cobalt, nickel and manganese are key components in the company’s electric vehicle batteries.
The company failed to complete a workplace hazard assessment for using the chemicals.
The company did not provide employees with proper safety equipment, such as eyewash stations and emergency showers.
The company did not ensure that respirators used when working with the materials were properly cleaned and stored.
OSHA Area Office Director Joshua Turner stated that the lithium battery industry has experienced unprecedented growth, bringing heightened responsibility to ensure worker safety.
Turner noted that materials used to produce lithium batteries can cause debilitating and permanent health issues.
Inspectors found that the Commerce facility exposed workers to unsafe noise levels.
The agency cited the plant for failure to develop a hearing monitoring program as required by law.
SK Battery contested OSHA’s findings before the federal agency’s review commission.
A company spokesperson stated that safety remains the utmost priority for workers at SK Battery America.

The spokesperson noted that SK Battery America has procedures in place that comply with workplace safety regulations and is reviewing the findings while working closely with officials to address concerns.
source_title: SK Battery America cited for safety, health hazard violations at Georgia plant | WardsAuto

facts: SK Battery America is a subsidiary of South Korea-based SK On.
SK Battery America is laying off hundreds of workers from its production facility in Commerce, Georgia.
The layoffs are occurring as electric vehicle battery demand slows and federal incentives dry up.
SK Battery America filed a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification on Friday.
The notification informed Georgia of job cuts affecting 958 people.
Affected workers will receive pay and benefits for the next 60 days.
Friday, March 6, was the last day of work for the affected employees.
The Commerce site previously had 2,566 workers, and the layoffs reduced the headcount to 1,600.
Joe Guy Collier serves as SK Battery America's spokesperson.
The company will work with affected workers during the transition.
SK Battery America stated it remains committed to Georgia and building a robust U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing.
The company is pursuing future customers in the Battery Energy Storage System arena.
Many automakers and battery companies are making cutbacks due to slower sales and a pullback of federal incentives under the current administration, according to the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas.
Companies in the sector are looking to cater to growing data center demand for backup power and energy storage systems.
In December, Ford Motor Co., a customer of SK Battery America, announced it is shifting its electrification plans to include hybrid vehicles and smaller, more affordable EVs.
Ford also announced plans to grow its energy storage business.
Ford expects to record a $19.5 billion write-down due to this shift.
Ford dissolved its BlueOval SK battery joint venture with SK On as it shifts away from EVs.
Ford canceled the electric F-150 Lightning due to sluggish sales.
In 2023, SK Battery America announced plans to expand its Commerce site workforce by several hundred employees to meet rising EV demand.
The expansion exceeded the company's previous goal of 2,600 employees.
The Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) fined SK Battery America twice for exposing workers to respiratory hazards.
Law firm Strauss Borrelli announced an investigation into SK Battery America for allegedly failing to notify the 958 employees per the WARN Act.
The WARN Act mandates that employers notify workers in writing at least 60 days before a plant closure or job cuts take effect.
Spokesperson Joe Guy Collier stated that SK Battery America's workforce reduction complies with WARN Act guidelines.
source_title: SK Battery America lays off nearly 1,000 workers at Georgia plant

facts: Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp made one last appeal to President Joe Biden to save the Korean-owned battery factory that has threatened to vacate a $2.6 billion investment in the state without presidential action.
President Biden has until Sunday to overturn an International Trade Commission ruling barring SK Battery from importing the parts and materials needed for production at its Commerce, Ga., plant.
The ITC ruling included carveouts to serve existing orders from Volkswagen and Ford.
SK Battery stated that without President Biden’s intervention, the plant would no longer be economical and would be forced to shut down before beginning earnest production due to the import ban.
At stake are 2,600 jobs in the first phase alone.
SK Battery revealed it would ramp up to more than 6,000 jobs on $5 billion of total investment, double what it had previously announced.

Gov. Kemp stated that a decision in support of Georgia jobs would make up for the economic blow from the loss of a Major League Baseball game, which he blamed on a 'disinformation campaign' by President Biden over new Georgia voting laws.

Kemp said: 'However, the president now has yet another decision before him that will make or break a $2.6 billion investment in our state. The jobs of at least 2,600 Georgians depend on President Biden’s upcoming decision on the ITC ruling, and I sincerely hope he will use his authority to do the right thing.'
The ITC separately ruled that SK had not infringed on LG Energy Solution’s patents, a decision seen as giving SK more leverage in the rivals' ongoing legal dispute.
Both SK Battery and LG Energy Solution had previously stated they were open to a settlement in the initial case.

Lawyers for SK pointed out the ruling was decided based on the idea that the company 'spoliated evidence' by destroying documents, rather than on the intellectual property merits underlying the dispute.
If President Biden decides not to act, SK could file a lawsuit in federal court to get the import ban removed, or the companies could reach a settlement that would nullify it.
LG Energy Solution has already filed an infringement suit in U.S. district court.
Former acting U.S. Attorney General Sally Yates, now working in Atlanta for King & Spalding, stated the district court is the appropriate venue for the lawsuit.

Yates, hired by SK to review the ITC ruling, agreed that destruction of evidence is a serious charge but said the trade commission only had an import ban as punishment, which hurts third parties and harms U.S. environmental goals.

Yates stated: 'The district court can consider both the trade secret and spoliation claims, and, if LG can prove its case, mete out a proportional remedy that awards LG damages and sanctions SK for spoliation. In contrast to the ITC exclusion order, this accountability would not undermine critical climate initiatives, kill thousands of clean-energy jobs, set back the Georgia economy, give China a leg-up in the EV supply chain, or create USMCA compliance problems for U.S. auto companies. The district court is the most appropriate forum to resolve these issues.'
source_title: Kemp Makes Final Appeal to Biden to Save Korean Battery Plant as Deadline Looms - Global Atlanta

canonical_name: SK On and Hyundai Motor Group EV Battery Manufacturing Facility
facts: Hyundai Motor Group and SK On selected a site in Bartow County for a new electric vehicle battery manufacturing facility that will supply Hyundai Motor Group's plants in the U.S.
Stakeholders estimate the project will create more than 3,500 new jobs through approximately $4-5 billion of investment in Bartow County.
Hyundai Motor Group and SK On recently signed a memorandum of understanding regarding the partnership for a new EV battery facility in the U.S., with details still in development.

Georgia Governor Brian Kemp stated that Hyundai Motor Group and SK On are valued partners and key players in the state's automotive industry, praising their investment in the "No. 1 state for business."
Established in 2021, SK On is the lithium-ion battery subsidiary branch of SK Innovation and currently employs more than 2,000 Georgians at its SK Battery America facility in Commerce.
In July, SK Battery America announced it would partner with the Work for Warriors Georgia program to hire veterans, servicemembers, and their families at its Georgia operations.

Subject to execution of relevant agreements and final board decisions, the new facility will be located at Bartow Centre, a zoned manufacturing and industrial site on Highway 411, aiming to begin operations in 2025.
Bartow County Sole Commissioner Steve Taylor expressed pleasure with the decision, noting the project will benefit regional job seekers and establish Bartow in Georgia's EV ecosystem.

Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development's Global Commerce team on the project, partnering with Georgia EMC, Development Authority of Bartow County, Cartersville-Bartow County Department of Economic Development, and Georgia Quick Start.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted HMG and SK have been pioneering partners for decades, driving Georgia's automotive renaissance, and highlighted efforts to create a fully integrated supply chain connecting battery manufacturers with recyclers.
Excluding this announcement, EV-related projects announced in Georgia since 2020 total approximately $17 billion in investment and more than 22,800 new jobs.
Separately, Hyundai Motor Group announced in May plans to invest $5.54 billion in Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, an EV manufacturing facility in Bryan County.
Hyundai Motor Group broke ground on the Bryan County facility in October.
source_title: Bartow County to get $5 billion EV battery facility

facts: SK Battery is counting on the Biden Administration to reverse an order that threatens the future of its electric car battery plant in Jackson County.
A rival Korean battery manufacturer says the only way to save 2,600 jobs is to pay up.
On Wednesday, the United States International Trade Commission sided with LG Energy Solution, agreeing SK Battery’s parent company SK Innovation tried to hide evidence in a trade secrets dispute.
As punishment, SK faces a 10-year ban from using the battery technology in the United States, including its Jackson County plant.
SK had already lined up two big customers for its batteries: the new Ford Electric F-150 Pickup and Volkswagen’s ID.4 SUV being built in Chattanooga, TN.
LG Energy Solution’s top intellectual property attorney Song Jung stated that LG's property and technology were stolen and that SK must adequately compensate LG Energy Solution under law.
Already 250 people work inside the SK Battery plant, which is still under construction along I-85 in Jackson County.
The plant was on track for mass production by next year, eventually employing 2,600 people.
Parent company SK Innovation says it now has 'serious concerns' about the future of the plant.
The state of Georgia agreed to $300 million in tax incentives to land the SK plant two years ago.
Much of the $300 million in tax incentives won’t kick in unless the promised jobs appear.
The ITC is temporarily allowing SK to build those batteries for a few years, giving Ford and VW time to find a new supplier.

A Ford spokesman said the ITC decision supports their plans to bring the all-electric Ford F-150 to market in mid-2022, calling it an important part of their plan to revolutionize electrified vehicles and a top priority for the company.
LG attorney Jung says SK should strike a financial deal with his company that would allow the technology to still be used and the plant still stay on schedule.
SK is pursuing a strategy of appealing to President Joe Biden to reverse the decision.
SK Battery stated they are hopeful that the Biden administration will take seriously the negative economic and environmental impact of this decision and reverse it so they can continue to operate.
President Biden campaigned on an environmentally friendly platform, promising 10 million green jobs.
LG attorney Jung said he doesn’t believe the ITC decision in any way hinders Biden's progress toward those green jobs.
Governor Brian Kemp called on Biden to intervene, saying the decision puts the future of the Jackson County plant 'in peril.'
Governor Kemp stated that litigation in these disputes is always complex, with several additional levels of review prior to a final resolution and the possibility of a settlement.

Governor Kemp noted President Biden and his administration have the opportunity to support thousands of hardworking Georgians and their communities who would benefit from SK Innovation’s continued success in the state, and sincerely hopes Georgia’s congressional delegation will join him in advocating for swift presidential action.
The White House had no comment on the situation.
Presidential vetoes of ITC rulings are rare.
Biden has 60 days to make a decision regarding the ITC ruling.
LG says it is waiting for a call from SK to make them a 'viable' offer, while people in Jackson County are left to wait and worry.
Some SK Battery construction workers have complained about safety issues at the plant.
Federal authorities say they found Korean nationals illegally hired by subcontractors to help build the factory.
SK Battery insists its worksite is safe and says contractors are punished if they bring illegal workers on the job site.
source_title: Appeal or Deal: SK Battery has two choices to save 2,600 jobs | FOX 5 Atlanta

facts: Solvay is a Belgian-French multinational chemical company established in 1863, with its headquarters located in Neder-Over-Heembeek, Brussels, Belgium.
Since the end of 2023, following its demerger with the creation of the new Syensqo entity, Solvay has specialized in essential chemistry and employs over 9,000 people in 40 countries.
In 2023, Solvay reached €4,880 million in revenues and €1,246 million of underlying EBITDA.
On December 8, 2023, shareholders approved the plan to spin off Solvay into two independent, publicly listed companies: Solvay and Syensqo.
Solvay retained its essential chemistry activities and employs 9,000 people and generates an annual turnover of €4.88 billion.
The traditional chemical activities, such as soda, hydrogen peroxide and adhesives, will continue under the Solvay name and will be led by Philippe Kehren.
The other activities, such as plastics for electronics and rechargeable batteries, have been transferred to a new company called Syensqo with Ilham Kadri as chairman.
Syensqo began its specialty chemicals activities with 13,200 employees and an annual turnover of €7.9 billion.
In 2024, Solvay announced its plan to supply Europe with rare earth metals for permanent magnets used in EVs and wind turbines from its refurbished plant in France.
SolviCore is a joint venture by Umicore and Solvay in the field of fuel cells that is already pre-marketing membrane-electrode assemblies for different types of fuel cells for portable or mobile use.
New generation lithium batteries for hybrid vehicles components make use of Solvay fluorinated polymers in order to reduce carbon dioxide emissions.
Its United States subsidiary, Solvay America, Inc., is based in Houston, Texas.
In 2020, Solvay Specialty Polymers USA, now Syensqo, was the subject of litigation for its contaminating water sources and soil with per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS).

In November 2020, the New Jersey Attorney General announced its filing of two lawsuits against Solvay Specialty Polymers USA, LLC and Arkema Inc., alleging they are two companies responsible for widespread contamination from toxic PFAS emanating from a Gloucester County facility.

In 2021, Delaware has reached an agreement with Solvay Specialty Polymers mandating a thorough environmental investigation under the supervision of the DNREC to assess the extent of the contamination.

Founded in 1863 by Ernest Solvay and his brother Alfred Solvay to produce sodium carbonate by the Solvay process, the company has diversified into two main sectors of activity: chemicals and plastics.
It was formerly also active in pharmaceuticals, but agreed to sell that entire division to Abbott Labs for €4.5 billion in September 2009, a deal completed in February 2010.
In April 2011, the firm agreed to the €3.4 billion acquisition of French-based chemicals company Rhodia which was completed in September 2011.
On December 3, 2015, Solvay launched a share issue sent to existing shareholders, completing funding for the $5.5 billion purchase of Cytec Industries Inc.
Key people include Pierre Gurdijan (Chairman) and Philippe Kehren (CEO).
Executive committee includes Alexandre Blum (CFO), Lanny Duvall (COO), and Dr. Mark van Bijsterveld (Chief People Officer).
source_title: Solvay S.A. - Wikipedia

source_title: Solvay Speciality Polymers USA, LLC || Current

facts: Magnet wires are increasingly used in electric vehicles for applications such as inverter drive motors, motor starter generators, and transformers.
The invention provides magnet wire insulation using a thermoplastic polyaryletherketone (PAEK) composition without requiring an adhesive layer.
Conventional enamel insulation requires successive baking passes that lower adhesive force and can cause solvent blisters or reduced flexibility.
The new insulation composition eliminates the need for adhesive agents, enamel, or promoters between the conductor and the thermoplastic layer.
The insulation composition comprises at least one PAEK polymer and 6 to 40 wt.% of talc relative to the total weight.
The composition must have a melt viscosity of at least 120 Pa s when measured according to ASTM D3835 at 400°C and 1000 s⁻¹.
Talc content can range from 5.0 to 10.0 wt.% or 10.0 to 26.0 wt.%, with the latter range providing good adhesion to the conductor.
The composition may use a combination of two PEEK polymers (PEEK1 and PEEK2) that differ in melt viscosity.
PEEK1 may exhibit a viscosity between 350 and 600 Pa s, while PEEK2 may exhibit a viscosity between 100 and 200 Pa s.
The weight ratio of PEEK1 to PEEK2 may range from 50/50 to 70/30.
Commercial PEEK is available as KetaSpire® polyetheretherketone from Solvay Specialty Polymers USA, LLC.
The composition may optionally include a polyarylethersulfone (PAES) polymer such as polyphenylsulfone (PPSU), polyethersulfone (PES), or polysulfone (PSU).
PPSU is commercially available as RADEL® from Solvay Specialty Polymers USA, L.L.C.
PES is commercially available as VERADEL® from Solvay Specialty Polymers USA, L.L.C.
PSU is commercially available as UDEL® from Solvay Specialty Polymers USA, L.L.C.
The amount of PAES polymer may range from 1.0 to 45.0 wt.% based on the total weight of PAEK and PAES polymers.
A preferred composition ratio includes 55.0 to 70.0 wt% PEEK and 45.0 to 30.0 wt% PPSU.
Talc particles may have a D50 particle size lower than 10.0 μm, preferably between 1.0 and 5.0 μm.
Particle size distribution analysis can be performed using equipment supplied by Micromeritics Instruments Corporation in Morcross, Georgia, USA.
Nucleating agents such as boron nitride, graphite, graphene, or mica may be added to speed up crystallization and increase wire coating throughput.
Optional plastic additives like colorants, UV stabilizers, heat stabilizers, antioxidants, and flame retardants may comprise no more than 50 wt.% of the composition.
The insulation layer can be applied directly to a conductor via extrusion or after surface treatments like flame treatment, mechanical abrasion, or chemical etching.
The technology addresses high-temperature adhesion challenges and windability requirements for EV motor outputs that generate increased heat.
Square or rectangular wire shapes present additional stress challenges on corners and edges compared to round wires.

facts: Stryten Energy is helping to create a domestic supply chain that will contribute to job growth and economic development.
Tax credits, grants and investments in the workforce are creating high-quality manufacturing jobs through the clean energy supply chain.
The state of Georgia is second in the nation in clean energy investments, with projects worth $23.12 billion and creating almost 29,500 jobs.

Since the Inflation Reduction Act and Bipartisan Infrastructure Law were passed, clean energy companies have announced more than 271,713 new jobs in 47 states, representing over $352 billion in new investments.

This law includes vital Investment Tax Credits for domestically sourced and manufactured standalone clean energy storage intended to lower costs, increase energy efficiency and strengthen energy security for America.
Stryten Energy is headquartered in Georgia.
Stryten Energy offers advanced lead, lithium, vanadium redox flow or a hybrid of battery chemistries.
Customers receive the optimal solution for their application and energy storage duration requirements based on this range of technologies.

Stryten’s battery technology strategy and investments continue to innovate, build and strengthen U.S. manufacturing and material supply chains for lead, lithium and vanadium batteries to ensure they are sustainable for years to come.

Scott Childers, Vice President, Essential Power, stated that the path to energy independence in the United States begins in Georgia and Stryten Energy is helping to create a domestic supply chain that will contribute to job growth and economic development.

Domestic manufacturing of battery energy storage systems is a crucial part of creating a future of cleaner, abundant energy while ensuring energy security and resiliency for businesses, consumers and the country, according to Childers.
At the 2024 Georgia Technology Summit, Stryten Energy was recognized as a Top 10 Innovative Company in Georgia by the Technology Association of Georgia (TAG).
This annual program recognizes Georgia-based companies of all sizes for their contributions to innovation and economic growth within the state’s technology sector.
Governor Brian Kemp declared March 28 as Georgia Technology and Innovation Day during his opening remarks at the summit.
Kemp stated that Georgia’s technology industry and innovative culture are essential parts of its continued economic success.
Melissa Floyd, Vice President, Communications and Digital Marketing, delivered an overview of Stryten Energy to attendees at the summit.
Floyd highlighted Stryten's work contributing to the transition to clean energy.

Melissa Floyd stated that Georgia has long been known as a leader in software technology but is now seeing a shift toward investments in standing-up manufacturing plants that produce batteries, solar panels, EVs and other next-generation technologies.
Stryten Energy is proud to be part of the growing energy ecosystem centered in the southeast and concentrated in Georgia, according to Floyd.
Georgia ranks among the top 10 states in the nation in total energy consumption.
U.S. power consumption is projected to triple by 2050.
To meet increasing demand for energy, production from renewable energy sources will need to scale up 9-13 times in the next 30 years.
Stryten Energy is advancing energy storage in Georgia with a wide range of projects.
In August 2023, Stryten Energy installed a VRFB system with Snapping Shoals EMC, a non-profit electric cooperative.
The installation provides an opportunity to test flow battery technology in a utility setting for future R&D and scaling up to megawatt-hour systems.
This project serves as a proof point for emerging LDES technology.
This project is the first VRFB energy storage system manufactured and installed in Georgia.
Stryten plans for this installation to be the first of many as it helps the state prepare for future energy demands.
A lead battery energy storage (BESS) project is already underway for VICARS Community Center, connecting the BESS to a new microgrid, solar array and the legacy power grid.
This resilience hub will provide neighbors with a location where they can safely gather in the aftermath of a power outage or severe weather event.
Groundbreaking for this project is scheduled for April 30, 2024.
Stryten Energy is collaborating with the Georgia Tech Carbon Neutral Energy Solutions Laboratory (CNES) to fine-tune its advanced battery technology.
A lead BESS and a VRFB BESS are being placed at CNES to serve as a “living laboratory” to further develop the products and applications.
Another project will target solar arrays plus BESS microgrids for EV Charging Stations, providing support for sustainability and resiliency on the Georgia Tech campus.

Through these innovative projects, Stryten Energy is helping shape the future of energy storage technology in Georgia that will be important on a national scale for U.S. energy independence and security.
source_title: Stryten Energy Is Advancing Georgia’s Clean Energy Industry with Innovative Battery Technologies

facts: Georgia Institute of Technology (Georgia Tech) and energy storage manufacturer Stryten Energy are collaborating to give new life to lead batteries, a technology with more than 160 years of history.
A new lead battery energy storage system (BESS) was unveiled last week and is now located on Georgia Tech’s Atlanta campus.
The BESS will serve as an experimentation site for advanced research on medium-duration energy storage solutions and applications.
The lead BESS is designed to round out Georgia Tech’s clean energy offering alongside a previously-installed solar array and a new electric vehicle (EV) charging testbed.
The system will enable bi-directional EV charging and load shifting of peak solar power generation.
The system will provide backup power and support Georgia Tech’s microgrid and grid-interactive efficient buildings program.
The project creates a real-time, real-world testbed for understanding how lead batteries perform in advanced energy applications, particularly in institutional environments.

A renewed focus on lead batteries for stationary storage reflects the rapidly diversifying energy storage landscape, which is growing more saturated with novel and legacy chemistries alongside dominant lithium-ion batteries.
Lead batteries offer advantages over lithium-ion devices, including a proven safety record, recyclability, and domestic availability of materials.
At comparable deployment costs, lead is ideal for smaller, behind-the-meter applications, according to Scott Childers, vice president of essential power at Stryten Energy.
The growing US policy focus on building secure supply chains is making lead batteries increasingly appealing.
Stryten’s lead systems are 100% domestically built.
Stryten has institutionalized a 98%-plus closed-loop recycling behavior for its lead systems.

Researchers from Georgia Tech’s Carbon Neutral Energy Solutions Laboratory and the Strategic Energy Institute (SEI) will collect and analyze data on system performance, lifecycle impact, and grid interaction.

Richard Simmons, director of research and studies at the SEI, stated the BESS will complement in-house microgrid and distributed-energy research aimed at identifying and quantifying benefits, and help optimize control strategies.
Georgia Tech plans to integrate BESS modeling and experiments into undergraduate and graduate-level courses in energy systems and sustainability.
Researchers have already developed a digital twin model of the battery system for students to simulate the operational and economic performance of various battery profiles.
The digital twin will demonstrate how battery profiles interact with locally generated rooftop solar energy and real-time electricity prices.
For Stryten Energy, the project provides an opportunity to validate its technology in a live operational setting while contributing to long-term research for next-generation batteries.
Scott Childers stated that driving commercial adoption is a core goal, with data from the collaboration fueling further innovation in the space.
Childers added that Georgia Tech’s third-party validation of real-world equipment use will drive confidence for businesses and investors to adopt distributed energy resources.
source_title: US' Georgia Tech, Stryten Energy tap lead battery innovation - Energy Storage

facts: Stryten Energy announced it would expand its American manufacturing capacity to 24 GWh.

Mike Judd stated that Stryten Energy is committed to growing its domestic energy storage capacity to serve the needs of customers and partners in the United States.

The company has battery manufacturing sites in Arkansas, Georgia, Iowa, Kansas, and Virginia.

The company will invest in increasing its processing capacity of recycled plastic from spent batteries to use in new battery components.
Stryten recently opened a lithium battery assembly plant in Georgia that supports military and industrial battery needs with its proprietary lithium module technology.
The company is partnering with Dragonfly Energy to bring advanced lithium batteries to its automotive aftermarket and battery distributor partners.
Stryten recently announced the launch of Storion Energy LLC by one of its affiliates, Stryten Critical E-Storage LLC, with a subsidiary of Largo and Largo Clean Energy Corp.

Storion Energy is focused on removing the barriers to entry for battery manufacturers to domestically source price-competitive electrolytes used in vanadium redox flow batteries (VRFB) for long-duration energy storage applications in the United States.

facts: Stryten Energy LLC is a U.S.-based energy storage solutions provider.
The company added to its in-house battery testing and analysis capabilities with a new laboratory space.
A ribbon-cutting celebration for the new battery lab was held on August 26 at Stryten Energy’s new corporate headquarters.
Attendees at the event included staff members from the offices of Senator Jon Ossoff, Senator Raphael Warnock, Representative Rich McCormick, Representative Andrew Clyde, and local community leaders.
Event attendees toured the new battery lab and an onsite working solar plus energy storage microgrid.
Lieutenant Governor Burt Jones issued a proclamation recognizing the celebration.

In his proclamation, Lieutenant Governor Burt Jones stated: “Through innovative projects, Stryten Energy is helping shape the future of energy storage technology in Georgia that will be important on a national scale for U.S. energy independence and security. Stryten Energy forging a brighter future for economic development in Georgia and fulfilling a shared commitment to innovation and growth, harnessing collective talents for the prosperity of the state of Georgia.”
Mike Judd serves as CEO and President of Stryten Energy.

Mike Judd stated: “For the U.S. to achieve long-term energy resiliency and energy security, we’ll need a range of solutions across battery chemistries,” and emphasized the need to stress test lead and lithium batteries to achieve market readiness for newer chemistries such as vanadium.
With the expanded lab, Stryten Energy will collaborate with new and existing customers to find additional applications for various battery chemistries and strengthen existing ones.

The lab supports Stryten Energy’s engineering, research and development, and quality assurance departments, as well as manufacturing facilities, in new product development, technology evaluations, warranty investigations, and root cause analysis.

The expanded facility includes a Corporate Materials Lab that performs tests on various battery materials, including electrolyte, polypropylene, lead, and lead alloys for use in automotive, heavy-duty commercial, industrial, lithium, and vanadium redox flow batteries (VRFB).

The facility includes a Lead Battery Test Lab that performs electrical performance and teardown testing on automotive, industrial, and heavy-duty commercial batteries, including enhanced flooded (EFB), conventional flooded, and absorbed glass mat (AGM) batteries.

The facility includes a Lithium Battery Test Lab, the newest addition, which focuses on conducting non-destructive functional charge/discharge, environmental, and UL-related testing on lithium battery products.
The new lab will serve as a model to other local energy technology companies and further strengthen Georgia’s claim as one of the key power players in the clean energy sector.

facts: Stryten Energy cut the ribbon on a 20 kW/120 kWh demonstration vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) at Snapping Shoals EMC, a utility provider in Georgia, US.
The system is the first VRFB system made and installed in the state of Georgia.
The electrolyte for the system was manufactured in Maryland.
Stryten Energy is evaluating other suppliers located in Utah and Nevada.
Stryten Energy designed and sized the smaller system to fit the needs of Snapping Shoals EMC so that testing can last more than six hours.

The system allows simulation of almost any operating schedule to gauge battery compatibility as defined by DOE national labs for applicability with renewable energy sources and other FTM/BTM applications.
Testing is expected to take six months.
Testing covers applications including energy cost control, peak shaving, avoiding curtailment, and renewable integration.
Future batteries of this scale will be mostly behind the meter.
Department of Energy data cited by Stryten shows Georgia has some 3.6 GW of solar, wind, and storage capacity.
Almost 1.1 GW of additional planned clean energy capacity is in the works in Georgia, which will power more than 155,000 homes.

facts: NeoVolta has raised about 23 million dollars in gross proceeds through financing deals in December 2025 and January 2026.
The capital is intended to support working capital and fund the company's commitments to its joint venture, NeoVolta Power.
The joint venture involves NeoVolta, system integrator PotisEdge, and solar company Longi.
Longi increased its stake in PotisEdge to approximately 62% in November 2025, gaining full control and marking its entry into the energy storage sector.

Both companies described this as a partnership, with Longi launching an Energy Storage One-Stop Solution that includes solar, hydrogen, and storage, initially for markets like the UK, Germany, Italy, and Spain.
Also in November 2025, NeoVolta secured a 13 million dollar private placement led by Infinite Grid Capital, partly to fund the battery energy storage system manufacturing facility in Georgia.
The company has finalized its initial 7 million dollar investment in the joint venture, which is the first of three planned funding milestones.
NeoVolta stated this funding reduces its immediate capital risks and provides sufficient working capital to support its core residential and commercial energy storage business expansion.

The total planned capital contribution to the joint venture occurs in three phases: an initial 7 million dollars completed in January 2026, an 8 million dollar second milestone by April 30, 2026, and a 10 million dollar amount at the plant's commissioning.
The operating agreement also allows for up to 15 million dollars in extra contributions until June 30, 2027, if needed for capacity, growth, or working capital.
The Georgia plant is designed for an initial annual production capacity of 2 gigawatt-hours, with potential expansion to 8 gigawatt-hours.
Mass production is expected to begin by mid-2026.
NeoVolta intends to manufacture utility-scale and commercial and industrial energy storage products there, broadening its focus from residential and smaller commercial segments.
The company noted the facility is structured to qualify for Section 45X Advanced Manufacturing Production Tax Credits and meets domestic content requirements.

It highlighted the plant's revenue potential, illustrating that 2 gigawatt-hours of annual production could represent approximately 400 million dollars of annual revenue at an illustrative price of 200 dollars per kilowatt-hour at full utilization, clarifying this example is not a forecast or guarantee of future results.
source_title: NeoVolta Funding for Georgia Battery Plant: $23M Raised, Production Starts Mid-2026 - News and Statistics - IndexBox

facts: Storion Energy LLC intends to become a leading U.S.-based manufacturer of vanadium electrolyte and develop additional vanadium flow battery solutions for the long duration energy storage sector.
The company is established as a joint venture between Largo Clean Energy Corp., a subsidiary of Largo Inc., and Stryten Critical E-Storage, LLC, an affiliate of Stryten Energy LLC.
Storion will operate from locations in Wilmington, Massachusetts and Alpharetta, Georgia.
Upon closing, Stryten will pay US$1 million in cash directly to Largo and contribute a total of US$6 million in cash to Storion over time to fund operations.
LCE and Stryten will each hold a 50% equity interest in Storion upon closing, with customary pre-emption rights and anti-dilution protections.
Board representation for Storion will be generally proportional to ownership, with Stryten holding one additional seat as long as LCE and Stryten hold similar ownership interests.
At closing, Largo will amend its Safekeeping and Supply Agreements with LPV to adjust commercial terms and assign the Safekeeping Agreement to Storion.
At closing, Largo and Storion will enter into a separate supply agreement providing Storion a right of first offer to purchase vanadium products from LPV.

Francesco D’Alessio, Chief Commercial Officer of Largo and President of LCE, stated the partnership aims to maximize the value of Largo’s vanadium products and leverage Storion’s patented purification process to accelerate vanadium electrolyte manufacture and deployment.
The transaction is expected to increase demand for vanadium from Largo Physical Vanadium Corp. and benefit the vanadium sector as a whole.

Storion Energy aims to remove barriers to entry for battery manufacturers by providing domestically sourced, price-competitive electrolyte for vanadium redox flow batteries used in long-duration energy storage solutions greater than four hours.

VRFB technology is described as a safe and reliable option to provide long-duration energy storage greater than four hours to help ensure grid stability and facilitate increased utilization of renewables for businesses and consumers.
Gallatin Capital LLC acted as financial advisor to Largo, and Linklaters LLP acted as legal counsel for the transaction.
The joint venture is subject to customary closing conditions and forward-looking statements regarding execution of strategic goals and ability to raise additional funds.
source_title: Largo and Stryten Energy to Form Storion Energy for the Manufacture of Vanadium Electrolyte and Battery Components for Long Duration Energy Storage - Junior Mining Network

facts: Stryten Energy LLC is a leading U.S.-based energy storage solutions provider.

Stryten Energy will showcase its advanced battery energy storage solutions (BESS) at CES 2026 in Las Vegas, taking place January 6-9.
Visitors to the Stryten Energy booth (6929) will learn how the company’s battery-first, technology-agnostic approach is driving energy resilience and independence for critical sectors.
Mike Judd serves as the CEO of Stryten Energy.

Mike Judd stated that at CES, Stryten is showing how it is driving transformation with advanced battery solutions built in the U.S., turning energy storage into a strategic advantage to strengthen communities, secure supply chains, and power America’s energy future.
According to Battery Council International (BCI), U.S. electricity demand is projected to quadruple by 2030, primarily driven by AI adoption and the electrification of business sectors.
Batteries support surging energy demand by storing excess energy, balancing supply and demand, and providing reliable backup power.
Domestic battery production strengthens energy security by reducing reliance on foreign supply chains.
Stryten Energy’s portfolio includes advanced lead, lithium, and vanadium technologies.
The company uses a battery-first, technology-agnostic model to ensure customers have the right chemistry for the right application.
Lead BESS delivers grid resilience and reliable backup power through rapid response, voltage stabilization, and a modular design that scales from microgrids to large industrial sites.
Lead BESS integrates with renewable and hydrocarbon energy sources for peak shaving and safeguarding against power outages.
The domestic circular economy of lead batteries makes them one of the most sustainable energy storage options available.
Stryten’s Lithium Mobile Microgrid utilizes a modified Jeep to bring power to remote areas and provide disaster relief with emergency power resources.

The hybrid diesel-electric vehicle uses proprietary software to efficiently manage carbon-based and renewable energy sources, storing power in lithium and/or lead batteries for use during prolonged power outages.

Stryten Energy will host two in-booth panel discussions at CES 2026 addressing AI demand, microgrids, and distributed energy.

The second panel, "Local Power, National Impact, the Blueprint for Building Resilience Behind the Meter," is scheduled for Wednesday, January 7, at 11:00 a.m.
