facts: Proposed SungEel battery recycling facility is scheduled to be located at Hayestone Brady Industrial Park in Stephens County, Georgia.

Groundbreaking on the Hayestone Brady facility was originally announced for March 2023 but has been delayed due to equipment upgrades and building drawings, with a new timeline pending general contractor selection.
In July 2022, an explosion at SungEel HiTech’s Hungary plant seriously injured one worker and left three others with minor injuries.

The Hungarian explosion was caused by a gas leak due to faulty equipment installation by an outside vendor prior to the facility opening; the site was not yet in operation for battery recycling at the time.
The Georgia facility will use vacuum-type vessels instead of positive pressure type vessels, which will not be used in US facilities.
SungEel Hungary operations were fined by Hungarian authorities because battery scrap was stored beyond permitted volume, prompting the company to implement its own management process for compliance.

A spokesperson addressed reports of inspectors suffering rashes from touring the Hungary plant, stating it was an exaggerated local article and SungEel never received hospital diagnosis notifications or bills for such incidents.

The company withdrew plans for a battery recycling plant in Endicott, New York, due to public outcry in 2020, and similarly withdrew plans for a location two hours north of Nuremburg, Germany, earlier in the year.
Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) stated they communicate with community partners during site selection and expect all companies to comply with federal, state, and local laws.
SungEel will have fire extinguishing systems such as sprinklers and sensors for initial detection in accordance with FM Global standards, which are US fire regulations/insurance regulations.
SungEel met with local fire personnel and E911 last year and plans a workshop with the Fire Marshall and E911 before operation to ensure confidence in safety commitments.

Feedstock/scrap will be stored in a safe condition and discharged by water or electric discharge before coming to the warehouse, with oxygen input eliminated in the process to eliminate fire risk during workflow.
Governor Brian Kemp announced in August 2022 that SungEel Recycling Park Georgia, LLC would invest more than $37 million in the new recycling facility.
The project will bring 104 new jobs to Stephens County, including positions for technicians, operators, administrative and sales staff, with no management positions mentioned.
The average pay range for the 104 jobs is above the county’s current average pay wage of $22.85/hour.
SungEel plans to utilize North Georgia Technical College’s services for employee training and hiring, with full expectation to hire locally qualified individuals.
Stephens County Development Authority (SCDA) Chairman Brian Akin noted the SCDA worked with SungEel for eight months learning about the company and its processes before the August 2022 announcement.

GDEcD joined Kemp in welcoming the facility, citing Georgia’s official interest in the electric vehicle industry and believing SungEel’s proximity to other members of Georgia’s electric mobility ecosystem will lead to success.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated, “SungEel is a company at the cutting edge of sustainable technology, and we believe SungEel’s proximity to other members of Georgia’s electric mobility ecosystem will lead to amazing success.”
The facility will not use an incinerator or natural gas combustion, utilizing an electric-powered dryer instead.
The recycling process has been used since 2008, but equipment has been upgraded for the Georgia site.
The process is fully compliant with Federal and State permitting regulations, and the air permit will be obtained prior to starting operation and made available to the public upon submission.
All materials are recycled for beneficial reuse, with nothing going to a landfill or city wastewater system that cannot be processed without issue.

SCDA President and CEO Brittany Ivey noted negotiations were ongoing for an MOU including incentives, job creation commitments, investment timeline, and land sale terms, noting the SCDA is asking full price for the land in Hayestone Brady.
If the MOU stays the same, the project will put SungEel on the tax digest within the first two years of operation.
Ivey noted a shift from 2017 when they gave land away to now asking full price for it with SungEel.
In September 2022, SungEel stated an expected production start date of December 2023.
The Georgia proposal uses an electric-powered dryer instead of the natural gas-fired dryer included in the New York proposal, and will recover more electrolyte material.
SungEel confirmed there are no PFAs released in the process and is actively working with EPD to ensure nothing exceeds state or federal regulations.
The facility is not a storage facility; it will have separate areas for raw materials and short-term finished goods storage.

SungEel committed to six-month employee training in fire precaution procedures required at all other facilities, after meeting with local fire personnel, the EMA director, and the county’s commercial building inspector.
source_title: Final installment of SungEel Q&A series - WNEG

Area Development Magazine notes that SungEel is at the cutting edge of sustainable technology and its proximity to other members of Georgia’s electric mobility ecosystem will lead to success.

facts: Lithium-ion battery recycler and raw materials provider SungEel Recycling Park Georgia, LLC., will locate its first U.S. recycling facility in Georgia.
The company is a subsidiary of the Korea-based global industry leader SungEel HiTech Co., Ltd.
The company will invest more than $37 million in the new facility.
The new facility will create 104 jobs.
SungEel Recycling Park Georgia’s new facility will be located at the Hayestone Brady Business Park, a Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified site in Toccoa.
The GRAD Program is a proactive effort by Georgia’s economic development community to develop a portfolio of available sites ready for fast-track development.
Brian Akin serves as Chairman of the Stephens County Development Authority and has worked with SungEel HiTech over the past eight months.
Brian Akin stated that investments in infrastructure, workforce development, and education were significant factors in SungEel HiTech's selection process to expand to Toccoa-Stephens County.
With a base in Stephens County, the plant will be located near SK Battery America in Commerce, another South Korean company.
Operations at the new facility are expected to begin in early 2024.
Over the next few years, the company will be hiring for technicians, operators, administrative, and sales roles.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that SungEel is a company at the cutting edge of sustainable technology.
Pat Wilson believes SungEel’s proximity to other members of Georgia’s electric mobility ecosystem will lead to amazing success.
Pat Wilson noted that preparing key industries like the auto industry for the next technological revolution means creating the jobs of the future and protecting the opportunities of today.
Pat Wilson expressed gratitude to partners in Stephens County for their investment in speed-to-market solutions like site preparation.
With more than 10 years of patented technology, SungEel specializes in complete recycling technology using a 100 percent full circulatory system.
The recycling system processes end-of-life batteries and battery manufacturing scrap with a metal recovery rate of more than 95 percent.
Recovered metals from the process include nickel, cobalt, and lithium.
SungEel Representative Suk Jae Yim noted that the company’s entry into Georgia is the last piece of the puzzle to build a sustainable ecosystem of Georgia’s electric vehicle supply chain.
Suk Jae Yim stated that his company will build a U.S. eco-friendly industrial ecosystem in line with the expectations of the State of Georgia and Stephens County.

Georgia’s prime location, extensive infrastructure, skilled workforce, and business-friendly climate have made it an attractive location for rapidly developing industries focused on creating a sustainable future.
Georgia is cultivating a vertically integrated supply chain to help companies increase efficiencies by reducing reliance on imported materials.
EV-related projects have contributed more than $13 billion in investments and more than 18,100 new jobs to Georgia since 2020.
SungEel HiTech Co., Ltd. (KOSDAQ, KQ 365340) is headquartered in Korea.
SungEel HiTech operates facilities in South Korea, China, Malaysia, India, and Hungary.
SungEel HiTech has new facilities underway in Poland and Germany.

SungEel HiTech’s objectives are to further improve the environment and create value for customers, stockholders, and communities through innovation in recycling technology, material processing, and products.

facts: Chairman Lee Kang-myung stated that rebounding metal prices and customer demand for non-Chinese materials will allow Sungeel Hitech to return to profitability in the fourth quarter of this year.
The company recorded 152.9 billion won in revenue last year.
Sungeel Hitech reported an operating loss of 54.9 billion won last year due to a slowdown in electric vehicle and battery markets.
Depreciation of its new recycling and hydro facility, completed in June, contributed to the poor earnings.
The Gunsan facility crushes scrubs and dead batteries into black mass to produce nickel, cobalt, lithium, and other raw materials.
Nickel and lithium prices increased 10% in the first quarter of this year compared to the fourth quarter of 2024.
Chinese companies are struggling to maintain low prices for these raw materials despite being the largest producer.
In October last year, the US House of Representatives passed a law forbidding the use of Chinese batteries, which will take effect in October 2028.
The US has already imposed 45% tariffs on Chinese batteries.
Sungeel Hitech plans to start operations of its first North American recycling factory soon at a Recycling Park in Indiana that will collect scrubs and dead batteries.
A second North American factory will extract usable materials from the collected batteries.
Another Recycling Park is planned in Georgia, which will process scrubs from partners including Samsung SDI, SK On, and Hyundai Motor.

facts: Jacobsen and Textron Specialized Vehicles will combine their operations by the close of 2017.
The combination will allow them to better serve shared customers and operate more efficiently through a common supply chain and efficiencies of scale in manufacturing and back-office operations.
Kevin Holleran, president and CEO of Textron Specialized Vehicles, stated the combination will be nimble, optimally sized, and well equipped to meet evolving customer needs.
The companies are working carefully to ensure a seamless transition that is invisible to customers.
They will maintain the level of support and service – before, during, and after the sale – that both Jacobsen and TSV customers expect and demand.
Jacobsen’s operations in Charlotte, North Carolina, will relocate to Textron Specialized Vehicles facilities in Augusta, Georgia.
The first phase of relocation is expected to be completed by the end of March.
All operations are planned to be relocated by the close of 2017.
Jacobsen’s field sales and service teams will remain in place.
Jacobsen’s operations in Coatesville, Indiana, and Ipswich, U.K., will continue.

facts: Textron’s Georgia plant sustained heavy storm damage as severe weather rolled through Augusta, Georgia Monday night, according to a report from CBS News 12.
The building was badly damaged by storms overnight Tuesday.
Repair efforts are ongoing throughout the day Wednesday.
An official with Textron tells News 12 that some of the 1,200 employees returned to work Wednesday.
While they said the cleanup won’t take long, the E-Z-Go plant sustained shattered glass, uprooted trees and roofs torn off buildings.

Brandon Haddock, director of communications for Textron Specialized Vehicles, stated that they are still assessing the extent of that damage to determine what kind of impact is expected on their operations.
Textron officials say they aren’t sure when workers will come back, but they’re working to make it happen as soon as possible.

facts: Located in Augusta, GA, Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc. and its Textron Off Road brand launched the Wildcat XX, a new high-performance side-by-side designed for off-roading.
The Wildcat XX was designed in collaboration with off-road racing legend Robby Gordon.
The vehicle features an off-road racing-inspired suspension system delivering best-in-class handling with more than 18 inches of travel in the front and rear.
It is equipped with FOX 2.5 PODIUM QS3 Shocks with Bottom-Out Control that are precision-tuned at the factory.
The Wildcat XX includes an all-new TEAM front gear case and rear transaxle providing 2WD/4WD and 4WD lock functionality.
It is powered by a 125HP, 998cc three-cylinder EFI engine, which is the highest horsepower aspirated engine in its class.
John Collins, Vice President, Consumer for Textron Specialized Vehicles, stated that the Wildcat XX was inspired by extreme off-road racing vehicles and built to outperform competitors.
The front suspension features unequal-length A-arms that angle the tire into every turn to better negotiate hairpin turns.
A rear trailing arm design results in 80 percent less track width change in the rear compared to competitive vehicles, providing a smoother ride.
The vehicle is built with durability and serviceability in mind, featuring a race-inspired cage, double-shear joints, a forged aluminum front knuckle, and HSLA tubing throughout.
The engine is top-mounted to avoid damage during bottom outs.
The front steel clip and rear bottom skid are easily removable for service access to the powertrain, transaxle, engine, and front gear case.
The cargo box is removable without requiring any tools.
The Wildcat XX features the largest cab in the industry, ergonomic seating, and an automotive style interior.
It includes a standard 65-amp alternator to power accessories.
An integrated wiring system cuts light bar installation time in half.
Electronic Power Steering (EPS) is standard.
The cargo box has a class-leading 300-lb capacity and fits up to a 32-inch spare tire.
It features a class-leading four-gallon glove box to protect gear in the cab.
The vehicle is available in two packages with more than 30 purpose-built accessories from Textron Off Road.
Ground clearance is 14 inches.
It comes equipped with 15-inch custom KMC wheels and 30-inch CST Behemoth tires.
The Wildcat XX is available in Lime Green, Satin Charcoal Metallic, or a limited edition Horizon Blue.
The vehicle is covered by a six-month warranty.
Textron Off Road's product line includes gas and electric off-road vehicles for work and play, including the Prowler, Stampede™, Havoc™, and Wildcat™ lines of side-by-sides and Alterra™ ATVs.

Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc. manufactures golf cars, utility and personal transportation vehicles, snowmobiles, side-by-sides, all-terrain vehicles, professional turf-care equipment, and ground support equipment under brands including E-Z-GO®, Cushman®, Arctic Cat®, Textron Off Road, Jacobsen®, Dixie Chopper®, Ransomes®, TUG™, Douglas™, Premier™, and Safeaero™.

Textron Inc. is a multi-industry company leveraging a global network of aircraft, defense, industrial, and finance businesses, known for brands such as Bell Helicopter, Cessna, Beechcraft, Hawker, Jacobsen, Kautex, Lycoming, E-Z-GO, Greenlee, Textron Off Road, Arctic Cat, Textron Systems, and TRU Simulation + Training.

facts: E-Z-GO, a Textron Specialized Vehicles business, launched its ELiTE Series lithium golf cars as a fleet offering on January 26, 2017.
The ELiTE Series vehicles are activated by Samsung SDI lithium technology and feature zero-maintenance batteries backed by a five-year unlimited amp-hour warranty.
E-Z-GO vehicles are designed and manufactured in Augusta, Ga. by Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc., a Textron Inc. (NYSE: TXT) company.

Michael R. Parkhurst, Vice President, Golf for Textron Specialized Vehicles, stated that the partnership with Samsung SDI resulted in a giant step forward for high-efficiency vehicles and zero-maintenance battery power.

The ELiTE Series vehicles are powered by hundreds of Samsung SDI lithium cells loaded into a single battery pack controlled by an advanced Battery Management System that monitors efficiency, temperature, state of charge, and health.
These batteries eliminate the need for watering, terminal post checkups, and cleaning required by traditional lead-acid batteries, reducing maintenance time and increasing vehicle uptime.
The ELiTE Series vehicles are 59 percent more efficient than the Club Car Precedent and 52 percent more efficient than the Yamaha Drive AC.

The vehicles support opportunity charging, allowing courses to plug them in for quick sessions between rounds to rapidly restore energy, significantly reducing overall charging time compared to lead-acid batteries.
Golf course managers can cut energy costs and increase revenue from all-day uptime due to the lower power requirements for charging ELiTE Series vehicles compared to leading lead-acid competitors.
The ELiTE Series vehicle batteries are half the size and a fraction of the weight of traditional lead-acid batteries, which reduces turf damage and soil compaction caused by vehicle weight.
E-Z-GO tested the ELiTE Series vehicles at Tijeras Creek Golf Club in Rancho Santa Margarita, Calif., where 73,000 rounds of golf were played last year on challenging hilly terrain.
Rob Heslar, Director of Golf at Tijeras Creek Golf Club, reported that the ELiTE Series vehicles operate for 36 to 54 holes a day without needing to be recharged during that timeframe.
The exclusive ELiTE lithium technology will be available in six 2017 models: RXV® ELiTE, Freedom® RXV ELiTE, Freedom RXV 2+2 ELiTE, TXT® ELiTE, Freedom TXT ELiTE, and Freedom TXT 2+2 ELiTE.
The ELiTE Series vehicles were scheduled for display during the PGA Merchandise Show in Orlando, Florida.
E-Z-GO was founded in 1954 in Augusta, Georgia, and became part of Textron Inc. in 1960, operating today within the Textron Specialized Vehicles division.

Textron Specialized Vehicles markets vehicles under multiple brands including E-Z-GO®, Cushman®, Textron Off Road, Jacobsen®, Dixie Chopper®, Ransomes®, TUG™, Douglas™, Premier™, and Safeaero™ for environments ranging from golf courses to airports and theme parks.
Textron Inc. is a multi-industry company leveraging aircraft, defense, industrial, and finance businesses, known for brands such as Bell Helicopter, Cessna Aircraft Company, and E-Z-GO.

facts: Russian capital is growing in Georgia across communication, water supply, mineral waters, banking, and energy sectors.

Russian citizen Mevlud (Evgeny) Bliadze possesses 70 percent of shares of Energy LLC, which owns Dariali HPP, Larsi HPP, and Shilda HPP.
According to the National Statistics Office of Georgia, the largest portion of investments made by Russia into Georgia in 2011 can be credited precisely to Bliadze.
Energy expert Eugene Kogan states that having a big share of Russian capital in Georgia means Russia maintains economic and financial leverage over Georgia.
Several other hydropower objects owned by Russians include Avani HPP, Algeti HPP, and Skurdidi HPP.
SakRusEnergo, which runs the country’s electricity transmission system, is jointly owned by the Georgian and Russian side.
Research author Nata Dzvelishvili notes that the sale of strategic energy facilities to Russians started in the 2000s when Inter Rao entered the market.
After the August War in 2008, Enguri HPP was given to Inter Rao, followed by Khrami HPP-1 and Khrami HPP-2.
Both Khrami-1 and Khrami-2 were sold directly without allowing other investors an opportunity to participate in a competition.
Eugene Kogan says Russia wishes to maintain control over Georgia not only politically but also economically and socially.
Kogan claims that Russian investment does not provide the promised extra funds or extra employment for Georgia.
The current Georgian government is described as being dead scared of the influx of Russian capital but maintains silence and is too timid to undertake anti-Russian measures.
Liza Tavdumadze, head of Investment Projects Department of Ministry of Energy, stated that the ministry does not see the threat of any country becoming an energy monopolist in Georgia.
Kogan suggests Russia intends to get a hold of the majority of energy companies to dictate its own terms, mainly concerning price regulations.
The price of electricity in Armenia is currently set by Russia.
The research analyzing Russian capital was prepared by damoukidebloba.com and translated with financial support from IDFI (Institute for Development of Freedom of Information).
The article was authored by Tamta Jijavadze.
source_title: Russian Capital in Georgian Energy sector - GeorgianJournal

facts: ALBAform is a Georgia Mountains Works Partner.
ALBAform is hosting fellow GMW Employer Partners ONLY for a tour of its manufacturing facility.
The manufacturing facility is located in Flowery Branch, Hall County, Georgia.
source_title: Georgia Mountains Works Employer Partnership TOUR – ALBAform – Georgia Mountains Works

facts: Global EV adoption is surging, driving demand for charging infrastructure that aligns with environmental and economic goals.
Photovoltaic energy storage charging stations (PSCS) integrate solar power generation, energy storage, and EV charging into a single system.
A study published in Zhejiang Electric Power presents a novel approach to optimizing PSCS energy storage capacity by accounting for user charging behavior and PV uncertainty.
The research was led by Jiang Yu from the School of Electric Power Engineering at Nanjing Institute of Technology.
Co-authors include Lü Ganyun, Jia Dexiang from State Grid Energy Research Institute Co., Ltd., and Yu Xiangyi, Shan Tingting, Yu Ming, and Wu Qiyu from State Grid Jiangsu Electric Power Co., Ltd.
The study addresses balancing cost, reliability, and sustainability for renewable-powered charging infrastructure amid unpredictable solar generation and fluctuating demand.
China's government aims for peak carbon emissions by 2030 and carbon neutrality by 2060, catalyzing investments in clean energy technologies like PSCS.
PSCS uses on-site solar panels to generate power, stores excess energy in batteries, and supplies it to EVs during high demand or low solar output.
Proper sizing of the energy storage system is critical; oversizing increases capital expenditure, while undersizing compromises reliability and revenue potential.
Traditional optimization models rely on average PV output data, failing to capture solar variability due to weather fluctuations, cloud cover, and seasonal changes.
Traditional models also overlook dynamic EV user behavior influenced by daily travel patterns, temperature, and pricing incentives.
The researchers used an improved K-means clustering algorithm to generate representative PV output scenarios from a full year of historical solar data from a city in southern China.
The improved K-means modifies initial cluster center selection using maximum distance and uses a Gaussian kernel function instead of Euclidean distance for similarity measurement.
This approach identified three distinct PV output patterns, each associated with a specific probability of occurrence.
The team evaluated clustering quality using the silhouette coefficient; the improved K-means achieved a score of 0.628, outperforming traditional K-means (0.478) and K-means++ (0.598).

EVs were categorized into three types with distinct usage patterns: buses follow fixed routes and schedules requiring predictable charging windows, taxis operate for extended hours charging twice daily, and private cars exhibit flexible behavior often charging after work or on weekends.

The model incorporated data on daily mileage, departure times, and battery specifications; private cars were assumed to travel an average of 70 kilometers per day with a per-kilometer energy consumption of 0.149 kWh under standard conditions.
A cubic polynomial function was used to describe the relationship between ambient temperature and EV energy consumption based on empirical data.
The model accounted for air conditioning load during driving, which varies with speed and climate.

The Monte Carlo method was employed to handle stochastic driving and charging decisions by running thousands of simulations sampling departure times and mileages according to statistical distributions like log-normal for mileage.
Demand response using price signals was incorporated to influence consumer behavior and shift private car charging away from peak hours, reducing strain on the grid.

Time-of-use pricing structures were optimized to incentivize charging when solar generation is high or grid prices are low, maximizing PV self-consumption and minimizing reliance on expensive grid power.
A price elasticity matrix quantified how changes in charging prices affect load distribution across different time slots, including self-elasticity and cross-elasticity.
Dynamic pricing for private car owners flattened the overall load curve, decreasing peak demand and increasing off-peak consumption to reduce operational costs and enhance grid stability.
The economic model included carbon emissions based on the grid's hourly emission factor to reflect true electricity costs and encourage minimizing grid purchases during high-emission periods.

The optimization objective function minimizes total daily cost, which includes grid electricity purchase cost, carbon emission cost, operation and maintenance expenses, revenue from selling excess solar power to the grid, and revenue from charging EVs.
A case study validated the approach on a residential-area PSCS with a 350 kW PV installation and a maximum charging power of 100 kW.

Five configurations were compared using MATLAB and the CPLEX solver: no PV/no demand response, typical PV/no demand response, typical PV/with demand response, multi-scenario PV/no demand response, and multi-scenario PV/with demand response.
The optimal energy storage capacity for the multi-scenario PV with demand response scenario was calculated to be 461 kWh, yielding a daily profit of 4,334.17 yuan.
Systems using typical PV data required either more storage at 496 kWh or delivered lower profits at 4,218.21 yuan, highlighting the inefficiency of oversimplified models.
The multi-scenario PV with demand response scenario achieved the lowest daily CO₂ emissions at 8,218 kg, compared to over 9,300 kg in the baseline case without solar.
Enabling price-based load shifting increased daily revenue by over 100 yuan and reduced emissions by nearly 300 kg compared to the same PV model without demand response.

The study suggests extending the model to include vehicle-to-grid capabilities, allowing EVs themselves to act as distributed energy resources to enhance grid flexibility and open new revenue streams.
source_title: Optimizing Energy Storage for Solar-Powered EV Charging Stations – Electric-Vehicle.org

facts: Agriculture has defined and dominated Southwest Georgia’s economic landscape for generations.

In 2021, food and fiber production and related processing industries directly represented a value of nearly $14.7 billion to Georgia’s economy, according to a report by the University of Georgia’s Center for Agriculture and Economic Development.
The report cites data from the 2021 Georgia Farm Gate Value Report, which states these commodities represent $73.2 billion in output to Georgia’s economy.
These agricultural commodities supported 340,837 jobs in industries in the agricultural supply chain in 2021 beyond their farm gate value.
Southwest Georgia counties hold a lock on some of the top nationally ranked commodities in total production.
The state ranks No. 1 in total peanut and pecan production.
The state ranks No. 2 in cotton lint, cotton seed, and watermelon production.
The state ranks No. 3 in blueberries and cantaloupe production.
In 2021, more than half of the nation’s peanuts were grown in Southwest Georgia.
Cotton ranks first in production value in Georgia and second in the nation.
Anovion Technologies, an electric vehicle (EV) battery materials manufacturer, announced it will invest $800 million and create 400 jobs in Decatur County.
The new manufacturing facility being built at the county-owned Downrange Industrial Park will be 1.5 million square feet.
The facility will produce high-quality synthetic graphite anode, a material critical in the EV battery supply chain.
Rick McCaskill, head of the Development Authority of Bainbridge and Decatur County, stated that $50 million was put into the park’s infrastructure as a major commitment.
Anovion selected the Southwest Georgia location because of its proximity to existing and planned low- and carbon-free energy sources for power.
The company also cited short supply chains, access to existing rail infrastructure, a skilled workforce, and a business-friendly environment as selection factors.
Anovion was looking at 38 states before choosing Bainbridge.
Decatur County is a member of Locate South Georgia, a regional economic development entity comprised of 22 counties, many in Southwest Georgia.
Locate South Georgia offers an annual bus tour covering a portion of the more than 10,000-square-mile region it serves to provide project managers a firsthand look at available sites and buildings.
In November 2022, the Locate South Georgia tour included Decatur County.
A Georgia Department of Economic Development project manager saw the site during the tour and thought it might be a fit for the Anovion project.
The request for proposal (RFP) was quickly sent and accepted after the site visit.
Decatur County became Georgia’s newest member of Georgia’s EV battery ecosystem following the announcement.
Before the Anovion announcement, Locate South Georgia touted $324.5 million in new investment and 575 jobs created in 2022 among its members.
Grant Buckley, executive director of the Cordele-Crisp County Industrial Development Council and chair of Locate South Georgia, noted that the region may be starting to see EV-related spin-offs.
Buckley reported hearing inquiries from Tier 2 or Tier 3 suppliers related to the EV industry among his colleagues.
McCaskill emphasized that regional marketing efforts by Locate South Georgia and a mindset that projects help all counties have been a real economic development benefit.
source_title: Southwest Georgia: A Region on Radar - Development Authority of Bainbridge and Decatur County

facts: Anovion Technologies plans to invest $800,000,000.00 to build out 1,500,000 square feet of new space in Bainbridge, Georgia.
The company plans to occupy the new space in Bainbridge on or about October 1, 2025.
Anovion is formed by combining key assets contributed by affiliates of Amsted Graphite Materials with the acquisition of Pyroteks battery materials division.
Amsted Graphite Materials is a world leader in graphite materials science.
Pyroteks battery materials division manufactures synthetic graphite anode material critical for lithium-ion batteries.
The combined businesses create a leader in North Americas rapidly growing battery materials supply chain.
Anovion operates the largest commercially operational production capacity of synthetic graphite anode material in the United States.
The combined entities have over 140 years of history in synthetic graphite production.
Anovion will serve a growing, diverse set of demanding customers with anode technologies for lithium-ion batteries.
Eric Stopka serves as the Chief Executive Officer of Anovion Technologies.
source_title: Anovion Technologies to spend $800 Million to occupy 1,500,000 square feet of space in Bainbridge Georgia. - intelligence360

facts: Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp escalated his attack on President Joe Biden's electric vehicle policy at the groundbreaking for Anovion Technologies.
Anovion Technologies received more than $100 million to refine graphite for electric batteries from the infrastructure law signed by President Biden.

Gov. Kemp stated that Georgia's electric mobility boom is happening because the state is second to none for companies looking to invest, relocate, expand, and innovate, not because of federal government favoritism.
The political remarks were unusually partisan for a factory groundbreaking.
Anovion Technologies has an $800 million investment promise.
Anovion promises 400 new jobs in rural Bainbridge, located in Georgia's far southwest corner.

Georgia has been a top beneficiary of a nationwide electric vehicle investment boom, with more than 40 electric vehicle-related projects since 2020 pledging $22.7 billion of investment and 28,400 jobs in the state.
Gov. Kemp remarked that when President Biden and others falsely try to take credit for Georgia's success, people should remember that next year is an election year.
Democratic Sen. Jon Ossoff was likely to share his stage at the groundbreaking.
Sen. Ossoff has been Georgia's most high-profile supporter of President Biden's electric vehicle policies.
Gov. Kemp and Sen. Ossoff could be rivals for the U.S. Senate seat in 2026.
Sen. Ossoff argued that Georgia's investment boom would not have happened without Democratic policies.

Sen. Ossoff told The Associated Press it is bizarre to attend a groundbreaking and launch a political attack on the policy that made it possible, calling the governor's actions a panicked political tantrum over federal manufacturing policies' success.
Gov. Kemp has always opposed the Inflation Reduction Act, which is pumping billions into electric vehicle subsidies.
Gov. Kemp disagreed with the Inflation Reduction Act's domestic content standards, which are meant to increase America's clean-energy manufacturing capacity.
These domestic content standards make tax incentives on electric vehicles available only when the vehicle, battery, and key raw materials are all made in the United States.
Hyundai Motor Group is building a $5.5 billion plant to assemble electric vehicles and batteries in Ellabell, Georgia, near Savannah.
Hyundai has stated that the tax credits are unfair because its electric vehicles are not currently eligible.
Gov. Kemp referenced Hyundai's criticism in his speech, stating that 'that approach simply doesn't work.'
Fellow Democratic senator Raphael Warnock proposed making the tax credits more flexible.
Sen. Ossoff emphasized that benefits will be available to Hyundai once the Ellabell plant starts production.

Gov. Kemp noted that Rivian Automotive announced a $5 billion plant east of Atlanta in December 2021, and Hyundai announced its project in May 2022, both before President Biden signed the Inflation Reduction Act.
Gov. Kemp wants to make Georgia the 'e-mobility capital of the nation' as his second-term legacy.
Sen. Ossoff has claimed credit for President Biden and Democrats, including for solar panel plant expansions in northwest Georgia.
Hyundai and LG Energy Solution announced a $4.3 billion electric battery plant in May at Hyundai's new complex.
Sen. Ossoff assertively trumpeted the Hyundai-LG battery announcement while Gov. Kemp was in Israel, which chafed some Kemp administration officials.
Gov. Kemp attributed Anovion's choice of location to state and local officials, saying they 'don't posture or showboat, and they don't try to steal credit.'

Many Republicans oppose electric vehicles; former President Donald Trump told the Georgia Republican convention he would abolish President Biden's electric vehicle policies on his first day in office.
Gov. Kemp did not attend that convention due to dissatisfaction with state party leadership.

Gov. Kemp has tried to persuade Republicans to break off their support for former President Trump while simultaneously opposing a Democratic president who is lavishing electric vehicle makers with billions in incentives.
Gov. Kemp stated that unlike top-down systems like China's, Georgia is not dictating how growth happens or picking winners and losers, but is letting the market drive innovation and expansion.
Anovion Technologies is a Chicago-based firm.
Anovion's Georgia factory will produce synthetic graphite, a key ingredient for lithium batteries.
The factory benefits from content standards that are boosting domestic graphite demand.
Anovion won $117 million in federal financing to build and improve its factories.
Anovion may be able to claim federal tax credits of 10% on the costs of producing graphite and 30% on its factory investment, both part of the Inflation Reduction Act.
Sen. Ossoff stated that manufacturing is coming back to America and Georgia as intended when infrastructure and manufacturing policies were passed.
source_title: Georgia Gov. attacks Biden's electric vehicle policy at battery material maker

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp, in conjunction with the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD), announced sustained momentum for business recruitment and expansions in Fiscal Year 2024.

Between July 1, 2023, and June 30 of this year, GDEcD’s Global Commerce team supported 429 facility expansions and new locations resulting in more than $20.3 billion in investment and the commitment of 26,900 new, private sector jobs statewide.
83% of expansions and new locations are directed to communities outside the 10-county Atlanta region, representing more than 18,300 new jobs and $12.5 billion in investment.
Metro Atlanta attracted nearly $7.8 billion in investments as a logistics and business hub.
Statewide, new locations account for $12.8 billion in investments and expansions are responsible for 9,800 new jobs.

Lt. Governor Burt Jones emphasized ensuring businesses have the necessary workforce, citing the Technical College System of Georgia, University System of Georgia, College and Career Academies, and private-public partnerships building the workforce pipeline.
Speaker of the House Jon Burns highlighted historic investments in education, workforce development, and infrastructure alongside tax cuts and regulatory relief.

During FY2024, Governor and First Lady Kemp led economic development missions to Korea and Switzerland that focused on strengthening existing relationships with key investors like Hyundai Motor Group and its supplier network.
The prior year, Governor and First Lady Kemp led similar missions to Israel, France, and Switzerland.

Georgia maintained its position as a global gateway for business, landing more than 8,100 new jobs and $5.9 billion in investments from international companies expanding or locating in the U.S. market this past fiscal year.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted the state’s strong partnerships, diverse industry landscape, and range of locations providing quality-of-life options for companies.
The State of Georgia celebrated more than 50 years of continuous representation in Europe and Japan in FY2024, which remain among the top sources of foreign direct investment.
The coming fiscal year will mark 40 years of continuous representation in Korea, a partnership that earned GDEcD the James A. Van Fleet Award from the Korea Society.
All markets where Georgia maintains representation in business and trade accounted for 29% of total new jobs and investments in FY2024.
GDEcD Commissioner Kristi Brigman highlighted the state’s transition from agribusiness and manufacturing to mobility and technology, celebrating a decade of excellence and project milestones.
Manufacturing, including the aerospace, automotive, and e-mobility sectors, accounted for over 50% of expansions or new locations in FY2024.
Kia Georgia added the EV9 to its lineup in Troup County.
Toyota Industries Corporation established a campus in Jackson County.
StandardAero operates in Richmond County.
International life sciences company Gerresheimer announced a recent expansion in Fayette County.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America suppliers announced new locations in Bryan, Candler, Chatham, Coweta, Effingham, Harris, and Laurens County.
SOLARCYCLE, an innovator in solar panel recycling, announced plans for a new location in Polk County to support Qcells’ operations.
Investments in food processing and agribusiness totaled more than $1.3 billion in FY2024.
Japanese probiotic beverage company Yakult located in Bartow County.
Korean bakery CJ Foodville located in Hall County.
Walmart will source milk from local dairy farmers at a future dairy processing facility in Lowndes County.
Technology and corporate hubs are creating at least 4,400 new jobs as a result of wins in FY2024.
Cargill Corporation is establishing a new office hub in the City of Atlanta.
U.S. Soccer’s future headquarters and national training center will be located in Fayette County.
Georgia earned the rank of No. 1 state for business from Area Development magazine for 10 consecutive years.
The state earned the No. 1 spot in Site Selection magazine’s annual Prosperity Cup rankings for successful investment recruitment in 2023.
Georgia was awarded a Gold Shovel by Area Development for achieving significant success in attracting new business investment and job creation in 2023.
Business Facilities magazine ranked Georgia as the No. 1 state for EV investments as well as a top 10 state for food processing, cybersecurity, aerospace and defense, and AI growth hubs.
GDEcD serves as the state’s sales and marketing arm and lead agency for attracting new business investment, encouraging industry expansion, and promoting the state.
source_title: Georgia Job Creation Remains Strong - AllOnGeorgia

facts: Ascend Elements’ facility in Covington, Georgia spans 150,000 square feet.
The facility is the largest electric vehicle battery recycling plant in North America.
The plant plays a crucial role in reclaiming materials and advancing sustainable EV technology.
The facility’s unique demands required specialized MEP engineering to support complex chemical and industrial processes.
Wolf Engineering’s team collaborated closely with the client to design precise electrical infrastructure.
The designed electrical infrastructure is capable of efficiently handling heavy industrial loads while balancing power demands with energy-saving strategies.
Wolf Engineering’s professionals’ work exemplifies innovation, environmental responsibility, and effective client-engineer collaboration.
source_title: Ascend Elements - Wolf Engineering

facts: Due to changing market conditions, Ascend Elements and the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) mutually agreed to cancel a $164 million grant intended to fund cathode active material (CAM) manufacturing infrastructure at the company’s Apex 1 project in Hopkinsville, Kentucky.
A separate $316M U.S. DOE grant for pCAM infrastructure remains active to help fund construction of the Apex 1 project in Hopkinsville, KY.
Demand for domestic pCAM has exceeded demand for CAM, so Ascend Elements now plans to produce only pCAM and Lithium Carbonate (Li2CO3) at the Kentucky facility.
Roger Lin, VP of Government Affairs at Ascend Elements, stated that current market conditions do not support advancement of the CAM project at Apex 1.
Ascend Elements remains 100% committed to completing construction of the Apex 1 campus in Hopkinsville, Kentucky.
The facility will only produce pCAM and Lithium Carbonate, a critical mineral, with buyers already lined up to purchase domestically produced pCAM and Lithium Carbonate.

Ascend Elements was originally selected for a total of $480M in funding from two U.S DOE grants in October 2022 as part of the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, comprising a $164M grant for CAM manufacturing infrastructure and a $316M grant for pCAM manufacturing infrastructure.
By voluntarily returning the CAM grant, Ascend Elements is freeing up $164M of federal funding for the current administration to reallocate as it deems most appropriate.
At Apex 1, the company is prioritizing pCAM manufacturing to help fill the 400-kiloton annual shortfall of pCAM that analysts expect in North America by 2030.
Most of the world’s pCAM is currently made in China, and the Apex 1 facility will be North America’s first commercial-scale manufacturer of pCAM when operational in Q3 2026.

The Apex 1 facility will serve as an important part of America’s domestic critical materials infrastructure and align with the administration’s stated goal of increasing domestic production of critical materials.
Ascend Elements is committed to creating good jobs in Kentucky, providing a domestic supply of lithium-ion battery materials, and enhancing U.S. energy independence.

Ascend Elements is a North America- and Europe-based battery materials leader that converts spent lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing scrap into premium precursor cathode material (pCAM) and battery-grade lithium carbonate through its patented Hydro-to-Cathode® process.
The company operates plants at scale with a proven technology platform, delivering circular supply for EV and energy storage markets.
source_title: Ascend Elements | Ascend Elements and U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) Mutually Agree to Cancel $164M Cathode Active Material (CAM) Grant

facts: Ascend Elements was founded in 2015 under the initial name Battery Resources, Inc.
The company was founded by Professor Yan Wang, a materials science expert.
The core technology developed is known as the Hydro-to-Cathode process.
The company's mission focuses on creating a sustainable, closed-loop supply chain for battery materials.
Early financial backing likely came from academic grants, small angel investments, and support from friends and family.
In September 2021, Ascend Elements closed a $90 million Series C funding round led by Just Climate, an investment platform created by Generation Investment Management.
Existing investors Fifth Wall, Hitachi Ventures, and Orbia Ventures also participated in the September 2021 Series C round.
The September 2021 investments played a crucial role in scaling operations, including the construction of the Apex 1 manufacturing facility in Hopkinsville, Kentucky.
The Apex 1 facility is designed to produce sustainable cathode active materials and precursor cathode active materials (pCAM).
The Apex 1 facility is projected to be North America's first commercial-scale plant for producing sustainable cathode active materials and pCAM from recycled lithium-ion batteries.
In September 2022, Ascend Elements secured a $300 million Series D funding round spearheaded by Decarbonization Partners, a collaboration between Temasek and BlackRock.
The September 2022 Series D round also saw participation from Apeiron Investment Group, Fifth Wall, LG Technology Ventures, and Orbia Ventures.
The $300 million Series D funding brought the total funds raised by Ascend Elements to over $542 million.

Major stakeholders in Ascend Elements include Decarbonization Partners (BlackRock and Temasek), Apeiron Investment Group, Just Climate (Generation Investment Management), Fifth Wall, LG Technology Ventures, and Orbia Ventures.
The Board of Directors includes investor representatives from major funding rounds, founder Professor Yan Wang, and independent directors with industry expertise.
Voting power within Ascend Elements aligns with equity ownership, meaning entities holding larger equity stakes have greater voting power.
Major strategic decisions, such as future funding rounds or potential public offerings, require board approval.
The company's competitors in the battery recycling and materials sector include Li-Cycle, Redwood Materials, American Battery Technology Company, and Cirba Solutions.
source_title: Who Owns Ascend Elements Company? – businessmodelcanvastemplate.com

facts: Ascend Elements is a U.S.-based engineered materials and lithium-ion battery recycling company based in Westborough, Mass.
On October 27, 2022, Ascend Elements announced it secured $300 million in equity and debt financing.
The funding includes $200 million in Series C equity investments from an international group of strategic and financial investors.
The funding round was led by Fifth Wall Climate.
SK ecoplant, the environmental unit of South Korean conglomerate SK Group, joined as an investor.
New investors include Oman Investment Authority, Lithium Americas Corporation, GLy Capital Management's New Mobility Fund, Mirae Asset Capital & LS, and Shinhan GIB.

Investors from previous funding rounds participated in the latest round, including Hitachi Ventures, Jaguar Land Rover's InMotion Ventures, TDK Ventures, Orbia Ventures, At One Ventures, TRUMPF Venture, and Doral Energy-Tech Ventures.
The funding is in addition to two recently awarded grants totaling $480 million from the Department of Energy.
The funding will accelerate commercialization of Ascend Elements' proprietary Hydro-to-Cathode™ direct precursor synthesis process.
The company's technology establishes a closed-loop, EV battery materials supply chain in North America.
Ascend Elements disclosed plans to invest up to $1 billion to build a sustainable lithium-ion battery materials facility in Hopkinsville, Ky.
The first-of-its-kind manufacturing facility is named "Apex 1".
Apex 1 will produce enough lithium-ion battery pCAM and sustainable CAM to equip up to 250,000 electric vehicles per year.

CEO Mike O'Kronley stated that the company produces sustainable, high-performance cathode active materials that go directly back into new EV batteries rather than simply recovering metals for global commodity markets.
The company's "Base 1" facility in Georgia is already generating revenue and processing end-of-life batteries and manufacturing scrap.
Base 1 will have 30,000 metric tons of EV battery recycling capacity by the end of 2022.
Base 1 will ultimately feed materials to Apex 1 in Kentucky.
The company expects to recycle more than 150,000 metric tons of lithium-ion batteries per year globally by 2026.
Ascend Elements efficiently transforms recycled batteries into high-performance, customized EV battery cathode active material precursor (pCAM) and cathode active materials (CAM).
The produced pCAM and CAM meet or exceed the performance requirements of top-tier battery manufacturers.
Peter Gajdoš, Fifth Wall Partner and Co-Lead of the Climate Investment team, stated that investment partners understand the urgent need to produce sustainable, lithium-ion battery materials.

SK ecoplant CEO Park Kyung-il said the investment continues their strategic partnership with Ascend Elements and lays the foundation for SK ecoplant as the first shareholder to dominate the global battery recycling industry.

Ibrahim Al Eisri, Director – Private Equity at Oman Investment Authority, stated they are pleased to partner with Ascend Elements to develop and transform the lithium-ion battery recycling industry globally.
The company's proprietary Hydro-to-Cathode™ technology produces new CAM from spent lithium-ion cells more efficiently than traditional methods.
This process results in reduced cost, improved performance, and lowered GHG emissions compared to traditional methods.
source_title: Ascend Elements Secures $300 Million in Funding

facts: Ascend Elements recently celebrated the grand opening of its first commercial-scale lithium-ion battery recycling facility.
The facility is located in Covington, Georgia.
The $50 million Base 1 facility is North America's largest electric vehicle battery recycling facility.
The facility began partial operations in August 2022.
It has an annual capacity to process 30,000 metric tons of used lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing scrap.
This processing capacity is equal to 70,000 electric vehicle batteries per year.
Ascend Elements CEO Mike O'Kronley stated that the facility is an important part of America's EV battery infrastructure and that it is just the start.
O'Kronley emphasized the industry's need to continue building a closed-loop battery materials supply chain to make electric vehicle batteries cleaner and more sustainable.
Ascend Elements recovers up to 98% of the critical battery metals in used electric vehicle batteries and gigafactory manufacturing scrap.

By recycling used lithium-ion batteries and scrap with its patented Hydro-to-Cathode process, Ascend Elements can reduce the carbon footprint of new electric vehicle battery cathode materials by up to 90%.
The ultra-clean and efficient facility is powered by renewable energy.
It features onsite wastewater recycling and treatment systems.
The facility currently employs about 100 team members, and the company plans to increase headcount to 185 by 2024.
source_title: Ascend Elements opens LIB-recycling facility in Georgia

facts: In a follow-up to one of last year's largest cleantech private equity placements, Ascend Elements announced it has raised $162 million in new equity investments.
Major investors in the new round include Just Climate, Clearvision Ventures, and IRONGREY.
The new equity financing brings the company's 12-month funding total to $704 million.
This total includes a $542 million funding round led by Decarbonization Partners, Temasek, and Qatar Investment Authority in September 2023.
Goldman Sachs & Co. LLC acted as sole placement agent for the Series D funding round.
The new funding will advance construction of Ascend Elements' Apex 1 facility in Hopkinsville, Kentucky.
The Apex 1 facility will be North America's first sustainable cathode precursor (pCAM) manufacturing facility open in early 2025.
When complete, the 1-million-square-foot facility will produce sustainable pCAM and CAM (cathode active materials).
Production capacity at the facility will support up to 750,000 electric vehicles per year.
The project is supported in part by the U.S. Department of Energy and the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law (BIL).
pCAM and CAM are engineered materials made to precise microstructure specifications for use in electric vehicle batteries.
While most of the world's pCAM and CAM are made in China from primary metals, U.S.-based Ascend Elements is commercializing an ultra-efficient method to make sustainable pCAM and CAM from black mass.
The patented Hydro-to-Cathode® direct precursor synthesis process eliminates several intermediary steps in the traditional cathode manufacturing process.
This process provides significant economic and carbon-reduction benefits.
Several peer-reviewed studies have shown Ascend Elements' recycled battery materials perform as well as similar materials made from primary (mined) sources.
CEO Mike O'Kronley thanked new and existing partners for investing in North America's critical EV battery infrastructure.
Managing Director at Just Climate Aruna Ramsamy stated the company is backing technology that transforms spent lithium-ion batteries into critical materials used in the creation of new batteries.

Aruna Ramsamy noted Ascend Elements has the potential to unlock the supply of critical battery materials to accelerate the roll out of electric vehicles with its first pCAM facility in construction in the United States.

Aruna Ramsamy highlighted that Ascend Elements' Hydro-to-Cathode® technology provides a sustainable option for production of critical battery materials, championing circularity in an industry poised to scale significantly.
Based in Westborough, Mass., Ascend Elements is the leading provider of sustainable, closed-loop battery materials solutions.

From EV battery recycling to commercial-scale production of lithium-ion battery precursor (pCAM) and cathode active materials (CAM), Ascend Elements is revolutionizing the production of sustainable lithium-ion battery materials.
Its proprietary Hydro-to-Cathode® direct precursor synthesis technology produces new pCAM from spent lithium-ion cells more efficiently than traditional methods.
The technology results in reduced cost, improved performance, and lowered GHG emissions.
With fewer batteries going to landfill and a cleaner manufacturing process, Ascend Elements is taking the lithium-ion battery industry to a higher level of sustainability.

source_title: Ascend Elements raises additional $162 million to build sustainable lithium-ion battery materials in United States | Just Climate

facts: Lithium-ion battery materials firm Ascend Elements has secured $320 million to build a precursor cathode active material (pCAM) manufacturing facility in Poland.
The plant will be the US firm’s first pCAM manufacturing facility in Europe and enable it to bring its Hydro-to-Cathode® technology to the region.
The technology produces nickel, manganese and cobalt pCAM material from spent lithium-ion cells.
The firm hopes the plant will create a sustainable European circular battery materials supply chain.
The pCAM facility will be solely owned and operated by the Massachusetts-based company.
A cash subsidy of 1.22 billion Polish Zloty was offered by the Ministry of Economic Development and Technology in Poland.
The grant is one of the largest ever offered by the Republic of Poland and is designed to support Europe’s transition to a net-zero economy.
The funding is part of the EU’s Temporary Crisis and Transition Framework (TCTF).
Pawel Pudlowski, Vice President of the Management Board at the Polish Investment and Trade Agency (PAIH), stated that battery sector projects are a key part of its investment portfolio.

Pudlowski said: “The project is one of the largest US investments in recent years, strengthening Poland’s role in the battery supply chain and supporting regional development, including technology development and cooperation with local educational institutions.”
In 2024, Ascend Elements and Elemental Strategic Metals established a joint venture to own and operate the AE Elemental JV battery recycling facility in Zawiercie, Poland.
Ascend Elements owns and operates one of North America’s largest battery recycling facilities in Georgia, US.
A new lithium recovery line at the Georgia plant is expected to produce up to 3,000 metric tons of Lithium Carbonate (Li₂CO₃) annually.
The company is building a Hydro-to-Cathode® pCAM manufacturing facility in Kentucky, US.
When operational in late 2026, the Kentucky facility is expected to be one of North America’s largest NMC pCAM manufacturing facilities.
Linh Austin, President and CEO at Ascend Elements, met with Michal Jaros, Secretary of State in Poland’s Ministry of Economic Development and Technology, in Warsaw, Poland.
source_title: Ascend Elements secures $320M funding to build pCAM Li-ion materials plant in Poland - Best Magazine

facts: Poland's Ministry of Economic Development and Technology offered Ascend Elements up to 1.22 billion Polish zloty (approximately €290 million or $320 million) in grant funding.
This represents one of the largest grants ever awarded by the Polish government.
The financial incentive aims to support the construction of an advanced production facility for recycled cathode materials in Poland.
The investment aligns with the European Union's broader goals to develop domestic battery supply chains and reduce dependency on imported materials, particularly from Asia.
Poland's investment directly supports the EU Battery Regulation (2023/1542), which mandates that by 2030, new batteries must contain 12% recycled cobalt, 4% recycled lithium, and 4% recycled nickel.

Pawel Pudłowski, Vice President of the Management Board at the Polish Investment and Trade Agency (PAIH), noted: "This project strengthens Poland's role in the European battery supply chain and supports regional economic development."
According to the European Commission's Critical Raw Materials Act, the EU aims to source 10-40% of its raw materials from recycling by 2030.
Poland currently accounts for approximately 3% of EU battery production (Eurostat, 2024).
The country leverages EU funding mechanisms like the Just Transition Fund to build recycling infrastructure.
Northvolt's €1 billion Swedish recycling plant became operational in 2023.
The Bruegel Institute's 2024 analysis noted: "Poland's investment in battery recycling technology addresses a critical vulnerability in Europe's strategic autonomy plans."

Ascend Elements is a U.S.-based company specializing in innovative battery recycling technologies that transform end-of-life lithium-ion batteries into high-value materials for new battery production.

The company plans to implement its proprietary Hydro-to-Cathode™ technology in Poland—a direct synthesis process (patent US2022156789) that creates new precursor cathode active material (pCAM) from recycled lithium-ion cells.
This breakthrough process recovers more than 95% of critical metals from spent batteries (Ascend Elements, 2024).
The technology bypasses traditional intermediate processing steps, directly converting battery black mass to pCAM containing nickel, manganese, and cobalt.
This streamlined approach reduces energy consumption and produces 70% lower CO₂ emissions per kilogram of NMC material compared to traditional mining operations (Circular Energy Storage, 2023).
Ascend Elements has established operations in Poland through a joint venture with Polish company Elemental Strategic Metals.
The partners currently operate a battery recycling facility in Zawiercie that processes over 10,000 tons of battery waste annually.

Tomasz Poznar, Ph.D., Senior Vice President of Commercial at Ascend Elements, explained: "Our technology helps bridge the growing gap in cathode precursor supply and contributes to the EU's strategic net-zero goals."
The new plant would be independently owned and operated by Ascend Elements, representing a significant expansion of their European presence beyond the Zawiercie joint venture.
While specific job figures for the Polish facility haven't been announced, similar battery recycling operations in Europe typically create 300+ direct jobs.

The project is expected to foster technology development through collaboration with local educational institutions, potentially supporting Poland's "Electromobility 2025" workforce development program.
Linh Austin, President and CEO of Ascend Elements, stated: "We're committed to building a sustainable supply chain for battery materials on both sides of the Atlantic."
The facility aims to address Europe's growing cathode precursor supply deficit, which according to Benchmark Mineral Intelligence (2024) will reach approximately 500,000 tons per year by 2030.
Research from Fraunhofer ISI (2023) indicates that recycling lithium-ion batteries reduces CO₂ emissions by approximately 50% compared to using virgin mined materials.
Umicore's Belgian recycling plant already supplies approximately 20% of the EU's cathode material needs.
Ascend Elements currently operates a large battery recycling plant in Covington, Georgia, which processes approximately 30,000 metric tons of lithium-ion waste annually.

Construction of its planned hydro-to-cathode production facility in Hopkinsville, Kentucky—designed for 60,000 tons per year capacity—has been temporarily paused, with operations now scheduled to begin in late 2026.
In February 2025, Ascend Elements agreed with the U.S. Department of Energy to cancel a $164 million grant for cathode active material (CAM) production at its Kentucky plant.
The Polish grant provides direct upfront funding, while the U.S. Inflation Reduction Act offers tax credits of up to $35 per kilowatt-hour for battery materials.
The global battery recycling landscape is projected to reach $33.6 billion by 2030 (Grand View Research, 2024).

Eric Gratz, co-founder and chief technical officer at Ascend Elements, noted that U.S. tariffs could benefit domestic battery recycling operations: "Unlike battery manufacturers relying on virgin materials that would be subject to import tariffs, recyclers using domestic materials would be less impacted."
The U.S. has imposed 25% tariffs on Chinese lithium products (USTR, 2024).
Recycled cobalt currently trades at approximately $45/kg compared to $55/kg for virgin material (London Metal Exchange, May 2025).
The EU is implementing its Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism, which will increase costs for imported battery materials with high carbon footprints.
A location in Poland has already been identified for the battery recycling plant, though the company has not yet made a final investment decision.
Construction of pCAM plants typically requires 18-24 months once permits are secured.
The project will need to navigate Poland's permitting processes, which must comply with EU environmental regulations like the Industrial Emissions Directive.
Ascend Elements has announced plans to scale up its business with recycled lithium carbonate in both the USA and Europe beginning in 2026.
Currently, only about 5% of lithium-ion batteries in the EU are recycled (European Environment Agency, 2024).
Tomasz Poznar emphasized: "This project marks a critical step in transforming Europe's battery materials landscape and supporting the EU's net-zero goals."
The new EU Battery Regulation (2023/1542) imposes a 70% recycling efficiency mandate by 2030, along with specific recovery targets for individual metals.
Closed-loop recycling systems reduce reliance on cobalt from the Democratic Republic of Congo, which currently supplies approximately 75% of global cobalt.
The EU's REACH regulations standardize recycled materials to meet the same quality and safety standards as virgin materials.
Cobalt prices have ranged from $30 to $60 per kilogram between 2020 and 2025 (London Metal Exchange).
Poland's energy costs are approximately €50/MWh compared to €40/MWh in Sweden.
Redwood Materials is investing €3.5 billion in European expansion (Financial Times, 2024).
Supply chain bottlenecks for collecting black mass could present a challenge as multiple recyclers compete for the same waste streams.
Northvolt's Swedish facility has an €1 billion investment value, 125,000 tons/year capacity, and is operational.
Redwood Materials plans a €3.5 billion total European investment with 200,000 tons/year planned capacity in the expansion phase.
Umicore's Belgian facility has a €500 million investment value, 150,000 tons/year capacity, and is operational.
BASF/Fortum/Nornickel's Finnish facility has a €400 million investment value, 50,000 tons/year capacity, and is under construction.
Tomasz Poznar stated: "This project is more than a strategic investment in cutting-edge battery materials production — it marks a critical step in transforming Europe's battery materials landscape."

source_title: Poland Attracts Ascend Elements Battery Recycling With Millions

facts: Ascend Elements began construction of its largest electric vehicle battery recycling and engineered materials manufacturing facility, named Apex 1, with a ceremonial groundbreaking on October 20, 2022.
The Apex 1 facility is located in Hopkinsville, Kentucky (Christian County).
The project involves a 140-acre campus and a 500,000-square-foot manufacturing facility.
Ascend Elements plans to invest close to $1 billion in the Apex 1 campus and facility.
When finished, the Apex 1 facility will produce enough engineered precursor (pCAM) and battery-ready cathode active materials (CAM) to equip 250,000 electric vehicles per year.
The facility will employ up to 400 full-time jobs.
The project is expected to generate up to $4.4 billion in economic impact to Kentucky over the construction period and first 10 years.

Apex 1 will utilize Ascend Elements’ Hydro-to-Cathode™ direct precursor synthesis process technology, a closed-loop system that produces new cathode active material from spent lithium-ion cells with minimal waste and carbon emissions compared to traditional manufacturing.
Governor Andy Beshear stated the investment has grown to nearly $1 billion and will create 400 full-time jobs, making it the single largest investment in Western Kentucky.
Governor Andy Beshear noted that Kentucky has quickly become a national leader in the electric-vehicle space due to a substantial supplier network including innovative companies like Ascend Elements.

Michael O’Kronley, CEO of Ascend Elements, stated that the facility represents a domestic source of sustainable lithium-ion cathode material for EV batteries critical for U.S. energy independence and national security.

Ascend Elements is based in Westborough, Massachusetts, and is a leading provider of sustainable, closed-loop battery material solutions from EV battery recycling to commercial-scale production of pCAM and CAM.
source_title: Ascend Elements | Ascend Elements Begins Construction of Apex 1 in Southwestern Kentucky

facts: Aspen Aerogels plans to construct an advanced manufacturing facility at Bulloch County’s Southern Gateway Commerce Park near Statesboro, Georgia.
The company intends to invest a minimum of $325 million in the project.

Hiring for personnel is expected to begin in the second half of 2022 across skilled manufacturing operators, advanced process engineers, quality and maintenance technicians, and professional support roles.
The new facility will span 500,000 square feet on a 90-acre parcel in southeast Georgia.
Aspen plans to create more than 250 advanced manufacturing jobs at the site.
Production is expected to begin in late 2023.
When combined with Aspen’s original East Providence, Rhode Island plant, the Georgia facility is designed to triple Aspen’s nameplate aerogel capacity.
The project supports Aspen’s thermal barrier expansion plans in the fast-growing electric vehicle market.
Aspen aims to capitalize on increased demand for its Aerogel Technology Platform™ as EV OEMs adopt its PyroThin® aerogel thermal barrier solutions.
PyroThin materials provide thermal management, mechanical stability, and fire protection properties specifically for EV battery systems.
The company’s carbon aerogel program seeks to increase the performance of lithium-ion battery cells to help EV manufacturers extend driving range and reduce vehicle costs.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the project, highlighting Aspen's confidence in Georgia and its growing electric mobility sector.
Don Young, President and CEO of Aspen, cited efficient ports, a strong available workforce, and secure, low-cost utilities and critical raw materials as key site advantages.
Billy Allen, Vice Chair of the Development Authority of Bulloch County, welcomed the company and emphasized the region's commitment to innovation in the electric mobility ecosystem.
Assistant Director Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce division on the project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia EMC.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson expressed confidence that Georgia employees will collaborate with Aspen’s network of scientists and engineers to address mobility challenges.
Southern Gateway Commerce Park holds Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) certification from the Georgia Department of Economic Development.
The region’s education resources, including Georgia Southern University and Ogeechee Technical College, are expected to provide critical workforce support for Aspen.
Aspen Aerogels is headquartered in Northborough, Massachusetts, and currently manufactures products at its East Providence, Rhode Island facilities.
source_title: More about Gov. Kemp: Aspen Aerogels to Open Advanced Manufacturing Facility in Bulloch County

facts: Aspen Aerogels, Inc. has stopped construction of its planned second aerogel manufacturing facility in Register near the interchange of I-16 and US Highway 301.
Hundreds of new jobs will no longer be coming to Bulloch County due to the halted project.
The plant was touted by Georgia Governor Brian Kemp and the Development Authority of Bulloch County.
The facility was expected to bring $325 million of investment when it was announced.
The project was expected to create more than 250 manufacturing jobs when announced.
The plant was intended to make thermal barriers that insulate the batteries on electric cars.
In an investor call this week, officials with Aspen Aerogels said its existing facilities will instead increase their capacity.
The company says it will be assessing options to relocate some equipment from the Register plant to upgrade and expand its existing plants.
The Register site was being built on land owned by the Development Authority of Bulloch County, which negotiated local incentives for the project.
The Development Authority of Bulloch County stated it is disappointed that Aspen Aerogels has indicated it will cease construction of the planned advanced manufacturing facility in Bulloch County.
The Development Authority noted that taxpayer protection is a priority and clawback provisions are in place to recoup local investment should the project not move forward any further.

Ricardo C. Rodriguez, Chief Financial Officer and Treasurer, stated that in early 2023, pre-empting a reset in EV demand expectations, Aspen Aerogels decided to right-time the construction of its planned second aerogel manufacturing facility in Statesboro, Georgia.
Rodriguez noted the company subsequently ramped up its external manufacturing capacity for its Energy Industrial business.
The company claims its external manufacturing model has fully demonstrated its ability to efficiently increase aerogel supply.
Aspen Aerogels believes a capital-light and modular capacity plan provides the most efficient path to creating value.

Rodriguez stated the company is fully funded by a strong balance sheet that enables it to evaluate opportunities including additional paths for organic and inorganic growth, optimizing the capital structure, and returning capital to shareholders.
source_title: Aspen Aerogels, Inc. stops construction of manufacturing facility in Statesboro

facts: Aspen Aerogels, Inc. (NYSE: ASPN) is a technology leader in aerogel-based sustainability and electrification solutions.

On February 17, 2022, Koch Strategic Platforms (KSP), a subsidiary of Koch Investments Group, agreed to make a $150 million investment in Aspen through the purchase of convertible notes and common stock.
As part of the investment, KSP agreed to purchase convertible notes in the aggregate principal amount of $100 million.
KSP also agreed to purchase 1,791,986 shares of Aspen's common stock at a price of $27.902 per share, resulting in proceeds to Aspen of $50 million.

Donald R. Young, Aspen's President and CEO, noted the company's strategy leverages its Aerogel Technology Platform into high-value, high-growth markets driven by PyroThin® thermal barriers which address thermal runaway in electric vehicles (EVs).
KSP's investment will support growth and allow Aspen to address additional high-value applications in ESG-driven markets, including battery materials, hydrogen energy, carbon capture, and filtration.
Aspen announced plans to construct an advanced manufacturing facility at Bulloch County, Georgia’s Southern Gateway Commerce Park.

Combined with its original East Providence, Rhode Island plant, the planned Georgia facility is designed to triple Aspen's nameplate aerogel capacity in support of thermal barrier expansion plans in the EV market.
Aspen aims to capitalize on increased demand for its Aerogel Technology Platform as EV OEMs continue to adopt its PyroThin thermal barrier solutions.
Mintz has represented Aspen since 2010 in numerous matters including the company's 2014 IPO, additional financings, and strategic partnerships targeting new product and market opportunities.
source_title: Energy & Sustainability Client Feature — Aspen Aerogels | Mintz

facts: Northborough-based Aspen Aerogels disclosed that the US Department of Energy approved a loan of up to $671 million to finance the construction of a manufacturing plant in Georgia.
Shares in Aspen Aerogels rose by more than 13 percent on Wednesday following the disclosure.
The Georgia plant will manufacture PyroThin aerogel blankets, which serve as thermal barriers to prevent out-of-control vehicle battery fires.
Aspen already operates one manufacturing plant in East Providence, R.I.
Aspen opened an engineering and prototyping center in Marlborough last year.
The company currently outsources some of its manufacturing work to suppliers in China.
Aspen currently employs about 550 people.
Aspen plans to employ 255 full-time workers at the new Georgia plant.
Aspen chief executive Donald Young stated that the Department of Energy loan will fully fund the company’s capital expenses for building the Georgia factory.
source_title: Aspen Aerogels receives federal loan to build new plant in Georgia

facts: TD Cowen maintained a stable outlook for Aspen Aerogels with a reiterated Buy rating and a $41.00 price target.
The Department of Energy (DOE) granted conditional approval for a loan up to $670.6 million aimed at reviving Aspen's halted Georgia manufacturing facility.

The financial boost is intended to expand capacity to meet growing demand for electric vehicle (EV) thermal barrier products, with expectations for the plant's demand to rise in the second half of 2027.

An analyst from TD Cowen highlighted that the DOE loan underscores the market's need for increased production capabilities to supply original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) with specialized EV thermal barriers.
The company's immediate priority is to finalize the loan agreement, which will play a crucial role in securing necessary funding for the project's advancement.
Aspen Aerogels reported preliminary third-quarter revenue of $117 million, surpassing the estimated $95 million.
Aspen Aerogels reported an impressive third-quarter EBITDA of $25 million.
The uptick in revenue and EBITDA is propelled by the acceleration of General Motors' EV production.
Once operational, the Georgia plant is expected to significantly enhance Aspen Aerogels' production capacity.
The facility's focus will be on manufacturing thermal barrier materials that are essential for the thermal management of batteries in electric vehicles.
Oppenheimer maintains an Outperform rating and a $32.00 price target on Aspen Aerogels.
Piper Sandler has maintained positive ratings on Aspen Aerogels.
Aspen Aerogels secured a significant $670.6 million loan guarantee from the Department of Energy (DOE) to facilitate the expansion of its facility in Georgia.

The company's technology is recognized for strategic positioning within the electric vehicle market and is seen as pivotal in the development of next-generation chips and various industrial heat management solutions.
Aspen Aerogels secured a $125 million term loan facility and a $100 million asset-based revolving credit facility, enhancing its financial flexibility.
According to InvestingPro data, the company showed a 144.55% increase in quarterly revenue as of Q2 2024.
The company's market capitalization stands at $1.97 billion.
The 1-year price total return is an impressive 170.04%.
Analysts anticipate sales growth in the current year, consistent with expected demand surge for EV thermal barrier products.
Operating with a moderate level of debt may have contributed to Aspen Aerogels' successful pursuit of the DOE loan for the Georgia facility.
source_title: Aspen Aerogels holds firm with Buy rating, $41 target amid DOE loan news By Investing.com
