facts: DENSO Manufacturing is expanding its Athens, Tennessee facility with a $190 million investment.
The expansion will add 320 new jobs to the Athens plant.
Officials from DENSO and Tennessee Governor Bill Haslam made the announcement on Wednesday morning (February 28, 2018).
DENSO’s Athens facility manufactures fuel injectors, fuel pumps, oxygen sensors, ignition coils, monolithic carriers and spark plugs.
The investment will add four production lines in Athens, increasing the company’s ability to produce and deliver key parts for automakers across North America.
The 320 new jobs will span production, facility operations, engineering, administration and other skilled-traded positions.
DENSO will introduce a new Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI) production line, one low-pressure fuel pump line and two high-pressure fuel pump lines.
This expansion is part of DENSO’s strategic investments in North America and its commitment to make Tennessee a primary manufacturing center.
In 2017, DENSO announced a $1 billion investment in its Maryville facility, creating 1,000 jobs focused on electrification and safety systems.
Tennessee Governor Bill Haslam stated that DENSO is one of Tennessee’s largest employers and has been a valuable part of the state for 30 years.
Community Development Commissioner Bob Rolfe noted that DENSO employs approximately 1,375 people in Athens, more than any business in McMinn County.

Hugh Cantrell, director of Administrative Services for DENSO Manufacturing Athens Tennessee, stated that increasing manufacturing capacity closer to North American customers will continue to power DENSO’s growth in the region.
DENSO has its global headquarters in Japan and has had a presence in Tennessee for three decades.
DENSO operates three locations in Tennessee: Maryville, Athens and Jackson, employing roughly 4,500 people across the state.
Prior to this expansion, DENSO has invested approximately $3 billion in Tennessee.
McMinn County Mayor John M. Gentry stated that the expansion will improve the quality of life for several hundred McMinn County families and have a lasting impact on the local economy.

City of Athens Mayor Chuck Burris stated that the announcement exemplifies the success of Athens' development strategies to help existing companies grow and praised DENSO as a standard bearer for corporate citizenry in Athens.
source_title: Denso manufacturing to add 320 new jobs to Athens plant

facts: Tennessee Department of Economic and Community Development awarded more than $108.5 million in FastTrack economic development grants between 51 businesses in the first half of 2021.
The largest grant was a $60 million award to Ultium Cells for its Spring Hill plant project.
Ultium Cells is making a $2.3 billion investment to create 1,300 jobs at its 2.8-million-square-foot facility in Spring Hill.
The Spring Hill plant will manufacture vehicle battery cells.
The $60 million grant amounts to more than $46,000 per job created for the Ultium Cells project.
This per-job incentive exceeds the previous high of $21,500 per job, which was offered to Denso Manufacturing for its 2017 expansion in Blount County.
TNECD Commissioner Bob Rolfe stated that the investment supports advanced manufacturing efforts and strengthens Tennessee's position as the leader of the electric vehicle industry in the Southeast.
At least 16 projects received at least $1 million in FastTrack grants during the first half of 2021.

FastTrack grants are awarded for job training, infrastructure development, or from a special fund covering relocating/purchasing equipment, building repairs/improvements, and temporary office space related to relocation or expansion.
These economic development funds are reserved for exceptional circumstances where they will make a proportionally significant economic impact on the affected community.
TNECD typically announces a new project with its investment size and scope, then adds the FastTrack grant incentive amount to the department’s database within 15-30 days.
Microvast Power Solutions received a $3 million FastTrack grant in Montgomery County for battery technology operations.
PUREGraphite LLC received a $3 million FastTrack grant in Hamilton County for graphite and battery material operations.
source_title: TN Doles Out $108.5M In Economic Development Grants First Half Of 2021 | Tennessee Conservative

facts: DENSO Manufacturing Arkansas, Inc. is located in Osceola, Arkansas.
The facility achieved more than seven million production hours without a lost-time accident.
The Arkansas Department of Labor honored the facility for this milestone on Sept. 5.
DENSO Manufacturing Arkansas hosted a celebration of the milestone as part of “Safety Day.”
Lost time accidents are defined as job-related incidents where an employee is absent from the workplace for at least one day.
The facility's last lost-time accident occurred in 2013.
Since 2013, the facility has gone more than 2,240 days without an incident.
The facility employs nearly 500 workers who have worked approximately 7.2 million hours.
Over the last five years, the facility has maximized productivity while prioritizing employee safety.
George Harguess serves as president of DENSO Manufacturing Arkansas.
Harguess stated that everyone from junior employees to management fully supports the facility's culture of safety.
The facility has developed various safety measures to protect employees since its founding over 15 years ago.
In early 2016, the facility established the DENSO Manufacturing Arkansas Safety Committee.
The Safety Committee is comprised of production employees who monitor existing safety policies.
The committee educates employees on safety best practices and creates or updates safety initiatives.
Michael Edwards works as a basic skills training sub leader and is a member of the DENSO Manufacturing Arkansas Safety Committee.
Edwards stated that his daily priorities are productivity/efficiency and ensuring coworker safety.
Kenichiro Ito serves as a senior executive officer for DENSO Corp. and CEO of DENSO’s North American headquarters.
Ito emphasized that manufacturing practices must evolve to keep employees safe and that promoting strong safety policies is crucial for the company's success.
source_title: DENSO Arkansas Facility Celebrates 7M Production Hours Without Lost Time

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Dongwon Autopart Technology will invest $30 million to establish a new automotive components manufacturing facility in Emanuel County.
The project will bring an anticipated 200 new jobs to the region over the next several years.
The project further strengthens Georgia’s position as a national leader in automotive manufacturing.
The project highlights continued supplier growth across the state’s rural communities.
Governor Kemp stated that Georgia’s thriving automotive industry is creating new opportunities for Georgians statewide, no matter their zip code.
Governor Kemp expressed excitement for the project to bring new jobs to Emanuel County and see its impact on the community for years to come.
Dongwon Autopart Technology was founded in 1971.
The company supplies key components including door frames, seat frames, and battery frames to automotive original equipment manufacturers (OEMs).
The company currently supports more than 500 direct jobs across the Southeast.
Dongwon has an existing facility in Hogansville, Georgia, which will continue operating.
Dongwon has maintained a U.S. presence since 2003.
The company continues to scale alongside growth in the automotive and electric vehicle supply chain.
Heather Hollstein, Human Resources Manager at Dongwon Autopart Technology, stated pride in being part of Georgia’s growing automotive industry.
Hollstein noted appreciation for Emanuel County’s warm welcome and commitment to delivering high-quality products and service to customers.
The new production facility will be located at the Highway 297 Industrial Park near Swainsboro.
Construction is expected to move forward soon.
Hiring is expected to begin in 2026.
Planned positions include management roles, system leads, and production personnel.
The investment represents the largest new economic development project in Emanuel County in more than three decades.
Local and state leaders emphasized the project’s importance to rural job creation and regional economic growth.
Representative Butch Parrish, Chairman of the House Rules Committee, stated that the Georgia House of Representatives is committed to bringing high-quality jobs to rural communities.
Parrish noted that Emanuel County’s collaboration with state partners is delivering meaningful opportunities for the region.
Chairman Will Donaldson of the Emanuel County Development Authority noted the project reflects long-term economic development efforts.

Donaldson expressed pleasure and excitement at welcoming Dongwon Autopart Technology to Emanuel County, noting it is rewarding to see investment in economic development generate results for the community.
Ken Warnock, CEO of the Emanuel County Development Authority, added that the project will be transformational for the county.
Warnock stated it will be the single largest new economic development project for Emanuel County in over 30 years.
Warnock mentioned enjoying working with Dongwon’s leadership to secure their new state-of-the-art facility in the 297 Industrial Park.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) supported the project through its Global Commerce team.
GDEcD partnered with the Emanuel County Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start on the project.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that the automotive industry is expanding along the I-16 corridor as suppliers seek strategic access to OEMs and Georgia’s ports.
Wilson noted Dongwon’s second Georgia location underscores the strength of the state's automotive sector, which has been creating jobs statewide for more than a century.
Dongwon’s investment reinforces Georgia’s competitiveness in automotive manufacturing, particularly in supplier recruitment.
The state’s infrastructure, workforce training programs, and logistics connectivity continue to draw global manufacturers.
As electric vehicle and advanced automotive production grows across the Southeast, projects like this position Georgia and communities like Emanuel County to capture future supply chain investment.
source_title: Dongwon Autopart Technology to Invest $30M in Georgia, Creating 200 Jobs - Expansion Solutions

facts: EVCO Plastics was founded in 1964 by Don Evans as a custom plastics and contract manufacturing company on DeForest’s Main Street.
The company has expanded to 13 sites across the United States, China, and Mexico.
EVCO Plastics is marking its 60-year milestone in 2024.
The company remains family-owned and operated, with third-generation leadership roles held by Dale Evans' daughters and two cousins on the board of directors.
Dale Evans took over the company in the 1980s and continues to chair the board of directors.
Kate Bashir, Dale Evans' daughter, serves as CEO and oversees engineering and sales.
Anna Evans Bartz is EVCO’s vice president of communications.
EVCO employs 400 people at its three DeForest plants.
EVCO's facilities in Oshkosh, Wisconsin, and Calhoun, Georgia, employ an additional 850 people.
The company employs a total of 2,100 associates worldwide when including locations in China and Mexico.
EVCO specializes in plastic injection molding for complicated products and works with top 500 companies across various industries.
In 2018, EVCO built a 50,000-square-foot addition to its MED facility on EVCO Drive in DeForest.
In 2020, the company began a two-year process of moving its corporate headquarters from Main Street to the EVCO Drive location.
The EVCO Drive building houses a medical manufacturing facility that creates components for medical devices.
During the COVID-19 pandemic, EVCO manufactured components for respirators and diagnostic equipment, including COVID testing kits.
In 2019, EVCO expanded its Oshkosh campus by adding two new buildings totaling 117,473 square feet.
The Oshkosh expansion houses a 4,400-ton press, which is the largest of its machines at the facility, along with other equipment and warehousing space.
EVCO operates 270 injection molding machines and presses ranging from 28 tons to 4,400 tons in Oshkosh.
Kate Bashir anticipates hiring someone to oversee sales as she transitions more fully into the CEO role for the U.S. side.
Dale Evans has served the company for 50 years and is expected to retire once Bashir completes her transition.
The company fosters a culture of curiosity and aims to support employee development and retention.
EVCO anticipates a successful 2025.
source_title: EVCO Plastics marks 60th year in DeForest | Local News | hngnews.com

facts: Evco Plastics Inc., a family-owned injection molder based in DeForest, Wis., has purchased a building near Calhoun, Ga.
The company will add white room and liquid silicone rubber (LSR) molding capabilities at the new Georgia site.
Evco had been leasing the 40,000-square-foot building for warehouse space before purchasing it.
Kate Bashir, director of strategy, stated the purchase coincided with the need to expand their footprint in Georgia to accommodate new work.
With the new plant, Evco now has 1 million square feet of manufacturing space across 10 facilities.
The new site will introduce white room and LSR molding opportunities to meet demand from medical and commercial lighting customers.
Bashir noted the current need is for an existing customer in the lighting industry while actively quoting new customer work in this area.
The company is starting to discuss LSR with medical customers, aiming to 'walk' into the technology before 'running.'
The new facility has room for 20 injection molding machines up to 500 tons, including a separate room for new LSR machines.
Evco is moving smaller machines from Wisconsin facilities, purchasing new machines, and leaving spaces open to optimize barrel/screws for specific projects.
LSR molding will be a new capability for Evco, utilizing a task-force team of engineers and processing specialists with previous LSR market experience.
The company is working with machine manufacturers to help train its team on the tricky thermoset process.
The plant, named CAL 2, features two bridge cranes, four material silos, a 22,000-square-foot white room, and a 725-square-foot metrology room.
Evco is enhancing its highly automated lights-out molding capabilities with high-speed automation as a critical component of the manufacturing plan.
The facility will require trained automation team members to successfully operate lights-out processes and troubleshoot as needed.
The new Georgia facility will be fully operational in the fall.
Evco is adding 13,000 square feet to its automated molding facility in DeForest, dubbed AMP, to add more machines and increase raw material/finished goods storage.
Gary Fortier, director of Evco's U.S. operations, stated that reaching over 1 million square feet is a huge milestone driven by growing customer needs.
Bashir reported strong business conditions despite continued material shortages challenging increased worldwide demand.
The company is setting up to run 24/7 in plants needing extra time, optimizing schedules, and reducing turnover time to meet customer needs.
Evco kicked off 2021 with three months of all-time record sales with no signs of slowing down.
Founded in 1964, Evco employs more than 1,700 people and operates 235 injection molding machines ranging from 28 to 3,500 tons.
In addition to Georgia, Evco operates four molding plants in Wisconsin, three in Mexico, and one in China.
The company was named Plastics News' 2015 Processor of the Year and ranked No. 51 in the most recent PN survey of North American injection molders.
Evco's molding sales in the region are in the range of $188 million.
source_title: Owler Reports - EVCO: Evco Plastics buys Georgia plant to add LSR, white room molding

EVCO offers technologies and services across the entire value chain, including automation, packaging, labelling, and more.
Precision Molding is one of EVCO's four main divisions.
EVCO's products and solutions cover diverse industries ranging from agriculture and powersports to packaging and consumer goods.

facts: Flambeau Inc. has invested significantly in its operations recently due to surges in demand for plastic parts.
Flambeau added new presses at three of its nine North American plants: Columbus, Indiana; Middlefield, Ohio; and Madison, Georgia.
The press additions represent a company-wide expansion of capabilities for Flambeau’s manufacturing capacity.
A worldwide economic slowdown last year due to the Covid-19 pandemic led to a rapidly increasing company-wide backlog of orders for Flambeau.
Just months into the slowdown, Flambeau’s supply chain began to reach its limits, and demand for vital parts began to overwhelm supply.
Flambeau addressed the backlog by adding presses to its arsenal, among other efforts towards overall improvement.
In Columbus, Indiana, a new 750-ton press is primarily dedicated to specific proprietary products, including Flambeau Brute tool boxes, gun cases, and tackle boxes.
In Madison, Georgia, new 200-ton and 900-ton presses are utilized to increase production of a variety of stock and custom products for Flambeau’s fluids and packaging divisions.

In Middlefield, Ohio, three new presses ranging from 330- to 730-ton are primarily dedicated to manufacturing stock boxes and cases for Flambeau Outdoors, ArtBin and other packaging lines of products.

Flambeau implemented creative solutions to reduce backlog, including 24-hour operations and a new production scheduling methodology to improve forecasting practices and address various constraints on production.

Along with further expansion of injection and blow molding at its several plants, Flambeau is implementing a modern, company-wide ERP system to continually improve customer support and the company’s standing as a supply chain system.
source_title: Flambeau Inc. Invests in Operations | Medical Product Outsourcing

facts: A Norwegian battery company has been working since 2022 to open a supersize factory outside of Atlanta that will bring more than 700 jobs to the region.
Freyr Battery is developing a manufacturing process for lithium-ion batteries that it says will be less expensive and have less waste than the processes many competitors use.
The batteries would be available for stationary energy storage and for use in large electric vehicles such as buses.
The Georgia plant is one of the many projects whose existence is at least in part due to the Inflation Reduction Act, President Joe Biden’s 2022 climate law.
The company said in November that it would not meet a target for one of its technical milestones by the end of 2023.
It was delaying the opening of a large-scale plant in Norway.
One upshot was that the plant in Coweta County, already in the works about 40 miles southwest of Atlanta with a projected opening of 2026, would go online before the one in Norway.
Investors reacted negatively and the company’s share price dropped by about half.
This all occurred in Birger Steen’s first few months as Freyr’s CEO.
The company board appointed him to the role in August, and said the previous CEO, co-founder Tom Einar Jensen, would become executive chairman.
Steen has about 30 years of experience in the technology sector, including stints leading Microsoft’s operations in Norway and Russia.

He now faces some major challenges at the company’s technology and development center in Mo i Rana, a small city in northern Norway, where employees are taking a lithium-ion battery technology developed at MIT and trying to make it work in an automated manufacturing system.
The development of the manufacturing process is teeing up the opening of the plant near Atlanta, a complex that will cost more than $2.5 billion to build and employ 723 people.
Right now, the company has a head count of 213, most of them in Norway.
The project would benefit from the 45X Advanced Manufacturing Production Credit in the IRA, which is paid out based on the capacity, in megawatt-hours, of batteries made at the plant.
The credit is much better than anything Norway can offer to Freyr, which is a disappointment to leaders in Norway, as the New York Times reports this week, but a boon to the United States.
Birger Steen described the company's mission as decarbonizing transportation systems and storage systems by making the world’s most sustainable battery.

The company aims to help build a critical component of the future energy system and produce it in the West, noting that 95 percent of LFP [lithium iron phosphate] production capacity right now is in China.
Freyr successfully conducted automated casting trials of electrodes with active electrolyte slurry at its Norway facility.
Full automation is a precondition for getting to multiple gigawatt-hour scale.
The company recently did the first semi-automatic casting of electrodes, which took 30 hours instead of the scoped 30 days.

Troubles during automation trials included a web not bending down as expected and wrinkling at the edges, which caused a lack of homogeneity in the process; this also took 30 days instead of the planned 30 hours.

Freyr's original plan was to build a big plant in Norway due to 100 percent green energy from hydropower, local material supply prospects (lithium and graphite), proximity to a deepwater quay, good rail/road transport, and community support.
The company also planned to build a facility in Georgia as part of a selection process.

Freyr prioritized Georgia over other U.S. locations due to its central location, great airport nearby, railroads, access to port facilities by road or rail, strong local community partnership, and a strong base of resources with local skills ranging from trade school level to postdoc level.
The original plan was to build in Norway first and then Georgia second.

After the IRA was passed into law in August year before last (2022), Freyr concluded there would be no relevant policy response from European or Norwegian authorities, making it an easy choice to prioritize Georgia due to better project-level funding economics.
Birger Steen stated that the IRA makes a 'massive' difference compared to traditional programs that focus on cost of capital, development funding, or cheap land at the beginning of a project.
Unlike European policy initiatives, the IRA provides support based on production volume (paid out when producing gigawatt-hours), allowing the market to pick winners rather than the government.
Low lithium prices make battery products more affordable and expand the total addressable market as demand picks up.
source_title: A Battery Company CEO on the ‘Massive’ Effect of the Inflation Reduction Act - Inside Climate News

facts: NYSE-listed firm Freyr has cancelled its plans for a battery manufacturing plant in Georgia, US, following its pivot to becoming a US solar company.

Freyr sent formal notice to the Coweta County Development Authority on 21 January notifying it of its decision to discontinue plans to build a battery manufacturing plant in the county, along with its intention to terminate an associated grant and incentive arrangement.
Freyr stated the decision was made reluctantly due to realigned near-term strategic goals and does not reflect any concern with the support from local authorities.
The strategic realignment refers to Freyr's pivot from a European-focused battery manufacturing company to a US-focused and headquartered solar one.
As part of this pivot, Freyr recently acquired a 5GW solar module manufacturing facility in Wilmer, Texas, from Trina Solar following a difficult period for its battery activities.
The company was established as Freyr Battery in Norway in 2018.
On 10 February, Freyr announced that its headquarters will now be located in Austin, Texas.

The sale of the Coweta County site to an undisclosed party will generate US$50 million in proceeds, amounting to US$22.5 million in net proceeds after repaying previously received state and local grants.

In mid-2022, Freyr was targeting 100GWh of battery manufacturing capacity mainly from its Norway and European projects, with energy storage accounting for a huge chunk of provisional offtake agreements.
Freyr purchased the Coweta County site in late 2022 to expand to the US.

In late 2023, Freyr announced a 'pause' on all European investments to focus on its US project because the Inflation Reduction Act’s (IRA) generous incentives for clean energy manufacturing made European projects less competitive, according to then-CEO Birger Steen.

Freyr acquired the Texas solar factory from China-based Trina Solar on the day of the 2024 US presidential election result, which saw Trump win a second term and led to speculation that Trina sold due to concerns about potential measures against Chinese companies.
The Georgia battery project cancellation follows US firm Kore Power cancelling its plans to build a gigafactory in Arizona and instead continuing to focus on using China to manufacture cells.

Freyr and Kore’s decisions coincide with dramatic price falls from China, which dominates the lithium-ion battery industry, making new US battery manufacturing projects less competitive even with tax credit incentives.
Freyr still has assets and manufacturing projects in development in Norway but based on recent disclosures and statements looks likely to divest them.
source_title: Freyr continues US solar pivot, cancels Georgia gigafactory plan

facts: FREYR has completed its first production trial of unit cells using the full capabilities of the Casting and Unit Cell Assembly at the Customer Qualification Plant (“CQP”).

With this achievement, FREYR is the first company globally to successfully demonstrate the functionality of giga scale production line equipment for the second generation of the 24M Technologies SemiSolidTM platform.
The CQP team will now move on to producing unit cells using the full automation of the production line equipment in a continuous process.

Following that milestone, teams at the CQP plan to proceed to manufacturing multi-layer pouch battery cells in Q2 2024 for customers while continuously improving the electrochemical and electromechanical performance of the technology platform.

FREYR teams at the CQP will continue to fine tune processes before commencing continuous operation to harvest production data and product quality evaluations at scale through powder preparation, slurry mixing, electrode casting, unit cell merge, stacking, pouch singulation, charging, formation and aging.
The operational update covers progress at the CQP in Mo i Rana, Norway.

Teams, vendors, and partners at the CQP have reached the company’s most significant technical milestone to date by producing unit cells with the full functionality of the Casting and Unit Cell Assembly machine, the novel component of the SemiSolid™ platform.

While significant work remains to further streamline automation, improve material composition, and complete the final stages of commissioning, this accomplishment marks the completion of critical path equipment commissioning.
The milestone is expected to enable FREYR to begin increasing production speeds and manufacturing multi-layer battery sample cells in Q2 2024.
Mike Brose serves as FREYR’s Head of the Asset Mo team.

Mike Brose commented that demonstrating the ability to manufacture cells with the next-generation Casting and Unit Cell Assembly positions the team to move into trial production using a continuous process, perform data and performance analysis, surge production speeds, and proceed to manufacturing mechanically complete sample cells in Q2.
Adam Holland serves as CEO of Mpac Group plc.
Adam Holland commented on behalf of the Mpac team that Mpac is proud to contribute to FREYR’s mission to create a sustainable future with next-generation battery technology.

The production trial was enabled by the successful integration of casting webs across the cathode, anode, and merge units of the Casting and Unit Assembly equipment using the highly advanced Multi-Carrier System (“MCS”).
The MCS is the largest of its kind globally according to FREYR’s automation partner Siemens AG.
Brian Holliday serves as Managing Director at Siemens Digital Industries.

Brian Holliday commented that the achievement was an amazing team effort between FREYR, Mpac, Festo and Siemens working together at the leading edge to highly automate battery production, marking a huge step forward for next generation technology designed to enhance industry sustainability.
Following the trial, FREYR is following a three-step road map to producing sample cells in Q2 2024.
The first step involves conducting a cell production trial using a continuous process with no interruptions to fine tune the equipment.
The second step involves surging production speeds for short periods of time to demonstrate GWh scale manufacturing capability for the casting of electrodes.
The third step involves producing multi-layer, mechanically in-spec pouch battery sample cells in Q2 2024.
Birger Steen serves as FREYR’s Chief Executive Officer.

Birger Steen commented that proving the ability to navigate electromechanical challenges of producing batteries on novel manufacturing equipment demonstrates FREYR’s technical and operational qualifications as an emerging industrialization partner, and the team is moving to replicate what was just done in a continuous process before commencing production of multi-layer pouch battery sample cells.

Conventional lithium-ion battery technology is estimated to account virtually all current global commercial production, with only 6% originating from the U.S., according to a recent International Energy Agency report.
The development of novel, U.S.-based technologies such as the SemiSolidTM platform has the potential to enhance regional energy security and long-term competitiveness of U.S. battery producers.
In conjunction with the milestone, FREYR is evaluating additional opportunities to leverage the CQP to incubate additional technological advances in cooperation with key stakeholders in the U.S.
Tom Einar Jensen serves as Co-founder and Executive Chair of FREYR.

Tom Einar Jensen commented that FREYR has demonstrated the ability of its teams and global partners to scale a novel U.S. IP centric battery technology platform, noting that the work is just beginning despite representing a proud moment for the company.

The cost of imported battery solutions to target markets is highly dynamic, so the development of U.S. origin technologies at scale is critical if the U.S. is to establish a viable competitive position in the global battery ecosystem.
Currently, 95% of battery solutions for the energy storage market are imported from Asia.

With the energy transition accelerating in the U.S., homegrown supply of critical energy infrastructure is a fundamental pillar to regional energy security as well as job creation and sustained economic growth.

FREYR’s aspiration is that the CQP, as a first of its kind facility based on U.S. Intellectual Property, could ultimately serve a unique role as a center of innovation and scaling platform for competitive battery solutions and the backbone of a new, increasingly localized energy system.

FREYR Battery is a developer of clean, next-generation battery cell production capacity with a mission to accelerate the decarbonization of global energy and transportation systems by producing sustainable, cost-competitive batteries.
FREYR seeks to serve the primary markets of energy storage systems (ESS) and commercial mobility, and maintains an ambition to serve the passenger electric vehicles market (EV).
FREYR is operating its Customer Qualification Plant (CQP) for technology development in Mo i Rana, Norway.
FREYR is commencing development of the Giga America battery manufacturing project in Coweta County, Georgia, in the U.S.

The press release includes a cautionary statement noting that forward-looking statements cover the development, construction, timeline, capacity, and usefulness of production facilities or gigafactories, as well as the ability to complete trials, surge speeds, produce sample cells, achieve improvements, commence automated production, stay on track with completion cadence, implement cathode testing/technology, and execute business/technology strategies.

These forward-looking statements involve significant risks and uncertainties that could cause actual results to differ materially from expected results, with many factors outside FREYR’s control being difficult to predict.

Additional risk factors are detailed in SEC filings including Form S-3 (filed Sept 1, 2022), Form S-4 (filed Sept 8, 2023, amended Oct 13, 19, and 31, 2023), Form 10-K (filed Feb 29, 2024), and Form 10-Q (filed May 8, 2024).
FREYR intends to use its website’s Investor Relations sections as a channel of distribution to disclose information to investors and the public.
FREYR also intends to use social media channels X (former Twitter) and LinkedIn to communicate with the public and investors about its progress, products, and other matters.
source_title: FREYR Battery Provides Operations Update - Battery Tech Association

facts: FREYR Battery reported a net loss of $10 million or $0.07 per share for the quarter ended September 30, 2023.
The company's net loss for the same period last year was $94 million.
Net income from last year's period included a $70 million non-cash loss on warrant liability fair value adjustment due to changes in stock price.
For Q3 of this year, FREYR recognized a $24 million non-cash gain on the warrant liability item.
The company reported a net loss of $48 million or $0.34 per share for the nine months ended September 30, 2023.
Net cash spent during the first nine months of 2023 was $235 million, including $169 million on capital expenditures.
During Q3, FREYR spent $41 million on capital expenditures, with $32 million allocated to Giga Arctic and about $7.5 million to the customer qualification plan and test center.
Capital expenditures were partly offset by a receipt of a $3.5 million grant in the United States.
FREYR ended Q3 2023 with $328 million in cash, cash equivalents, and restricted cash, and holds no debt.
The company is advancing its Giga America battery manufacturing project located in Georgia.
Giga America will utilize a 24M semi-solid platform technology as one of two parallel development tracks.
A second track for Giga America involves leveraging conventional battery technology to mitigate technology risk and potentially accelerate US market entry.
The Georgia site is large enough to accommodate both a conventional and a 24M production facility with plenty of room to spare.
FREYR terminated its previously discussed two-line fast track project (Phase 1A) for Giga America due to decreased value of the time advantage related to CQP delays.
The company is now focusing on the larger Phase 1B project at the Georgia site, which aligns with its DOE financing plan.
FREYR aims to achieve a potential Final Investment Decision (FID) for the larger 24M-based project, along with DOE and equity financing, sometime late in 2024.
The company is progressing through the US Department of Energy Title 17 loan process for Giga America and is awaiting invitations to part two of the application.
Section 45X of the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) provides annual production tax credits that support the financing of Giga America.
FREYR is staying vigilant for applicable federal grant opportunities in the US for its business.

The company plans to redomicile from Luxembourg to the United States, specifically Delaware, to expand equity index inclusion potential and align with the country offering the highest bids for battery manufacturing at scale.
A special shareholder meeting to approve the redomicile transaction is set for December 15, requiring a 50% vote of outstanding shares and a two-thirds majority of those voting in favor.
FREYR expects to close the redomicile transaction by year-end 2023.
The company initiated a cost rationalization program to reduce its total run rate cash spending by over 50% in 2024.
This cost-cutting initiative extends the company's liquidity runway to two-plus years and into 2026.
FREYR will not authorize any significant new capital expenditures in 2024 until new project-level financing is secured for Giga America.
The company targets an annual cash burn rate in 2024 less than half of that in 2023, with a quarterly burn rate well under $30 million.
FREYR will not raise common equity from shareholders in 2024 due to its liquidity position and lower burn rate.
Project-level equity financing for Giga America remains available but is tied to getting the CQP up and running with testable batteries expected again in 2024.
The company is pursuing conventional technology partnerships to complement its 24M semi-solid technology and unlock avenues to financing and commercial development.
FREYR is leveraging its Energy Transition Acceleration Coalition (ETAC) to explore partnership-based upstream opportunities and address supply chain decarbonization.
Jeremy Bezdek serves as Executive VP of Corporate Development and President of FREYR Battery US, leading the Giga America update.
Birgir Steen serves as Chief Executive Officer of FREYR Battery.
Oscar Brown serves as Chief Financial Officer of FREYR Battery.
Jan Arve Haugan serves as Chief Operating Officer of FREYR Battery.
Jeffrey Spittel serves as VP of Investor Relations for FREYR Battery.

Dr. Dan Steingard, a FREYR Board member and co-director of the Columbia University Electric Chemical Energy Center, leads a newly formed technology advisory board to assist with scaling challenges at the Customer Qualification Plan (CQP).

The company has implemented changes in project governance and heightened coordination with vendors and ecosystem partners at the CQP to address delays in achieving automated production of inspect cells.
Commissioning of the casting unit cell assembly equipment at the CQP is proving more difficult and time-consuming than previously envisaged.
The timeline to achieve automated production of inspect cells has pushed beyond the previous goal of the fourth quarter 2023.
FREYR is not currently forecasting any capital expenditures for Giga Arctic in 2024, prioritizing liquidity for the CQP scale-up and Giga America development.
The company recently announced it was awarded a €100 million grant for Giga Arctic by the European Innovation Fund (EUIF), though no cash has been received yet.
FREYR will continue to work with Norwegian and European government stakeholders to establish framework conditions that place Giga Arctic on globally competitive economic terms.
The company's strategic focus includes advancing the commissioning and 24M scale-up processes at the CQP while pursuing conventional technology partnerships and US redomicile.
source_title: FREYR Battery (NYSE:FREY) Q3 2023 Earnings Call Transcript - Insider Monkey

facts: A $2.57 billion FREYR Battery production plant will soon be built in Coweta County, Georgia.
The developer, FREYR Battery, recently purchased a 368-acre plot at the Bridgeport Industrial Park site for the project.
The site location was selected based on key operational, logistical, and financial criteria.
FREYR and its joint venture partner, Koch Strategic Platforms, have plans of building the new battery plant in multiple phases.
Both companies are contributing a total of $70 million to commence the initial phase of the FREYR Battery production plant.
Koch is investing $50 million for the initial phase.
FREYR is providing the remaining 20 million for the initial phase.
FREYR will also apply for U.S. Department of Energy funds and other grants to raise more funding.
The focus of the new plant will be mostly on battery energy storage solutions.
Energy storage is particularly important for wind and solar energy systems in the U.S.
The project’s initial phase will include a cell production module of approximately 34 GWh.
The cell production module was designed based on the next generation of 24M Technologies for the SemiSolid™ production platform in the U.S.
The new Battery production plant is expected to create about 723 jobs.
The project is likely to provide capital investments worth more than $2.6 a billion by 2029.
FREYR is targeting a broader global plan of building up to 200-gigawatt hours of capacity by 2030.
Governor Brian Kemp welcomed the developers to the state and expressed pleasure with the new plant.
Governor Brian Kemp said FREYR was the latest company to bring transformational investments and opportunities to communities in the Peach State.
Governor Brian Kemp said he was looking forward to witnessing their growing impact on Coweta and the surrounding region.
source_title: $2.57 bn FREYR Battery production plant for construction in Georgia

facts: Norway’s battery cell start-up Freyr is about to go public on the US stock exchange through merging with Alussa Energy Acquisition Corporation.
Upon closing, the combined company will be renamed Freyr Battery and listed on NYSE under the ticker symbol ‘FREY’.
Following Northvolt, Freyr is another Nordic company planning to build battery cell factories in the gigawatt range.
The merger is expected to provide equity proceeds of approximately $850 million.
This includes a $600 million PIPE at $10.00 per share with Koch Strategic Platforms and Glencore having already joined the investment.
Koch Strategic Platforms represents Koch Industries’ attempt to buy into new technology.
Glencore is connected to the gas and oil industry.
Freyr signed an LOI with Glencore to supply up to 3,700 tonnes of high-grade sustainably-sourced cobalt metal cut cathodes produced at Nikkleverk, Glencore’s Norwegian operations.
Freyr plans to install a battery cell production capacity of up to 43 GWh in four years.
The company previously hoped to get 32 GWh online by 2023 as reported in August 2019.
Earlier plans included building fast-track production plants with a capacity of 2 GWh per year alongside local site partner Mo Industrial Park.
Daniel Barcelo, Chief Executive Officer and Founder of Alussa Energy, stated that Norway with entrepreneurial cities like Mo i Rana provides a great foundation for Freyr’s Gigafactories.
Barcelo noted they evaluated over 75 investment opportunities across the global energy and energy transition sectors before selecting Freyr as a frontline player.
The CEO stressed a commitment to environmentally-friendly production, claiming they would build one of the most environmentally friendly battery cells in the world.
Torstein Dale Sjøtveit, Founder and Executive Chairman of Freyr, stated that growth potential is enabled by cutting-edge technology and access to clean, renewable energy.
Freyr intends to supply battery cells at more competitive prices than other suppliers using energy from renewable sources.
In January, Freyr signed a licensing agreement with cell specialist 24M Technologies.
The US American spin-off formerly known as A123 develops semi-solid technology and granted Freyr rights to unlimited battery cell production based on current and all future technologies.
Freyr expects the partnership with 24M Technologies to materially reduce manufacturing costs and provide a highly competitive market position.
Freyr estimated the total required capital at $4.5 billion or 40 billion crowns (4.5 billion US dollars or 3.4Bn euros).
To start construction that year, the company stated it would need two billion dollars or 1.6 billion euros.
After the merger, the pro forma equity value of the combined company was expected to approximate $1.4 billion.
Freyr introduced Siemens Energy and Elkem as interested customers in October 2020.
A memorandum of understanding is in place with both groups covering cell delivery and the supply of anode active materials.
Elkem plans to produce anode materials in Herøya Industrial Park as part of a joint commitment to develop and test new anode active materials.
Freyr Battery or Pubco’s common stock is expected to start trading on NYSE as FREY in the second quarter of 2021.
Current shareholders will own approximately 30% of the combined company after transaction close.
The boards of directors of both Alussa Energy and Freyr have approved the proposed business combination, which is now subject to shareholder approval.
source_title: Norway's Freyr merges to list on NYSE - electrive.com

facts: FREYR Battery has announced the selection and purchase of a site in Coweta County, Georgia for its planned Giga America battery plant.
The company selected the Bridgeport Industrial Park site through a rigorous evaluation process based on key operational, logistical, and financial criteria.
The site was chosen for its suitability to co-locate modular upstream and downstream projects with the Giga America cell manufacturing facility.
FREYR is planning to initiate detailed project engineering of Giga America in the coming months.
The initial phase of Giga America is planned to be a cell production module of approximately 34 GWh.
The initial phase will utilize the next-generation of 24M’s U.S.-based SemiSolid™ production platform.
The initial phase has an initial projected capital cost of $1.7 billion.
The company is evaluating value accretive upstream and downstream modules as well as additional cell production lines.
These additional modules and lines are expected to bring total capital investments to more than $2.6 billion through 2029.
Upon completion of all contemplated construction phases, the Giga America complex is expected to be one of the largest battery cell manufacturing developments globally.
FREYR’s Co-Founder and Chief Executive Officer Tom Einar Jensen stated the announcement underscores FREYR’s ambition to develop a very strong and near-term operational footprint in the United States.
Jensen noted expanding into the U.S. has been a foundational aspect of FREYR’s long-term strategy from its inception.

Jensen commented that with the recent passage of the Inflation Reduction Act, they expect U.S. demand for ESS, passenger EV and other electric mobility applications to grow rapidly over the next decade.
Governor Brian Kemp said job creators and innovators from all over the world bring their operations to Georgia because they know they will have success here.
Kemp stated Georgia is proud to welcome FREYR as the latest company to bring transformational investments and opportunity to its communities.
Kemp noted FREYR will be a valued addition to Georgia's No. 1 state for business ranking.
Kemp expressed looking forward to their growing impact on Coweta and the surrounding region.
Coweta County Board of Commissioners Chairman Bob Blackburn said they are thrilled to welcome FREYR Battery to the Coweta County community.
Blackburn stated with the addition of FREYR, Coweta County is quickly becoming a premier destination for top-tier manufacturing industries that support the U.S. economy and beyond.
Blackburn expressed looking forward to the innovation, opportunities, and success that FREYR will generate.

In accordance with ambitions to accelerate development of Giga America in parallel with the ongoing construction of Giga Arctic in Mo i Rana, Norway, the company is intensifying and broadening its financing efforts.
The total package of financial incentives for Giga America includes robust assistance collectively provided by The State of Georgia and Coweta County.
The incentive package also includes expected production tax credits associated with the recently passed Inflation Reduction Act.
FREYR intends to apply with the U.S. Department of Energy for packages that could include a grant and/or direct loans to assist with the development of Giga America.
The U.S. energy storage market is already the largest in the world.
According to Rystad Energy, to satisfy a 1.6-degree scenario, cumulative collective storage demand of 4.5 TWh from the U.S. and Europe will be required through 2030.
Under the same scenario, Rystad expects annual collective storage demand in the U.S. and Europe to reach 1.4 TWh by 2030.
This represents a 34-fold increase from projected corresponding annual 2022 demand.
Jensen added that FREYR is deeply committed to decarbonizing the transportation and energy sectors.
Jensen stated they expect to make meaningful investments to spur job creation and the eventual development of localized, decarbonized supply chains in the U.S.
Jensen noted these efforts aim to enhance energy security and economic activity and meet the growing demand for energy storage solutions in the U.S. market.
The Giga America project will be based on the SemiSolid™ technology of FREYR’s U.S.-based licensing partner, 24M.
The SemiSolid™ platform enables capital and energy efficient production of lithium-ion batteries at scale.
FREYR is currently evaluating clean power supply solutions for the Giga America project with U.S. Independent Power Producers (IPPs).
The company’s long-term ambition is to replicate the strategy employed at Giga Arctic to produce next-generation batteries that are among the cleanest anywhere in the world.
At 10:00 am EST (16:00 CET) FREYR Battery will host a launch event live from Coweta County, Georgia.
The webcasted presentation will be streamed online in English.
A recording of the event will be made available after the presentation on FREYR’s website and YouTube channel.
source_title: North American Clean Energy - FREYR Battery Announces Plans for U.S. Gigafactory in Georgia

facts: Shares of tire maker Goodyear Tire & Rubber Co (GT +5.16%) jumped 10.1% in September after the company outlined growth plans for the rest of the decade.
At an investor meeting, Goodyear said that it plans to increase annual segment operating income to $3 billion by 2020.
Goodyear plans to generate cumulative free cash flow of $4.3 billion to $4.9 billion between 2017 and 2020.
The company has an $8.6 billion market cap and $5.7 billion in debt on the balance sheet.
Management said it would spend $4 billion on a combination of capital expenditures, restructuring, debt repayment, and returns to shareholders.
The dividend was increased to 10 cents per share, which will be payable to shareholders of record on November 1, 2016.
Goodyear noted that tires are needed for cars, trucks, electric vehicles, and ride-sharing platforms.
Goodyear is optimizing its business to take advantage of industry trends, such as complex large rim tires.
The article states Goodyear is well positioned to generate strong returns and cash flow in the next five years.
source_title: Why Goodyear Tire & Rubber Co's Shares Popped 10% in September | The Motley Fool

facts: Goodyear is one of the world’s leading tire companies, with one of the most recognizable brand names.
It develops, manufactures, markets and distributes tires for most applications and manufactures and markets rubber-related chemicals for various uses.

The company has established itself as a leader in providing services, tools, analytics and products for evolving modes of transportation, including electric vehicles, autonomous vehicles and fleets of shared and connected consumer vehicles.
Goodyear was the first major tire manufacturer to offer direct-to-consumer tire sales online.
It offers a proprietary service and maintenance platform for fleets of shared passenger vehicles.

Through its worldwide network of aligned dealers and wholesale distributors and its own retail outlets and commercial truck centers, Goodyear offers its products for sale to consumer and commercial customers, along with repair and other services.
It is one of the world’s largest operators of commercial truck service and tire retreading centers.
Following the acquisition of Cooper Tire in 2021, the company manufactures its products in 57 facilities in 23 countries and has operations in most regions of the world.
Its two Innovation Centers are located in Akron, Ohio, and Colmar-Berg, Luxembourg.
In 2021, Goodyear launched the ElectricDrive GT, the first electric vehicle replacement tire in North America.
The ElectricDrive GT is an ultra-high performance, all-season tire that delivers long-lasting treadwear and a quiet ride for EV drivers and passengers.
Cooper Tire became part of the Goodyear family when it was acquired in 2021, with its portfolio complementing Goodyear’s product lineup across the value spectrum.
The acquisition closed in June 2021, making Goodyear an even stronger U.S. leader in the global tire industry.
The transaction nearly doubled Goodyear's presence in China, a market with tremendous growth prospects.
The combined company expects to deliver $250 million of synergies, representing a 50% increase from the initial plan.
A complete integration of Cooper Tire's business is expected to continue into 2023.
Goodyear tires are designed to handle the added stresses of increased vehicle weight, regenerative braking and higher torque associated with electric vehicles.
The company's tire technology helps extend vehicle range through reduced rolling resistance.
Goodyear has secured a presence on nearly half of the electric vehicle platforms produced in Europe.
In Europe, Goodyear launched the FuelMax Endurance, described as its most versatile and fuel-efficient commercial tire yet.
Goodyear added DrivePoint to its Total Mobility offering, a tool enabling optimal tire maintenance and improved tire longevity and fuel efficiency.
The company launched Goodyear SightLine, a suite of technologies using sensors with cloud-based algorithms to identify and address problems in real-time.
SightLine became the first tire intelligence solution for cargo van fleets, initially available in North America and Europe.
At the 2020 Consumer Electronics Show, Goodyear announced AndGo by Goodyear, a vehicle servicing marketplace platform connecting mobility companies with qualified service providers.
Last year, the company experienced five-fold growth in vehicle maintenance jobs through its fleet solutions business.

Goodyear formed a partnership with Gatik, the first autonomous middle-mile logistics service provider in North America, to advance mobility solutions for the autonomous B2B short-haul logistics industry.
Gatik’s medium-duty fleet will use tires equipped with tire intelligence technology powered by Goodyear SightLine.
Goodyear began partnering with AmpUp, an EV-charging technology leader, to bring charging solutions to fleet customers considering electrification.
An investment was made through Goodyear Ventures, the company's venture capital fund, to participate in AmpUp's journey.
Goodyear formed a strategic relationship with Plus, a global provider of self-driving truck technology, to provide connected “intelligent” tires and optimized autonomous trucking solutions.
The company began piloting select tire monitoring solutions with Voyomotive, a connected car technology company.

In an industry first, Goodyear developed a non-pneumatic (“airless”) tire and wheel assembly to support autonomous vehicle transportation in a city setting with the Jacksonville (Florida) Transportation Authority.
The airless tire was also adopted by Starship Technologies, a global provider of autonomous delivery services operating self-driving delivery robots.
In Europe, Goodyear partnered with automotive leader ZF to integrate fleet and tire management solutions through a customizable telematics-based offering.
Goodyear began collaborating with Dutch research organization TNO on a demonstration vehicle to test the interface of connected tires with a vehicle's anti-lock brake system.
Goodyear announced a goal to achieve net-zero greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions by 2050, with minimal reliance on offsets.
The company established new intermediate-term emission-reduction targets aligned with Science-Based Targets initiative (SBTi) standards.
Goodyear announced a plan to procure 100% renewable energy in most of its facilities across Europe and Turkey by year-end.
The company is working with third-party experts and developers, including Monolith, to develop carbon black produced from methane and biomethane suitable for tires.

In January, Goodyear scientists and engineers constructed a demonstration tire with 70 percent sustainable-material content out of thirteen featured ingredients, including technical grade polyester sourced from recycled plastic bottles.
The company is on track to reach its goal of a 100% sustainable-material and maintenance-free tire by 2030 and complete petroleum oil replacement in its products by 2040.
Net sales were $17,478 million in 2021, compared to $12,321 million in 2020.
Tire units sold reached 169.3 million in 2021, reflecting a 34.3% increase compared to 2020.
Research and development expenditures totaled $496 million in 2021.
Capital expenditures were $981 million in 2021.
The company had 72,000 associates as of the end of 2021.
Goodyear is experiencing shortages of qualified and reliable workers, particularly in the U.S., leading to manufacturing inefficiencies, increased training costs, and higher wages.
To address labor shortages, Goodyear accelerated hiring, increased training capacity, and started adjusting future investment plans to consider worker availability.
A severe winter storm in the first quarter of 2021 caused temporary shutdowns of three chemical facilities and limited production at three tire manufacturing facilities in the U.S.
The company estimates the negative impact of the Q1 2021 winter storm on its earnings was approximately $54 million ($44 million after-tax and minority).
source_title: Full text of "Goodyear Tire & Rubber Co. 2021 Annual Report"

entity_name: Goodyear Tire & Rubber Co. Topeka Plant
canonical_name: Goodyear Tire & Rubber Co. Topeka Plant
facts: AAM Field Coordinators Mike Mitchell and Meghan Hasse recently toured the Goodyear Plant in Topeka, Kan.
The Goodyear Tire & Rubber Co. plant in Topeka originally opened in 1944 to help with the American war effort during World War II.

The federal government picked Topeka for the plant because of its location near several military installations and because it is 140 miles from the exact center of the United States, making it safer from attack by foreign armies.
When the war ended in 1945, the federal government sold the plant to Goodyear to manage.
Since its sale, the plant has served as one of the biggest private employers in the state.
At one point, the Topeka plant made every tire that Goodyear produced, including for automobiles, bicycles, and aircraft.
The plant hit its peak employment at about 4,400 people in the 1970s.
Today, about 1,550 employees work at the plant on a 6 2/3 schedule, meaning operations run 24 hours a day except for a brief period on Sundays.
Workers at the plant make a range of tires, including for buses, off-road vehicles, medium radial truck tires for semi-trucks, and military vehicles.
Goodyear also makes the largest tire in the world at the plant — the 59/80R63, which is 13 feet tall and weighs close to 12,000 pounds.
The Topeka plant is the second largest tire plant in the world at 3 million square feet, which equals 69 acres under roof.
Topeka’s workers are represented by the USW Local 307 and the NCFO SEIU Local 7.
Collectively, workers at the plant make $150 million each year in wages and benefits.
More than $24 million a year returns to the community in local spending from employees, not including capital projects.
Employees have given more than $1.3 million to the United Way since 2008.
Goodyear Topeka is one of the largest supporters of Junior Achievement in the state.
Employees hold four blood drives every year.
At one time, it looked like Goodyear would close the plant.
Since 2008, Goodyear has invested more than $600 million in this plant.
Company officials maintain that Goodyear is committed to “making its operations work in North America.”
The article advocates that policymakers should check out AAM’s Blueprint for the Future to support domestic manufacturers and ensure a level playing field for American workers.
Brigadier Gen. John Adams writes in ReMaking American Security that reliance on foreign suppliers puts America’s military at risk.
source_title: Field Update: AAM Visits the Goodyear Plant in Kansas - Alliance for American Manufacturing

facts: Great Dane is being recognized by the Georgia General Assembly in commemoration of the company’s 125th anniversary.
Rick Mullininx serves as president and COO of Great Dane.
The 125-year milestone was marked by proclamations read in both the House and Senate chambers.
Great Dane received a special commendation from Governor Brian Kemp.
The recognition highlights the company’s enduring history of innovation, quality, and leadership in the transportation industry.
State Rep. Ron Stephens from Savannah, Ga., sponsored the House proclamation.
Savannah, Ga. is the city where Great Dane was founded in 1900.
State Sen. Ben Watson from Savannah sponsored the Senate proclamation.
Chris Hammond, Executive Vice President of Sales at Great Dane, accepted the Senate proclamation during the Senate session.
Great Dane stated that its 125 years of success reflects the resilience of its teams and the trust built with customers and partners across generations.
The company underscored its focus on the future, enabled by technology and customer relationships.
source_title: Great Dane honored by the Georgia General Assembly - NewsBreak

facts: JCB Inc., a U.K.-based manufacturer of farming and construction equipment, moved its North American headquarters from Baltimore to Savannah in 2000 and added a manufacturing plant.
Graeme Macdonald is the president of JCB Inc.
Since arriving in Savannah, JCB grew from 150 employees to more than 500 and expanded its product line from one to four.
JCB added 100 workers last year to begin filling an initial $140 million order for 365 backhoe loaders capable of reaching speeds of 60 miles per hour.
Over the next four years, JCB will build more than 800 units under a U.S. Army contract.
The backhoes will be sent to Iraq and Afghanistan to keep up with military convoys.
The Army contract is the largest in JCB’s 62-year history.
JCB is a global company, privately owned, with one shareholder (the chairman), allowing it to make quick, long-term decisions without quarterly stock market pressure.
JCB collaborates with SCAD (Savannah College of Art and Design) on industrial design projects through The Creative Coast Alliance (TCCa).
Chuck Stallworth is vice president of the Savannah Economic Development Authority.
SCAD has a huge industrial design capability and acts as a business incubator that births ideas 24 hours a day.
The Savannah Economic Development Authority is working with SCAD to connect students with local business talent through a program called Coastal Connect, a program of The Creative Coast Alliance.
A group of about 60 wealthy individuals formed a private investment group called the Ariel Savannah Angel Partners (ASAP) to fund ideas from SCAD students and others.
In the last three years, The Creative Coast Alliance generated over 200 jobs and $11 million in salaries for knowledge-based companies.
SCAD has a formal collaboration with Electronic Arts (EA) where students work like apprentices producing actual work for current games.
Peter Weishar is dean of Film and Digital Media at SCAD.
Rick Winger is president of the Savannah Economic Development Authority.
A startup company called Carbon Motors, founded by former Ford executives, is in capital formation with ASAP investors.
Carbon Motors is designing a purpose car for law enforcement.
SCAD designed a seat for Carbon Motors that allows gun-wearing officers to more easily exit the vehicle during emergencies.
Chatham County Commissioner Pete Liakakis notes Savannah has the fastest growing port in the country, bringing in some $15 billion to all of Georgia.
The military (Army) at Fort Stewart and Hunter Field brings about $1.5 billion a year to the Savannah-Chatham County area.
In 2006, 600,000 tourists spent $1.4 billion in Chatham County.
Industries like JCB and Gulfstream bring in about $8 billion to $9 billion a year to the region.
Chatham County is funding a massive infrastructure improvement program topping $326 million within the next three years.
Some $100 million will be spent to build a new jail near I-16 on the city’s western border.
Valdosta-based Ambling Companies, Inc. announced plans for an $800 million mixed-use development on 54 acres along Savannah’s riverfront earlier this year.
The project, named Savannah River Landing, will add 280,000 square feet of retail space, the same amount of office space, 365 new hotel rooms, 600 condos, 100 townhouses, and 17 riverfront homes.
The state put up $8.8 million to extend the Riverwalk by 2,200 linear feet.
The Riverwalk attracts 6.5 million tourists to the city each year.
An explosion at the Imperial Sugar Company refinery in Port Wentworth claimed 12 lives and left dozens injured as of press time.
Imperial Sugar had owned the Port Wentworth facility since 1997.
Chatham County population was 238,410 in 2005; Savannah population was 128,483.
Unemployment in Chatham County was 4.6 percent in Jan. 2008; Georgia was 5.2 percent.
Per capita income in Chatham County was $31,691 in 2004; Georgia was $29,782.

Top manufacturing employers include Gulfstream Aerospace (5,400), Georgia-Pacific Savannah River Mill (1,400), International Paper (740), Great Dane Trailers (545), Imperial Sugar (450), JCB Americas (500), Savannah Morning News (380), Derst Baking Company (308), Colonial Chemical Solutions (285), and Tronox (280).
source_title: Savannah: Intellectual Capital - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: Great Dane Trailers is closing its manufacturing facility in Savannah, Ga.
The facility closure announcement was posted to the TruckersReport.com forum on October 15, 2008.
Forum participants clarified that Great Dane is a trailer manufacturing company rather than a trucking company.
Industry discussions indicated that Great Dane and other companies were laying off employees or facing impending layoffs due to disappearing jobs.

links.context: Original news article about the facility closure

source_title: Great Dane Trailers to close manufacturing facility in Savannah, Ga. | TruckersReport.com Trucking Forum | #1 CDL Truck Driver Message Board

facts: Great Dane has a trailer dealer and service presence near Douglasville, Georgia (GA).
Great Dane delivers high-quality trailers in Douglasville, Georgia (GA) built for durability, safety, and efficiency.
Great Dane trailers are crafted for long-haul trucking and heavy-duty loads.
Dry van trailers feature reinforced frames for maximum strength.
Reefer trailers are available for temperature-sensitive cargo.
Flatbed trailers are designed for versatile hauling needs.
Trailers include precision suspension systems for smoother rides and fuel efficiency.
Great Dane has a long-standing reputation for keeping fleets on the road through trained technicians providing expert trailer repair and maintenance.
Service covers all types of trailers, from dry vans to refrigerated and flatbeds.
Routine inspections and preventive maintenance help truckers avoid costly downtime, extend the life of equipment, and ensure deliveries stay on schedule.
Great Dane service centers are equipped with the latest diagnostic tools and replacement parts, capable of handling everything from minor adjustments to major overhauls.
Great Dane was one of the first trailer manufacturers to implement a nationwide network of certified service centers.
Trucker Guide is used to locate truck parts and service centers for Great Dane trailers in Douglasville, Georgia (GA).
source_title: Great Dane Trailer Dealer in and near Douglasville, Georgia (GA) | Trucker Guide

facts: Great Dane’s new refrigerated trailer plant is located in Statesboro, Georgia.
The facility operates under a 450,000-square-foot roof.
It is America’s newest truck trailer production facility.
Great Dane also builds refrigerated trailers in Brazil, Indiana, and Wayne City, Nebraska.
Michael Chitty serves as sales engineering for Great Dane.
The plant was designed with objectives including efficiency, product quality, environmental concerns, and ergonomics.
The company relied heavily on automation and designing the plant to minimize travel distance for raw materials, subassemblies, and components.
The company’s Savannah plant (now closed) required parts to travel between multiple buildings, resulting in excess travel that was reduced by a factor of nine in the Statesboro facility.
Management allowed the plant to gradually ramp up production following last year’s grand opening.
The plant built its first custom trailer late last summer for Rowland Transportation of Dade City, Florida.

Throughout the fall and winter, assembly increased as employment grew and the new workforce became increasingly familiar with the new plant layout and its technology that includes robotic welding and automated foaming.
Production is building momentum, especially with Great Dane’s receipt of a 200-unit order for Celadon.
The plant is approaching its capacity—50 refrigerated trailers per week with one shift.
The plant has room to grow toward targeted production of 100 trailers per week at full two shift capacity.
The building includes 12,000 square feet of office space and a 3,000-square-foot climate-controlled customer preview center.

The Statesboro plant is tooled to manufacture Great Dane’s new Everest refrigerated trailers, the TL model reefer for truckload carriers and the CL model reefer built especially for the needs of multi-temp and foodservice operations, especially those in the Southeast.
The Statesboro plant received a big boost recently with the receipt of a 200-unit order from Celadon.
The plant completed the first unit last month.
The reefers represent the first refrigerated trailers that Celadon, one of North America’s largest truckload carriers, has purchased from any manufacturer.
Dave Gilliland is Great Dane’s vice-president of national accounts.
The reefer trailers being built for Celadon are based on Great Dane’s Everest TL model.

The fleet also specified ThermoGuard reefer liner, which features a patented metal barrier layer in the sidewalls, ceiling and floor subpan to minimize thermal degradation of the trailer’s insulation over time.

Other features of Celadon’s customized Everest TL order include a wedge design for maximum rear opening height, Hendrickson HD air ride suspension, Bridgestone R197 Ecopia tires, Transtex side skirts, Carrier 2100 APX reefer unit with auto fresh air exchange and Intelliset temperature control, rear door switch, StarTrak GPS, side air chute, air return bulkhead and pallet stops.
The trailers are also designed for occasional rail usage.
The Statesboro plant was scheduled to complete the 200-trailer order at the end of July.
Engineers placed an L-shaped assembly line within the square 450,000-square-foot structure.
The trailer assembly line starts along the back wall of the plant and turns 90 degrees as it flows along the adjacent wall and back toward the front of the building.
Subassemblies flow from the front of the plant to the trailer assembly line, with termination points at the exact spot where they are needed on the assembly line.
Raw materials are delivered and unloaded close to where they are needed for subassemblies.
The plant has two sets of doors for deliveries—one set near the start of subassembly production through which about 70% of materials and components enter the facility.
A second set of doors primarily feeds materials used for floor assemblies.
Supplied parts are kept in a supermarket inside the plant, slashing the amount of motion inherent in any sort of outside storage.
The company continues to work on ways to reduce inventories by improving real-time communications.
Computer monitors are being installed on the plant floor to enable production workers to view drawings and specifications in order to improve accuracy and reduce lost time.
Great Dane shopped the world to develop specialized equipment for producing its trailers, including its proprietary foaming system.
Under controlled conditions, each cavity is automatically injected with the exact amount of foam required to fill it.
The foaming presses at the Statesboro plant are designed to resist several hundred tons of force generated during the foaming operation.
The foaming presses are 60 feet long, giving Great Dane the capability of manufacturing a 60-foot-long trailer or sidewalls for two 28-foot pups.
A robotic work cell automates the welding of rear door frames, and gantry-mounted guns take the work out of welding reefer flooring.
Automated rivet inserting equipment has been performing reliably, enabling conveyors to carry sidewalls filled with rivets to the rivet press for upsetting.

Automated torque equipment tightens lug nuts and U-bolts to required specifications, monitoring and recording actual torque specifications and generating a record of the torque applied on each trailer serial number at the time it was assembled.
Trailers move from one workstation to the next on conveyors to workstations throughout the plant.
“Air skates,” which use a thin cushion of compressed air to virtually eliminate friction, move trailers through areas where such devices offer advantages over the conveyor line.
The company tries to recycle 100% of what it doesn’t use.
The plant has different colored dumpsters for various types of scrap that the plant generates, including mild steel, stainless, and aluminum.
A building management system automatically controls lights, air quality and building temperature.
Load monitors are used to ensure optimal energy consumption.
There is extensive use of energy-saving compact fluorescent bulbs.
A water reclamation system is used for the wash bays.
Evaporative coolers add comfort in the plant on summer days, dropping temperatures 15 degrees from ambient but without the extensive electrical consumption of standard air conditioning systems.
Four large fans change the air inside the plant every 15 minutes.
The grand opening was held a little more than a year after Great Dane broke ground on the 118-acre site.
Bill Crown is president and chief executive officer of Great Dane.
During the planning process, the trailer industry suffered through its most significant downturn in a generation.
Great Dane’s newest manufacturing facility is built on a 118-acre site in Bulloch County, just 50 miles from the company’s roots in Savannah, Georgia.
The location was selected for a variety of reasons, including its economy.
As the state unemployment rate hovers above eight percent, the plant will bring more than 400 greatly needed jobs to the area.

Statesboro was selected for several reasons including availability of suitable space, easy access to the Interstate highway, and access to a qualified workforce (without heavy competition from other manufacturing concerns).
Proximity to Savannah was a key factor in selecting Statesboro.

Great Dane stopped manufacturing trailers in Savannah in 2009, but corporate offices remain there, including sales, marketing, customer service, administration, and engineering, along with research and development functions.
The company has further committed to the Savannah area by breaking ground on a new research and development campus, on the existing corporate office facility in Savannah.
Brandie Fuller is vice-president at Great Dane.

Before discontinuing manufacturing in Savannah, Great Dane had previously stopped producing dry vans there around 2001 due to the availability of five other strategically located Great Dane van plants across the US.
The plan for Statesboro was to keep a reefer manufacturing presence in the Southeast, which is described as their historical home market.
source_title: It’s lean. It’s green. And Great Dane’s new refrigerated trailer plant is hitting its stride | Trailer Body Builders

facts: Local and state leaders set aside one week annually to recognize the impact manufacturing has on the Georgia economy.
Bulloch County contains six manufacturing facilities: Braswell's, Briggs & Stratton, Brodie Meter, Great Dane Trailers, Loxscreen, and Viracon.
These manufacturers directly employ 1,729 people in Bulloch County.
The direct employment figure of 1,729 people is based on a study conducted by the Georgia Southern University Bureau of Business Research and Economic Development.
Manufacturing is identified as a key part of the Bulloch County economy.
source_title: Inside Bulloch Business with DeWayne Grice - Manufacturing key part of Bulloch economy - Statesboro Herald

facts: Gainesville-Hall County Metro Area, Georgia is ranked #1 in the Milken Institute’s 2025 Annual Ranking of the Nation’s Best-Performing Cities among small metropolitan areas.
The metro area tops the index after being featured among the BPC’s top 10 small metropolitan areas every year since 2016.
Raleigh, NC tops the index in large metropolitans.
Gainesville-Hall County and Raleigh’s rankings were fostered by strong job and wage growth and a robust high-tech presence.

Greater Hall Chamber of Commerce Chairman Glennis Barnes of Georgia Power stated gratitude for the #1 ranking, affirming long-term efforts to invest in infrastructure, support workforce development, and maintain a business-friendly environment.

Barnes noted that collaborative efforts have achieved consistent economic development success that has made the Gainesville-Hall County Metro Area a top performer for quality jobs, technology investment, and small business growth.

The Milken Institute Best-Performing Cities index is published annually since 1999 and reflects cities’ effectiveness at leveraging resources to promote economic growth and provide residents with access to essential services and infrastructure.

The index is based on job creation, wage growth, and the high-tech sector’s output growth, in addition to measures of access to economic opportunities such as housing affordability and broadband coverage.

Milken Institute senior director of research Maggie Switek, PhD, noted that Raleigh and Gainesville-Hall County have experienced remarkable employment and wage growth coupled with robust performance in their access to economic opportunities indicators.
The 2025 version of the index emphasizes jobs, wages, high-tech growth, housing affordability, and broadband coverage, in addition to metrics of community resilience and income inequality.
Gainesville Mayor Sam Couvillon called the Milken Institute selection an incredible honor, reaffirming the city’s meticulous actions and helping identify areas for improvement.
Mayor Couvillon stated the distinction will further motivate the Gainesville City Council, city management, and staff to keep economic trends and the community moving forward.

Gainesville-Hall County, Georgia is home to 47 Fortune 500 firms, more than 330 manufacturing and processing concerns, and more than sixty international companies representing twenty-one foreign countries.

The Gainesville-Hall County Economic Development Council is a public-private partnership of the Greater Hall Chamber of Commerce, Hall County, and the Cities of Gainesville, Flowery Branch, Lula, and Oakwood.

The Economic Development Council provides one-stop marketing and economic development services to support existing industry retention, workforce development, small business development, and business recruitment and retail development.
